In the process of the child's mental development, as study shows, not only is there internal reconstruction and improvement of separate functions, but interfunctional connections and relations change in a radical way. As a result, new psychological systems arise that unite in complex cooperation a number of separate elementary functions. We arbitrarily call these psychological systems, these units of a higher order that replace the homogeneous, single, elementary functions, the higher mental functions. Everything we have said thus far compels us to admit that the real, psychological formation that in the process of the child's development replaces his elementary, practical, and intellectual operations cannot be designated as anything but a psychological system. This concept also includes the complex combination of symbolic and practical activity which we have been continuously considering, the new relation of a number of single functions which are characteristic of the practical intellect of man, and the new unit to which this whole entity, heterogeneous in composition, has been brought in the course of development. We thus come to a conclusion directly opposite that which E. Thorndike44 establishes in his study of the intellect (1925). As we know, Thorndike proceeds from the assumption that higher mental functions are nothing other than a further development, a quantitative growth of associative connections of one and the same order and that these are the connections that form the base of the elementary processes. In his opinion, like phylogenesis, ontogenesis also exhibits a principal identity of connections of a psychological nature that form the base of lower and higher processes. Our study speaks against this assumption. Our study compels us to admit that connections of a different order are characteristic for those specific neoformations 61

62

Chapter 5

that we call psychological systems or higher mental functions. Since Thorndike's position, as he himself admits, is directed against the traditional dualism in the teaching on lower and higher forms of behavior and since the problem of overcoming traditional dualism is one of the basic methodological and theoretical problems of all of contemporary scientific psychology, we shall certainly analyze what kind of response to this problem (dualism or unity of higher and lower functions) we must give in light of the experimental studies we have done. But first we must explain one possible misunderstanding. The objections to Thorndike's theory may be directed most of all not along the line that interests us in this case, but along the line of elucidating the general insupportability of associationism and the whole mechanistic concept of intellectual development that is affirmed on the basis of this point of view. We shall put aside for now the question of the insupportability of the associative principle. We are interested in something else. Nevertheless, whether we admit the associative or the structural character of mental functions, the basic problem remains completely valid: can the higher mental functions be reduced in substantial, specific patterns to the lower or are they only a more complex and involved expression of the same patterns that prevail in lower forms, or, in their essence, construction, and method of activity are they subject due to their origin to the action of new laws unknown in the plan of elementary forms of behavior? We think that resolving this problem is connected with the change in basic point of view on which K. Lewin insists in contemporary psychology and which he designated as a transition from "the phenotypic to the conditional-genetic" point of view. Further, we think that psychological analysis, penetrating beyond phenomena that are externally visible and disclosing the internal structure of mental processes, specifically the analysis of the development of higher forms, compels us to admit unity, but not identity, of higher and lower mental functions. The question of dualism of lower and higher functions is not eliminated with the transition from the associative to the structural point of view. From this we see that even within structural psychology, there is always an argument between representatives of the two indicated views of the nature of higher processes. Some insist on admitting the distinction between the two types of mental processes and arrive at a strict delimitation of the two basic forms of activity, one of which is usually designated as a reactive type of activity and the other, whose distinctive point is that activity seems to arise primarily from personality, as a spontaneous type of activity. Representatives of this direction defend the position that we are forced in psychology to proceed from a mainly dualistic conception of both processes. A living being, they say, is not only a system that encounters stimuli, but also a system that pursues goals. Opponents of a sharp delimitation of higher processes as spontaneous activity and lower as reactive activity defend the opposite point of view. They attempt to show that this sharp dualism, this metaphysical opposition between two types of activity that are usually brought forth, does not actually exist. They try to disclose the reactive character of many factors, intraspontaneous forms of behavior, and the active character of factors that depend on the internal structure of the system itself in the reactive processes. They show that even in so-called spontaneous processes of behavior, the organism depends also on the nature of the stimulus and, conversely, in reactive processes, behavior also depends on the internal structure and state of the system itself. Others, like Lewin, see the solution to this problem in the concept of need, based on the fact that objects of the external world can have a definite relation to needs. They can have a positive or negative "character of an imperative."

Methods of Studying Higher Mental Functions

63

Thus, we see that neither rejection of the associative theory nor the structural point of view in themselves, without a special study of the problem, resolves it, but they eliminate or bypass the problem that interests us. 1Iue, the new point of view assists in overcoming the metaphysical character of traditional psychological dualism and acknowledges the essential unity of higher and lower functions with respect to internal and external factors acting in the processes of the one and the other. But here inevitably two new problems arise of themselves to which we cannot find the basic answer in the solutions usually recommended. The first consists in the fact that external and internal factors necessarily present in processes of the one type and the other may have different relative significance and consequently may determine the whole process of behavior in a qualitatively different way in each case. Are we not supposed to separate the higher processes in comparison with the lower empirically, not metaphysically? And the second consists in the fact that the separation between spontaneous and reactive forms of behavior may not coincide with the delimitation of actions controlled predominantly by internal needs and actions controlled by external stimuli. The Use of Tools by Animal and Man As our studies show, in genetic, functional, and structural respects, the higher processes represent such significant diversity that they must be placed into a special class, but delimiting the higher and lower functions does not coincide with separating the two types of activity of which we have spoken above. The higher form of activity is present wherever there is mastery of processes of one's own behavior and, first of all, its reactive functions. In subjecting to his will the process of his own reactions, man enters in this way into a substantially new relation with the environment, comes to a new functional exploitation of elements in the environment as stimuli-signs which he uses, depending on external means, and directs and controls his own behavior, controls himself from outside, compelling stimuli-signs to affect him, and elicits reactions that he desires. Internal regulation of goal-directed activity arises initially from external regulation. Reactive action elicited and organized by man himself ceases being reactive and becomes goal-directed. In this sense, the phylogenetic history of man's practical intellect is closely linked not only with mastery of nature, but also with mastery of himself. The history of work and the history of speech can scarcely be understood one without the other. ~~~~~~~~~~~~cl~~~~~

his will the forces of nature, but also the stimuli that induced and regulated his own behavior, subjecting his own powers to his will. This can be noted even at the earliest stages of human development. K. Buhler tells about special sticks for digging to which small sticks were attached at the upper end which were found in Borneo and the Celebes. Soil is plowed with the stick when rice is being sown, and the small stick makes a noise. This sound is something in the nature of a work cry or command which is intended to set a rhythm for the work. The sound of this gadget attached to the digging stick replaces the human voice or fulfills a function similar to it. This internal merging of the sign and the tool that found a material symbolic expression in the primitive digging stick indicates how early the sign (and later, its higher form, the word) begins to participate in man's operation of using tools and to fulfill an incomparable, unique, functional role in the general structure of these operations which are at the very beginning of the development of human work. This stick differs from the stick of the monkey in a very radical way, although it

64

Chapter 5

is undoubtedly connected with it genetically. If we ask ourselves what the radical psychological difference is between man's tool and that of the animal, we must answer our question with another question posed by Buhler in connection with considering the actions of the chimpanzee directed toward the future and guided by a conception of the external conditions that must sooner or later follow: to what kind of limitation of abilities of the chimpanzees should we ascribe the circumstance that they display not the least rudiments of cultural development regardless of the fact that a whole series of facts usually found only in civilizations, even the most primitive, can, without a doubt, be found in these animals? Kohler develops this idea further: the most primitive man prepares a digging stick even when he is not directly preparing to dig, when the objective conditions for using the tool are not realized in any kind of perceptible way. The fact that he provides himself with a tool in advance is undoubtedly connected with the beginning of culture. Man's action arising in the process of cultural-historical development of behavior is free action, that is, action not dependent on directly acting need or a directly perceived situation, an action directed toward the future. According to Kohler's observation presented elsewhere, monkeys are slaves of the visual field to a much greater extent than adult humans. This must have its basis, and it is not difficult to see that such a basis is at the same time a reliable criterion for genetic, functional, and structural delimitation of the two types of activity of which we spoke above. But instead of a metaphysical basis for this delimitation, prompted by our studies, we propose a historical basis, which fully agrees with the facts established by Kohler regarding the behavior of the chimpanzee. Thus, two types of activity which the psychologist must distinguish are the behavior of the animal and the behavior of man: activity that is the product of biological evolution and activity that arises in the process of the historical development of man. Life over time, cultural development, work, everything that distinguishes man from animal in the psychological sphere, is closely connected with the fact that in the process of historical development of man, mastery of himself, his own behavior, proceeded parallel to mastery of external nature. The stick of which Buhler speaks is a stick for the future. It is already a tool for work. In the beautiful expression of F. Engels, "work created man himself" (K. Marx and F. Engels, Works, Vol. 20, p. 486), that is, created the higher mental functions that mark man as man. Using a stick, primitive man masters from outside, with the help of a sign, processes of his own behavior and subordinates his actions to a goal, making external objects serve his activity-tools, soil, rice. In this sense, we can again return to the observation of K. Koffka which we have touched on in passing above. He asks, does it make sense to call the actions of the chimpanzee in Kohler's experiments voluntary actions? From the point of view of the old psychology, these actions, like non instinctive, nonautomatic, and therefore sensible actions, must undoubtedly be classified as voluntary actions. But the new psychology is completely justified in giving a negative answer to this question. In this sense, Koffka is unconditionally right. Only the action of man, subjected to his will, is voluntary action. In a beautiful analysis of the psychology of purposeful actions, K. Lewin completely clearly distinguishes free and voluntary purpose as a product of historical cultural development of behavior and as the distinguishing trait of human psychology. He says that surprising in itself is the fact that man has an exceptional freedom for the formation of any, even senseless, purposes. This freedom is characteristic for a cultured man. It is proper to an incomparably lesser degree to the child and, evidently, also to primitive man and probably distinguishes man from the animals

Methods of Studying Higher Mental Functions

65

closest to him to a greater extent than his more developed intellect. This delimitation coincides with the problem of mastery. As we tried to show above, the development of freedom of action is directly functionally dependent on the use of signs. The unique relation, word-action, which we have been studying the whole time, also occupies a central place in the ontogenesis of the practical intellect of man regardless of the fact that in the area of higher functions, ontogenesis repeats phylogenesis even less than in the area of elementary functions. Whoever follows the development of the free action of the child from this point of view will agree with Buhler's statement that the history of the development of the child's will has not yet been written. In order to lay a foundation for this history, we must first of all elucidate the relation of word to action that stands at the beginning of the formation of the child's will. At the same time, we will take the first, but decisive step toward elucidating the problem of the two types of human activity of which we spoke above.

Word and Act For some psychologists, the old saying "in the beginning was the word" still retains its validity. New studies, however, leave no doubt that the word does not stand at the beginning of the development of the child's mind. As Buhler correctly notes, on a similar basis, it was said that speech stands at the beginning of the creation of man. Perhaps. But prior to it, there is instrumental thinking. Practical intellect is genetically older than the verbal; action precedes the word, and even mental action precedes the mental word. But now, in confirming this correct idea, the act is usually overvalued to the detriment of the word. Usually it is thOUght that the relation of the word and the act (independence of the act from the word and primacy of action), which is characteristic for the earliest years, is maintained at all subsequent stages of development even OVer the whole course of life. This same Buhler, more careful than others, but expressing essentially the same opinion, believes that in man's later life, technical and instrumental thinking is to a much lesser degree connected with speech and concepts than other forms of thinking. This conviction is based on an erroneous assumption that the initial relations between separate functions remain unchanged over the whole course of development. Research also teaches the opposite. At each step, it teaches us to admit that all of the history of development of higher mental functions is nothing other than a change in initial interfunctional relations and connections and the appearance and development of new mental, functional systems. This pertains, wholly and completely, specifically to the interfunctional relation of word and act, which interests us now. Thgether with Gutzmann, we say: "If even following Goethe, we reject the higher evaluation of the word as a vocal word and together with him transform the biblical statement to ~t the beginning was the act,' it is possible nevertheless, to read those verses and understand them from the f:0int of view of development with a different stress: 'In the beginning was the act.'" 5 But Gutzmann46 makes another mistake. Objecting completely justifiably to the teaching on apraxia of H. Lipmann,47 who is inclined to consider the relation between action and speech and their disturbance in apraxia and aphasia as a relation of the general to the particular, he himself takes the position that word and action are completely independent. For Lipmann, aphasia is only a special case of apraxia, and speech as a special form of movement is only a specific case of action in general. Gutzmann justifiably objects to this conception that makes the word a

66

Chapter 5

specific function in the general conception of action. He indicates that only movement as a more general concept can encompass expressive movements (speech) on the one hand and, on the other, action as coordinate, parallel, coordinated, interrelated, more special concepts. To consider speech as a more particular case of action means to depend on an incorrect definition of the concept of action. The conception according to which speech and action are logically parallel and independent concepts and processes inevitably leads to the antigenetic point of view, to rejecting development, and to the metaphysical raising of this (and consequently to non intersection of speech and action) to the rank of an eternal law of nature and to ignoring the changeability of functional systemic connections and relations. Gutzmann for an instant, as he himself indicates, takes the point of view of the history of development, but only in order to delimit what was earlier from what was later. In the old pronouncement in which only the beginning was mentioned, he changes nothing except the logical stress. He is interested in what was in the beginning and what developed later and, consequently, what belongs to the more primitive, elementary, lower forms of behavior and what must be ascribed to more developed, complex, and higher functions. He says that speech always implies a higher degree of human development than even the highest expression of action, the act. But at the same time, Gutzmann, like most authors, takes the formal-logical position. He considers the relation of speech and action as a thing and not as a process; he takes it statically and not dynamically, not in movement; he considers it as eternal and unchangeable, while it is historical and assumes a different concrete expression at each stage of development. All of our studies lead us to the conviction that there cannot be a single formula that would encompass the whole range of variability of relations between speech and action at all stages of development and in all forms of disintegration. In truth, the dialectical character of development of functional systems cannot be adequately reflected in anyone constructive formallogical scheme of relations of concepts-neither in Lipmann's scheme nor in Gutzmann's scheme-since neither the one nor the other considers the movement of concepts and the processes that stand behind them, the changeability of relations, the dynamics and dialectics of development. As Gutzmann formulates his idea, practically implemented action has nothing in common with speech even if this word were taken in the broadest sense. If the premise of Gutzmann is correct for the beginning of development and describes the primary stages in the development of action, then for later stages of this process, it becomes radically erroneous. It reflects one instant, but not the whole process as a whole. For this reason, theoretical and clinical conclusions that can be reached from this position are valid in a very limited sphere, specifically, in the sphere of initial stages of development of the relation that interests us; to present them as a description of the process as a whole is to fall inevitably into contradiction with factual data on the development and disintegration of higher forms of action. We shall consider the contradiction between theory and facts. Gutzmann sees the basic difference between action and word to be that, following Wundt, he considers volitional action as an affect, as a clearly expressed unilateral, personal relation of the person acting to the external world; here communication of internal states, typical for speech and all expressive movements, is relegated to the background and has a peripheral significance. While the internal character of action is predominantly personal and egocentric even with altruistic goals, the nature of expressive movement is opposite to this. Even with an egocen-

Methods of Studying Higher Mental Functions

67

tric content, it displays, let us say, its own kind of altruism: Gutzmann maintains that it is "thoucentric"· and has an inevitably social character. But the most remarkable part of what happens with action and word in the process of development is left aside here: the development of egocentric speech and "thoucentric" action, a conversion of the social method of behavior into a function of individual adaptation, an internal transformation of action with the help of the word, the social nature of all higher mental functions, including practical action in its higher forms. It is not surprising therefore that volitional action becomes comparable to affect with the difference that it leads to external changes that eliminate affect itself. Mastery as an essential internal factor of volitional action is left outside the visual field of the investigator. This new relation of action to the personality, which arises due to the word and leads to mastery of action, this new relation of the actor to the external world that is manifested in free action, controlled and directed by the word-none of this arises at the beginning of the process of development and, for this reason, is not taken into account at all. Moreover, we can trace along the whole chain of facts how the action of the child is socialized in the process of development and how with the loss of speech, in aphasia, practical action drops to the level of an elementary zoopsychological form. Whoever pays no attention to this inevitably perceives the psychological nature of both speech and action in a false light since the source of their change lies in their functional merging. Whoever ignores this basic fact and tries to present speech and action as two parallels that never intersect in order to preserve the purity of a classification scheme necessarily limits the true amplitude of the one and the other since the amplitude of the content lies primarily in the connection between the one concept and the other. H. Gutzmann limits speech to expressive functions, communication of internal states, and communicative activity. But the whole individual-psychological aspect and the whole transforming internal activity of the word simply remain apart. If this parallel and independent relation of word and act were to be preserved over the whole course of development, speech would be powerless to change anything in behavior. The action aspect of the word is mechanically excluded, and for this reason there inevitably develops an underestimation of volitional action, of action in its higher forms-action connected with the word. As study of the connections between the word and the act during childhood and in aphasia shows, the whole essence of the matter is that speech raises action, formerly independent of speech, to a higher level. Both development and disintegration of higher forms of action confirm this. Despite Lipmann's conclusion, which considers aphasia as a special case of apraxia, Gutzmann claims that apractic disturbances must be placed in parallel to aphasia. It is not difficult to see here a direct continuation of his original idea of the independence of action and speech. But clinical data speak against such a view. Disturbances of higher forms of action connected with the word, disintegration of higher forms and the detachment of action and its functioning according to independent, primitive laws that occurs in this case, a general return to more primitive organization of actions in aphasia and a drop on the whole to a different genetic level, which we were able to establish in all of our experiments-all of this shows that the pathological disintegration of action and speech again, like its construction, does not occur along two independent, nonintersecting parallel lines. ·Editor's note: from the word "thoulsm," which Vygotsky uses as an antonym of the word "ego."

68

Chapter 5

However, we considered this in sufficient detail when we presented our subject; essentially, all of the content of our work was devoted to this alone. Now the task is to condense the whole content into a compressed formula that would express with the greatest precision possible the very essence of everything we have found in our clinical and experimental studies of higher mental functions and their development and disintegration, specifically in the study of practical intellect. From all that has been said thus far, we cannot decide with sufficient clarity how this follows from either the Gospel formula or from Goethe's formula-regardless of which word is given the stress. We cannot, however, help but note that all of these formulas, including Gutzmann's formula, of necessity require sequels. They speak of what was in the beginning. But what happened next? The beginning is only the beginning, that is, the initial point of movement. The process of development itself must, of necessity, include in itself the rejection of the initial point and movement to the higher forms of action, forms of action that lie not at the beginning, but at the end of the whole path of development. How is this accomplished? The attempt to answer this question inspired us to write this work. We tried in its continuation to show how the word, itself being intellectualized and developed on the basis of action, raises action to a higher level, subordinates it to the will of the child and places the stamp of the will on action. But since we wanted to present all of this in a short formula, in one sentence we might say: if the act, independent of the word, stands at the beginning of development, then at its end stands the word becoming the act. The word, which makes the action of man free.

THE TEACHING ABOUT EMOTIONS Historical-Psychological Studies 1

1 C. G. Lange,2 the author of a well-known theory of emotions, calls Spinoza one of the people whose teaching on emotions preceded the organic theory of emotions. As we know, the latter theory was developed almost simultaneously by two investigators independently-Lange in 1885 and W. James in 1884. 3 Thus, in the expression of J. W. Goethe, some ideas mature at certain times just as fruit falls simultaneously in different orchards. Lange says: "I do not know whether a similar theory of emotions was proven at any earlier time in scientific psychology; at any rate, I cannot find any indication of that. Perhaps Spinoza comes closest to such a view when he not only does not consider bodily manifestations of emotions as depending on mental movements, but places them on the same plane with them, or even moves them virtually to the forefront" (1896, p. 89). Lange has in view the well-known definition of affect in Spinoza's teaching. Spinoza says: '~s affects, 1 understand states of the body that increase or decrease the capability of the body itself to act, are advantageous to it or limit it, as well as ideas about these states" (1933, p. 82). In analyzing the genesis of the organic theory of emotions as it was formulated by Lange, G. Dumas4 points to the shaT disagreement of the theory with that of the evolutionists, particularly C. Darwin and H. Spencer,6 and to a "certain kind of anti-English reaction in the opinions of Lange" (cited from the book: C. Lange, 1896, p. XI). Actually, Lange reproaches Darwin, and partisans of the theory of evolution in general, for misinterpreting the problem of affective state and for placing the historical point of view above the mechanistic and physiological. He says: "It is doubtful in general whether we should greet as a fortunate event the sharply evolutionist direction that the newest psychology, especially English psychology, has adopted under the influence of Darwin's studies-I think that we certainly should not. At least, as long as we are speaking of the psychology of affects, since here the evolutionist direction resulted in scorning physiological analysis especially and, in this way, compelling psychology to abandon the one correct path to which physiologists tried to direct it and along which they would have reached their goal if such basic phenomena as vasomotor functions were known in their time" (ibid., p. 85). The fact we have just noted has an exceptionally important significance for the correct understanding of the very essence of the organic theory of emotions. Later it will serve as the working point for our critical analysis, the task of which will be to disclose the whole antihistorical quality of this theory. At present this fact is of interest to us in a different connection. From the negative aspect, it not only elucidates very well the ideological precursors of the organic theory of emotions but also shows to which trends of philosophical and scientific thought it is kin and to which it is openly antagonistic.

71

72

The Teaching about Emotions

Speaking of Lange, Dumas says: "He very willingly cites French adherents of the mechanistic worldview, and actually, he is their latest pupil. Breaking down joy and sorrow into motor and mental phenomena, eliminating imagined essences of poorly defined forces-all of this is done according to the traditions of N. Malebranche' and Spinoza" (ibid., p. XII). E. Titchener8 states that "it would be completely wrong-and for James and Lange, this would be a small compliment-to propose that this theory is something completely new" (1914, p. 163). Indications of the organic basic parts of emotions are actually as old as systematic .psychology. Titchener finds them beginning with Aristotle and ending with H. Lotze (H. Lotze, 1852, p. 518) and H. Maudsley, \0 that is, with contemporaries of Lange and James. Finding everything more or less close to the organic theory of emotions, Titchener does not identify any kind of trend in philosophical or scientific thought, including the philosophy of Spinoza, as a basic historical precursor of the theory under consideration. But he indicates that Spinoza gives definitions pointing in that direction and cites the definition of affect given above which appears in Ethics (Spinoza, 1933, p. 82). It is true that James himself does not recognize, as Lange does, the historical or ideological kinship between his theory and Spinoza's teaching on the passions. On the contrary, in spite of Titchener's opinion and the almost universal opinion established in scientific psychology, James is inclined to consider his theory as something absolutely new, a child without forebears, and to contrast his teaching with all the studies of emotions of a purely descriptive nature wherever they might be found-in novels, or in classical philosophical works, or in psychology courses. This purely descriptive literature, in the words of James, beginning with Descartes to our time, presents the dullest section of psychology. In studying him, you have little sense that the subdivision of emotions proposed by psychologists is in most cases a simple fiction or completely unimportant. Thus, if James himself is not inclined to see a hereditary connection between the Spinoza theory of passions and the theory of emotions which he developed, others do this for him. We are not at all speaking of the expert evidence of Lange, Dumas, and Titchener presented above; their statements refer, strictly speaking, in equal measure to the theory of James as well as to the theory of Lange. Both these theories are, in any case, a single theory, from the point of view of its main ideological content, which can interest us only in explaining the genesis of any theory; the disagreements between them pertain, as is known, to more detailed physiological mechanisms that determine the genesis of emotions; we will further concentrate our critical analysis on this. In order to conclude the review of the thesis we developed, which states that Spinoza's teaching about passions is usually connected with the theories on emotions of James and Lange, we shall refer only to the detailed and convincing study of G. Sergi,11 the results of which we will use subsequently. 1i"acing the origin of the organic theory of emotions, Sergi focuses in on the critical point of this theory, specifically, on the inevitable reduction of emotion to a confused, poorly differentiated, global sensation of a general organic state that follows from the sequentially logical development of this doctrine. In this case, it seems that there is neither passion nor emotion, but only sensation. In the words of the investigator, the result which the organic theory approaches at its critical point frightens James to such a degree that it compels him to lapse into the Spinoza theory. In passing, we will note that, on the whole, with respect to the true genesis of the theory of emotions, Sergi comes to conclusions that differ substantially from the generally accepted views cited above. Subsequently, we shall use these conclusions again and depend on them in explaining certain fundamental problems connected with the basic prob-

The Teaching about Emotions

73

lem of our studies. At this point, this circumstance interests us only to the extent to which it supports the "objectivity and impartiality" of the position stated above on the Spinoza-like nature of the James theory. We will not continue further the enumeration of various views on the problem under consideration; there is no need for this. All these differ from each other in shades and nuances of ideas and agree with each other in the basic tone of what they claim. Reviewing them as a whole, we cannot but note that all of them represent an opinion quite firmly rooted in contemporary psychology and that, with respect to this opinion, as the French proverb has it, the more it changes in separate expressions, the more it remains the same. Even if, on closer inspection, this opinion developed to be nothing more than an error or prejudice, we would nevertheless have to begin with an investigation of this position since the battle around the theory of James and Lange that is being played out before our eyes leads us directly to the very center of the problem in which we are interested. Here, according to the widespread view, something is taking place that is not only substantially important for the whole fate of the psychology of emotions but is also something directly connected with the teaching of Spinoza on passions. Even if this connection is presented in the widespread view in a distorted form, even if it develops to be a prejudice, some kind of objective threads must nevertheless be hiding behind it that tie the teaching of Spinoza to the current battle and the reconstruction taking place in one of the most basic chapters of scientific psychology of our day. For this reason, if we want to study the fate of the Spinoza theory on passions in the living tissue of contemporary scientific knowledge, we must begin by explaining its connection to the ideas of Lange and James on the nature of human emotions.

2 But first, we must consider the James-Lange theory itself and find what is true and what is false in it from the point of view of strict testing of theoretical thought and the facts, testing to which it was subjected from the moment of its first formulations right up to our day. It is true that the empirical theory created more than a half century ago survived to our day regardless of the destructive criticism to which it was subjected from various quarters. It is true also that thus far, it is still the living center around which, as around a basic axis, a turn is being taken in psychological teaching on the nature of human feeling. We are evidently present at the final act in the resolution of this whole scientific drama which opened in 1884-1885, in the last century. We are present at the elucidation of the final historical verdict on this theory and at the resolution of the fate of the whole direction of psychological thought which was not only of major importance for psychology in the past, but which is directly connected to determining the future course of development of this chapter in scientific psychology. 1hJe, thus far, opinion has held that this theory has withstood with honor a continuous half-century of scientific testing and stood firm as an immovable base for contemporary teaching on the psychology of human feelings. In any case, this is how the matter stood in most psychology courses. However, not only school psychology, accustomed to the necessities of instruction, holds firmly to this theory, which seemingly is only waiting for elimination, but even presenters of the very newest psychological trends frequently try to renew this theory, which, in their eyes, seems not to age, and to present it as a most adequate representation of the objective nature of emotions. In any case, in many variations of American behavioral

74

The Teaching about Emotions

psychology and in certain trends in Soviet psychology this theory is considered, if you please, as the only definitive and supportable theoretical construction that can be transferred as a whole from the old psychology to the new. It is rather remarkable that in the most extreme trends of contemporary objective psychology, this chapter is directly transcribed from or retold with the words of Lange and James. It impresses contemporary reformers of psychology mainly due to two circumstances. First, what ensured exclusive dominance for this psychology over the course of a half century is connected with the nature of its presentation. Titchener notes caustically: "The James-Lange theory with its wide distribution among English-speaking psychologists undoubtedly owes much to the nature of its presentation. The presentations of mental movement in psychology texts had a somewhat academic, somewhat conventional character, and James presented to us raw material and brought us to· the well-spring of actual experience" (1914, pp. 162-163). Actually, this theory is, if you please, the only one that, with a coherence approaching the paradoxical, satisfactorily resolves the problem of the nature of emotions with such obvious simplicity, with such conviction, with such an abundance of factual evidence that is confirmed daily and available to everyone so that involuntarily an illusion of truth and irrefutability of this theory is created, and somehow readers and investigators not only forget or do not note the fact that, according to the valid observation of P. Bard,12 its authors did not confirm it with any kind of experimental evidence, but that it was based exclusively on speculative arguments and theoretical analysis. A second circumstance that recruited as supporters of this theory the most radical reformers of contemporary psychology consists of the following: in explaining emotions, this theory brings the organic base of emotions to the forefront and for this reason poses as a strictly physiological, objective, and even uniquely materialistic conception of emotions and feelings. Here a surprising illusion arises again which continues to exist with remarkable persistence regardless of the fact that James himself was careful to explain his theory from the very beginning as a theory that was not necessarily connected with materialism. In connection with this theory, James wrote: "My point of view cannot be termed materialistic. It contains no more or less materialism than is present in every view according to which our emotions are due to nervous processes" (1902, p. 313). For this reason, he considered it logically absurd to argue against the proposed theory with reference to the fact that it leads to a base materialistic interpretation of emotional phenomena. This, however, developed to be insufficient for understanding that it is just as logically absurd to defend this theory by reference to the materialistic explanation it gives for human feelings. This double illusion was so strong that even now it is still thought that the seemingly organic theory of emotions had withstood continuous scientific testing with honor and stood firmly as a secure base for contemporary psychological teaching on the feelings of man. From the moment of its appearance, the authors proudly opposed their theory to everything that was formerly termed teaching on the emotions. We have already spoken of how James regarded the whole period that preceded this teaching: over the whole course of its history, James found "no fruitful guiding beginning, no basic point of view" (ibid., p. 307). (We note parenthetically: this was after Spinoza developed his remarkable teaching on the passions in which he gave a guiding beginning, fruitful not only for the present, but also for the future of our science. It is difficult to imagine greater historical and theoretical blindness than that which James exhibits in this case. We will easily be able to disclose the reason for this subsequently.)

The Teaching about Emotions

75

James continues: "Emotions differ and are set apart endlessly, but you will not find any logical generalizations in them" (ibid.). Lange pronounces a verdict no less severe. He says: "Since the time of Aristotle, we have an almost infinite literature on the problem of the influence of affects on the body but truly scientific results on the question of the nature of emotions were not achieved with all the information accumulated over the centuries because in essence, there is nothing pertinent to that except notes ... Actually, without exaggeration, we could state that in a scientific way, we understand absolutely nothing about emotions, that we have not even a shadow of any theory on the nature of emotions in general or about each of them in particular" (1896, p. 19). It is Lange's opinion that everything that we know about emotions is based on nebulous impressions that have no scientific basis. Some statements on the nature of emotions by chance developed to be true, but scarcely any actual conception of the subject is connected even with these true positions. In a historical study similar to ours on the analysis of past and future in the development of teaching on passions and their analysis in light of contemporary scientific knowledge, we cannot but note that Lange and James almost literally repeat Descartes, who 300 years before their time said the same thing about the whole preceding history of this teaching. He said: "Nowhere can we see as distinctly as in the treatment of the passions how great are the inadequacies of the sciences transmitted to us by the ancients" (1914, p. 127). The teachings of the ancients on passions seemed to him to be meager to such a degree and shaky in most of their parts to such a degree that he saw himself "forced to abandon completely the usual paths in order to come nearer to the truth with a certain confidence." "I am forced," he said, "for this reason to write as if I am dealing with a subject that no one has touched on before" (ibid., p. 127). Moreover, a simple historical inquiry, cited correctly by Titchener, clearly demonstrates that both the problem of Descartes and the problem of James-Lange were familiar and close even to Aristotle. According to the idea of Aristotle, a representative of speculative philosophy says that anger is a striving for revenge or something similar. A representative of natural philosophy says that anger is a boiling of the blood that surrounds the heart. Which of these is a true philosopher? Aristotle replies that the true philosopher is the one who unites these two positions. This coincidence does not seem to us to be accidental, but its true sense will be disclosed for us in the further course of study. No matter how mistaken the authors of the organic theory of emotions might be concerning the absolute newness of their ideas, in the eyes of their followers, this mistake retained the importance of indisputable and unadulterated truth to our day. Even in our time, K. Dunlap,13 summing up the fifty years of existence of this doctrine, maintains that it not only became rooted in scientific thought so firmly that at present it is practically the basis for the study of emotional life, but it also led to the development of the hypothesis of reaction or response as the basis for all mental life as a whole (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 106-124). R. Perryl4 seconds this: this famous doctrine is so firmly supported by evidence and confirmed by experience so many times that it is impossible to deny the truth of its essence. Regardless of the carefully worked out refutations, it shows no signs of becoming obsolete (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 106). But we will say at the very outset: the James-Lange theory must be considered more likely to be error than truth in its teaching on the passions. In this way, we have expressed in advance the basic idea, the main thesis of all of the present chapter of our study. Let us consider more closely the basis for this idea.

76

The Teaching about Emotions

The illusion of invulnerability and critical impermeability of the James-Lange theory, like every illusion, is harmful first of all in that it prevents things from being seen in a true light. Striking evidence of this is the fact that a series of new studies, which under objective and careful consideration deal a crushing blow to the theory we are analyzing, is perceived by followers of this doctrine as new evidence of its strength. An example of this kind of error is the fate of the first experimental studies of W Cannon,15 who undertook a systematic experimental study of the problem of organic changes that arise in emotional states. His studies translated into Russian 16 contain, strictly speaking, a deadly criticism of the organic theory of emotions. But they were perceived and recognized by our scientific thought as completely indisputable evidence of its correctness. The foreword to the Russian translation of these studies written by B. M. Zavadovsky17 states directly that the ideas of James on the nature of emotions, brilliant in insight, in our eyes, assume the real, concrete forms of the biological experiment (in: W B. Cannon, 1927 p. 3). This statement is reinforced with a reference to the revolutionary quality of the ideas of James, who prominently stressed the material, purely physiological roots of mental states. This general idea, indisputable for any biologist who does not imagine mental activity without its material base, becomes the common denominator which, owing to the illusion mentioned more than once, permits identifying the ideas of James with the facts presented by Cannon regardless of the fact that they are in irreconcilable contradiction. Cannon himself clearly shows that Zavadovsky is not alone in his error in evaluating the significance of his [Cannon's] experimental work. The error was shared by everyone who shared this illusion with him. In the words of Cannon, the multifaceted changes (which he studied in detail) that occur in internal organs as a result of great excitation were interpreted as confirming the James-Lange theory. But from the facts presented in these studies, it must be clear that such an interpretation is wrong. What did Cannon's studies show? If we stop to consider their most essential and basic result, which alone can interest us in the present study, we must say the following: they disclosed experimentally that pain, hunger, and strong emotions such as fear and anger elicit deep bodily changes in the organism. These changes are distinguished by their reflex nature, representing a typical organic reaction that arises due to hereditary automatism and, consequently, these changes exhibit a biologically expedient character. The bodily changes during the time of excitation, as Cannon's studies show, are elicited by an increased secretion of adrenaline by the adrenal glands; the changes are analogous to those elicited by an adrenaline injection. Adrenaline elicits increased decomposition of carbohydrates and increases sugar content of the blood. It promotes the flow of blood to the heart and its outflow from the inhibited organs of the abdominal cavity. Adrenaline rapidly eliminates muscle fatigue and increases blood coagulation. Such are the principal changes that are observed in strong excitation connected with a state of hunger, pain, and strong emotion. Their basis is the internal secretion of the adrenal glands. All of these changes exhibit, as we have said, internal dependence and a merging with each other, and all of them as a whole clearly exhibit their adaptive and expedient significance. In his studies, W Cannon discloses, step by step, the significance of the increased sugar content in the blood as a source of muscle energy; the significance of increased adrenaline content in the blood as an antidote to muscle fatigue; the significance of the change in blood supply to the organs under the influence of adrenaline as a circumstance that promotes the greatest muscle tension; the analo-

The Teaching about Emotions

77

gous significance of changes in respiratory function; and the expedient significance of increased blood coagulation that prevents blood loss. The key to explaining the biological significance of all of these phenomena is correctly seen by Cannon in the old idea, newly enunciated recefltly by McDougall,18 on the interrelation between the flight instinct with the emotion of fear and the fight instinct with the emotion of anger. Under natural conditions following the emotions of fright and anger, there may be an increase in activity of the organism (for example, fleeing or fighting) that requires prolonged and intensive tension of a large group of muscles. For this reason, it seems completely probable that increased secretion of adrenaline as a result of reflex affect of pain or strong emotions may play the role of a dynamic factor in producing muscle work. If it is true, as Cannon establishes experimentally, that muscle work is done mainly through the energy of sugar and that substantial muscle work can markedly decrease the quantity of reserve glycogen and circulating sugar, then we must admit that an increase in sugar content in the blood that accompanies strong emotions and pain significantly increases the capacity of the muscles for prolonged work. Further studies showed that adrenaline freely entering the blood has a marked effect on rapid recovery of fatigued muscles that have lost the initial excitability and potential for rapid reaction of fresh muscles, and strengthens in this way the effect of the nervous system on the muscles, preparing them for maximal work. Blood supply to the organs and change in respiration evidently have the same purpose; urgent need of attack or flight requires an abundant supply of oxygen for the working muscles and a rapid excretion of exhausted carbon dioxide from the body. Finally, the expediency of increased blood coagulation can be considered as a process that is favorable for the organism. Generalizing these data, Cannon proposes considering all the reactions of the organism elicited by painful stimulation and emotional excitation as naturally arising defensive, instinctive reactions. These reactions may be justifiably interpreted as preparation for severe tension that the organism may need. Thus, Cannon says, from this general point of view, bodily changes that accompany strong emotional states may serve as an organic preparation for coming battle and possible injury and naturally promote the reactions that pain in itself may elicit. If we wanted to summarize briefly the general significance of the facts Cannon found, we would have to agree with his pointing to the dynamogenic effect of emotional excitation as a basic factor. Here Cannon closely follows Sherrington,19 who more energetically than anyone else pointed to this aspect of emotional processes. He said that emotions control us from the very beginning of life on earth and increasing intensity of emotions becomes the commanding stimulus for strong movement. Each bodily change that occurs in the internal organs-cessation of digestive processes (which is accompanied by a release of a supply of energy that may be used by other organs), outflow of blood from internal organs whose activity is decreased to organs that participate directly in muscle tension (lungs, heart, central nervous system); strengthening of heart contractions; rapid elimination of muscle fatigue; mobilization of large reserves of sugar that contains energy-each of these internal changes results in a direct benefit, fortifying the organism during great expenditure of energy elicited by fright, pain, or anger (see R. Creed et aI., 1935). In this connection, very important is the circumstance that during a period of strong excitation, there is frequently a feeling of colossal power. This feeling appears suddenly and raises the individual to a high level of activity. During strong emotions, excitation and feeling of strength are merged and in this way they free stored energy, unrealized before, and lead to consciousness of an unforgettable feeling of possible victory.

78

The Teaching about Emotions

Before we move on to a theoretical analysis and evaluation of these evidently indisputably established positions, we cannot but return to the basic problem of our investigation which is present the whole time on every page of our considerations: to the teaching of Spinoza on the passions. But the somewhat unusual and strange path we selected for investigation, which emanated inevitably from the essence itself of the problem we posed, resulted in a situation in which the superficial impression may be that we were diverted from solving the basic problem with which we are concerned. In essence, a review of the teaching of Spinoza on the passions in light of contemporary psychoneurology cannot but be to the same extent a review of the contemporary state of the problem of the nature of emotions in light of the teaching of Spinoza on the passions so that we could with equal justification title our investigation with the latter words. For this reason, we cannot but take advantage of the first factual position that we derive from the first experimental study of emotions in order to connect it with the corresponding idea of Spinoza that forms a point of departure of all of his teaching on the passions. If we recall the definition of affects cited above, given in Ethics, we cannot but see that experimental evidence of the dynamogenic effect of emotions that raise the individual to a higher level of activity is at the same time empirical evidence for the idea of Spinoza, which defines affects as states of the body which increase or decrease the capacity of the body itself for action, promote or limit it, as well as ideas of these states. But we noted above that Lange cites specifically this definition of Spinoza as bringing Spinoza's teaching on passions close to the organic theory of emotions. For this reason, it is easy to conclude that the empirical confirmation of Spinoza's ideas, together with experimental evidence, favors the James-Lange theory. This is how these studies were perceived initially. And actually, at first glance, with superficial consideration, it may seem that this theory finds complete confirmation in Cannon's studies and is celebrating a greater triumph. The serious organic changes that Lange and James advanced as the source of emotional processes, based on everyday observations, introspective analysis, and purely speculative constructs, appeared to be not only a completely real fact, but now seem to us to be much more deeply involved, more all-encompassing, more significant for the general change in vital function, more radical and fundamental than the boldest idea of the founders of this doctrine could anticipate. But now, if we want to remain true to the spirit of critical investigation that always guides our thought, we must ask ourselves: are we not once again falling into the historical illusion that circumscribes with a vicious circle the famous paradox on the organic nature of emotions and, in confirming its great triumph which it shares with the victory of Spinoza's ideas, are we not in this way accepting error for truth?

3 Let us look more intently at the fact just described and we will then note that together with the apparent confirmation of the organic theory of emotions, it also contains conclusions that clearly do not speak in its favor. In order to disclose them, we must move from an absolute to a relative consideration of this fact. In itself, the fact removes from all doubt the position that strong emotions such as fear and anger are accompanied by very deep organic changes. But, of course, the essence of the question is not contained in this position in itself. It could scarcely evoke

The Teaching about Emotions

79

serious doubt in anyone even before Cannon's experiments. His experiments disclosed the physiological mechanism, the structure, and the biological significance of these organic reactions. But they added hardly an iota of authenticity to the fact itself of the existence of these changes. Consequently, the essence of the problem is not the presence of changes in themselves that accompany emotions, but in the relation of these bodily changes to the mental content and structure of the emotions on the one hand and the functional significance of these changes on the other. Even the classical theory of emotions, against which Lange and James spoke, considered bodily expression of emotions as an inherent factor in any emotional process. But it considered these bodily changes to be the result of emotional reactions. The new theory began to consider these reactions as the source of emotions. The whole paradoxical quality of the new theory in comparison with the classical consists, as we know, in the fact that it brought forth as the cause of emotions what was formerly considered to be their consequence. The authors of the new theory were not only well aware of this but placed it in the center of their construct as its principal and dominating idea. C. G. Lange, defining the basic problem, completely clearly recognizes that he is putting the question upside down (feet upward). As a result of his studies, he comes to the question that we indicated above as the central point that separates the classical theory from the organic theory of emotions. He says: "We now have before us a question that is of substantial interest with respect to psychophysiology and, at the same time, is the central point of our study-the question of the nature of the relation that exists between emotions and the physical changes that accompany them. Usually turns of phrase are used such as physiological manifestations elicited by emotions, or physiological manifestations accompanying emotions ..." (1896, p. 54). Moreover, the question of the relation between emotion as such and the physiological manifestations accompanying it has never been posed with sufficient clarity. Lange says: "It is strange that this relation has never yet been defined in a precise way. I do not know of even one study that attempted to explain its true nature ... Regardless, however, of this lack of clarity, nevertheless it must be said that scientific psychology also shares the theory that emotions elicit and promote the bodily manifestations that accompany them. It does not ask itself what this feature of emotions really consists of that they should have such power over man" (ibid., pp. 54-55). Lange criticizes the classical theory of emotions according to which "emotions are the essences, the beings, the powers, the demons that control man and elicit in him both physical and mental manifestations" (ibid.). Lange formulates in two basic points the insupportability of the traditional theory of emotions according to which "every event followed by emotion initially directly elicits purely mental action which is the real emotion-true joy, true sorrow, etc.-bodily manifestations of emotions, although they always accompany emotions, are only peripheral, in themselves completely nonessential" (ibid., pp. 55-56). The traditional theory seems to him to be as insubstantial as all metaphysical hypotheses in general. Without hindrance of experiments, they clothe mental processes with any properties and powers they find convenient, and these always provide whatever service is required of them. Can mental fright explain why people grow pale, tremble, etc.? It is no calamity if they do not understand this. An explanation can be made up even without understanding it. Of course, people became accustomed to reassuring themselves in this way. For Lange, the second point of attack on this theory consists of the position that "feeling cannot exist without its physical manifestations. Eliminate all symptoms of fear in a frightened man, make his pulse beat calmly, restore his steady gaze

80

The Teaching about Emotions

and a healthy color to his face, make his movements quick and confident, his speech strong and his thoughts clear-what then will be left of his fear?" (ibid., p. 57). For this reason, Lange continues to assume that bodily manifestations of emotions can be accomplished purely physically and that the mental hypothesis is superfluous. In a positive formulation of his own theory, Lange tries to reduce all physiological changes that occur with emotions to one general source and in this way to establish a mutual connection between these manifestations, simplifying to a high degree the totality of the whole relation and also facilitating its physiological understanding, which would be understood with difficulty if we should have to assume a direct primary origin for each of these manifestations. Lange believes the common source that unites all physiological changes to be the general functional changes in the vasomotor system. In a classical formulation of his basic idea, Lange even puts forward the vasomotor reaction as a source and essential basis of the whole emotional process. He says: "We are obliged to the vasomotor system for the whole emotional part of our mental life, our joys and sorrows, our happy and sad days. If impressions we perceive with our external organs of feeling did not have the ability to arouse its activity, we would pass our life as apathetic and indifferent spectators; all impressions from the external world would enrich only our experience and increase the sum of our knowledge without arousing in us either joy or anger, without causing us grief or fear" (ibid., p. 77). In keeping with this, Lange sees the true scientific task for the given set of manifestations to be a precise determination of the emotional reaction of the vasomotor system to various kinds of influences. After what has been said, it is hardly possible that any doubt should remain that the center of the theory around which the whole construct is developed is not in itself the presence of physiological reactions during emotion, but the relation of these reactions to the emotional process as such. With no less clarity the same thing follows from the theory of James. James himself formulated this in a classical excerpt which we will also recall: "It is usually thought that in raw forms of emotion, the mental impression perceived from a given object elicits in us a mental state termed emotion, and the latter entails a certain bodily manifestation. According to my theory, bodily excitation follows directly after perception of that which elicits it, and our realization of this excitation as it comes about are the emotions. This is usually expressed as follows: we lost a fortune-we were distressed and cried; we met a bear-we were frightened and began to run; we were insulted by an enemy-we became angry and hit him. According to the hypothesis that I am defending, the order of these events must be somewhat different, specifically: the first mental state is not replaced immediately by the second; between them, there must be bodily manifestations and for this reason this is most rationally expressed as follows: we are saddened, therefore we cry; we are angered, therefore we hit someone; we are afraid, therefore we tremble and do not speak; we cry, hit, tremble because we are sad, angered, frightened. If the bodily manifestations did not follow the perception immediately, then the latter would be a purely cognitive act in its form-pale, devoid of coloring and emotional 'warmth.' In such a case, we could see a bear and decide that it would be best to run, we would suffer an insult and find it fair to retaliate, but we would not feel fright or indignation" (1902, pp. 308309). As we can see, for James also, the problem consists not in adding any substantial point to the traditional description of the emotional process, but exclusively in changing the sequence of these points, establishing the true relation between them, and introducing as a source and cause of emotion what was formerly considered to be its consequence and result. The substantial difference between James

The Teaching about Emotions

81

and Lange is reduced to only two points that are secondary from the point of view that is of interest to us. First, Lange bases the change in the traditional relation between emotion and its bodily expression on materialistic tendencies, while James clearly sees that this theory contains no more and no less materialism than does any view according to which our emotions are due to nervous processes, although his formulation contains a certain cryptic objection addressed to Platonizing psychologists who consider mental phenomena as phenomena connected with something exceptionally base. But James understands that even the Platonizing, that is, consistently idealistic psychology, can be reconciled with his theory. The second point of difference lies in the physiological mechanism of emotional reactions itself. If, for Lange, the change in the vasomotor system is of exclusive importance in this mechanism, James brings to the forefront the functional changes in internal organs and skeletal musculature. As to the rest, the theories resemble each other like twins. Thus, we see that in order to resolve the problem of whether the facts found by Cannon speak for or against the organic theory of emotions, we cannot limit ourselves to considering these facts in themselves in their absolute meaning, but necessarily must, first of all, study their relation to the essence of the emotional processes and ask what these facts say with respect to the cause-and-effect dependence that James and Lange concordantly advance as being of paramount importance to their whole theory. Consequently, the problem must be posed thus: do these facts confirm the position that organic changes must be considered as the direct cause, the source and essence itself of the emotional process without which emotion stops being what it is, or do these facts speak in favor of the opposite view which is inclined to see in the bodily changes the more or less direct consequences of mental processes that lie at the base of emotions, that is, to see the bodily changes as only secondary phenomena, to put it in Lange's language, which, although constantly present, are in themselves completely nonessential. In other words, the problem can be precisely and briefly translated into this form: in the light of these facts, must we accept that organic changes in the presence of emotions comprise the principal and basic phenomenon and their reflection in consciousness only an epiphenomenon, or, conversely, must we assume that conscious experience of emotions represents the basic and principal phenomenon and the accompanying bodily changes, only an epiphenomenon? Specifically this is the essence of the debate, the point of the whole controversy between the two theories of emotions. Let us turn to the resolution of the problem posed. As we are now beginning to see, we need only pose the problem in this way: in Cannon's experimental studies, there is no small amount of material that is unfavorable to the organic theory that can serve to seriously diminish the triumph of this doctrine, which many saw in the light of the new factual data. What is unfavorable appears most distinctly in two basic conclusions that can be reached from these studies. The first conclusion: organic changes, no matter how profound and biologically significant they may seem to us, no matter how serious the organic shock they mask, appear as surprisingly similar in emotions that are most different or even opposite from the point of view of experience. The elucidation of this situation, most important for the problem in which we are interested, was furthered by both the more precise determination of the physiological mechanism of these reactions, concealed in the processes of internal secretion, and by the careful and systematic study of these reactions under experimental conditions. Cannon's earlier research established the following. Visceral manifestations that accompany fear and anger are made evident through the participation of neurons of the sympathetic system. We must recall that these neurons serve mainly for generalized and not for strictly organic reactions. Although

82

The Teaching about Emotions

we are speaking of two completely different emotions (fear and anger), facts known to physiology indicate that the accompanying visceral changes do not differ very sharply from each other. Moreover, there are facts that convincingly show why visceral changes in fear and anger cannot be different, but, on the contrary, are more likely to be similar. As has already been indicated, these emotions accompany preparation of the organism to act, and for the same reason that conditions which elicit them lead to flight or to fight (it may be that each of them requires extreme tension), in each of these reactions, the need of the organism is identical. The mechanism of the sympathetic section also begins to act as a whole or partially with emotions of a moderate type, for example, joy or sorrow or aversion, when they appear with sufficient intensity. Thus, it seems that not so much the psychological nature of emotions as the intensity of their manifestation and their course are primarily responsible for the profound bodily changes that are elicited more likely by the high degree of excitation of the central nervous system that affects the threshold of stimulation of the sympathetic section and disturbs the function of all organs innervated by this section. Consequently, the organic changes seem to us to be processes not strictly modified according to the psychological nature of emotions, but, more likely, by the standard, intense, typical reaction that is activated in a uniform way in the most various emotions. From this, w. Cannon justifiably reaches a conclusion that is fatal for the basic premise of the James-Lange theory: "If different strong emotions can thus be manifested in a widespread activity of one section of the autonomic system, a section that accelerates the work of the heart, inhibits movement in the stomach and intestines, results in contraction of the blood vessels, raising of the hair, release of sugar and secretion of adrenaline, then we may consider that bodily conditions, which, as some psychologists proposed, may make it possible to distinguish some emotions from others, are not suitable for this purpose, that these conditions must be sought anywhere except in the internal organs . . . We are not willing to say, as James does, that 'we are crying because we are sad,' but we are crying either from sadness or from joy or from strong anger or from a tender feeling; when one of these different emotional states is present, nerve impulses along sympathetic paths are directed toward various internal organs, including the tear glands. In fear, anger, or great joy, for example, at least in man, reactions of the internal organs seem to be too much alike to give us a suitable method of differentiating those states colored by different subjective shades. In view of this, I am inclined to think that visceral changes simply communicate to the emotional complex a more or less indeterminate, but nevertheless real feeling of those disturbances in organs of which we are usually not conscious" (1927, pp. 155-158). Essentially speaking, these words already contain the final verdict on the theory that saw the solution of the problem of the nature of emotions in the conscious perception of multifaceted, finely differentiated reactions corresponding to the type of emotional process. Cannon himself modifies the quite unambiguous basic position of James in such a way that the main relation between organic reactions and emotional processes can in no way be understood as a relation of cause and effect. In place of the position taken by the classical: we are crying because we are sad, Cannon formulates the following: we are crying either from sorrow, or from joy, or from strong anger, or from a tender feeling. Leaving aside for the time being the problem of the blow that this formulation inflicts on the traditional theory of emotions, we cannot but see that basically, it returns us to the idea so disputed by Lange and James, specifically, the idea of dependence of bodily manifestations on the emotional process as such.

The Teaching about Emotions

83

As we know, in his hypothesis, C. G. Lange insists that direct physical expressions that accompany emotion create changes in functions of the vasomotor apparatus that are different for each emotion. He even constructed a diagram of organic changes for seven emotions: disappointment, sadness, fear, embarrassment, impatience, joy, and anger. James assumed that his theory would lead to a radical reorganization of the whole problem of classification of emotions. A question arose earlier as to what variety or type a given emotion belongs, but now what is under discussion is explaining the causes of emotions, what kind of modifications one object or another specifically elicits in us, and why it elicits specifically these modifications and not others. From a superficial analysis of emotions, we thus move to a study that is deeper and of a higher order. Classification and description are lower steps in the development of science which are placed on a secondary level as soon as the problem of the causal connection in a given scientific area of investigation appears on the stage. Once we elucidate that the cause of emotions are innumerable reflex acts that arise under the influence of external objects and are immediately perceived by us, then we will immediately understand why an innumerable multitude of emotions may exist and why they may vary infinitely in different individuals in both composition and in motives that elicit them. The fact of the matter is that in the reflex act there is nothing unchangeable or absolute, the most various effects of the reflex are possible, and these effects, as we know, vary infinitely. To put it more briefly, any classification of emotions may be considered as true and natural as long as it satisfies its purpose, and questions as to what the true or typical expression of anger or fear is have no objective meaning. Instead of resolving such questions, we must undertake elucidation of how one expression of fear or anger could have occurred, and this comprises, on one hand, the problem of physiological mechanics, and on the other, the problem of the history of the human mind, that is, the problem which, like all scientific problems, is essentially solvable, although finding its solution may be difficult. We shall speak later of what history has to say of the human mind in connection with the theory under consideration. Physiological mechanics, to which James appeals, however, has scarcely spoken its final word on this, and this word not only does not defend the hypothesis of James, but is in irreconcilable opposition to it. At the same time that Lange maintains that the difference between emotions must be explained by the difference in vasomotor reactions and James believes that the point of view he proposes explains the astounding variation of emotions, physiological mechanics establishes the incontrovertible fact that organic changes in emotions arise as a standard reaction, identical for the most various affects, similar to innate reflexes of a lower order to which it belongs, for example, the nasal reflex. Cannon summarizes: "The facts I presented and the observations of Sherrington lead us to think that the internal organs play an insignificant role in the emotional complex, especially in the sense of recognizing the nature of the emotions" (1927, p. 157). Experiments that indicate the uniform visceral reactions support the idea that visceral factors play an insignificant role in the genesis of differences in emotional states. Evaluating the significance of this fact for confirming or refuting the theory, P. Bard finds that it is a sharp argument against the assertion of Lange, but is not as strong when applied to the later formulation that James gave to his basic hypotheses ten years after the first publication. In a later formulation of his views, James no longer insists as sharply as he did before on the possibility of differentiating emotions on the basis of differences in bodily changes. However, even with respect to the later formulation, the critical argument remains since even there

84

The Teaching about Emotions

James stresses the significance of visceral factors in the whole emotional reaction, factors which he maintains are the essential cause of any affective state. In response to the objection that laughter from tickling and shivering from cold elicit purely local bodily perception, but not the real emotions of happiness or fear, he responds that under these circumstances the production of emotional reactions is not complete. Visceral factors difficult to localize are absent, but evidently they are most essential of all. When for some internal reason they are added, we have emotion present and then the subject seems to be enveloped by pathological or groundless terror, sorrow, or anger.
