Thus, even with respect to the later formulation of the theory of James, as we see, this damaging argument basically retains full force. To the same extent, the persuasiveness of these ideas remains in our eyes to be unyielding even after the commentaries which many followers of the theory of James use to try to protect the teaching from the destructive force of this argument. Thus, J. Angell 20 admits that the presence of a significant stereotypic basis of substantially identical visceral changes is possible in all emotions, but assumes that differential traits can be found in extravisceral disturbances, especially in differences in tonus of the skeletal musculature (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 108). R. Perry also points to proprioceptive structures, to the motor aspect of emotional expression, in which elements distinct for various affective states can be found. The sense of the commentaries is completely clear: they are trying to sacrifice the factual and concrete content of the theory in order to save its ideological and theoretical nucleus. Suppose that the concrete mechanisms of emotional reactions to which Lange points (the central unifying significance of the functional changes of the vasomotor system) and those that James had in mind (visceral reaction) prove to be insupportable in light of the newer physiology; the main significance of the theory may be retained fully if we assume that we must look for these mechanisms among the extravisceral, especially the motor and proprioceptive processes. In this case, the theory would need factual correctives and perhaps even a radical revision of the physiological part, but the basic psychophysiological idea underlying it could be preserved. We must take these considerations into account; they provide only half of the basis on which the organic theory was consolidated, and for this reason we must investigate further the data that pertain to the possibility of retaining the theory of James on a different factual base. We shall limit ourselves for the time being to what hopelessly compromises the factual aspect of the theory even in the eyes of the principal followers of James-Lange. As Cannon properly notes, Lange does not give any place to the possible new source of genesis of emotional processes in his theory and James ascribes a smaller role to it in comparison with the main participation of the visceral and organic part of bodily changes in the genesis of emotion. Let us add to these considerations only what is indicated by data from everyday experience, with which Lange and James predominantly operate, referring to the presence in the experience of emotions of components emanating from the perception of organic changes; these data also primarily favor the visceral and organic components, but not the extravisceral, specifically, the motor components. We will not make a definitive judgment before we consider the whole polemic that has flared up between critics and partisans of the organic theory. At this time, we will add only one thing: with all of the uniformity, with all the sameness, standardized nature of a stereotypic reaction as it is described above, it is understood that we observe certain variations in its course. Thus, we cannot deny the fact that in different emotional states, we have different changes in the blood vessels (pallor or reddening of the face). However, as Cannon notes, even these changes are of little significance from the point of view of what we are interested in: the uniformity

The Teaching about Emotions

85

of the passage of the organic reactions. Cannon says that the sympathetic system acts as a unit but there may be insignificant variations, for example, the presence or absence of perspiration; in the basic traits, however, the integration of reactions always maintains its characteristic appearance. We can now move on to the second conclusion connected with the first, but even more lethal for the James-Lange theory. The conclusion flows directly from Cannon's earlier studies from which we drew our first critical argument. Its essence is that the uniform, stereo typic organic reaction, which, as we have said, is no basis for differentiating the most opposite affective states according to their psychological nature, can be observed in the same form also in states that have nothing in common with emotional excitation. Consequently, it contains nothing in itself that is characteristic not only for separate emotional states, but also for emotional states in general; more likely, it is the result of a high degree of excitation of the central nervous system, whatever the causes of this excitation might be and under whatever kinds of circumstances it might have arisen. We cannot but see that this new consideration definitively paralyzes the attempt of Angell to assume an identical stereotypic organic base for all emotional reactions in general, a base on which extravisceral components specific for each emotion are constructed. With all the unalterability of the logic of facts, studies show that the common and identical organic base does not contain anything specific to an emotional state as such and that it is completely identical for many other states of an indisputably nonaffective nature; consequently, it may characterize an emotional reaction not in that it is distinct and special in it, making it what it is, but only in that it has something in common with other nonemotional states. Even Cannon's first study established that the stereotypic reaction of the sympathetic section can be observed not only in fear and anger, but also in such states as pain and asphyxia. Manifestations elicited by asphyxia are similar to those elicited by painful stimulation and strong emotional excitation. Further studies wholly confirmed this observation and showed that the same reaction occurs with severe chilling, in fever, in hypoglycemia, asphyxia, and stressful muscular work (for example, in running). In all the indicated states, the sympathetic system is activated in exactly the same way as it is in strong emotional states. In the words of Cannon, this occurs in all strong excitation in any circumstances. As Bard and Cannon correctly noted (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927), this manifestation is found in irreconcilable contradiction with the basic theses of James. If we recall the following: according to James, sensation in the crudest forms of emotion is the result of its bodily manifestation; further, James saw additional evidence in favor of his theory in the fact that in eliciting external manifestation of one emotion or another, we must experience the emotion itself also; finally, if we recall James' objections to the argument brought against his theory, the fact of absence of emotion in shivering from cold and laughter from tickling, then it becomes completely clear that according to the James theory, we must have experienced a stronger emotional excitation in all the nonemotional states listed above in which the typical reaction of the sympathetic section is observed. Of course, present here is the whole complex of bodily manifestations as they are found in fear and anger, here are the visceral factors in the absence of which James saw an identical cause for the fact that tickling induces laughter but joy does not, and cold induces shivering but fear does not. Finally, here the requirement that James himself advanced, which flows from his theory, is fully realized, that is, the bodily manifestations corresponding to an emotional state are present, but the result, the consequence of the emotion itself, which we should have expected according to James, is not manifested.

86

The Teaching about Emotions

P. Bard says that a complete refutation of James' observation cited above on laughter from tickling and shivering from cold is the fact that shivering from cold occurs with the same visceral changes that are observed in actual fear. In this nonemotional state and in others (for example, in running) the full reaction, including visceral changes, is the same reaction as in fear, but there is, nevertheless, a remarkable absence of emotion that might have been expected according to the James theory. Cannon states the same thing as a basic conclusion from these studies. He says: "If emotions arise from afferent impulses coming from external organs, we would have to expect not only that fear and anger would be experienced in the same way, but that supercooling, hypoglycemia, asphyxia and fever would be felt in precisely the same way. But in reality this does not happen" (W, B. Cannon, 1927, p. 110). We see that the James-Lange theory cannot withstand the criticism of facts in the first attempt to study it experimentally. It appears to be an idea that is not consistent with its objective; consequently, in accordance with the basic axiom of Spinoza, it must be admitted to be a mistake rather than the truth. 21

4

Over the course of still another chapter, we must conclude the first part of the study we have begun that has as its purpose ascertaining the extent to which the James-Lange idea, which was assumed to be a vital continuation of the Spinoza teaching on passions, is consistent with its objective. Consequently, we must continue the critical analysis of the theory from the point of view of its factual validity. Having concluded the analysis, we can turn directly and immediately to the final critical experiments and to the data of pathological psychology of emotional life grouped around these experimental and clinical facts (they cast no small light on the problem that concerns us), all supplementary and auxiliary critical considerations that are part of this pointed polemic that evidently is the last page, or even the epilogue, in the history of a renowned and paradoxical teaching. As we know, Lange and James saw the basic evidence in favor of their theory not so much in the fact that emotional states are accompanied by physiological changes (this was recognized in classical theory), as in that without physiological changes, emotion itself cannot exist. From this they reached the conclusion that emotion is the direct result of what was formerly assumed to be its bodily manifestation. Factual testing of this idea was inaccessible to the authors of the theory. They could reproduce the required experiments only in imagination and theoretically anticipate the results of clinical studies of cases which would be suitable for confirming or refuting their theory. We have already cited the position of Lange: "Eliminate in the frightened man all physical symptoms of fear ... what will then remain of his fright?" (1896, p. 57). His is the theory that feeling cannot exist without physical manifestations. James expresses the same thing in a still more radical form: "Now I want to approach the presentation of the most important point in my theory, which consists of the following. If we imagine any strong emotion and try in our imagination to exclude from this state of our consciousness one after another all sensations of bodily symptoms connected with it, then in the final analysis, nothing of the given emotion will remain, not any kind of 'mental material' from which the given emotion could be formed. In what remains, there will be a cold, undifferentiated state of purely intellectual perception ... I cannot at all imagine what sort of emotion

The Teaching about Emotions

87

of fright will remain in our consciousness if sensations are eliminated that are connected with increased heart rate, rapid breathing, trembling of the lips, weakness of the extremities, with 'goose bumps' and with excitation of the internal organs. Can anyone imagine a state of anger and at the same time imagine no agitation in the chest, no flow of blood to the face, no expansion of the nostrils, no clenching of the teeth and no urge toward energetic action, but conversely, muscles in a relaxed state, calm breathing and a tranquil face? The same reasoning is applicable also to the emotion of sorrow: what would sorrow be without tears, sobbing, a decreased heart rate, pangs in the pit of the stomach?" (1902, pp. 311-312). According to James, in all of these cases, anger and sorrow as such, as emotions, would be completely absent, and in what remains, there would have to be calm, indifferent judgment belonging wholly to the intellectual sphere. pure thought about whether a certain person deserves condemnation for his sins or whether circumstances are quite sad, and nothing more. He says: "The same thing can be observed in an analysis of any other passion. Human emotion devoid of any bodily backing is an empty sound" (ibid .• p. 312). Naturally, from such a position, two consequences inevitably follow. The first: "If we suppress the external manifestations of passion, it must die off. Before yielding to an outburst of anger, try counting to ten-and the reason for the anger will appear to you to be laughably insignifi,~ant" (ibid., p. 315). It is noteworthy that Lange, completely independently of James, also cites counting as a means of suppressing anger. He recalls "the hero of the classical comedy of L. Holdberg,22 Herman von Bremen, who always counts :0 twenty when his wife beats him and then he is able to remain calm" (1896, p. 79). Lange says that when the hero "counts to twenty, then with this insignificant mental effort, he removes so much blood from the motor part of his brain that he loses all inclination to fight with his wife" (ibid., p. 79). The second consequence: .fames says: "If my theory is correct, it must be confirmed by the following indirect evidence: according to it, by eliciting in ourselves voluntarily when we are inwardly .:alm so-called external manifestations of one emotion or another, we must experience the emotion itself" (1902, pp. 314-315). Lange maintains the same thing: emotions can be elicited by a multitude of causes that have absolutely nothing in common with agitation of the spirit, and frequently they may be suppressed or ameliorated by purely physical means. It remained to us to test the two positions experimentally and clinically: (1) is it possible for emotion to arise in the absence of its bodily manifestations, and (2) is it possible for emotion to arise in the absence of any mental activity exclusively by eliciting it through artificially induced bodily manifestations? We did this in a number of studies which we shall now consider. An answer to the first question was rrovided by Sherrington in the well-known study in which he cut the vagus nerve 2 and the spinal cord and cut off all the main internal organs and large groups of skeletal muscles from the influence of the brain. In this way, in his experiments, the main bodily manifestations of emotions which arise through reflexes were excluded by surgical means. However, there was no doubt that under appropriate conditions, the experimental dogs exhibited emotional reactions without noticeable changes in the manifestation of characteristic symptoms that are usually taken as signs of anger, fear, satisfaction, or aversion. Thus, the only conclusion that can be reached from these studies is the conclusion that Sherrington himself reaches: the brain continues to produce emotional reactions even after it seems to be disconnected from the internal organs and significant groups of skeletal muscles. Sherrington says that if we believe the signs that are usually accepted as an indication of satisfaction, anger, fear, and repulsion, we cannot doubt that the ani-

88

The Teaching about Emotions

mals exhibit these symptoms after the operation exactly as they did before it. The author refers to the example he observed of fear in a young monkey that had been operated on when approached threateningly by an old macaque. The dropped head, face turned away and frightened, and ears bristling indicated the presence of emotion just as lively as emotion that the animal exhibited before the operation (see R. Creed et al., 1935, p. 184). In the next series of experiments, Sherrington went still further. Mter the animals recovered from the first operation, he cut both vagus nerves at the neck and disconnected the brain from the whole body except for the head and the pectoral girdle. In this way, any doubt that remained after the first operation concerning external manifestations of emotion that might have been established previously with the help of afferent impulses from the remaining internal organs was also subjected to experimental testing. Mfective reactions of experimental dogs did not change even after the second operation. A very emotive dog that had undergone both operations continued to produce intensive and appropriate reactions of anger, satisfaction, and fear. The only doubt that arose after Sherrington's experiments in which a practically complete elimination of visceral reactions and reactions of almost all the skeletal musculature was achieved was formulated by C. Lloyd Morgan 24 ; connecting paths were severed after the visceral and motor changes determined the genesis of emotion, according to the hypothesis that assumes this origin for emotional reactions. Thus, regardless of the fact that actual visceral and motor influences were suppressed, in Sherrington's experiments, traces and results of the first influences were not excluded (see R. Creed et al., 1935, p. 187). For this reason, it might be assumed that we are dealing with simple mimetic reactions of a nonemotional nature, similar to those elicited by V. M. Bekhterev25 in animals without a brain cortex. Finally, one more objection might be entertained: Sherrington's dogs, having been tested over the course of earlier life when emotions were caused peripherally, were not tested again after the operation when emotions arose along a purely cerebral path outside their normal peripheral conditions. To the first objection, Sherrington replies with a reference to a ten-week-old pup he operated on, which was not moved from his place from the day of birth but nevertheless displayed repulsion toward dog meat. In this case, it would scarcely be possible to assume that we were dealing with a reaction that had been established in previous experience and was now activated anew. However, regardless of the completely clear sense of his experiments, Sherrington nevertheless refrains from a definitive conclusion on the doubtfulness of the theory of Lange and James because even after the operations, the animals retained a sufficient quantity of peripheral elements (muscle, skin, vessels of the head and neck) to cause and exhibit emotion. Moreover, Sherrington cannot but note that his experiments do not provide any confirmation for the theories of Lange, James, and Sergi on the nature of emotions. "We must return to the statement that the visceral expression of emotions is secondary and that the activity of the cerebral hemispheres and the corresponding mental state are primary" (see: R. Creed et al., 1935, p. 187). We will mention in passing the experiments of Pogano and Gemelli and Deseaumere and Heymans in which an attempt was made to achieve conditions similar to Sherrington's by pharmacological means and which basically reinforce his conclusions. We cannot but agree with the observations of A. Pieron 26 relative to the incompleteness of the experiments of the latter two authors and, consequently, the inconclusiveness of any conclusions that may be drawn from these experiments (A. Pieron, 1920). However, we cannot but see with A. Binet the enormous historical significance of the first step Sherrington took in a new direction: Binet said that,

The Teaching about Emotions

89

for the first time, a physiologist had tackled a problem posed by psychologists and approached it with the method of vivisection characteristic for that study. The idea at the base of Sherrington's experiments was realized not long ago by others, in a much bolder way by Cannon, J. Lewis, and S. Britton (w. B. Cannon, J. T. Lewis, S. W. Britton, 1927) in experiments that removed the whole sympathetic section of the autonomic system. Thus, after the operation, all vasomotor reactions, adrenaline secretion, visceral reaction, bristling of the hair, and release of sugar in the liver, were precluded in the animals. In animals subjected to sympathectomy, no noticeable changes in emotional reactions arising through the completely normal way (with the exception of bristling) were found in pertinent situations. The absence of afferent impulses from internal organs did not in any way change their usual emotional behavior. Experimental cats exhibited a completely normal emotional reaction in the presence of a barking dog. In 1929, Cannon and his colleagues published further observations of animals that had undergone this operation. The observations wholly confirmed what was established in the very beginning. The standard reaction of the sympathetic section of the autonomic system that was so carefully studied in Cannon's earlier works as an obligatory accompaniment of strong emotions was absent in the animals observed and, at the same time, after a bilateral sympathectomy, the animals did not exhibit any changes in normal emotional behavior. In order to conclude the review of this perhaps most important argument against the James-Lange theory, it remains for us to interpret briefly certain points connected with the studies cited. The first point: the experiments of Sherrington and Cannon provide no direct evidence that sensations from internal organs do not playa significant role in the development of the mental aspect of the reaction and that this state precedes the bodily manifestation of emotion (Angell) just as it might be assumed that together with the exclusion of these sensations, emotion stops being experienced in a specific way as a feeling in the consciousness of the animal (Perry). Actually, on the basis of the experiments in which there is no direct evidence on the mental experience of the animals, we must admit that we have no direct possibility of confirming or denying the presence of one feeling or another in the emotional reaction. Obviously, direct evidence might have been obtained only with a person who could place at our disposal data of an introspective nature. We shall yet turn to such data. But even now we cannot but note that this objection is based on a certain logical error: it proves somewhat much and for this reason, it proves nothing. In any case, it proves much more than it wants to. Of course, in general, our judgment on the emotional experience of an animal is always based on inferences from external manifestations of some state; consequently, if this criterion is doubtful, we must give up altogether any right to ascribe to the animals any kind of feelings and experiences and in this way adopt the point of view of Descartes who considered animals as robots, as reflex machines. If we assume that for normal animals we are justified in deducing from external manifestations of any emotion the presence of an emotional mental state similar to that of a human being, although infinitely removed from it, then we have no basis for making an exception with respect to the animals operated on by Sherrington and Cannon that retained all the behavioral symptoms that compel us always to presume the presence of a mental component in an emotional reaction in normal animals. As Sherrington justifiably notes in response to this objection, "it is difficult to think that a perception that elicits a full manifestation of anger and corresponding behavior is nevertheless powerless to elicit a feeling of anger" (see R. Creed et aI., 1935, p. 188).

90

The Teaching about Emotions

The second point that requires interpretation consists of the fact that Cannon's new experiments confront us with serious theoretical difficulty since they are in apparent contradiction to the interpretation which we, following the author, accepted above with respect to his earlier works. We saw that the organic changes that arise as a result of strong emotions exhibit an undoubted biological expediency, the explanation of which is no small achievement of psychological thought. These reactions, as we have seen, serve as a preparation of the organism for increased activity that usually follows strong emotions in situations that require flight or fight. Meanwhile new experiments seem to favor the opposite. They establish that complete exclusion of organic reactions does not produce any noticeable change in the behavior of the animals. Emotions occur precisely as they did before the operation and the behavior of the animal remains just as adequate to the situation, biologically sensible both with complete disconnection of the brain from the internal organs and with full removal of the sympathetic section of the autonomic system. This contradiction would be an insurmountable difficulty for experimental and theoretical criticism of the organic theory of emotion if it were real and not only seeming. Actually, between the first results of experimental studies and the new, there is not only no contradiction, but, on the contrary, there is complete agreement. Cannon says that in comparison with normal animals, under quiet laboratory conditions, there are no differences in the behavior of the animals with a removed sympathetic section of the autonomic system. At first glance, for this reason it may appear that the sympathetic system has no great significance for normal bodily functioning. But such a conclusion is erroneous. Under conditions of real life, in critical, real situations, an animal that had been operated on could scarcely be compared with a normal animal as to the real possibility of self-preservation. As was established in connection with Cannon's earlier studies, the biological significance of organic reactions that arise as a result of emotion and accompany strong emotions consists exclusively in preparing the organism for activity (flight, fight), for an increased expenditure of energy, for intense muscular work. Thus, the biological significance of these reactions is connected not so much with emotion in itself, with emotion as such, as with the functional consequences of strong emotions. Specifically because of the fact that functional consequences (increased muscle work) are the same for such different emotions as fear and rage, the corresponding organic reactions not only appear to be factually identical, but, speaking theoretically, they cannot be different. Consequently, the fact that emotion as such is preserved even with complete elimination of organic reactions changes nothing in our representation of the biological significance of these organic changes, but, on the contrary, only confirms it again: the significance can be reduced exclusively to preparation of the organism for activity naturally flowing from the emotion. From this point of view, it becomes clear that, under laboratory conditions, the animal that had been operated on does not differ in any way from the normal animal. Like the normal animal, it exhibits the emotion of fear and anger, but under natural conditions, the difference between them must be apparent immediately and very forcefully. Because of the absence of organic changes that usually accompany emotions and prepare the organism for increased expenditure of energy, the animal operated on must be unprepared for fighting or fleeing, which under natural conditions follow directly after the emotions of anger or fear and consequently, at the first serious confrontation with real danger, the animal must die. Emotions that are observed in these animals as completely preserved under laboratory conditions are, so to speak, impotent emotions, emotions devoid of their biological significance, if I may say so, emotions that have been deprived of their stinger: the animal operated on can adequately experience and exhibit the affect of anger, but it is powerless

The Teaching about Emotions

91

when the situation requires from him the natural consequences of this affect-fight and attack. 10 agree with the interpretation presented above of the two debatable points that develop as a result of new studies, we unavoidably must come to the basic conclusion that Cannon reaches from these studies. . Of course, we do not have any real basis for confirming or denying the presence of emotional experience in the animals operated on. But we have a solid basis for judging the relation of these experiments to the James-Lange theory. James ascribes the main role in emotional experience to visceral sensations. Lange ascribes it wholly to sensation of the vasomotor system. Both maintain that if we mentally deduct the organic sensations from emotional experience, nothing will be left of it. Sherrington and Cannon and their colleagues subtracted the sensations surgically. Their animals were without the possibility of reflex impulses from internal organs. According to James, emotional experience would have had to shift to a significant degree toward nothing. According to Lange, it must have disappeared completely. (I will remind you that without excitation of our vasomotor system, impressions of the external world would elicit in us neither joy nor sorrow, would cause us neither concern nor fear.) However, to the extent that nerve connections permitted this, the animals showed no decrease in intensity of emotional reaction. In other words, the operations, which according to the given theory were supposed to destroy emotion very significantly or completely, regardless of this, left anger, joy, and fear in the behavior of the animals to the same extent as they were manifested before the operation. Together with Sherrington, however, we prefer to refrain from a final judgment on the theory under consideration on the basis of these experiments alone: these data will acquire their true significance only in comparison with all other experimental results, on the one hand, and, on the other, with clinical facts that will provide us with indispensable evidence on the conscious emotional experience of man.

5

The persuasiveness of the experimental argument considered above would increase significantly in our eyes if we also had evidence reciprocal to that which was developed experimentally by Sherrington and Cannon. In other words, if we had experimental data pertaining to artificially elicited organic reactions that accompany strong emotions, we would be able to have greater confidence in the conclusions that thrust themselves on us from the studies under consideration. Then we would have before our eyes direct and reciprocal theorems demonstrated with identical logical force: the one and the other taken together would allow us to reach suffi.::iently solid conclusions. Let us recall that James and Lange developed purely speculatively an under~tanding favoring the theory of emotions along the same logical path, seeing the two main pieces of evidence for their theory in the idea that emotion must disappear when bodily manifestations are suppressed, and that emotion must just as inevitably arise when bodily manifestations are artificially elicited. It is natural that '~xperimental confirmation of the theory also should have been conducted along [he same two paths. We already find the first attempts to demonstrate the reciprocal theorem (emotion does not arise even if all its bodily manifestations are present) in the experiments considered above that show that such unemotional states as

92

The Teaching about Emotions

supercooling, overheating, and asphyxia elicit organic changes similar to those that can be observed in fear and fury and emotion does not arise immediately following these changes. A direct transition from the mental experiment of James and Lange to the real experiment was made in the studies of G. Maranon 27 (in W B. Cannon, 1927, p. 113). Maranon's experiments actually are a kind of evidence for the reciprocal theorem in comparison with that which was based on the experiments of Sherrington and Cannon. These experiments showed that an adrenaline injection in doses sufficient to induce organic manifestations typical of strong emotions does not induce emotional experiences in the subjects in the true sense of the word regardless of the fact that all the bodily manifestations are present. What is new in Maranon's experiments is the use of self-observation, which gives us evidence of direct experiences of the subjects. This is the advantage of the latter experiments in comparison with those that were conducted with animals. Thus, with respect to the new studies, there is a definite impression that we do not have direct evidence of the presence or absence of emotional experiences corresponding to bodily manifestations. In Maranon's experiments, both planes, the objective and the subjective, are found within the field of sight of the experimenter. The investigator was able to produce changes that occur in the consciousness of the subject and bodily manifestations of emotion simultaneously and to study their relation to each other. The experiences of the subjects consisted in perceptions of heart rate, diffuse arterial pulsation, a tightness in the chest, constriction of the larynx, trembling, cold, dryness in the mouth, nervousness, indisposition, and feeling unwell. In association with these sensations, in some cases there was an indeterminate affective state that was devoid of real emotion and was coolly evaluated by the subject. The indications of the subjects were of this kind: "I feel as if I were frightened"; "as if I were anticipating great joy, as if I were deeply moved"; "as if I were going to cry, not knowing why"; "just as if I were experiencing great fear and still remained calm" etc. As a result of his studies, Maranon notes a clear difference between perceiving peripheral phenomena of vegetative emotion (that is, bodily changes) and truly mental emotion that did not arise in his subjects, the absence of which let him respond about sensing the vegetative syndrome with complete calmness without real feeling. It is true that there was genuine emotion in a small number of subjects during the experiments, usually distress with tears, sobbing, and sighing. But this occurred only when an emotional predisposition of the subject was noted earlier, especially frequently in hyperthyroidism. In some cases, this state developed only if adrenaline was introduced after a conversation with the subject about his sick children or his deceased parents. Thus, these cases also show that adrenaline has an auxiliary emotiogenic effect only when there is an earlier corresponding emotional mood. We note an exceptional, significant new circumstance that we see in Maranon's experiments and that is usually lost from sight when they are used unilaterally for resolving the serious problem-"for" or "against." This circumstance consists in a very close merging of mental and organic, or, more precisely, cerebral and somatic components of the emotional reaction. At this point, Maranon's experiments indicate not only the relative independence of the one type of component and the other and the possibility of their separate arousal, but also the fact that the one type of component may facilitate the development and reinforcement of the other and that they may exhibit mutual support, may intermingle, and in this way arouse the full affect, undoubted in all its reality from both the aspect of experience and the aspect of its bodily manifestations.

The Teaching about Emotions

93

In cases where in Maranon's experiments, the development of such full and genuine affect is observed, the mental and somatic components elicited by different paths go forth as if to meet each other so that at the point of intersection, at the moment of their meeting, a true emotional agitation is aroused. Let us recall indications of this intermingling, which we mentioned above but stressed insufficiently, which we meet in the formulations of James and Lange and which comprise just about the only true point of their theory. Thus, Lange, in enumerating all the physical symptoms of fear that must be eliminated so that nothing might remain of fear itself, also enumerates clear thinking and firm speech, together with a steady pulse and confident movements. In this point, Lange sins gravely against the logical harmony of his argument: who would stop to argue with his paradoxical argument if serious attention were paid to that part of it which at first glance seems small, but is actually of paramount importance? In fact, in translation into theoretical language, this implies a radical change in the basic position of Lange: instead of his thesis-eliminate in a frightened person all the physical symptoms of fright, what then will remain of his fright?-in essence, he would have had to say: eliminate in the frightened person all the physical and mental symptoms of fright-and then one could not but agree with him. For what else can be implied by the requirement to make his speech firm and his thoughts clear, if not a change in the whole state of his consciousness? The same thing slips through, but less distinctly, in James. In the formula given above on the nonrepresentative quality of anger in the absence of its bodily manifestations, together with distension of the nostrils and clenching of the teeth, James also mentions an urge toward energetic acts, that is, a point that not only more likely characterizes a state of the consciousness than a state of the internal organs and muscles of an angry person, but also an experience that differs radically from perception of bodily manifestation of emotion to the extent that a general urge can be distinguished from a simple feeling or perception. If we evaluate this point in all its theoretical significance, we cannot but note, as with respect to the thesis of Lange, that here we are dealing with a certain logical inconsistency in James' reasoning. This inconsistency James probably would willingly have corrected if he had paid attention to it; but actually it is almost the only grain of truth in the whole theory, a grain that contains the idea that emotion is not simply the sum of feeling the organic reaction, but primarily an urge to act in a certain direction. We shall still have the opportunity to return to this grain of truth in the organic theory of emotions, which we came upon incidentally. Now, however, we cannot but note that it is only in this point in which the theory changes itself, establishing an internal interweaving of experience and organic reaction in the composition of affect and not their cause-effect mechanical dependence, that Maranon's experiments confirm the position of James and Lange; in everything else, these experiments speak against the theory. An injection of adrenaline elicits in man all the typical bodily manifestations that accompany strong emotions, but these manifestations are experienced as sensations and not as emotions. In certain cases, the ,ensations resemble former emotional experience, but do not arouse and do not lctualize it anew, and only in exceptional cases of emotional sensitization prepared in advance can bodily changes lead to the development of genuine affect. Cannon notes that these cases are the exception and not the rule as the James-Lange theory proposes; in ordinary cases, bodily manifestations do not elicit emotional experience as a direct result (W B. Cannon, 1927). Maranon's experiments form a natural transition to data of clinical studies since they confront us directly with man, bring into the field of sight of the invesdgator the subjective psychological plane, and make direct analysis of consciousness

94

The Teaching about Emotions

accessible. By the same token, these experiments allow us to speak not only in the language of the authors of the theory themselves, but also to replace the inferences relative to mental states corresponding to one set or another of bodily manifestations with direct factual observation of the states themselves. Practically speaking, the first experimental studies related to confirming the James theory took this path also. What is new in Maranon's experiments is only the possibility of direct and profound experimental influence of purely physical means on the organic changes that accompany emotions. Of the old studies, we remember the work of A. Lehmann28 (1892), who, on the basis of self-observation, maintained that even the initial idea that elicits emotion is colored with a sensual tone before emotionally colored organic sensations are formed. Consequently he concluded that emotional tone cannot be considered as a sum of organic sensations. In Lehmann's experiments, the sensual tone arose simultaneously or almost immediately after the initial perception. Circulatory disturbance, on the other hand, occurred only one or two seconds after stimulation, therefore after the sensation arose. We are including Lehmann'S data only because objective experimental confirmation was developed in later studies. Summarizing the latter, Cannon, without mentioning Lehmann, presents his conclusions as a new argument against the James theory; visceral changes arise somewhat slowly and cannot for this reason be considered as the source of emotional experience. He compares 29 the data of C. Stewart,30 E. Sertoli,31 J. Langley,32 I. P. Pavlov, and others]3 who established that the latent period of the visceral reaction exceeds significantly the latent period of the affective reaction (w. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 112), which E Wells34 (ibid., p. 112) determines as 0.8 sec, thus shortening the time established by Lehmann. According to the James-Lange theory, the affective reaction arises as a result of reflex impulses from internal organs, but this seems to be impossible if we consider the long latent period of the reaction of these organs to which we must also add the time required for the return of nerve impulses to the brain. In this comparison of the old experiments of Lehmann with the new work, we again see how productive the path of investigation is that combines analysis of objective and subjective aspects of the affective reaction. We find higher forms of such combination in natural experiments in clinical studies of psychopathology of affective life. We can say without exaggeration that without these data, the basic questions connected with the James-Lange theory could not have been resolved, but the main thing is that we could not have approached with a more adequate understanding the nature of affects and their bodily organization. For this reason, data from clinical studies must necessarily be taken into account if we want to reach a definitive judgment in connection with this controversy; in the resolution of this controversy we are always trying to find a solid point of support both for a true understanding of the nature of affects and for a true evaluation of the philosophical teaching on passions, which is of interest to us. The authors of the organic theory themselves turned to data of pathology hoping to find there a direct confirmation of their teaching. James says: "The best evidence that the direct cause of emotions is the physical effect of external stimulation of the nerves are the pathological cases in which there is no appropriate object for emotions. One of the principal advantages of my point of view of emotions is the fact that with its help, we can unite pathological and normal cases of emotions into one general pattern" (1902, p. 310). Thus, James not only recognized the admissibility of factual verification of the theory with psychopathological data, but saw in them the best evidence for this theory and believed its main advantage to be that it would explain normal and pathological affects equally well. In every

The Teaching about Emotions

95

insane asylum, he expected to meet samples of affects motivated by nothing, which in his opinion would be the very best evidence for the validity of his position. For this reason, it is completely natural to turn to a consideration of the extent to which these samples, these pathological cases, actually speak in favor of the theory under consideration (or against it) and to what extent this theory actually is capable of bringing normal and pathological affective life into a single pattern. C. G. Lange also assumed that the latter point of support for the hypothesis on mental emotion disappears as soon as we turn to abnormal functioning of emotional processes. He even thought that "the vasomotor apparatus is subjected especially severely to the danger of functioning abnormally and for this reason, it forms that part of the nervous system that relies least on respiration and experiences emotional storms more frequently than other parts. When disturbances of this kind happen in any subject, then, according to the circumstances, the subject is depressed, fearful, or irrepressibly cheerful, shy, etc.-and all of this with no reason, although the person expressing these emotions knows that he has no reason to be angry, fearful, or happy. Where in this is the point of support for the hypothesis of 'mental emotion'?" (1896, pp. 62-63). As we see, Lange again remarkably agrees with James not only in the formulation of his general positions, but also in the details of the argumentation that he develops. In contrast to Lange, however, James clearly understood that the general and overall reference to pathological, unmotivated affects is only indirect and quite shaky evidence in favor of his theory because this kind of reference, practically speaking, presents nothing new in comparison with the observation of a normal course of affects since even in cases of unmotivated affect (if we leave aside for the moment the phenomenon of so-called sincere distress and sincere fright in which, in the opinion of James, emotion is simply a sensation of a bodily state and is due to a purely physiological process), the question that is central to the whole theory remains completely without explanation: in unmotivated affect what should be considered as the cause and what as the effect if the mental state and organic changes are taken together exactly as they are in normal cases? In fact, pathological factors are distinguished from normal only and exclusively in that in them, perception, the cause that elicits the affect, is absent, but, in fact, the whole James-Lange theory is not concerned with this. They attempt to demonstrate that emotion as such, and not the cause of it, is the result of bodily manifestations of affect, but this cardinal position remains as unproved and unprovable as it is in normal emotion. Obviously, for direct confirmation or refutation of the theory, pathological phenomena of an entirely different character are required. James understood this. He indicated that for his theory, only such pathological cases could serve in which we could observe preservation or loss of emotionality in nonparalyzed subjects characterized at the same time by complete insensibility. James himself indicates certain observations approaching such phenomena, and interprets them in a sense favorable to his theory. Like the data of Revault d'A1lones and G. G. Meyerson, we will leave these aside since they, according to the valid comment of C. Landis,35 are interesting case histories and, consequently, should be considered as such since they are all connected with primary unbalance and disturbance of affective life and for this reason must serve now more from the point of view of psychoanalysis rather than from the point of view of physiological psychology. W James himself believed the conditions he advanced for a critical experiment to be incapable of being realized, but they were realized first in the psychophysiological experiment of Sherrington, who, according to the valid observation of Du-

96

The Teaching about Emotions

mas, approached the requirements of James, and then also in clinical observations conducted by C. Dana36 (c. Dana, 1921). These clinical studies not only allow us to use data of self-observation for the practical study of emotional experiences in pathological cases, but also elucidate still another exceptionally important area of facts that we have thus far left aside, which the organic theory of emotions broaches. Thus far, we have dealt mainly and almost exclusively with the visceral aspect of bodily manifestations of emotions, that is, with the functions of the sympathetic section of the central nervous system. Because of the conditions of the physiological experiment, we left aside motor and mimetic manifestations of emotion, which playa role in the theory of James, although only a secondary role, but nevertheless the role of a significant factor in producing emotion. Moreover, as was the case in the experiments of Cannon and Sherrington, we frequently had to depend on the retention of motor and mimetic aspects of bodily manifestations as evidence of the presence of the mental part of emotions also. Clinical studies, which we will now proceed to consider, also allow us to form an answer regarding this group of facts and, thus, they bring us closer to definitive results, to a definitive resolution of the problem.

6

As we have seen, those who support the organic theory are trying to save it by introducing into it a substantial factual corrective that might reconcile this theory with the unambiguous data of experimental studies. These data indicate that visceral changes cannot be considered as a source of emotions and, consequently, it must be assumed that sensations of tension and movement of the skeletal musculature are the real source of the emotional state and vary correspondingly with different emotions. S. Wilson 37 described cases of pathological manifestations of laughter and tears in which clearly expressed external manifestations of emotion did not at all correspond to the actual feelings of the patients. Their emotional experiences proceeded completely normally, and they suffered from the fact that their real feelings were contradicted in their expression. The patients could experience sorrow while laughing loudly; with all external manifestations of happiness and joy, including also accompanying reactions of visceral mechanisms, these persons could not only not feel happy, but the corresponding state of their consciousness could be in open conflict with the external expression of their emotions (S. Wilson, 1924, pp. 299-333). It is completely clear that the James-Lange hypothesis must be changed radically if it is to be brought into agreement with observations of this kind in which a full merging of peripheral and cerebral components is absent. S. Wilson cites opposite cases that also indicate absence of parallelism between mental and somatic elements of emotion. These are cases of emotional paralysis of the face in which patients experience feeling and are keenly aware of their normal emotional states in appropriate situations. They exhibit a mask-like facial expression, but behind the mask there is a completely normal play of emotional reactions. The patients suffer from a complete absence of expressive movement of the face. According to Wilson's evidence, they were very sensitive to this circumstance and saw it as a great misfortune that interfered with their showing others that they were eTseriencing joy or sorrow. According to the graphic comparison of N. V. Konovalov, 8 in patients of the first and second type described by Wilson, we always see this kind of noncorrespondence of emotional experience and its external

The Teaching about Emotions

97

expression, which resembles a mask. But if in patients of the second type, the face resembles a death mask placed on a person who has an amplitude of lively emotions, then in patients of the first type, the face resembles a mask of a Greek actor with exaggerated pathetic emotional expression that can clash sharply with the internal state of the hero or the actor portraying him and with the words of the role that he speaks. Practically speaking, in patients of this type, we see that which Victor Hugo described in the novel L 'Homme Qui Rit. S. Wilson cites data from the self-observation of his patients, who protest that their laughter and tears are taken by others as indications of their real affective state. Wilson says that this cannot be reconciled with the conclusion that bodily manifestations, as James calls them, form emotion. Conversely, patients with facial diplegia can retain a mask-like facial expression and experience "internal laughter." Wilson summarizes his observations in the form of a general thesis. From the point of view of clinicians, in his words, he must agree with physiologists when they assume that organic changes have relatively little significance in comparison with the cerebral changes with which mental components of emotional reaction are connected. But as Bard notes, cases of retention of normal emotional life in patients suffering from complete or almost complete immobility of skeletal musculature have a still greater significance. C. Dana reports that in such patients, normal subjective emotional reactions were retained. Dana is the person who described a remarkable observation which, in the opinion of Bard, gives a direct answer to the objection that is frequently raised with respect to the failure of Sherrington's experiments to prove the presence of emotional experience in animals that were operated on. A highly intelligent female patient, age 40, broke her neck at the third and fourth cervical vertebrae. She suffered complete paralysis of the skeletal musculature, the body and four e~remities, with complete loss of superficial and deep sensitivity of the whole body from the neck down. She lived about a year and during that time, Dana observed her emotions of grief, joy, dissatisfaction, and affection. It was impossible to note any changes in her personality or character. She had control only of the musculature of the skull, the upper part of the neck, and the diaphragm. The possibility of emotional discharges of sympathetic impulses was precluded. From the point of view of the peripheral theory, it is difficult to understand how ner emotionality suffered no changes while her skeletal system was practically eliminated and the sympathetic system was also wholly excluded. Dana reaches the following basic conclusion from these observations: emotions are localized centrally and spring from the activity and interaction of the cortex and the thalamus. Centers that control the activity of the vegetative nervous system are found mainly in the brain stem. These centers are excited primarily when the animal perceives something that requires defense, attack, and active striving. In turn, they excite the muscles, internal organs, and glands, and they also communieate with the cortex and excite emotions corresponding to the perceived object or the idea generated. It remains for us to consider the positive part of the theoretical considerations of this clinician. Like the negative conclusions, this part coincides with conclusions that physiologists reached as a result of their experimental studies. Now we shall continue to consider these most important clinical data, which are probably capable of giving a direct answer to the question that is of interest to us, f.nd, in combination with previously considered data of the experiment, can almost definitely untangle the knot of contradictions that grew around this famous theory over the decades. H. Head described cases of a unilateral affliction of the thalamus opticus: a typical symptom in patients was the tendency to excessive reaction to any affective

98

The Teaching about Emotions

stimuli, a somewhat strange change in emotional tone expressed in the fact that a pin prick, painful pressure, heating, or cooling made a much greater emotional impression on the affected part of the body than on the normal part. Pleasant stimuli also were experienced emotionally more intensely in the affected part. A warm touch elicited in the patients an intense feeling of satisfaction exhibited as facial expressions of joy and in the expression of pleasant satisfaction. Complex affective stimuli, for example, perception of music or singing, could elicit emotional experiences of such great power (on the affected side) that they were unbearable for the patient. One of Head's patients was unable to stay in his place in church because he could not tolerate the effect of the singing on his affected side. In listening to singing, another patient experienced a terrible feeling in the affected side. One of the patients said that after an incident that made him especially sensitive to pleasant and unpleasant feelings on the right side of his body, he became more sensitive. He said that the right hand always required comforting. To the patient, it seems that on his right side, he constantly pines for sympathy. His right hand seems to be more "artificial." Thus, the part of the body with the diseased thalamus opticus reacts more forcefully to affective elements of both external stimuli and internal mental states. There is an increased sensitivity of the affected part of the body to states of satisfaction and dissatisfaction. E. Kiippers,39 who goes further than others in evaluating the role of the thalamus opticus as a source of mental states, formulates the results of observations of patients of this kind and says that a patient with unilateral thalamic disease has a different soul on the right than on the left. On one side, he requires comforting, is more sensitive to pain, is more "artificial," tender, and impatient than on the other. Putting aside for now the interpretation that James gives these cases, we must derive from them what pertains directly to the problem we are discussing. Two points are of primary interest for us. The first: as Head established, in a patient of this kind, a significant difference is noted in the sensual tone of separate sensations. While some sensations and perceptions have no substantial emotional effect, others intensely affect the diseased side. Specifically, as Cannon correctly notes, sensation arising with different positions of the body and different poses that are completely devoid of emotional tone acquires exceptional significance. From this, it follows that afferent impulses from skeletal muscles in which the followers of James were inclined to see, as was noted above, the principal extravisceral source of emotion, after excluding visceral sensations in critical experiments, cannot be considered as the actual source of emotion since they have no such specific requisite quality (sensual tone) that alone could compel us to see in them the real cause of an emotional state. Consequently, the last resort of those that try to save the organic theory of emotions seems to have been destroyed by Head's clinical studies. Neither reflex impulses from internal organs nor impulses from innervated muscles can serve as the source of the specific quality of emotions. The second point that is of interest to us: in Head's studies, we encounter facts that are, in general, completely inexplicable from the point of view of James' conception and, consequently, are in irreconcilable contradiction to it. Actually, from this point of view, how could we explain the fact of unilateral change of affective experiences while retaining the basic assumptions that the authors of this theory proposed? As Cannon notes, neither the organs of the chest area nor the abdominal organs can function by halves, and the vasomotor center also is a unit; Head's patients do not, of course, exhibit only right- or left-sided laughter or tears. Thus, impulses sent from disturbed peripheral organs must be the same from both

The Teaching about Emotions

99

sides. To explain the unsymmetrical feeling, we must resort to the organ that is capable of functioning unsymmetrically, that is, to the thalamus opticus. In concluding the review of the clinical data on the problem that is of interest to us, we see that we acquired in these new facts a new and most important point of support for solving the theoretical controversy that we are investigating. As we have noted, in publishing his theory, James himself turned to the clinic, saying that if his theory is to be definitely confirmed or repudiated, this will be done in the clinic because only the clinic has the data required for this. On the basis of various observations, the clinic has accumulated enough facts that were unknown when this theory was formulated and has thus obtained a real means for confirming or repudiating James' hypothesis. This being said, there can scarcely remain any doubt that clinical studies unambiguously and definitely speak for repudiation rather than for confirmation of this hypothesis. In clinical studies, we find still another point that can take our studies, which have been stuck on one point for so long, further. Studying and interpreting clinical data pertaining to affective life in pathological conditions, we cannot limit ourselves only to drawing from them supplementary evidence that compels us to reject the organic hypothesis as clearly not corresponding to reality. We would have to be theoretically blind not to see the substantial reversal of the whole theory of emotions that is so starkly evident in these clinical data. Should we want to define in one sentence the content of the radical reversal of the theoretical thought that tries to penetrate the nature of emotional life, we would have to say along with Bard: the greatest service that the new theory accords us consists of moving experimental study from the periphery to the brain. This radical shifting of the attention of investigation and theory of emotion by 1800 actually entails a complete reversal in scientific conceptions of the nature of affective processes. But in order to elucidate definitively for ourselves the real significance of this reversal and its relation to the basic problem of our studies, we must critically examine the argument that arose in connection with it.

7 If we stop, or more correctly, pick up at the point to which we have brought the consideration of the enormous experimental and factual material that has accumulated over more than the half century that has passed since the formulation of the organic theory (of which material we could bring only an insignificant portion), and try to take in at a single glance the picture as a whole of the situation as it developed at this time in the teaching on emotions, we cannot but note that very substantial changes have occurred in most recent times. Whereas the first decades brought with them specific critical ideas and facts capable of shaking the stability of the reigning theory of emotions, such as the studies of Lehmann, Sherrington, and others, in recent decades, owing to the enormous accumulation of material coming from various parts, we simply cannot go on accumulating such uncoordinated data. The very course of the development of scientific thought brought about a need to coordinate and-what is most important-it brought about the attempt at a different interpretation and construction of a theory capable of adequately explaining the accumulated factual material. Thus, the task of criticism and verification moved to the background in comparison with the tasks of developing the indicated theory of emotions. Confirming or rejecting the theory of James was not enough. We were

100

The Teaching about Emotions

confronted by the need to create a new theory for new facts, to compare it with the old theory, and include in it everything in the hypothesis of James and Lange that was true and had withstood actual testing. In one way, their hypothesis had already proved itself historically in that it engendered a number of studies and in this way nudged scientific thought toward discovering hitherto unknown phenomena of reality which in themselves predetermined the direction for the movement of theoretical thought. Thus, the change in the situation and the whole state of the problem of emotions that has occurred in the last decades consists primarily of the fact that we seem to have two theories of emotions opposing each other. Earlier studies, each separately, were capable neither of deciding the fate of the old theory nor of developing any kind of theoretical hypotheses to replace it. Investigators such as Sherrington refrained from definitive judgment and limited their work to purely critical tendencies. This specifically is what explains the paradoxical situation in which, in spite of the critical material that accumulated systematically decade after decade, the theory of James-Lange not only continued to exist as generally accepted scientific truth, but became even more fixed in its significance and viability, spreading its basic principle to the whole area of psychology, creating in its own image and likeness whole new directions in our science that originated from the reflex principle in explaining human psychology on the whole. Not one of the blows inflicted on the organic theory could kill it, but, according to common assumption, what doesn't kill me, makes me stronger. This is wholly applicable to the historical fate of the James-Lange theory: its critics were not able to coordinate it, they were not able to compare it to the other more strongly armed theory, they could not reinforce their attacks with a positive interpretation of new facts. These tasks fell wholly to the last decade. It-this decade-honorably met the tasks that confronted it, and was able to correlate the uncoordinated work of former decades, enrich it with data of scientific study and new tools previously unavailable, and inflict a crushing and definitive blow to the old theory. With all of this, however, it carried out only the first and most elementary part of the work. As we have seen from the review of studies presented above, and as we shall see even more clearly later in considering the theory that was born in the struggle, in the process of criticism and testing, we still even now-in the criticism of the old and in constructing the new-have not left the small circle of problems to which the theoretical thought of the James-Lange hypothesis drove us. We could not rise above the formulation of the problem that was given in their hypothesis. Practically all who oppose this hypothesis were, to a lesser degree than its partisans, timid students of James and Lange in the formulation itself of the basic problems of the psychology of affects. For this reason, criticism of the fallacious theory was itself tied to the root of the false formulation of the problem that lay at the base of the theory itself, which predetermined to no small measure its erroneousness. To a significant degree, this affected the character of the new theory that was born in the process of critical testing of the James-Lange theory. The new theory, like the criticism, developed its structure on the same foundation on which the old had been built. One of the founders of the new theory-Cannan-terms it an alternative to the hypothesis of James. Actually, both theories, the old and the new, are alternatives in the sense that they propose two mutually exclusive concrete solutions of one and the same problem. That the new theory could not be anything greater than an alternative to the old and, consequently, that it could not rise above the plane over which psychological thought extended and to which it

The Teaching about Emotions

101

was firmly nailed to the ground during the last half century was fixed as firmly as possible by this designation. If we ask ourselves what, in the final analysis, as a total result, the half century of criticism has given to the old theory and what the new theory, which claims to replace the former, gives us now, we cannot but respond in a contradictory fashion: both very much and very little. Much-in the sense of concrete refutation of the old positions that in the light of factual verification proved to be false and, consequently, also the theory constructed on it to be unsubstantiated. Much-in the sense of explaining the extremely significant and substantial factual circumstances that cast no small light on the organization and activity of emotions, on their biological significance, their connection with other vital processes, and their place in a series of other forms of nervousmental activity. Finally, much-in the sense of theoretical correlation of an enormous amount of factual material, predominantly of a psychological and neurological nature, a correlation logically sequential, elegant, and quite convincing for encompassing and explaining most of the facts known to us. Nevertheless, in the final analysis, both criticism and the new theory produced little. Little-with respect to the fact that criticism did not pull out the philosophical stinger of the old theory, did not expose or destroy the pathological bases on which it was constructed, and did not unmask the psychological error in the formulation of the problem itself, but, on the contrary, adopting it totally, thereby included these errors in the new construction. Little-in the sense that the new theory, like the old, did not in the least approach the solution to the main and basic problem - to constructing a psychology of human affects, not to speak of the high theoretical significance of this chapter of our science, or of solving the essentially philosophical problems of psychological investigation, the psychological theory of passions, without which, it seems, the problem of affect itself cannot be properly placed in human psychology. In his introduction to the last symposium,4o which gathered opinions on the nature of emotions of the most outstanding representatives of psychological science, M. Bentley41 notes correctly the constraint imposed on all of contemporary psychology of emotions by the formulation of the problem as presented in the JamesLange organic hypothesis. In the fateful domination of the old theory, Bentley sees the characteristic trait of all of the contemporary psychology of emotions. The past suppresses the present. The new constantly wrestles with the old, but it fights with the arms of the old and for this reason, regardless of seeming victories, it is always a slave to the old and vainly defeated fallacy. The dead seizes the living. The domination of the dead over the living in the contemporary psychology of emotions creates the desert that compels Bentley to place at the threshold of the symposium the basic question: "Is emotion something greater than simply the title of a chapter?" (M. Bentley, 1928, p. 17). Bentley says that for the last half century the teaching on emotions occupied a place as a new and quite important chapter in general courses and treatises on psychology. But what was the content of this chapter? A section on classification of emotions; a section on James-Lange (usually the longest); a section on expression of emotions; sometimes a short description and purely practical discussion regarding the use and difficulties of emotional disturbances. Why, asks Bentley, do we continue even now to whet our knives on broken pieces of this hard fossil formed from exaggerations? Is it because psychology still has theories that command very little respect that it is afraid to allow one of them to disappear or die? (ibid., p. 18).

102

The Teaching about Emotions

. The position of critics on this paradoxical and everlasting theory is remarkably like the humorous tale cited by V. I. Da1'42 about the hunter who proudly exaggerated before his comrades that he had got a bear. When his comrades suggested that he bring them the animal, the hunter replied: "But I can't!"-"Go yourself!""But he won't let me!" Just as in this story it is never clear who caught whom-the hunter the bear or the bear the hunter-the critics of the notorious theory themselves seem to be under its power and not only can they not bring their catch as they wish to the point where the advancing course of development of the teaching on affects requires them to bring it, but they themselves cannot take even a single step away from what they have caught. 'frue, problems not resolved by criticism and the new theory, of which we have just spoken, are problems that can be handled probably only over a great number of years and with the help of extensive and profound studies. In their very essence, they cannot be resolved in the process of criticism even of the most productive fallacy. On the contrary, criticism is a necessary precursor for the formulation itself of these problems. It opens the door for them; nevertheless we think that the time has come to make the first attempt to go through this partly opened door and try to note completely freely and without preconceptions at least the most general bases for developing new problems in the psychology of affects, problems about which the ancient sages did not even dream. The present study makes this first attempt, of necessity a quite limited and modest attempt. It may seem strange that we are trying to take the first step along the new path from the top-from the philosophical heights of the teaching on the passions. An objection might be raised completely reasonably that there is an abyss between this theory of a physiological and neurological order, which promises the development of a new teaching from below, and the heights of theoretical inferences that provide a view of the whole field of future studies and a whole series of new problems that fill this field: it can be filled only by intense and long-term efforts of collectors of new facts and blazers of new trails. But it seems to us that the path selected is a perfectly legitimate path because the new investigations must themselves be made possible: from below, without which materialistic scientific thought would not be able to move and develop at all on the plane of the more complex and intricate problems pertaining to the psychological nature of human passions; and from above, without which it not only would not be able to overcome the methodological roots of the fallacies of the organic theory of emotions, but would not at all be able to see the direction in which investigation must proceed in order to obtain firm and reliable knowledge as a result. If we compare the contemporary state of knowledge about affects in this respect with other basic sections of contemporary psychology, we cannot but note that it is a unique and sad exception in a series of other chapters in it. This circumstance can be easily understood as inevitably caused by the whole history of development of scientific psychological thought. Moreover, it places teaching on the passions in a position of sad exclusion due to which-we can state with no vacillation-this almost basic chapter of psychology seems to be much lower than all other chapters. It seems to be paralyzed in forward development. Within, it is a desert, usually filled, as Bentley correctly notes, with dead material, and it raises doubts among astute investigators: in general, does it represent anything more than only a noisy headline above pages filled with nothing? Casting even a quick glance at contemporary teaching on perception, the contemporary theory of memory, the teaching on thinking so well developed in the last decade, and the psychology of speech that has been developing so vigorously

The Teaching about Emotions

103

recently, we cannot but be impressed by the sharp contrast that is disclosed in comparing these chapters of current psychology with the teaching on affects. There are contrasts not only in that the chapters enumerated are exceptionally rich in theoretical thought, extensively developed from the factual aspect, and full of vital and rapidly developing content, whereas teaching on affects is thus far chained, like a convict to a wheelbarrow, to the point at which history tied the famous knot of the organic theory. All of this is more the result of than the basis for the difference. The difference consists mainly of the fact that other chapters of psychology worked out the paths along which they arrived at authentically scientific and authentically psychological study of appropriate problems. For this reason, they face the future directly and boldly. Only the teaching on passions was blind, without a path, in a dead-end, facing backward, toward the distant past. It has not even developed its own problematics and thus far has not questioned the validity or falsity of the old formulation of basic and central problems of its whole teaching. We believe the cause for the difference to be hidden in the fact that in all sections of psychology with the exception of the section on emotions, we observe one general phenomenon that appears so regularly, so consistently in the most diverse areas, so productively for every area separately and for psychology as a whole that we cannot at all consider it to be accidental. On the contrary, it seems to us to emanate inevitably from the crisis that contemporary psychology is experiencing and to be the only saving way that can lead and is, before our eyes, already partially leading science out of this crisis. This phenomenon consists of a profound philosophical tendency that permeates the most various areas of contemporary psychological investigation. Essentially, the tendency to bring philosophical and psychological problems together, to resolve philosophical problems on the basis of concrete material of the human mind, to disclose philosophical points immanently contained in the most concrete and empirical problem of human psychology is, on closer consideration, reciprocal. It can be easily traced from two ends. On the one hand, philosophical study, passing from historical analysis of philosophical systems and dogmatic development of advanced old or renovated systems to concrete analysis, of necessity is confronted with the inevitability of studying living reality as it is represented in contemporary science, specifically, in contemporary psychology. We might cite the old studies of H. Bergson43 on the problem of memory (1896) and the newer studies of E. CassirerM on the psychology of speech (1925) to illustrate this fact, new to philosophy, that to solve his problems, the philosopher immerses himself in concrete experimental and clinical material obtained in modern times, and philosophical thought, almost torn away from concrete analysis of living reality and dependent on scientific systems of the distant past, during the recent period, is trying anew to meet directly, face to face, with this reality, most of all through concrete scientific knowledge, specifically, psychological knowledge. But even psychological study comes, of necessity, to the point of development when it begins, not noticing this itself, to solve problems of an essentially philosophical character. A situation arises, not unusual in contemporary psychology, which one of the subjects of N. Ach 45 (1921), taking part in experiments on the formation of concepts, defined in words quoted by the author in the introduction to the whole study: but, of course, this is experimental philosophy. The studies by Ach of the formation of concepts, by J. Piaget of the development of children's logic and its basic categories, by M. Wertheimer46 and W. Kohler of perception, and by E. Jaensch of memory may serve as examples of such experimental philosophy that penetrates into psychological studies. As has been said, for contemporary psychology, this phenomenon is rather the rule than the exception. All of it seethes

104
