The Teaching about Emotions

with philosophical problems-the authentic ferment of development of the major contemporary psychological theories. Only the teaching on passions is an exception. nue, even here, that of which F. Engels47 spoke is occurring: whether natural scientists want it or not, philosophers are guiding them. One of the basic tasks of our investigation is to disclose the philosophical thought that directs the old and the contemporary natural scientists in their theories on affective life. But it is understood that there is a substantial difference between unconscious and conscious service of one philosophical idea or another. While other chapters of psychology spontaneously took the path of being included in the general system of philosophy that alone is capable of leading them out of the crisis, the teaching on passions remains thus far at the point of solidifying basic empiricism. It is obvious that in the teaching on the passions, we are confronted by the task of raising it to the level of other chapters of contemporary psychology. Speaking more simply, we are confronted by the task of creating even if only the initial bases of a psychological theory of affects that is conscious of its philosophical nature, not fearing the greatest generalizations adequate with respect to the psychological nature of passions and worthy of becoming one of the chapters of human psychology, perhaps even the basic chapter. The construction of such a theory cannot, of course, be resolved in one study or one of an abstract character, but, as in any complicated matter, a division of work is indispensable here. No doubt this theory can be created only as a result of a series of studies. And so, it seems to us, in the development of these studies, a time has come that, completely independently of the three-hundred-year jubilee of Spinoza, places before investigators the task of correlating the whole path taken and marking the future path. According to the valid observations of Goethe,48 for all people together to comprehend nature, in the common comprehension, cooperation based on a division of labor is indispensable. It seems to me that, owing to this division of labor, to our lot has fallen the task (inevitably arising before our eyes also in all the other sections of psychology) of collecting together and correlating the uncoordinated factual material, disclosing behind the struggle of concrete psychological teachings the struggle of philosophical ideas, noting the philosophical understanding of the psychological problem of affects and, thus, laying a path for future investigations. This task cannot be resolved otherwise than by special study. We included in the subheading of the work the words, "Historical-Psychological Study," because we see in it the indispensable part of the work on creating a new theory of emotions. Within the study itself there is another division of labor: not only the collection of facts, but also their analysis and correlation and the disclosure of the ideas that illuminate them is the primary task of the study; the real work appears to us to be investigation, not because it includes producing with our hands a certain addition of a concrete and factual nature to scientific knowledge in the process of direct experimentation, but because the path itself to authentic correlation, to comprehension of higher theoretical points of teaching on the passions cannot, we are convinced, be anything other than only investigation. We selected for investigation a strange and naive path -comparing the old philosophical teaching to contemporary scientific knowledge, but now this path seems to us to be historically inevitable. We do not think we will find in the teachings of Spinoza on the passions a ready theory meeting the needs of contemporary scientific knowledge. On the contrary, in the course of our investigation, depending on the truth in the Spinoza teaching, we mean to point out his fallacies. We think that in our hands there is no more reliable and powerful weapon for a criticism of

The Teaching about Emotions

105

Spinoza than a testing of his ideas in the light of contemporary scientific knowledge. But we assume that even contemporary scientific teaching on the passions can be led out of the historical dead-end only with the help of a great philosophical idea. Contrary to the established opinion that sees in the psychology of Spinoza only separate accurate generalizations and comparisons, declaring it on the whole a definitive achievement of the past, we are trying in our investigation to disclose its living part. For this reason, the basic point of view of the present investigation may be expressed most distinctly and clearly specifically by contrasting it with the traditional view as formulated by one of the investigators of Spinoza's Ethics, who assumes that his teaching on passions for the psychologist of our time may be of only historical interest. In contrast to this, we assume that the Spinoza teaching on passions may be of genuine historical interest for contemporary psychology-not in the sense of elucidating the historical past of our science, but in the sense of a turning point of the whole history of psychology and its future development. Purified of fallacy, the truth of this teaching, it seems to us, will pass, hard and sharp, through an array of basic problems advanced by perception of the psychological nature of passions and all of human psychology, and will resolve them as diamond cuts glass. It will help contemporary psychology in what is most basic and major-in the formation of the idea of man, which would serve as a type of human nature for us.

8

But let us return again to the problem of the truth and fallacy of the old and the new theory of emotions. We have already said that in the protracted critical review of the organic hypothesis, an enormous amount of new data has accumulated which is in urgent need of elucidation and correlation. Criticism inevitably had to move on to the development of a new hypothesis. The movement of theoretical thought met and merged into one flood with studies that came from another area-from neurology and the clinic-and were permeated with the same tendency toward creating a different explanation for the facts discovered. Thus, from the crossing of two series of studies there developed what might now be considered the most widely accepted and dominant theory of emotional reactions which, after its central point, came to be called the thalamic theory. We will first consider in a most general way the second series of investigations, to which we have thus far not given any attention. The new theory, like the lames-Lange theory, arises from the extremely close relation that exists between sensation and emotions. However, it resolves the problem of the interrelation of two basic classes of mental processes differently than does the organic theory. The latter dissolved emotions in sensations, reduced the former to the latter, seeing in them only sensations of a special kind, specifically sensations arising as a result of stimulations of an intraorganic nature. The new theory turns attention primarily not to reducing feeling to sensation, but to the close approach and sometimes complete merging of the one with the other. This circumstance finds a direct expression both in the phenomenological analysis of our experience and in the autonomy and physiology of the brain. To designate the merging of directly experienced sensation and feeling, K. Stumpf introduced the term, "sensation of feeling." E. Kretschmer49 says that this might best be explained on the basis of the sensation of pain. Of course, logically we could say: pain is a feeling of sensation "a" that is accompanied by a certain

106

The Teaching about Emotions

affect-a feeling of pain "b." Genuine, actual experience, however, is something else completely: "b" does not accompany "a," but "b" and "a" are completely identical in the experience. Purely phenomenologically, pain is sensed to the same degree as the feeling; they are simultaneous in a single indivisible act. This view also has a basic significance for our thinking in the area of the physiology of the brain. The sharp division of sensations and feelings is logically necessary and, in this case, is also a nonphenomenological abstraction. For the first time, only at higher levels of activity, perceptions and representations appear as content and affect in a more independent and variable relation to one another and make it possible then to consider them as actually separated in experience. The fact of the non differentiability of sensations and feelings in primitive consciousness at different levels of development was studied and developed extremely thoroughly and in detail by the Leipzig school of F. Kruger,50 who made it the point of departure for all of his developmental psychology. Common to most contemporary psychological directions is the idea that at the beginning of development, we meet not separate elements of developed mental life, but whole, undifferentiated formations that only at higher stages of development begin to be differentiated into more or less independent and definitely shaped families, species, and classes of mental processes. H. Volkelt, one of the representatives of the Leipzig school,S! speaks of such formations, typical for early stages of development: only when we can describe these wholes, which yield to description with great difficulty and are still relatively very diffuse, will we see to what extent these primitive wholes are close to feelings. Actually, no kind of experience of an adult, except feelings, can approach so close to these primitive complexes that are found in a state of diffusion both within themselves and to a certain degree also in relation to the environment. The lower we go into the world of the primitive, the more mental wholes in both their general form and in their structure approach the essence of feeling. These emotion-like sensations and perceptions were introduced by Kruger into the area of phenomena that he called, "the area of the feeling-image." In his new presentation of the teaching on the nature of feeling, this author sees the essence of feelings, which can become the basis of a systematic theory, in the complex of qualities that characterize experience of any whole mental formation. If in general theory, Kruger ascribes to feeling an exceptional and dominant significance in the whole organization of mental life, and in this way breaks away from many psychological directions, then in the specific statement on the merging of sensation and feeling at early stages of development, he finds support on the part of a great majority of contemporary investigators. As an example, we might point only to the positions being developed by contemporary structural psychology, which, in Koffka's words, claimed that at early stages of development, an object for consciousness is as intense as it is black, and that the first emotional-like perceptions must be considered the real point of departure for all subsequent development. A closer relationship, sometimes approaching complete merging of sensation and feeling, cannot but have anatomical and physiological bases. Such bases are developed in the teaching of a number of eminent representatives of contemporary neurology. The common conclusion at which these investigators (J. Muller,52 Herrick, and others) arrived is the position that all sensible and sensory paths that come from the periphery to the brain (with the exception of the olfactory) enter the thalamus opticus and are interrupted in it. Thus, anatomically, the thalamus opticus forms a major distribution center for all paths of sensation; there is in it a broad potential for regrouping of afferent impulses and for their distribution along paths of separate sensations that proceed farther to special projection fields in the cortex of the brain. On the one hand, this area has

The Teaching about Emotions

107

developed associative paths that connect it with the cortex; on the other, if we include in it not only sensory centers, but also motor centers and centers of motor coordination, this area is connected with the internal organs and the skeletal musculature. As Herrick says, under ordinary circumstances, no simple sensory impulse can reach the cortex of the brain unless it is first subjected to processing in the subcortical centers that activate complex combinations of reflex acts and various automatic actions corresponding to their preformed structure. In keeping with this, Milller developed a theory regarding the functions of the thalamus opticus (J. Milller, 1842). According to his theory, this area is considered specifically as the site in the brain where various sensations obtain their unique emotional coloring and sensual tone. Bodily sensations of pain or satisfaction arise in this area, while the brain cortex is important only for localization of sensations and perceptions. This area is also the transmission point in which excitations of sensible neurons are transferred to similar neurons of the autonomic system. From this point of view, the area of the thalamus opticus is the main center of sensory functions and of elementary affective life inseparably connected with them. Together with the centers of the autonomic nervous system and psychomotor centers of the brain stem located near the thalamus opticus, this area forms a center for visceralaffective reactions. Even before Milller, Head came to similar views; together with G. Holmes,s3 he ascribes to this area the functions of producing conscious states. Based on his observations of cases of unilateral damage to the thalamus opticus, Head reaches the conclusion that this organ is the center for consciousness of certain elements of sensation and responds to all stimuli that are capable of eliciting satisfaction or dissatisfaction or consciousness of change in general state. The emotional tone of somatic or visceral sensations is the product of its activity. Kilppers goes further than anyone in this respect; as we have seen, in interpreting cases with unilateral damage to the thalamus opticus, he proposes the idea that such patients have a different soul on one side than on the other. Thus, he is inclined to localize not only essential functions in this area, but almost the very soul as well. Probably independently of this series of studies and, in any case, depending on investigation of a different kind, Dana and Cannon proposed a complex theory. According to their idea, emotions arise as a result of activity of the thalamus opticus. Cannon formulates the basic position of the theory as follows: "The specific quality of emotions combines with simple sensation when thalamic processes are excited" (W, B. Cannon, 1927, p. 120). What is essentially new in this variant of the thalamic theory of emotions is the idea of interaction of the cortex of the brain and the thalamus opticus as a real physiological substrate of emotional processes. We have already cited above the conclusions that Dana reaches from his observations of retention of emotional experiences in the absence of bodily manifestations of emotions in patients. Let us recall that the basic point of these conclusions is the idea of the central localization of emotions flowing from activity and interaction of the cortex and the thalamus opticus. This theory, which Dana developed independently of Cannon, and which, as we see, was developed with convincing consistency and with remarkable coincidence in separate details simultaneously by certain investigators, again reminds us, like the coincidence of the James-Lange theory, of Goethe's thought about ideas coming to maturity at certain times like fruit falling simultaneously in different orchards. Obviously, the thalamic theory of emotions is actually such a simultaneous maturing of ideas of our time. It reached the greatest degree of maturity and development in the direction that interests us in the works of Cannon, who attempted not only to develop it into a systematic psychoneurological teaching on emotions, but also was able to compare it to the

108

The Teaching about Emotions

old James-Lange theory with all consistency and astuteness as the only sufficient explanation for a great number of most various facts known to us from the area of normal and pathological affective life. For this reason, we shall rely in the future on the works of Cannon in presenting this theory in the review of major evidence usually brought to its defense. We shall begin with an explanation of the radical divergence between the old theory and the new. The plan cited,54 which we are borrowing from Cannon, presents schematically and with the greatest simplification the nerve mechanisms that are the basis of emotions as they are assumed to be by the organic and the thalamic theories of emotional reactions. As is apparent from the plan, according to the James-Lange theory, any object stimulates the receptor organs and afferent impulses are directed to the cortex, which results in the perception of an object; in the cortex, centrifugal excitations arise directed to the muscles and internal organs which elicit in them complex and various changes. Afferent impulses from the internal organs and muscles return again to the cortex and, owing to this, the object simply perceived is converted into an emotionally experienced object: sensing of bodily changes as they occur is emotion-the aggregate of sensed associative and motor elements accounts for everything. According to the thalamic theory as it is presented in the plan, the neurological mechanism of emotional reactions differs from what has just been considered in two basic points. First, in the mechanism, paths three and four, which are presented in the first plan, are absent, that is, paths that carry afferent impulses from the skeletal musculature and internal organs back to the cortex-impulses that according to the old theory are the only source of emotional experience. These paths are eliminated in the second plan not because they do not exist, but because, in the opinion of the new theory, their significance for the study of emotions is more debatable. Obviously, the new theory seeks the main source of emotional experience in another place, and this is the second point of its divergence from the first plan; according to the new theory, sensory excitations proceeding from the periphery to the brain are stopped in the area of the thalamus opticus. The thalamus opticus is considered as a coordinating center of emotional reactions and has rich connections with the cortex and with the periphery. Processes arising in it are the sources of affective experience. The whole mechanism of origin and course of an emotion is presented by Cannon as follows. The external situation stimulates the perceiving organs, which transmit the developing excitation through impulses directed toward the cortex. The impulses in the cortex are associated with conditioned reflex processes which determine the direction of the reaction. Either because the reaction arises in the form of a certain structure and, as a result of this, cortical neurons excite the thalamic processes, or because impulses from the receptors on their centrifugal path themselves excite the thalamic processes, the latter are activated and ready to discharge. The fact that thalamic neurons act in a certain combination with a given emotional expression is evident from the stereo typic quality of the reaction in different affective states. These neurons do not require detailed innervation from higher centers in order to be activated. The first condition for their functioning is release, after which they produce a discharge rapidly and intensively. Neurons within and in close proximity to the thalamus opticus participating in emotional expression are located close to the break of sensory paths from the periphery to the cortex. We must assume that when there is a discharge of these neurons in a certain combination, they not only innervate muscles and internal organs, but also excite afferent paths going to the cortex either through direct connection or through irradiation. According to the

The Teaching about Emotions

109

theory, which of itself follows naturally, the specific quality of emotions is joined to simple sensation if thalamic processes are activated. We shall consider first of all the main and actual principles of the new theory. We must put in the first place the fact that when the whole frontal brain up to the thalamus opticus is removed in lower animals, their behavior, usually described as fury, becomes uninhibited; when the thalamus opticus is removed, however, the reaction disappears. In 1887, V. M. Bekhterev expressed the idea that emotional expression does not depend on the cortex of the brain because sometimes this expression cannot be voluntarily suppressed (laughing because of tickling, screaming from pain), because visceral changes always are a part of this reaction, being independent of cortical control, and, finally, because this reaction appears immediately after birth when the participation of the cortex in organization of behavior is still insignificant. Further, Bekhterev published results of his experiments with removal of cerebral hemispheres in various animals in which, even after the operation, appropriate stimuli continued to elicit reactions of an affective nature. These reactions disappeared only after removal of the thalamus opticus. From this, Bekhterev reached the conclusion that the thalamus opticus plays a preponderant role in emotional manifestations. Despite the attempts of R. Woodworth 55 (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 115) and of Sherrington to shake the significance of Bekhterev's position, pointing to the fact that in their experiments, physiological manifestations of strong excitation and so-called pseudoaffective reactions were retained in operated cats with completely removed thalamus, his position was confirmed in a series of new studies, and evidently must be considered as one of the most reliable and solid positions of contemporary teaching on localization of mental functions. The studies of Cannon and Britton, and later the study of Bard, wholly confirm Bekhterev's position and gave the authors a reason for concluding that the thalamus opticus is the area that reacts with impulses that elicit an extreme degree of emotional activity, visceral and muscular, when cortical control is eliminated. The difference between the affective reaction and the pseudo affective in animals in Sherrington's experiments is primarily that in the latter, the animals displayed very narrow limits of coordination of behavior. In the reactions, they never approached real acts of attack or flight, but when the thalamus opticus was retained, the affective reaction of the external part was fully retained. Similar phenomena were described repeatedly in clinical studies. In some forms of hemiplegia, the patients were not capable of voluntary movements of the facial muscles on the paralyzed side, but when these patients were overcome by a sorrowful or joyful affect, the muscles that were not subject to voluntary control began to act and to cause an expression of sorrow or joy to appear on both sides of the face. In these cases, the motor paths were broken in the subcortical area, but the thalamus opticus remained undamaged. Opposite phenomena were observed in unilateral damage to the thalamus opticus. For example, as a result of a unilateral tumor of the thalamus opticus, under appropriate conditions, one-sided laughter or a one-sided grimace of pain can be observed in the patients, regardless of cortical control of these muscles being bilateral. A patient described by S. I. Kiriltsev56 (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 117) could voluntarily control movements of both sides of his face symmetrically. But when he laughed or made a grimace of pain, the right side of his face remained immobile. Autopsy disclosed a tumor in the left half of the thalamus opticus. Such localization of the central nervous apparatus apparent in expression of satisfaction or suffering is connected with emotional manifestations usually observed in pseudobulbar paralysis. In these cases, there is usually bilateral paralysis of the

110

The Teaching about Emotions

facial muscles. Facial muscles that cannot be contracted voluntarily function normally, however, in laughter or screams, in frowning or raising the eyebrows. Emotional manifestations occur as if accidentally, without control and are of long duration. This was the description of a patient who started to laugh at ten o'clock in the morning and continued with small pauses till two in the afternoon. R Tilney57 and D. Morrison report on 173 cases of this disease (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 117). Among them, the investigators found such paroxysms of crying and laughing in 17, crying alone in 16, and laughter alone in 15%. These paroxysms occurred without any evident appropriate cause. The patients had the appearance of people shaking with laughter, but were experiencing nothing that would correspond to these bodily manifestations. S. Wilson described a number of similar cases that allowed him to establish the following: the more serious voluntary paralysis of the facial and motor musculature was, the more intensive was the involuntary innervation of the same mechanism (S. Wilson, 1924). Brisseau ascribes these disturbances to damage to a special part of the corticothalamic paths, as a result of which the thalamus opticus is freed from cortical control. Brisseau assumes that for the manifestation of spasmodic involuntary laughter and crying, the thalamus opticus itself must be preserved. Wilson contradicts Brisseau, indicating that the phenomena described may also occur when the thalamus opticus itself is involved in the disease process. We shall return later to interpreting these cases. Now let us recall a number of cases of Fulton and Bayley, who observed complete emotional negativism in patients suffering from a pathological process of the central part of the thalamus opticus. Thus, some of the patients who had lost all expression of emotion did display an irrational calmness of mind with complete absence of evaluating the seriousness of their physical state. In a case of narcolepsy with damage to the area of the third ventricle, expression and feeling of emotion can also be almost completely absent. Such patients take ridicule and insults with complete indifference and display no emotional manifestations in the most tragic occurrences. In some cases, tumors were found in these patients in the lower part of the thalamus opticus, frequently affecting the whole thalamus opticus. Finally, the third indicator supporting the basic position of the new theory is the fact of release of inhibition of the involuntary system and frequently prolonged reactions of crying or laughing with a temporary suppression of cortical control of the lower centers by anesthesia or with disturbance of this control by some kind of disease process. The latter indication, as Cannon notes, may be a significant argument in favor of thalamic localization of emotional manifestations if we only consider it in connection with the first two considerations given above. Pharmacological experiments with anesthesia of the brain cortex in which control of the higher centers is eliminated showed that the play of emotional reactions in these cases is expressed extremely sharply. The described experimental, clinical, and pharmacological data concordantly lead, first, to admitting the localization of emotional manifestation in the area of the thalamus opticus and, second, to the hypothesis that tries to explain all of these phenomena on the basis of the representation of the organization of cerebral activity that J. Jackson58 developed in his time. According to Jackson, the organization of the nervous system is a complex hierarchy of higher and lower centers; in it, primitive, archaic reactions of the old parts of the brain, which could always have disrupted more differentiated and finer forms of activity of the higher centers, are subject to an inhibiting influence of the higher centers, and because of this, cannot freely exhibit activity and playa dominant role in behavior in normal cases. When, under one set of conditions or another, cortical control over lower centers is weak-

The Teaching about Emotions

111

ened or totally eliminated, the lower centers-formerly subject to hierarchical control-become independent and free acting, and this results in the manifestation of their involuntary and extremely intensive activity. The weakest stimuli can elicit extremely excessive reactions under these conditions. According to the new theory, emotional manifestations are a product of the activity of lower subcortical centers organized according to Jackson's representation. In the opinion of Head, who developed Jackson's teaching, all involuntary emotional manifestations described above must be considered as phenomena of release of inhibition of the lower centers as a result of the weakening or elimination of cortical control. According to this interpretation, there is extreme intensity and easy excitability in animals and people with disturbed cortical control over lower centers. The unusual intensity of reactions indicates that the nervous apparatus that guides emotional manifestations is always ready for energetic discharge and only higher control inhibits a display of its activity. Perhaps only the presentations of Wilson speak against this hypothesis; he, in contrast to Brisseau, assumes, as we have seen above, that involuntary paroxysms of laughter and tears may arise not only as a result of a break in the corticothalamic paths with preservation of the thalamus opticus, but also with significant disruption of the thalamus itself. However, these objections are refuted convincingly, it seems to us, by Bard, who indicates that when the base of the thalamic area is involved in a disease process, then in the substantial part connected with the reaction of anger, we usually observe an absence of emotional manifestations. Recalling these facts, Wilson interprets them as the result of the break in the cortical path, but the persuasiveness of his arguments is weakened by the fact that not one case has been observed of emotional paralysis as a result of cortical damage. On the contrary, damage that separates the cortex from lower centers usually elicits extraordinary activity of emotional behavior. Thus, facts speak rather for the subcortical localization of emotional manifestations. Also in complete agreement with this idea are the studies of Head and Holmes cited above (in: W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 118), which show that unilateral damage to the thalamus results in a tendency toward excessive affective reaction to ordinary stimuli. The authors explain this phenomenon by the fact that the thalamus opticus is freed from cortical control. Their conclusion states that the activity of the thalamus is the physiological substrate of the affective aspect of sensation. Summarizing the factual bases considered in this chapter on which the thalamic theory of emotions is based and adding to them the representations and facts given in previous chapters, we cannot but agree with Cannon that this theory, alternative to the James-Lange theory, is in agreement with all the facts known to us at this time.

9

If it is true that the strength of the provability of any argument is comprehended only in comparison with the strength of counterclaims, then the new theory can be considered to be triumphantly confirmed scientific truth since it is not opposed by any serious factual objections. Recently E. Newman, R Perkins, and S. Wilson tried to present a systematic code of critical objections to the new theory and to mobilize everything that might serve to defend the organic theory. It is enough to look at this last wave of evidence for the paradoxical James-Lange thesis to see the total hopelessness of the position of the old theory. The evidence turns

112

The Teaching about Emotions

on the charmed circle that was delineated by the founders of the theory themselves; the motifs vary and repeat, but the authors have no direct or indirect data capable of corroborating the shaky structure of the organic theory. However, even in this collision of opinions, individual sparks of truth are engendered which no one can by-pass who wishes to weigh objectively the actual right of the new hypothesis to exist and be recognized. Perhaps the first and central objection from this point of view against the new theory consists in indicating its Achilles heel, its really weak position-specifically, the absence of any psychological analysis of emotions as such. The reader probably already noted the contradiction in the factual basis of the new theory in the course of our previous exposition. Actually, one cannot help but see that the new investigators use emotional manifestations as evidence for the presence or maintenance of emotion and at the same time, as a result of their work, arrive at a complete rejection of visceral and motor factors as sources of emotion. One asks: what then is this illusory thing, emotion? This objection retains its force, in any case, for all experiments with animals, which were cited above. The response to this objection actually brings us to elucidating the essential point of the new theory on the one hand, and to a more firm consolidation of its factual basis on the other. The new theory wholly accepts the definition of emotion, given by James, as some kind of sensual tone added to simple perception. The argument pertains only to the source of emotion. The old theory sees it in a sensation of bodily manifestations; the new theory assumes that the specific quality is added to perception as a result of activity of the thalamus opticus. Here, however, a branching within the new theory itself occurs. While some, following Head, Kiippers, and others, ascribe to the thalamus opticus the functions of consciousness of emotions and consider it as a center of consciousness, others, following Cannon, introduce a substantial supplement into this point of the theory. W. Cannon does not maintain that consciousness of emotion is immediately and directly connected with the activity of the thalamus opticus. On the contrary, stressing that anesthesia that results in complete elimination of emotional consciousness does not harm emotional manifestation, which is thalamic in origin, Cannon raises an objection against the localization of the center of emotional consciousness in the subcortical area. As he indicates, emotional reaction arising and being organized in the thalamus opticus is directed along paths of its discharge not only toward the periphery, facilitating emotional manifestations, but also toward the cortex, in which the feeling originates that unites to sensation, as is apparent in unilateral thalamic damage. In this variant, the new theory does not maintain that the thalamus opticus is the center of affective experiences, but maintains only that the thalamus opticus must be considered as the source of experiences of this kind similarly to how changes in the retina are the source of visual sensations. Thus, the new theory differs from the old not in that the old assumed a cortical localization while the new proposes a subcortical localization for affective experiences. The indicated difference may pertain only to the extreme partisans, mentioned above, of the thalamic theory. In the variant of the new theory developed by Cannon, Bard, and others, both theories agree in this point specifically. In both theories, emotional processes are enlisted as a physiological substrate of emotional consciousness, but their specific cause and specific source that could elucidate for us how these cortical processes differ from other cortical processes and act as the substrate for intellectual operations are localized differently in the two theories. One sees this source in peripheral changes, the other, in central processes. James' thesis, which states that in the brain, there are no special centers for emotions, must be modified in light of new data. The cortex on one side with the

The Teaching about Emotions

113

reflex arc and peripheral organs on the other end as a source of afferent impulses are a somewhat simplified organization that does not meet the actual complexity of emotional reactions. The thalamus is located between the cortex and the periphery; it is an integrating organ for emotional processes in which a stereotypic reaction of emotional manifestations arises on the one hand, and specific excitations directed toward the cortex on the other. Thus, in the new theory, the interactions of the cortical and subcortical centers are considered to be the basis of emotion. The alternative proposed by James-either special centers of emotions exist or emotions arise in general motor and sensory centers of the cortex-is unfounded. In place of the old, "either-or," the new theory proposes the existence of both cortical processes and special centers of emotional reactions. Only the one and the other together are capable of adequately explaining the variety of emotional processes. Dana defends substantially the same point of view. Bard notes that this theory can explain the circumstance that, under normal conditions, emotions always are accompanied by normal bodily manifestations (which also served as the basis for the development of the peripheral theory of emotions) as well as the fact that emotional manifestations and emotional experiences can also exist in special experimental and pathological conditions separately, independent of each other. The assumption at the base of the new theory that emotion is a process central in origin explains a third series of facts very well also-specifically, the disappearance of both the bodily manifestation of emotion and affective experience when the whole thalamus opticus is involved in a disease process, as happens in Fulton and Bayley's cases which were mentioned above. In the polemics of the partisans of the old and the new theory, this question arose in the form of the problem of the interrelation between emotional behavior and emotional experience, that is, between subjective and objective aspects of emotion. According to the James-Lange theory, both aspects are always inseparable: there can be no emotional behavior without emotional experience, just as there can be no emotional experience without peripheral changes. Finally, the new theory also explains a fourth series of facts-namely, that the presence of bodily manifestations, sometimes even artificially induced, under certain circumstances can facilitate the development or augmentation of emotion itself. Stated more briefly, the new theory, explaining quite convincingly both the presence of a connection as well as the possibility of a separate existence of peripheral and central factors of emotions, actually deals with the task of an unambiguous and logically consistent interpretation of all the wealth of facts known to us, and gives, first of all, a convincing elucidation of the fact that bodily manifestations and emotional expression frequently aid us, under normal conditions, to make a judgment on the presence of corresponding emotional experience as well. We shall not stop to consider the collision of opposite opinions caused by each point of criticism of the old and basis of the new theory. We touched on them to some extent in the course of our discussion and, to some extent, ignored them since they can scarcely play any kind of significant role in the definitive acceptance of the one theory or the other. We will only point out that all the objections pertain to secondary arguments like the position of Cannon on the extremely small sensitivity of internal organs, the result of the small number of afferent fibers in the autonomic nervous system. In the extremely low sensitivity of internal organs (sensory fibers in them are 1/10 those of motor fibers), Cannon saw more than enough evidence against considering changes that occur in these organs as a source of emotional experience. His opponents indicate the sensitivity of the thorax, the throat, and in vessels of the pancreas area. As Cannon correctly notes, what is concerned here is not the visceral organs in the true sense of the word, but sensations that

114

The Teaching about Emotions

arise outside these organs in the areas supplied by numerous sensory nerves that act only as a result of visceral changes. If we leave aside secondary objections, the polemics will be left with attempts to save, one way or another, the old theory, introducing into it one set of correctives or another corresponding to new data. Above we considered one such attempt to reject visceral sensations as the significant factor in emotions and to transfer the whole central weight of the old theory to the motor or kinetic sensations. Another attempt consists of making the two theories identical since authors are inclined to localize in the thalamus opticus the center of the motor and organic reactions which James indicated as the true and only source of emotions. But even this attempt, as Cannon explains, is essentially unfounded since the authors do not see the principal difference between peripheral and central theories of emotions-differences that contain the whole essence of the argument. We shall consider only three points that partisans of the new theory propose as its essential merits. For us, these points may be of primary interest both from the point of view of evaluating the new theory and from the point of view that we especially considered in this study. The first pertains to explaining the so-called higher, or finer, emotions. Both the old theory and the new have as their object of study the unrefined emotions directly connected with instincts, to a large degree common to animals and man, that evidently arose at very early stages of development-in a word, the lower emotions. With respect to higher emotions specific to man, James notes that bodily manifestations and intensity of sensations connected with them may be weak in these. True, James had to admit that such calm emotions, passing with no bodily excitation, may be ascertained in man. Thus, James does not deny that there may be fine pleasures, in other words, that there may be emotions due exclusively to excitation of centers wholly independent of centripetal currents. To such sensations, together with esthetic emotions, he refers the feeling of moral satisfaction, gratefulness, and satisfaction following the solving of a problem. James, however, tries immediately to take back his admissions that contradict his whole theory and to save it by indicating that, together with these central emotions, works of art may elicit extremely strong emotions in which experience harmonizes completely with the theoretical positions he presents. In esthetic perceptions (musical, for example) centripetal currents play the main role, regardless of whether or not internal organic excitations arise together with them. The esthetic excitation itself is the object of sensation and to the extent that the esthetic perception is the object of direct, total, vividly experienced sensation, to that extent esthetic pleasure is totally and clearly connected with it. Even more openly, James tries to take revenge for the momentary forced admission of the existence of purely central emotions with respect to the other feelings named above. He admits that they may be of a purely central origin. "But the weakness and paleness of these feelings when they are not connected with bodily excitations presents a very sharp contrast to the deeper emotions. In all persons gifted with sensitivity and impressionability, fine emotions always are connected with bodily excitation: moral truth is reflected in the sound of the voice or expression of the eyes and so forth ... " (1902, p. 317). If there is no bodily excitation, then according to James, there is simply intellectual perception of phenomena, which should be classified as a cognitive rather than emotional, mental process (ibid.). It is sufficient to cite this reasoning of James on higher emotions to make obvious the internal contradiction into which he falls in explaining them. On the one hand, he admits them as emotions that differ essentially from lower emotions, as emotions arising through a purely central, but not a centripetal path, as emotions

The Teaching about Emotions

115

not accompanied by bodily excitation, and in this way, he admits that the theory he developed cannot serve as an adequate explanation for higher emotions, but covers only the area of unrefined or lower emotions not specific to the human mental capacity for feelings. On the other hand, he denies them, classifying them as an intellectual and not an emotional state of consciousness and assuming that they become emotions only when they exhibit the required traits of unrefined emotions, that is, bodily excitation and peripheral origin; consequently, James also extends to them his basic theory, refusing to see the essential difference between the lower and the higher emotions. Thus, two mutually exclusive paths opened before James: either open dualism in the interpretation of the nature of higher and lower emotions or complete identity of the one type and the other. As is apparent, James always vacillated as to which of the two paths to take. In later formulations of his theory he admitted its inadequacies and introduced into it substantial changes. These pertained to two main points which are emphasized with particular insistence by the Russian investigator N. N. Lange. 59 First, in the new formulation, James admits "that a feeling of satisfaction or suffering itself precedes its bodily manifestations and elicits them and is not their result, although in their turn these bodily manifestations have a reciprocal effect and give emotion brilliance and intensity" (N. N. Lange, 1914, p. 280). The second change pertains to the nature of bodily manifestations of emotions. If formerly James considered them as a combination of simple reflexes, then in the new formulation, he is inclined to see in them more complex forms of centripetal reactions. They arise not directly from a specific character of external stimulation acting on innate nervous mechanisms, but always assume in the individual a consciousness of that particular significance or sense that he contributes to this external impression. The emotional reaction depends on how the individual understands the external impression and whether it is for him an object of fear or anger. "These two corrections introduced by James himself in his new formulation signify an essentially complete rejection of the narrow radicalism of his former theory" (ibid.). We can now consider James' vacillation in the definitive formulation of his theory exclusively as evidence of the internal limitations and inconsistency of the classical formulation of his hypothesis and its inadequacy for explaining higher emotions. As Cannon correctly notes, the problem of higher emotions, which presents insurmountable difficulties for James' theory, can find a satisfactory physiological explanation if it admits the thalamic hypothesis. Let us recall that in patients described by Head, emotions that arise from memory or imagination were experienced more intensely on the affected side on which the thalamus was freed from motor control of the cortex. This shows that cortical processes can elicit activity of the thalamus, which in turn reciprocally sends affective impulses to the cortex (W B. Cannon, 1927, p. 121). From this fact, Cannon reaches conclusions relative to the problem of higher emotions as it appears in the light of the new theory. He maintains that every object or situation can in this way add an affective color to any experience. Thus, we can understand the whole exceptional complexity, wealth, and variety of our emotional life. But apart from this advantage that the new theory has for explaining higher emotions, which presented a critical point for the old theory where it was forced either to change or, because of whatever pressures, to reduce lower and higher emotions to one denominator, the new theory proposes still another position which opens the possibility of a more adequate explanation for a number of phenomena of primary importance in the area of emotional life. This position pertains to the complex interrelation that was established between the cortex and the subcortical centers in the formation of emotional processes.

116

The Teaching about Emotions

From the point of view of the old theory, emotional discharge occurred automatically, by reflex, just as emotion arises automatically and by reflex. An affective storm is played out between two poles: arising in the brain, it shakes the body with agitation in order to agitate the brain with a reciprocal flow. The most ordinary phenomena of emotional life, well known from everyday experience, are not included in any way in this simple scheme. As an example, we will cite only two such phenomena. McDougall called attention to the first, which reproaches the JamesLange theory for putting the sensory aspect of emotions at the center. It does not pay attention to the constantly present and sometimes dominating impulsive character of emotional experience. The reproach is entirely valid. Considering emotion as consciousness of organic and peripheral changes, the James-Lange theory thus reduces feeling to sensation and, as a result of this, reaches a conclusion that is exactly opposite to the one it strives toward: the basic goal of its striving is to overcome intellectualism in the teaching on affects and to find the specific trait that distinguishes an emotional state from a purely cognitive, intellectual state of consciousness. But as a result of logical development of the original thesis, the theory comes to a complete dissolving of emotional states in a general aggregate of sensory processes of sensation and perception. In order to save the situation, it assumes that the object itself of these sensations is specifically distinct compared with the object of all other sensations. But different objects still do not make sensations themselves different in their psychological nature and for this reason, the old theory was persuaded to consider emotion essentially as a passive process, sensory in psychological nature, as sensation of a special kind, and to not pay attention to all the factors in the emotional process that closely involve in it striving, excitation to action, and impulse, which make our emotions stronger and more influential motives of behavior. W. B. Cannon (1927, p. 123) assumes that the new theory avoids this difficulty very easily. The localization of the standard reaction of emotional manifestations in the area of the thalamus opticus-in the area which, like the brain stem, acts directly through simple automatism if it is not inhibited by higher centers-explains not only the sensory aspect of emotional experience, but also the dynamic aspect, the tendency of the thalamic neurons to discharge. The presence of powerful impulses arising in the area of the brain and not connected with cognitive consciousness and, owing to this, exciting strong emotion blindly and autonomically, explains that this kind of emotion does not consist of sensation. Experiencing emotion, we find ourselves as if in the power of some outside force that compels us to act without weighing the consequences. Cannon derives this explanation from the teaching on double control that makes up a significant part of the new theory. From the same root, he also derives an explanation for the second phenomenon which was not understandable from the point of view of the James theory: the appearance of conflict or struggle between conscious intention and emotional tendency, or, to put it more simply, the interrelation between voluntary functions and emotions. And actually, just like the problem of the impulsive nature of emotions, this problem was an insurmountable obstacle for the old theory. The absolutely unique psychological relations that exist between consciously acting will, which is apparent in decision and intention, and affect, which controls our consciousness, which, as we shall see later, is the true psychological and philosophical center of the whole teaching on the passions, not only left the problem unexplained from the point of view of the old theory, but simply did not note it and treated it with silence. Regardless of the concealment, no one had any doubt that these phenomena can by no means be put into an extremely simplified pattern of the organic hy-

The Teaching about Emotions

117

pothesis and be understood with the help of the reflex mechanism that was proposed as an all-explaining principle of all of emotional life in all its variety and wealth of manifestation. According to the James-Lange theory, important processes that are the basis of emotions were generally considered to be beyond the limits of the brain-that principal organ of thought and conscious will-and placed at the periphery, and the brain itself was turned into a passive receiver of peripheral changes in which all other basic brain processes not only could not change anything, but in which they did not participate at all actively. Vital processes occurring every day in the consciousness of every person, processes of interaction between consciousness as a whole and its emotional part, were boldly crossed out, considered to be nonexistent. Specifically because it reduced emotions to peripheral processes reflected in the brain, the peripheral theory created a chasm between emotions and the rest of consciousness: emotions were removed to the periphery, the rest of consciousness was concentrated in the brain. The new theory, which establishes an extremely complex interaction of subcortical and cortical centers in the processes of emotion, comes significantly closer to the possibility of explaining the whole complexity of the real relations of affect and consciousness that comprise an indisputable psychological fact. It assumes an anatomic and dynamic organization of emotional processes in which the lower centers, being the true source of emotional excitations proceeding to the cortex and emotional discharges proceeding to the periphery, are themselves in a complex dependence on higher centers, forming their subordinate and controlled stage that acts under their guidance as an independent, but connected force. Only when the higher centers are weak or are removed from the stage subordinate to them does the latter become independent and begin to act in the autonomic fashion proper to it. In this case, the general neurobiological law formulated by E. Kretschmer6° with respect to hysteria is manifested in the following form: if within the psychomotor sphere, the action of the higher stage becomes functionally weak, then the next lower stage with its own primitive laws becomes independent. This complex hierarchical organization of the anatomic and physiological substrate of affect actually, as we shall see, can easily be brought into agreement at least with the basic psychological facts that are central, as we have indicated, to the whole teaching on passions. Now we shall consider only one point characterizing this organization, specifically, the teaching on double control. As is known, James himself tried to consider and refute two possible objections. The first consists of the fact that, "in the words of many actors, who splendidly reproduce, with their voices, facial mimicry, and body movements, external manifestations of emotion, they do not exactz experience any emotions. Other actors, according to the testimony of W. Archer, 1 maintain that in cases where they were able to play a role very well, they experienced all the emotions of the role" (w. James, 1902, p. 315). Here James touches on a well-known problem with a long history, the problem of specific reproduction of emotions to which we will return in the course of our study. In explaining James, we are now only interested in his admission that "in the expression of every emotion, internal organic excitation may be completely suppressed in some persons as well as the emotion itself to a significant degree, while others do not have this ability" (ibid., p. 315). Thus, James admits in his own words "that some persons can completely dissociate emotions from their expression" (ibid.). Another objection is as if the opposite to that which was just presented. It consists of the fact that "sometimes, inhibiting manifestations of emotion, we reinforce it. Acutely painful is the state of the spirit that you experience when circum-

118

The Teaching about Emotions

stances compel you to keep from laughing; anger suppressed by fear turns into stronger hatred. Conversely, free manifestation of emotion provides relief" (ibid.). James admits the possibility of increasing internal excitation "in cases where expression in facial mimicry is suppressed, or with the possibility of conversion of an emotion, when its manifestation is voluntarily inhibited, into a completely different emotion which is, perhaps, accompanied by different and stronger organic excitation" (ibid.). In the opinion of James, conversion of an emotion that is the result of a combination of the object eliciting it with an inhibition of its influence occurs through purely physiological means: excitation that cannot flow out through normal channels begins to be carried out through other channels; as a result of this, a new organic state and a new emotion corresponding to it arise. "If I had the desire to kill my enemy, but did not dare to do this, my emotion would be completely different compared with that which would seize me if I were to carry out my desire" (ibid., p. 316). We cannot but agree with Cannon that James gives a contradictory, ambiguous, and altogether unsatisfactory response to the possible objections. On the one hand, he denies emotions altogether. "Refuse to express passion and it dies. Count to ten before you show your anger and the reason for it will seem laughable to you." On the other hand, he believes that organic excitation with its voluntary suppression cannot be destroyed and must prepare a new path for itself, resulting in the conversion of one emotion into another. The new theory proposes the presence of double control-cortical and thalamic-over bodily processes. Such control leads to a very complex relation between both controlling points. It is clear that skeletal muscles control two points-the cortical and the thalamic. For example, we can laugh spontaneously depending on a funny situation (thalamic laughter), but we can laugh as a result of a voluntary act (cortical laughter). It is very clear that internal organs are under only thalamic control. We cannot by direct act of the will elicit an increase of sugar in the blood, an increase in heart rate, or cessation of digestion. With double control, cortical neurons under normal conditions evidently dominate and may keep from action excited neurons of the thalamus opticus (although we sometimes laugh or cry against our wishes). Because of this, a conflict is possible between the higher and lower control of bodily functions. But the cortex can inhibit only those bodily functions that are under voluntary control under normal conditions; just as the cortex cannot elicit, neither can it stop such intense processes as increasing the content of sugar in the blood, increasing heart rate, or cessation of digestion, characteristic for great excitation. When emotion is suppressed, it is, consequently, suppressed only in external manifestations. There are facts that lead us to think that with maximal manifestations, there is also maximal internal excitation. For this reason, it is probable that cortical suppression of external manifestation of excitation results in a weakening of internal disturbances which would be stronger if they accompanied free expression of emotions. Nevertheless, with conflict between cortical control and activity of thalamic centers that are not subordinate to the cortex, internal manifestations of emotions may attain significant force. True, as far as functions not subject to the cortex are concerned, the situation is more complex than may be apparent on the basis of the representations given here. As Cannon notes in another place, if the cortex does not have direct control over internal organs and cannot direct their functions, it can exert an indirect control over them. For example, we can go out to meet danger and elicit a tremor in ourselves, although we cannot elicit a tremor by simple willful decision. In a similar way, we can frequently avoid circumstances

The Teaching about Emotions

119

that elicit fear, anger, or repulsion and the visceral disturbances that accompany them. To do this, we need only avoid going near the point that agitates us. We developed the teaching on double control in order to show to what extent the new theory admits more complex conditions of interaction between affective and conscious voluntary processes in comparison with the old. In the application of the advantage of the thalamic theory of emotions over the visceral to the last criterion which interests us, this teaching has the deciding voice. What presented insurmountable difficulties for the visceral theory can be explained from the point of view of double control. Cannon says: "If there is double control over behavior, then it becomes easy to explain how internal conflict with its sharp emotional accompaniment and the subsequent partial weakening of feeling in a situation in which we experience intensive fright simultaneously with a very strong feeling of helplessness before some act of external behavior occurs and when corresponding behavior scarcely begins to be manifested, internal agitation begins to decline and bodily forces are directed energetically and effectively to achieving a positive result. Normal thalamic processes are established in the nervous organization itself. They are similar to reflexes in the sense of constant readiness for excitation of motor reactions, and when they can act, they do so with great force. However, they are subject to control by the cortical processes, processes that result from preceding impressions of every kind. Thus, the cortex can control all peripheral organs with the exception of the internal organs" (Cannon, 1927, p. 123). The inhibited processes in the thalamus opticus cannot activate the organism except for the parts that are not under voluntary control, but excitation of thalamic centers themselves may be elicited by emotions in the usual manner and, possibly, with great force specifically owing to inhibition. When cortical inhibition is removed, conflict immediately seems to be resolved. The two controlling factors that were formerly in opposition now begin to cooperate. The thalamic neurons, continuing to be activated energetically, create conditions necessary for continuation of emotion, as James requires, even as it is manifested. Thus, the new theory not only overcomes the difficulties that confronted the James-Lange theory, but also explains satisfactorily the fact of sharp emotional experience during inaction seemingly induced by paralysis. We have concluded the tedious and long path of investigating the theoretical controversy that during the last half century stood in the center of psychological teaching on affects and, to a significant degree, determined the whole development of scientific thought and scientific knowledge in this area. Conclusions that we reach as a result of the investigation are lucid and clear. There is no ambiguity in them. We have seen that the old peripheral theory of affects not only cannot withstand the crushing pressure of critical investigations that have inflicted fatal blows from all sides, but has fallen long ago. If we collect all the arguments brought forth against this theory over the course of half a century, then, united in the force of their persuasiveness and conclusiveness, they actually make defending the James-Lange theory with complex ceremonies an unnecessary and ridiculous enterprise, as Bentley ingeniously noted. To fight with it is to fight with the dead. And we would never think to undertake an investigation if its only result would be to certify the historical death of this paradoxical and brilliant teaching. We see the justification for our tedious investigations in something else. Studying and testing the old and dying teaching point by point, we could trace, :,tep by step, both the birth of the new theory and that which was viable in its :>redecessor, and adequately explain the enormous wealth of new facts accumulated

120

The Teaching about Emotions

by the tireless half-century exertions of thought. In itself, criticism of any obsolete teaching, no matter how productive the criticism might have been, can never be taken to mean the conclusion of a whole historical epoch in the development of scientific thought. Only when sprouts of new growth appear on cuttings of the old does one epoch in the history of scientific thought end and another begin. Finding a historical boundary that divides two epochs in the teaching on the passions was the direct and immediate purpose of our investigation. But together with this, without noticing it, we came to still another conclusion that is clearly contrary to our expectations. We undertook the investigation of the James-Lange theory exclusively because the accepted view was to see in it a vital scientific realization of Spinoza's ideas. If it is true that the teaching of Spinoza on passions is inseparably connected with the names of James and Lange and with their renowned paradoxical theory on emotions, then since that teaching remains a vital part of contemporary scientific psychology, it must share the fate of the ideas that dominated more than a half century and are dying before our eyes. The position, with which we did not want to agree, which maintains that the part of Ethics that deals with passions may perhaps be only of historical interest for the psychologist of our time, is proving to be correct.

10

But should there be some doubt perhaps about the position itself on the internal spiritual similarity that exists between the great philosophical teaching on passions and the psychophysiological paradox that represents a half century of scientific thought on the nature of human emotions? Perhaps they are connected not with a mark of similarity, but with a mark of oppositeness? Perhaps they are united not so much by historical succession as by the necessary and inevitable wave-like alternations of thesis and antithesis in the history of thought? Then perhaps it may develop that relegating the celebrated hypothesis to the past not only does not mean the same thing for the fate of the Spinoza teaching, but, on the contrary, opens a way for its future development in the sphere of psychological science. We are investigating whether this is so. If an attentive study is made of its genesis and its philosophical nature, the James-Lange theory actually is not at all connected with Spinoza's teaching on the passions, but with ideas of Descartes and Malebranche. The opinion that the roots of the James-Lange theory date back to Ethics is based on a fallacy. It is actually nothing more than an opinion in the sense in which Spinozian gnosiology uses this word to name the first and lower kind of cognition because the latter is subject to error and has no place where we are convinced, but only where we speak of surmise or assumption. This error owes its origin, on the one hand, to the philosophical carelessness of Lange himself and in part to that of James; they were little concerned with the idea of the philosophical nature of the theory they created. Lange expressed a surmise, which was based on essential ignorance of the Spinoza teaching, that the famous Spinoza definition of affect should be considered as almost the only anticipation of his theory, in any case, more than others that approached his outlook. Everyone believed this surmise, it became rooted and acquired the character of scientific truth when it entered textbooks and became the property of school wisdom. On the other hand, this mistaken opinion may have been adopted by all as truth-without criticism, investigation, and confirmation-only because of the fact

The Teaching about Emotions

121

that partly in the history of philosophy, but mainly in the history of psychology, an error, extensive in kind, has been dominant thus far: the opinion that internal kinship and historical continuity exist between the teaching on passions of Descartes and Spinoza. While in the area of metaphysics, the oppositeness between the ideas of Descartes and Spinoza was well recognized, in the area of psychology, mainly in the area of the teaching on passions, certain external similarity and formal closeness of both teachings hide from the eyes of investigators even now that most important oppositeness, based on the very essence of both teachings, that in reality exists between these teachings. Of course, it is a fact that the worldview of Spinoza developed historically in direct dependence on the philosophy of Descartes. However, with respect to the general spirit of the Spinozian worldview, no one doubts that the two systems are connected since the confirmation and refutation of the thesis and the antithesis are connected. H. Heine 62 has said that great genius develops with the help of another great genius not so much by assimilation as by clashing. One diamond polishes another. Thus, the philosophy of Descartes did not to any extent at all engender the philosophy of Spinoza; more likely, it needed it in order to appear. In accordance with this, Heine finds that the method used by both student and teacher is the factor common to both thinkers. The content of worldview itself and its internal sense and the spirit that animates it are more opposite than similar. But as far as the teaching on passions is concerned, most investigators are inclined to see in Spinoza only a student developing and partially transforming the ideas of the teacher. Investigators are inclined to see simple evolution and reform where there was actually one of the greatest revolutions of the spirit and a catastrophic overturn of the former system of thought. K. Fischer63 develops this point of view most radically and consistently. He says: "There was a time when Spinoza was a Cartesian in the sense of a student thirsting for knowledge. We must add: from a certain point of view, Spinoza always remained a Cartesian and can never stop being one for us. The oppositeness between thought and space expressed in such precise form with complete certainty, as an object of most clear and most distinct knowledge forms the nucleus of Cartesian teaching.... Whoever maintains this oppositeness in this form is and will remain a Cartesian in one of the most essential traits of his worldview. Whoever rejects this opposition is not a Cartesian" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 2, p. 274). Moving to the definitive resolution of the problem of the origin and sources of Spinoza's teaching, Fischer again confronts the question as to whether Spinoza was ever a Cartesian. As an answer, the investigator proposes to distinguish the narrow and the broader formulation of the question. Otherwise, the question itself will remain indeterminate and shaky. That Spinoza was a Cartesian in the narrow sense of the word cannot be proven on the basis of literary documents, but it is most natural to assume that in his development, there was a stage when his point of departure did comprise his worldview. If, on the other hand, we take the Cartesian form of thought in the broader sense, the meaning and tendency of which we have already considered, then our answer would be: Spinoza not only was a Cartesian, but (in this sense) he never stopped being one. There can scarcely arise any doubt that the statement on the Cartesian form of thought of Spinoza pertains primarily to the teaching on passions because, for Fischer, the criterion for this qualification of Spinozian worldview consisted in the idea of oppositeness of thought and space, that is, in the idea of psychophysical parallelism. Where can this idea manifest itself more clearly and directly if not in Spinoza's psychological teaching, if not in his study of the nature of affects? If, in reality, in the teaching on the origin and nature of affects, in the teaching on human

122

The Teaching about Emotions

slavery or on the force of affects, in the teaching on the power of the mind (over affects), or on human freedom, Spinoza consistently developed the idea of psychophysical parallelism, then we cannot but agree with Fischer that Spinoza never stopped being a Cartesian. If, on the other hand, investigation brought us to the firm conclusion that in this teaching, Spinoza developed the antithesis to parallelism and, consequently to the dualism of Descartes, we would inevitably have to consider Fischer's opinion wrong. This is the basic nucleus of the whole problem of the present investigation. True, Fischer, evidently having in mind not so much the main content of the teaching on passions as its concrete expression, calls this teaching Spinoza's masterpiece and the most original part of his whole system. He says: "The teaching on human passions is Spinoza's masterpiece. We know to what extent Descartes in his work on passions laid the path for our philosopher and how much the latter depended on his predecessor in his first treatment of this subject, although even then he denied the teaching on freedom. In Ethics, one can still note traces of this copious, preliminary work, but the methodological basis of affects is so independent and unique that here the philosopher discloses his full originality" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 2, pp. 432-435). Even from this, it follows that Fischer recognizes Spinoza's originality only with respect to methodological basis of affects, obviously not extending this affirmation to the essence itself of his principal views. With respect to the main content of the teaching on passions, as distinct from the methodological basis of affects, Fischer evidently continues to maintain his general point of view, according to which Spinoza consistently develops the main thought of the teaching of Descartes and transforms his principles accordingly. Fischer understands the historical dependence of Spinoza on Descartes specifically in this evolutionistic and reformist spirit: "To the very reliable and precise biographical evidence which indicates that the work of Descartes enchanted Spinoza and illuminated his thought, internal principles are united that clearly and distinctly show how Spinozism arises from Cartesian teaching. For this, it would only be necessary to recognize the problems that Descartes posed for philosophy, to recognize the method for solving these problems and explaining the contradictions in which the system of the teacher was caught in solving them. These contradictions were not hidden, but were obvious, and the way to solve them was indicated by Descartes himself so clearly that it remained only to proceed along it without vacillation" (ibid., p. 276). Thus, from Fischer's point of view, even where there is a clear and irreconcilable disagreement between the teaching of Spinoza and that of Descartes, Spinoza nevertheless remains first and last a student of his teacher, a pure Cartesian, who resolves the contradiction in the same way that Descartes himself indicated. It would be difficult to express more clearly the idea that even denying Descartes, Spinoza continues to remain a Cartesian. Since we are concerned here not with a secondary, but with a central point of our investigation, we must try to elucidate with all precision the opinion, the denial of which we see as our main task, the opinion according to which Spinoza in his teaching on the passions is a consistent Cartesian. Elucidating this presents no great difficulty; we need only to turn to the history of Spinoza's teaching on affects. In this history, Fischer notes two periods. In the period of the Short Treatise . ..,64 Spinoza was directly dependent on Descartes. In Ethics, he independently developed a methodological basis of affects and displayed full originality in this way. Thus, the Short Treatise . .. is opposite Ethics as, respectively, Cartesian and original periods in the history of the development of Spinoza's teaching on passions. We will turn to these works.

The Teaching about Emotions

123

In the Short Treatise ... , as Fischer correctly notes, "in enumerating and defining passions, Spinoza follows Descartes completely; the work of Descartes on the passions evidently guided him. First of all, we find the same six primary passions65 that Descartes recognized as the basic forms of passions ... Then the same groups and types of individual passions follow in almost completely the same order as determined by Descartes" (ibid., p. 232). From this, Fischer reaches the conclusion that Spinoza, in developing the theme on passions, follows Descartes and depends on him. According to Fischer, "We might have been surprised that Spinoza does not mention his predecessor, from whom he had borrowed so much. However, we must take into account also to what extent Spinoza differs from Descartes in his evaluation of the passions. He does not explain them, as his predecessor does, by the union of the soul with the body, but considers them simply as mental phenomena that are due exclusively to the nature of our cognition. Spinoza denies freedom of the human will, which Descartes maintained and which he opposed to the passions so that, in his opinion, the passions can and must be subordinate to freedom and made its weapons. For this reason, a judgment on the usefulness and value of passions as a whole or in details must come out differently in Spinoza than in Descartes" (ibid., p. 234). We think that what we had in mind above when we spoke of the criterion that Fischer uses in qualifying Spinoza's teaching as Cartesian cannot be said more clearly than this was done in the quoted excerpt. Spinoza's originality is limited to the methodological basis of affects and a number of particular differences which, as a whole, give a different form to the whole teaching on affects even in the Short Ireatise .... The whole argument consists exactly of what is to be considered the principal content and what, the methodological basis of affects. We think-and our investigation is basically concerned with the evidence for this-that the matter is in a completely opposite form as compared to how it is envisioned by Fischer.
