We think that even with respect to the Short Ireatise ..., the fact that Spinoza follows Descartes in the primary and individual passions enumerated is more a matter of methodological basis of affects than the theoretical essence of his teaching, but the fact that Spinoza enters into an open contradiction with Descartes in denying freedom of will in the teaching on the influence and fate of passions, on their dynamics in the total life of consciousness, in the teaching on the relation of passions to cognition and will, and, finally, in considering their psychophysical nature is a problem specifically of the theoretical essence of the Spinoza teaching. We shall try in the future to show that regardless of the fact that the Short Treatise . .. does not yet contain the main elements of the teaching on passions as it is developed in Ethics, in the main content of the teaching, it is, nevertheless, already a true antithesis of the teaching of Descartes. But this flows, really and truly, directly from the words of Fischer himself if we compare them with his words quoted above. Let us repeat, Fischer sees the difference between the Short 7reatise . . . and the teaching of Descartes primarily in the fact that Spinoza does not explain passions as does his predecessor by the union of soul and body, but considers them simply as mental phenomena that are due exclusively to the nature of our cognition. No matter how we interpret these words, there is no doubt that Fischer sees the divergence between Spinoza and Descartes primarily in understanding the psychophysical nature of passions, that is, in the relation of thought and space in the human being, since we are considering his affects. The problem of uniting the soul with the body, and thought and space in the psychological nature of passions comprises the basic point of the divergence between the Short Treatise . . . and the teaching of Descartes. But, of course, as was indicated above, specifically in solving

124

The Teaching about Emotions

this problem, Fischer saw the basis according to which Spinoza, in our view, always remained a Cartesian (but only in this sense). Fischer said that whoever solves the problem of the relation between space and thought in the spirit of Descartes, that person is and will remain a Cartesian. But Fischer himself maintained that in the Short Treatise . . ., Spinoza diverged from Descartes specifically as a result of the fact that he rejects the solution of the psychophysical problem as applied to the nature of passions that Descartes gave. Consequently, to be logical and consistent to the end, we must admit that in developing his teaching on the passions, Spinoza was not a Cartesian as early as in the Short Treatise .... lIue, here Fischer falls into the same interpretation of the divergence between Spinoza and Descartes that, at root, perverts the very sense of Spinoza's solution to the problem of the relation between the soul and the body to a problem of affect. We will encounter this interpretation again in the course of our investigation. Fischer sees the difference between the thinking of Spinoza and that of Descartes in the fact that Spinoza rejects the explanation of passions by a union of soul and body and considers them simply as mental phenomena that are due exclusively to the nature of our cognition. Fischer maintains that, in comparison with the tendency of Descartes toward spiritualism, Spinoza takes a step forward and converts the psychology of the passions into pure phenomenology of cognition. A similar interpretation of the ideas of Spinoza can be found in the work of many investigators not only with respect to the Short Treatise . . . but also with respect to Ethics. J. Petzoldt66 (1909), as V. F. Asmus67 noted, fell into the same error. The idealistic interpreters of Spinoza usually satisfy themselves with establishing parallelism. Many representatives of the theory of psychophysical monism popular among contemporary positivists do the same. But this understanding is inadequate. To stop at parallelism means not to understand Spinoza in the end. Under the cover of the theory of parallelism, Spinoza develops an essentially materialistic view. If Spinoza had limited himself to parallelism, then for him there would be no basic obstacles to placing cognition of the soul with all its states exclusively under the mode of thinking, considering the connection of states of the soul as completely independent of connection with bodily states. Then Spinoza could have constructed his psychology as phenomenology of pure connections of cognition without even resorting to an analysis of bodily processes. It is scarcely possible to imagine anything more foreign to the spirit of Spinozism. But it is specifically this phenomenological interpretation of the Short Treatise . . ., foreign to the spirit of Spinozism, that Fischer gives. In this, he agrees with Petzoldt, who sees only parallelism in the psychology of Spinoza. As Asmus notes, "everyone who does not go beyond parallelism in explaining Spinoza must necessarily agree with Petzoldt" (1929, p. 54). Asmus sees the merit of Petzoldt in the fact that "in honing his conclusions, he showed the absurdity of all ideological interpretations of Spinozism (ibid.). Perhaps the significance of the interpretations of Fischer and Petzoldt has another positive aspect. The possibility itself of such an interpretation of Spinoza compels us to turn our attention to the remarkable fact that has not yet been given due study: even in the first draft of Spinoza's teaching on the passions in the Short Treatise ... , there is nothing of Descartes' Treatise on the Passions . .. 68 in its basic content, but there is something completely new. Spinoza turns the problem itself to quite another side. If in Descartes, the problem of passions appears primarily as a physiological problem and a problem of the interaction of soul and body, then in Spinoza, the same problem appears from the very beginning as a problem of the relation of thought and affect, understanding and passion. This is, in the full sense of the word, the other side of the moon which remains unseen over the whole

The Teaching about Emotions

125

course of the teaching of Descartes. This alone compels us to admit that the theoretical content even of Spinoza's initial draft and the 1i"eatise on Passions . .. of his teacher do not coincide, but display the most profound differences, which can only be possible in approaching one problem from two opposite ends. In this respect, the teachings of Descartes and Spinoza are polar. They actually represent two opposite poles of one problem which, as we shall see later, have always stood opposite each other over the whole course of the history of psychological thought. This polarization of scientific ideas comprises the basic content of the contemporary clash of psychological directions in the teaching on the passions. To express this position in concepts and terms of the contemporary historical period of psychology, we might say that in the divergence between the Shol1 1i"eatise ... and the 1i"eatise on Passions . . ., there was noted completely definitively the divergence between naturalistic and antinaturalistic directions in the teaching on affects, between explanatory and descriptive psychology of emotions, that represents the basic and central divergence that now divides psychological thought into two irreconcilable parts. In this divergence, Descartes stood on the naturalistic and explanatory side, and Spinoza, on the side of antinaturalistic and descriptive psychology. Disclosure of the concrete sense and meaning of the position we have developed will be given in the future course of our investigation. We might even say that this will comprise its basic pivot because without explaining the real opposition between the Cartesian and Spinozist psychology of affects, there can be neither an accurate understanding of Spinoza's teaching in its relation to contemporary psychoneurology nor a correct idea of immediate paths for the development of the science itself of human consciousness. But even now we must say that in the position we have noted, there is something that cannot but seem at first glance to be extremely paradoxical. In fact, the paradox consists in the objective position of things and not in the formulation of our ideas. Actually, there is something paradoxical in the fact that Descartes' name is connected with natural science and causal, explanatory directions of psychological thought, most materialistic in their elemental tendencies, and Spinoza's name, with the phenomenological, descriptive, idealistic trend of contemporary psychology. But this is actually so. In certain respects, what has been said corresponds to the objective situation of things which we must establish, and in this lies that particle of truth that is contained in the interpretation of Fischer and Petzoldt. We shall try to find an explanation for the paradox later, but now we shall note the fact that Spinoza's teaching on the passions started not with a continuation and development of Cartesian ideas, but with the development of the same problem from the opposite end. The fact that clarifies the origin and general evaluation of Spinoza's teaching is of no small importance in itself. No less remarkable is the fact that from the very beginning, Spinoza puts at the center of the problem that aspect of it that, like the far side of the moon, was invisible to all naturalistic teachings in psychology and that, because of this, was developed most often from the idealistic point of view over almost the whole course of its historical path. Perhaps specifically because from the very beginning the center of the Spinoza teaching became a problem that more sharply than others divided the idealistic and materialistic tendencies in psychology, the teaching retained till now not just a historical but a vital significance so that in discussing it, we must always pivot on the sphere of the sharpest and most urgent problem of contemporary psychology. Of course, the task of genuine materialism is not to circumvent problems developed by idealistic thOUght and hide one's head in the sand from them like an ostrich, declaring them to be nonexistent. The task is to solve these same problems materialistically. This was the direct historical task of Spinoza. And here once more the

126

The Teaching about Emotions

well-known observation that intelligent idealism is much closer to true materialism than stupid materialism is confirmed. We can even now reach a firm and, evidently, reliable conclusion opposite that of Fischer as to what kind of solution of this problem we would come to in the future and what kind of explanation we would find for the paradox indicated above. We can state that from the very first appearance of the Spinoza teaching, it completely follows Descartes, whose work on passions obviously guided him exclusively in the methodological basis of affects, in the external disposition of their description, and in the order of classification. His independence and originality were displayed from the very beginning in the basic opposition of his idea to the Cartesian. As early as in the Short Treatise . .. , Spinoza not only was not a Cartesian, developing and transforming the system of the teacher and untangling its contradiction, but immediately appeared as an anti-Cartesian. The sharp Spinozist teaching appears even more markedly anti-Cartesian in Ethics. In the foreword to The Teaching on the Origin and Nature of Affects, 69 Spinoza contrasts his point of view not only to that which "was written about affects and the form of human life and was said, evidently, not about natural things that follow general natural laws, but about things that lie beyond the bounds of nature, and presented man in nature like a state within a state, believing that man more likely disturbs the natural order than follows it, that he has absolute power over his actions and is defined not otherwise than by himself. Although among all who write about affects, there were eminent persons who wrote much that was beautiful, nonetheless, no one, as far as I know, determined the nature and force of affects and the extent to which the soul is able to restrain them. True, the very famous Descartes, although he thought that the soul has absolute power over its actions, tried, however, to explain human affects from their first causes and to indicate as well the path the soul could take to gain absolute power over affects. But in my opinion at least, he expressed nothing except his great ingenuity, as I shall demonstrate at the proper time" (Spinoza, 1933, p. 81). Thus, Spinoza himself understood the relation of his teaching to Descartes' system. In his teaching on the passions, Spinoza consciously tried to develop an opposite and exclusive point of view that would show that in Descartes' famous Treatise nothing was expressed except the great ingenuity of its author. After this, there can remain scarcely even a shadow of a doubt that the originality of Spinoza's teaching was effective not in the methodological basis of affects, but in its theoretical content. In the foreword to The Teaching of the Power of the Mind or on Human Freedom,70 Spinoza most acutely contrasts his thought with that of Descartes. Spinoza claims that Descartes, by his teaching on the interaction of the soul and the body through the pineal gland, significantly promotes the false opinion that affects depend absolutely on our will and that we can control them infinitely. Spinoza says that he cannot "be surprised enough that a philosopher, having strictly held to reaching conclusions only on the basis of sources that are certain of themselves, and claiming only what he recognizes clearly and definitely, and so frequently reproving the scholastics for thinking to explain dark things by hidden properties, how this philosopher accepts a hypothesis that is darker than any dark property" (ibid., p. 194). Objecting to this teaching of Descartes, Spinoza concludes: "Finally, I am not speaking now about what Descartes maintained regarding the will and its freedom since I adequately demonstrated above that all of that is false" (ibid.) As we see, here Spinoza opposes his point of view to the Cartesian specifically at the point that Fischer cites as a criterion for making a judgment that Spinoza was and remained a Cartesian: in the teaching on the psychophysical nature of

The Teaching about Emotions

127

affect. Here we see a case, frequently repeated in the history of psychology, that H. H0ffding71 discusses in relation to the study of feelings of J. W. Nahlowsky72 (1862), a psychologist of the Herbart school,13 H0ffding writes: "It is apparent here how the spiritualistic theory on the relation between the body and the soul can interfere in a special psychological problem" (1904, p. 186). These words are fully and completely applicable to the argument between Spinoza and Descartes that we are now considering, which seems to be a prototype of all arguments on the psychology of emotions in which the spiritualistic theory on the relation between the body and the soul interferes in resolving a special psychological problem. We think that what has been said is fully sufficient to explain the first question of interest to us concerning the seeming Cartesianism of Spinoza that interests us. In disclosing their internal contrast, we found a valid relation of both teachings. Just as Hegel later developed the metaphysical and rationalistic bases of the Spinoza philosophy giving the only possible refutation to Spinozism, that is, by converting the substance of Spinoza into an absolute idea, into absolute soul, and in this way, presented an antithesis to Spinozist teaching, so in his time, Spinoza presented an antithesis with respect to Descartes, but a materialistic antithesis. Behind the relations that we disclosed of the two philosophical teachings stands a thousand-yearold struggle of two basic trends in philosophical thought, idealism, and materialism, a struggle that in this case found a full and concrete expression in the solution, it seems, of the special psychological problem that has, however, a most important basic significance. Regardless of the lack of explanation of a number of very important points in the genesis of Spinoza's teaching on passions, regardless of the serious internal contradictions of that teaching, nonetheless from the main and basic aspect, it appears before us as a teaching that is wholly opposite the Cartesian teaching on passions. This must serve as the first and last points-the alpha and omega-of our whole investigation. The two teachings oppose each other as only truth and fallacy and light and dark can be opposites: this also requires proof. A different impression can arise, it is true, owing to the fact that both thinkers worked on one and the same problem and seemingly had one and the same ultimate purpose-to resolve the problem of human freedom. But, as we have seen, Spinoza himself objects in the first place to the Cartesian teaching on freedom of the will. He says in one of his letters: you see that I perceive freedom not in free decision, but in free necessity. And actually, it remains only to disclose the concept of freedom in Descartes and Spinoza to see that they are completely different from each other and, speaking in the language of Spinoza, they could agree only in name just as Canis, the heaven~ constellation, and Canis /amiliaris, the howling animal, are similar to each other. 4 Moreover, many historians of psychology are still little aware of this oppositeness, particularly historians who analyze the theory of James and Lange. These historians proceed from opinion, which, according to the gnosiology of Spinoza, is susceptible to error and does not pertain to points that are certain, but only to those that involve surmise and opinion; they frequently name Descartes and Spinoza together and jointly with each other as true progenitors of the theory of affects. Like everyone who uses this first and inadequate kind of knowledge, in Spinoza's expression, they know about the subject as much as a blind person knows about colors. Yet there is some sense in comparing the two names when we speak of the historical fate of contemporary knowledge of affects; however it is not that which is usually introduced into this comparison. Least of all, as indicated above, could Spinoza together with Descartes have been the progenitors of the scientific view

128

The Teaching about Emotions

of the nature of human emotions that has been dominant over the last half century. This view can be recognized as either Cartesian or Spinozist. By the very nature of things, it cannot be one and the other simultaneously. If in this chapter we developed the thesis that we must prove that the James-Lange theory is connected not at all with the teaching of Spinoza on the passions, but with the ideas of Descartes and Malebranche, then, by the same token, we defend the idea that this theory is anti-Spinozist. But it would be completely unproductive and would make no sense to give so much attention in the study of the fate of Spinozist teaching in the contemporary scientific knowledge of this theory as we have given it if as result we could establish only that it has nothing in common with the teaching we are considering. Specifically because of the fact that the James-Lange theory can be considered as a living realization of Cartesian teaching, studying its truth and historical fate cannot but be placed at the beginning of the study of Spinozist teaching on the passions. As we have seen, at the very beginning of the development of this teaching and at its center is the struggle against the Cartesian idea. What occurred in the psychology of emotions in the last half century and what we have tried to consider in the preceding chapters is nothing other than the historical continuation of this struggle, the prototype of which we perceive in the oppositeness of the two teachings, the Cartesian and the Spinozist. And exactly as it is impossible without explaining this oppositeness to properly understand the Spinozist teaching without elucidating the fate of anti-Spinozist ideas in the psychology of affects, it is also impossible to determine correctly the historical significance of Spinozist thought for the present and future of all of psychology. Just as Spinoza did not think that he found the best philosophy, but knew that he recognized truth, so in the struggle of contemporary psychological theory, we are trying to find not the one that best meets our tastes, best satisfies us and for this reason seems to be the best, but the one that best meets its objective and thus must be recognized as truer because the goal of science, like the goal of philosophy, is truth. Truth, however, is in itself evidence of fallacy. Illuminating historical errors in psychological thought, we thus lay the path to recognizing the truth about the psychological nature of human passions.

11 It now remains for us to elucidate whether the James-Lange theory has as its source the teaching of Descartes on the passions. In other words, it remains for us to disclose the Cartesian essence of this theory. Thus, we hope to disclose behind the struggle of concrete and special psychological hypotheses the basic struggle of various philosophical views of the nature of human consciousness, particularly the struggle of Cartesian and Spinozist ideas in living contemporary scientific knowledge. The thought that not Spinoza but Descartes is the real progenitor of the visceral theory of emotions is beginning to penetrate more and more deeply into contemporary psychology although it is not recognized in its true significance. Usually only the significance of a factual corrective is ascribed to it, a correction to the thesis on the historical connection that exists between the James-Lange hypothesis and Spinoza's teaching on the passions, but not the significance proper to it in reality that consists of changing the whole basic evaluation of the philosophical essence of our theory. The idea that this theory is Cartesian just because of its

The Teaching about Emotions

129

origin and methodological basis, and because of this cannot be admitted to be Spinozist, is wholly foreign to contemporary psychology. Thus, if psychology recognized the fallacy of this traditional opinion, with the presentation of which we began our investigation and according to which the predecessor of James and Lange is Spinoza, then this recognition must be acknowledged as only partial and inadequate. The fallacy of this opinion is usually seen in the fact that alongside Spinoza and together with him among the predecessors of our theory, we must also name Descartes. As far as we know, no one has yet expressed the idea that the peripheral theory of emotions, being Cartesian in essence, is by reason of this fact anti-Spinozist. As has been said above, specifically for this reason, a number of investigators name Descartes together with Spinoza as founders of the teaching under consideration. Thus, in listing predecessors of the peripheral theory of emotions, Titchener says that there are in Descartes and Spinoza definitions along the same lines. He cites the studies of D. Irons (1895) that elucidate the dependence on Descartes of new theories of emotions. Irons is not the first to arrive at establishing an objectively correct conclusion on the fact that in contemporary scientific knowledge, the James theory represents the same idea that Descartes defended more than 200 years prior to the appearance of the new hypothesis. Whatever might be said in our time about contemporary science, in the words of Irons, the Ireatise on Passions. . . makes it possible to compare the teaching it presents with everything that has been done in recent years. It would be difficult to find a treatise on emotions that would surpass this in originality, depth, and impressiveness. Descartes takes the same position as James, but he is not satisfied with supporting in general terms the opinion that emotion is elicited by physical change. Coming to the conclusion on the existence of six primary passions, he tries to demonstrate that there is a special whole of organic states that accompanies the formation of each of them. Following Irons,75 J. Larguier des Bancels76 maintains that the James-Lange theory is fully contained in the teaching of Descartes. As T. Ribot1 7 notes, since the time of the development of the theory of Lange and James, some unjust attacks on the thought of Descartes, which he expressed in the treatise, Passions of the Soul, have been retracted (Ribot, 1897, pp. 106-107). Thus, Ribot correctly notes that the visceral theory of emotions not only was a scientific embodiment of Cartesian teaching, but also led to the resurrection and rehabilitation of this teaching before the court of scientific thought. The James-Lange theory resurrected in the contemporary scientific consciousness the old and erroneously judged Cartesian teaching, converting it into an empirically proven position and thus placing it in the center of the scientific psychology of emotions. This is how the thinking of Ribot might be formulated. In his words, the advantage of James and Lange consists of the fact that they clearly presented the teaching of Descartes and attempted to ,;orroborate it with experimental evidence. More precise historical studies showed that in the sense of ideational genesis, in addition to a direct connection to the teaching of Descartes, the James-Lange theory also displays a connection through later representatives of Cartesianism who developed the ideas of the teacher and brought them to a logical conclusion. Here the name of Malebranche comes first; his theory actually displays an undoubted correspondence with the James-Lange theory in basic and essential traits. Literally, in this respect, the name of Malebranche as a predecessor of the organic theory of emotions cannot at all be compared with Descartes. On the contrary, the correspondence of the empirical scientific James-Lange theory with the theory of emotions of Malebranche makes its connection with Descartes even less doubtful and more clear and once more discloses its Cartesian essence.

130

The Teaching about Emotions

As we have seen, in correctly elucidating the anti-English, anti-evolutionist tendency of the Lange theory, G. Dumas calls him the latest student of the French followers of the mechanistic worldview. Separating joy and sorrow into motor and mental phenomena, eliminating illusory essences and indeterminate forces-all of this was done according to the tradition of Malebranche and Spinoza. In the composition of the former, On the Investigation of Troth, Lange located his vasomotor theory and cites this place with obvious surprise. He might have found other places there as well that would just as clearly confirm his analysis of mental and motor elements of emotion. Malebranche terms as passions all emotions that the soul naturally experiences during unusual movements of vital spirits and the blood. Remove the theological expression about the relation between the body and the soul-and you will get, essentially, the Lange theory: emotion is only consciousness of nervous-vascular changes. This comparison might have been carried much further even without special difficulty to prove that, regardless of the difference in language, this same spirit was characteristic of both the Cartesian philosopher and the Danish physiologist. Dumas notes further that even in his errors, Lange resembles the Cartesians. His somewhat severe criticism of Darwin and evolutionist psychology is nothing other than conscious or unconscious aversion which every follower of the mechanistic worldview, including Descartes, naturally has toward historical explanations. It seems to us that in this position of Dumas, something significantly greater than a simple correspondence of concrete empirical content of the theory of Lange and the theory of Malebranche is established. The correspondence itself in the description of the psychophysiological mechanism of the emotional reaction is not the primary fact, but is dependent and derivative. It flows as a necessary consequence of the fact that the very same mechanistic and antihistorical worldview in science animates the Cartesian philosopher and the Danish physiologist. Both the attempt to explain the psychology of passions in a purely mechanistic way and the conscious or unconscious aversion toward a historical explanation, both-the Cartesian philosopher and the Danish physiologist-identically inherited from Descartes, that true father of the mechanistic worldview in contemporary science, and especially in psychology. Thus, Dumas is scarcely the first to reduce the problem of the connection between the James-Lange theory and Cartesian teaching on the passions not to elucidating how the two teachings coincide in concrete definitions and descriptions of the psychophysiological mechanism of emotions itself and of the factual representation of its structure and activity, but to disclosing the common methodological base, the common scientific worldview, the common philosophical nature of these teachings separated from each other by more than two centuries. The correspondence itself of concrete definitions and factual descriptions of the emotional mechanism is only the result, only the necessary consequence, of that which is common to both theories of the philosophical spirit. We believe that this kind of formulation of the problem should be accepted wholly. Regardless of what kind of concrete, historical, and biographical paths this connection between the progenitors of the mechanistic worldview and the creators of the scientific theory may have actually taken, regardless of the extent to which the creators of the theory were conscious of and accepted the spiritual and ideational kinship of their offspring with the treatises of Descartes and Malebranche, their theory is objectively the scientific embodiment of the Cartesian spirit and must be considered as such. Only by following this course can we come to a correct formulation of the problem of the relation of this philosophical system to a concrete

The Teaching about Emotions

131

scientific conception and find the common denominator that would make it possible to trace their internal relationship. The common denominator between any philosophical system and a concrete empirical hypothesis is always found to be, as in the given case, a scientific worldview, formulated in any more or less common generalization that rises somewhat above the level of a simple establishment and description of facts. In the welI-known expression of Engels (see: K. Marx and E Engels, Works, Vol. 20, p. 366), whether the natural scientists want it or not, philosophers always guide them. To disclose the philosophical idea that guides the whole construction of the lames-Lange theory means to find the true way to explain its connection with one of two internalIy opposite philosophical systems. In the conclusions of Dumas, there is still another position of primary importance. In opposition to the first, it establishes a point of divergence between the old teaching of the philosopher and his later scientific version. In the first place, Dumas establishes the difference in the languages that Malebranche and Lange speak. In itself, the difference pertains exactly to the area of factual description of the emotional mechanism. As we have already said, the description must be considered as the result of coincidence in methodological premises of the one author and the other. It is entirely natural that if one and the same idea directed both the Cartesian philosopher and the Danish physiologist, it would bring both investigators to a similar and almost identical description of the mechanism of the emotional reaction in the language of physiology that is contemporary to every scientist. But there is more to this than only the difference in language-there is a difference in concrete physiological concepts. In this case, it would be possible to limit oneself, as Dumas does, to a simple translation from one language to the other and to replace the movement of vital forces of Malebranche with the nervous-vascular changes of Lange. But, in producing such a translation, we not only replace the old physiological concepts of the seventeenth century with the views of the nineteenth century contemporaneous with Lange, but assume certain major changes in the spirit itself of the old teaching. 10 make the translation possible, it is necessary, in the expression of Dumas, to eliminate the theological expression of the relation between the body and the soul. Only by having carried out this operation on the position of Malebranche will we obtain the theory of Lange. But to do this means not only to replace one set of words with another, but also to introduce substantial changes into the thought itself that is expressed in the old words. Thus, in noting the difference between the old teaching and the new, Dumas indicates, together with factual divergences, divergences of a theoretical nature, as he did in explaining points of correspondence between the two teachings. In this sense, Dumas preserves in all purity the only possible and the only correct formulation of the problem. In addition, he notes fully the series of problems that confront investigators who wish to discover the actual relation between Cartesian teaching on passions and the organic theory of emotions. Both positions developed by Dumas-the correspondence and the divergence of the theories under consideration-were developed in the most recent literature. Both have their factual aspects and their theoretical aspects. The first cannot be considered otherwise than as the result of the second. If there is correspondence or divergence between the two teachings in any factual position that is essential for each of them, then behind ihis, we must seek correspondence or divergence of the same teaching in some of :he theoretical bases if, of course, we are not dealing with a definite individual ,~rror in thought or with an error of a logical or factual nature. In considering the further development of the position of Dumas, we shall limit ourselves to only the major concepts of these historical studies since their conclusions can interest us only to the extent that they serve to explain the problems

132

The Teaching about Emotions

of our investigation. The great majority of investigators come to conclusions that agree with the position of Dumas on the concordance of the philosophical spirit of both teachings. Here it is extremely important that the connection between the teachings always appears as a connection through a common mechanistic worldview. Specifically, it is just this connection that G. Brett78 identifies as basic in his study of the historical development of the theory on emotions. This author says: "Descartes, wanting passionately to develop his physiology, revived the method of Aristotle that compared animals to machines that are moved by internal forces the way marionettes are moved with the help of a wire. This was an easy way to avoid many difficult problems and to open up the possibility of reducing emotions to laws of mechanics. The dynamics of extension and contraction seemed to be enough to explain affective or passive states. This point of view began to spread rapidly because the whole theory was connected in time with the lucidity and precision of ideas formulated by Galileo. This was a deceptive simplification of a kind that is frequently accepted with great success. It could be easily combined with formulas that survived in the Aristotelian tradition. Hobbes, inspired by Galileo, tried to reduce all mental phenomena to various kinds of movements and produced word for word his own translation of Aristotle's Rhetoric. Malebranche, intoxicated by Cartesian wine, emphasized the role of contraction and extension with such insistence that he was declared the predecessor of James and Lange-a disclosure not very remarkable if we recall that Lange himself referred to Malebranche as a man who actually anticipated his theory. It remains a nonproductive theory, evidence of the futility of all attempts to reduce mental experience to artificial mechanical constructions" (G. S. Brett, 1928, p. 392). Thus, Brett comes to essentially the same conclusion as Dumas both with respect to the direct connection that exists between Cartesian wine and the half-century intoxication of psychological thought-a remarkable paradox also with respect to what lies at the base of this connection: the grandiose all-encompassing idea of explaining everything that exists, including human passions, with the help of mechanical laws. Another aspect of the conclusions of Dumas is being developed by H0ffding, among older authors, and Dunlap, among contemporary authors. H0ffding explains mainly the theoretical aspect of the divergence between the old and the new theory; Dunlap, the factual conflict that results from that divergence. H0ffding indicates the inverse relation that exists between the quality of feeling and the force that accompanies its organic changes. With strong mental agitation, the qualitative distinctness of a feeling frequently falls away and is replaced by a general excitement. The state that at first was caused mainly by the nature of the stimulus, event, or idea now depends exclusively on the organic actions on the brain. It begins with an idea, but ends with a feeling. In many cases, self-observation makes it possible to distinguish two stages of the development of a feeling: the first, when the effect of cognitive elements are clearly apparent and there is a special quality of feeling, and the second, which corresponds to the organic effect on the brain. Moreover, there is no basis for sharply dividing, as some authors of the spiritualistic camp have done, these two stages, proposing that only the latter, but not the former, is connected with physiological states. Thus, Descartes and Malebranche described this circuit as an interaction of soul and body. In more recent times, in complete opposition to the spiritualistic view, it is maintained that in every emotional agitation only sensations are actually present corresponding to the action of organs on the brain (H0ffding, 1904, p. 227). Here H0ffding has in mind the James-Lange theory, which he considers improbable on the basis of the fact that in some cases, a development of feeling over several stages can be observed. He also objects to the argument of Lange that emo-

The Teaching about Emotions

133

tional agitations may be elicited not only by ideas, but also through purely physical means. But, in fact, H0ffding believes that it is not indifferent whether or not some ideas affect feeling. In the first case, feeling assumes a distinct character and direction, and in the second, it is only an indefinite process of stimulation. This difference has great significance for self-observation, although this is not obvious to a bystander. Thus, H0ffding himself proceeds from the position that is rather more opposite to the James-Lange hypothesis than similar to it. While this hypothesis reduces all feeling to perceptible sensations, H0ffding establishes the reverse relation between them: "the stronger the element of feeling, the more the characteristically sensually perceiving or recognizing element fades away . . . In its most elementary forms, feeling is determined mainly by the strength of the stimulation and the degree to which it interferes with the course of organic life. This is especially so with stimuli that elicit instinctive movements. Their special quality is overshadowed by the sensual stimulation and the ardor which they arouse. But where the special quality of sensation can appear with such strength that it becomes something of an organ of feeling, then, like sensation, feeling assumes a certain form and character. What it loses in strength, it gains in wealth and variety of nuance as well as in independence from the direct struggle for existence. The same sum of energy that in the feeling of life is concentrated on its one question, 'to be or not to be,' on organic well-being, is diffused by qualitative feelings and dispersed by various currents. For this reason, winning or losing with respect to feeling due to qualitative differentiation depends on whether the energy of life of the feeling grows as a whole together with its qualitative nuancing" (H0ffding, 1904, p. 196). Emphasizing the mentally cognitive moment as playing the main role in the development of feeling from elementary to higher forms and noting the diminishing significance of the purely organic moment, H0ffding became one of the people who from the very beginning took up a definite position as its opponent in the struggle around the new theory. We shall return in the future to these concepts of his. Also, H0ffding cannot but establish that the James-Lange theory is a complete contrast to the spiritualistic view of Descartes and Malebranche, who described the circuit of passion as an interaction of soul and body. It is easy to see that H0ffding has in mind the same divergence between the two teachings spoken of by Dumas, who understood the need to remove the theological view of the relation between soul and body in order to move from the formula of Malebranche to the formula of Lange. Thus, the second principle of the scientific worldview that divides the two theories is identified: the spiritualistic view of the interaction between soul and body in the mechanism of passions and the theological conception of the psychophysical problem of emotions. Thgether with the mechanistic principle identified earlier, it forms the whole methodological basis for the Cartesian teaching on the passions. But just as correspondence of Cartesian teaching to the organic theory in the factual description of the psychophysical mechanism of emotional reaction inevitably flows from the general tendency toward a mechanistic explanation for passions, so a divergence also in the concrete factual description of the structure and course of the emotional process inevitably flows from the basic divergence of their views of the psychophysical nature of emotion. As we have already said, the aspect of the matter just described was especially emphasized recently by Dunlap, who sees in this most essential point the oppositeness of the teaching of Descartes to that Jf James and is inclined to accuse the new central theory of emotions of returning co the Cartesian point of view.

134

The Teaching about Emotions

"James thought that H. Miinsterberg79 was finished with the old theory proposed by Descartes according to which afferent currents stimulate the intellectual processes and efferent currents elicit passions of the soul. Evidently James was mistaken" (Dunlap, 1928, p. 154). In Dunlap's opinion, the old theory is revived in the new teaching that contrasts the peripheral hypothesis of the origin of emotions with the central hypothesis. Dunlap says that this problem is the watershed that divides the Cartesian theory (which proposes that emotion arises due to a discharge process that begins in the brain, that is, due to an innervation sensation in the old sense) on the one hand, and the James-Lange theory (which considers emotion, like perception, as the result of peripheral sensations) on the other (ibid., pp. 159160). K. Dunlap belongs to those followers of the organic theory who try to transform it in such a way as to bring it into agreement with new facts. He proposes that with a correct interpretation of new facts, usually developed as an argument against this theory, we can see in them its confirmation rather than refutation. Dunlap admits that James never fully accepted his own theory and not only adhered to psychophysical parallelism, but retained much of spiritual feeling that he did not want to subject to crude bodily dependence. Like other followers of the organic theory, Dunlap properly stresses the factual divergence between the Cartesian and the peripheral theories, and what C. Spearman 80 said concerning the theory of McDougall, the second attempt to resurrect the James theory, is fully applicable to him. Spearman names not only the direct predecessors of this theory, Ward, James, and others, but also its direct progenitor, Malebranche (c. E. Spearman, 1928, p. 40). Considering emotion as the dynamic background of all mental processes, Dunlap actually tries to preserve the James-Lange theory. The problem regarding visceral changes does not seem to him to be important for psychology and for this reason, he is inclined to consider the new theory as a further anatomical development of the James-Lange theory. Dunlap considers it his achievement that on the basis of this theory he predicted the sameness of visceral changes in certain emotional states, later confirmed in laboratory experiments. All emotions with which Cannon dealt are stimulating emotions and for this reason, they must of necessity be more similar to each other than different from each other (K. Dunlap, 1928, p. 159). However that may be, the results of Dunlap's studies cannot be considered in resolving the problem that interests us. These results seem to us to be ambiguous. On the one hand, since he basically retains the position of the peripheral hypothesis, Dunlap himself is, in the expression of Brett, intoxicated by Cartesian wine, although he is not conscious of this; on the other hand, he indicates the essential point in which the hypothesis he is defending is vehemently opposed to the Cartesian teaching. We must again return to this point, to the problem of innervating sensations, in the course of our investigation, but now I would like only to note that this undoubtedly existing objective divergence between the hypothesis of James and the teaching of Descartes is the direct result of the theoretical divergence of the two theories, which H0ffding pointed out. Actually, we cannot but see that the assumption Descartes made on the possibility of the origin of emotions through the centrifugal path depends directly on his whole conception of the psychophysical circuit of passion, a conception based on the spiritualistic view of the relation between the soul and the body. For us, this is now enough of this conclusion since it finishes the series of problems this part of our investigation faced. We might have collected the whole series into one. It encompasses two basic problems of which each can be split into

The Teaching about Emotions

135

two-a factual part and a part that deals with theory. On the whole, these four problems that we derived from the study of sources exhaust the whole circle of problems connected with explaining the true relation between the Cartesian teaching and the psychological theory that is dying before our eyes. This is the series: the mechanistic principle for explaining emotions and the factual description of the psychophysical mechanism of emotional reactions; the spiritualistic conception of the psychophysiological nature of emotions and the problem of the centrifugal origin of emotion. The first two elements of our series are common to both teachings, the second two divide them. We might have concluded this chapter of our investigation with this, the sole task of which is a concrete formulation of the problem of the ideational connection between Cartesian teaching and the James-Lange theory. We found everything that we were looking for. But before concluding this chapter, we thought it necessary to consider the opinions of the authors of this theory themselves on its spiritual ancestors. As we have seen before, James did not recognize them at all. Although he does not say this directly anywhere, we might say that, first, he thought little about the philosophical essence of his hypothesis, and, second, he was inclined to contrast it as an empirical scientific conception with all metaphysical philosophical conceptions that existed previously. In this respect, he did not differentiate even between the opposite philosophical teachings on the passions. He rejected them equally and had no thought that they might have at least some kind of meaning for the fate of the hypothesis he was developing. He says that purely descriptive literature on the problem he was interested in, beginning with Descartes and to our time, is the most boring section of psychology. James contrasted his hypothesis with all the old theories taken together, in which he saw no guiding beginning, no basic point of view, and no logical generalizations. He charges the old literature with considering every emotion as some kind of eternal, immune, spiritual essence similar to species that biology at one time considered to be unchangeable. If we add to this that James objected to the materialistic interpretation of his hypothesis and that, not without vacillation, he agreed to admit a basic difference in the psychological nature of fine emotions and lower emotions, we get almost everything James said about the methodological bases of his hypothesis. As we can see, all of this does not amount to very much. James' methodological carelessness is directly connected with the general trend to which he adhered in psychology, and which can be designated as radical empiricism. In his eyes, psychology represents nothing other than "a heap of raw factual material, an honest difference of opinion, a number of weak attempts at classification and empirical generalizations of a purely descriptive character ... But not one law in the sense in which we use this word in the area of physical phenomena, not one thesis from which conse:J.uences could be deduced ... In short, psychology is still not a science, it is something promising to become a science in the future" (w. James, 1902, pp. 412-413). Thus James treated psychological investigation as the accumulation of a heap of raw factual material and empirical generalizations of a descriptive nature. In this respect, he evidently did not make an exception for his own theory of emotions. For this reason, he remained blind to its philosophical essence and ideational origin. Nowhere does he indicate that he knew about the correspondence between his hypothesis and the Cartesian hypothesis or any other philosophical teaching-neither in the sense of identity of principles in its basis nor in the sense of factual establishment of the emotional mechanism. In this respect, Lange, an anatomist and physiologist, appeared to be more discerning than James, a philosopher by profession and in name. However, even

136

The Teaching about Emotions

the discernment of Lange did not make it possible for him finally to recognize his dependence on the philosophical teaching of the past. But his attention did not miss the fact that these teachings contained a description of an emotional mechanism that corresponded to his own. He writes: "It is remarkable that 200 years ago it was possible to create a complete vasomotor theory on bodily manifestations of emotions. Malebranche did this. With the right-mindedness of a genius, he discovered the true connection between phenomena at a time when physiological knowledge was just being conceived, when there was no knowledge of vascular muscles nor of vascular nerves" (C. Lange, 1896, p. 86). Citing the explanation of emotions in which Malebranche follows Descartes, Lange concludes: "'llanslated into the language of contemporary physiology, the theory of Malebranche means that every strong emotional impression increases vasomotor innervation, and in this way causes constriction of the arteries. If this constriction pertains to the arteries of the brain, then the brain gets somewhat little blood and the rest of the body somewhat much; anemia of the brain leads to general paralytic phenomena. But if, on the contrary, as happens in other kinds of emotion, the arteries of the head remain free while all the other arteries of the body constrict, then the head and the face are filled with blood. While physiology knew nothing about the active change in the caliber form of the vessels, the theory of Malebranche seemed to be an unfounded hypothesis and for this reason, was not accorded any attention. Regardless of the unavoidable incompleteness and errors in its details, it is, however, extremely remarkable in the author's brilliant view of the disturbances of blood circulation as a primary phenomenon on which all other physical manifestations that accompany emotion depend" (ibid., pp. 87-88). In contrast to James, C. G. Lange clearly saw that a complete vasomotor theory of bodily manifestations of emotions was devised 200 years before his time. But he was only half seeing. He turned his attention to the correspondence of his theory and the theory of Malebranche from their factual aspect. The theoretical aspect of the matter remained dark and unclear for him. In one of the first chapters, we already cited Lange's remark about Spinoza; Lange believed that Spinoza was closer than all others to the view he developed, since Spinoza not only did not consider bodily manifestations of emotions as dependent on mental movements, but placed them alongside or almost in the foreground. Lange, like James, did not even suspect his actual kinship to Cartesian teaching on passions and his complete oppositeness to the teaching of Spinoza. In the graphic expression of Brett, as in the case of Malebranche, intoxication with Cartesian wine was working here.

12 Let us begin with a thorough consideration of a more specific problem: of the same thing that was clear to Lange, regardless of his intoxication with the Cartesian wine and the triumph of the mechanistic worldview in his own theory, of the same thing that more than anything else struck the eye of the investigators and critics and led to the establishment of a relation between the mechanistic-spiritualistic theory and the theory of passions of the seventeenth century and its later embodiment in the nineteenth century. Identity of facts always strikes the eye sooner than identity of theories. Behind the dissimilar physiological languages of the seventeenth and nineteenth centuries, it was comparatively easy to see and distinguish the mechanism of emotional reaction that was basically identical. We have the support of a number of studies that, with surprising agreement, bring us to one and the

The Teaching about Emotions

137

same conclusion. Unfortunately, we will find neither similar support nor similar unanimity of opinions in resolving the problem of the theoretical correspondence of the two conceptions. Here we shall have to pave our own road. For this reason, we shall arm ourselves at the outset with a clear understanding of the factual aspect of the matter. G. Sergi, to whom we are indebted for the most solid and perhaps most definitive study of this problem, is appropriately indignant with James and Lange for not knowing or ignoring the true founder of the physiological theory of emotionsDescartes. While in his studies of the reflex, C. Sherrington recalls in detail the premonitions of Descartes, James does not cite him at all and Lange openly ignores him. He cites Spinoza because being oblivious of him would scandalize the readers. In Descartes, however, he finds the most antiphysiological, most intellectualistic sentence of the whole Treatise on Emotions . .., which contains the roots of his teaching. 8! In this respect, Lange was openly, clearly, and obviously wrong. Even Descartes' general definition of passions leaves no doubt that his teaching to a much greater degree than that of Spinoza must be considered as coming closest to the vasomotor theory. Descartes refers passions to the group of mental processes that he unites in his classification under the title of sensing or perceptions, and which are distinguished primarily by their passive nature. Together with perceptions pertaining only to the body-whether it be to our own body or another's-such as sensations, feelings (color, tone), bodily affects such as satisfaction or pain, and bodily urges such as hunger or thirst, as well as with perceptions that refer only to the spirit like involuntary perceptions of our thinking or desiring, Descartes also distinguishes perceptions of a third type. These are characterized primarily by the fact that they belong simultaneously to the spirit and to the body; with these perceptions, as a result of the influence and cooperation of the body, the soul itself suffers. Descartes terms this type of passive psychophysical processes passions. Consequently, for him, passion is a direct expression of the dual spirit-body human nature. It pertains to man just as movement pertains to the body. Passion for Descartes is distinguished by duality, by its spirit-body nature. With the exception of passions, Descartes finds no other factual data that would make it possible for us to recognize the common life of soul and body. In this respect, passions are a third basic phenomenon of human nature together with thinking and movement. The mind and the will are possible only in spiritual nature, movement, only in the bodily, and passions, only in the human, uniting in itself spirit and body. The dual nature of man is the only real basis of passions, and these in turn are the only basis for comprehending human nature. If we recall that for Descartes, in all of nature there exists only one body that is united with a spirit, specifically the human body, that all other bodies are without spirit and soul, that all of them, even the bodies of animals, are simply machines, then it becomes clear that human passions are for Descartes not only the only manifestations of the joint life of spirit and body in human nature, but also an altogether unique object-the only phenomenon in all of the universe, in all that actually exists, in which two substances are united that are not united anywhere else. It is understandable that because of this, the teaching on passions occupies a ,~ompletely exceptional place in Descartes' system: first, passions are the only phenomenon in which we can find the dual nature in all its fullness, the joint life of the spirit and the body; second, in the whole system, this teaching is the only point of intersection of the spiritualistic teaching of Descartes on the spirit and on his mechanistic teaching on bodies. Also understandable is the fact that, owing to such

138

The Teaching about Emotions

a formulation of the problem, human passions in the system of Descartes appear not only as something completely incomparable to all other manifestations of human life, but also as something that has no similarity to anything else, something absolutely unique in all the universe. In accordance with this understanding, Descartes defines passions as perceptions, as sensations, or as movements of the soul that are its property and are evoked by the activity of vital spirits and that sustain and reinforce it. If we recall that for Descartes, vital spirits are not mediators between matter and spirit, but are, according to his own definition, only bodies, finer than a particle of blood, most mobile and hottest, produced in the heart, a kind of distillation, then for us, the closeness of this classical definition of passions to the formulas of Lange and James becomes clear at once. Descartes compares vital spirits to a light breeze, a pure and living flame, continuously rising in a large mass from the heart to the brain and from there entering the muscles through the nerves and transmitting movement to all the members. These most mobile and lightest particles of blood, always material, move according to mechanical laws, producing in the organs sensations and movements and directing authentic vital functions. In the physiology of Descartes, they are a general and quite vague concept in which blood circulation and transmission of nervous excitation are still undifferentiated. But, in any case, there is no doubt that as vital spirits, Descartes understood a very fine bodily mechanism brought into action by warmth of the heart and moving according to pure laws of mechanics, which are identical to laws of nature, a mechanism that together with the organization of our organs determines all actions and functions common to man and animals just as the movement of clocks is produced only by the power of their springs and the shape of their wheels. We shall not try to present in detail the physiological conception of Descartes. It is actually of no more than historical interest. For us, more important is the general structure, the general idea of the structure of this spirit-body mechanism on which Descartes bases his explanation of human passions. According to the valid observation of Sergi, the physiological representations of Descartes must be replaced by new representations, and his vital spirits must give way to motor nerves. The very small and only nerve center of Descartes, the pineal gland, must be replaced by an immeasurably great hierarchy of many centers in order for us to see that as a result of such translation into contemporary physiological language, the teaching of Descartes remains the doctrine by which we live even now. In order to be convinced of this, it is enough to remember that the basic spirit of all of Lange's argumentation consists in exposing the insupportability of the hypothesis on the mental nature of emotions, the uselessness of this hypothesis, and in the proof of the fact that emotions can arise in a purely physical way just from the mechanics of disturbances of our vasomotor apparatus alone. Lange's assertion that we owe to the vasomotor system the whole emotional part of our mental life, our joys and sorrows, our happy and unhappy days, is essentially a translation into contemporary psychological language of the Descartes formulas which state that passions are nothing other than perceptions of the soul that are elicited, sustained, and reinforced by the activity of vital spirits, that is, the lightest and most mobile particles of the blood. As Sergi notes, the same wholly pertains also to another point of this teaching. Descartes differentiates between passions and two other kinds of perceptions since we refer passions not to external objects nor to our body, but exclusively only to our soul. This position agrees completely with the idea that is established in contemporary psychology and which has the James-Lange theory as its source. Lange cites Bard's thesis that affective phenomena are purely subjective and cannot in any way

The Teaching about Emotions

139

be used for realizing external reality, that they always are experienced as an actual state of our "I," but not as a property of certain objects. From 1650 to 1923, when these words were written, Sergi notes, the "I" and purely subjective phenomena have replaced the soul. In other respects, the difference between the old and the new teachings is more significant. Here it has a purely verbal character and Descartes' idea remains our idea. There are two more points in this general definition of passions that deserve our attention: the passive perceptive character of emotions and the distinction and uniqueness of the movement of vital spirits that arouses emotion in the soul. There can be scarcely any doubt that Lange and James reduce emotion to sensation or perception of organic changes. If we consider it from a purely phenomenological aspect, this is the weakest aspect of the whole theory. Actually, according to this theory, it is difficult to understand how feeling can be identified with bodily sensations, with sensations of shivering, accelerated heart rate, or shedding tears; in these cases, we have before us completely distinct or more vague sensations as such. In what secret way the aggregate of sensations, remaining sensations the whole time according to the strict sense of the theory, is converted into feeling, is absolutely unknown and scarcely allows a rational and understandable explanation from the phenomenal point of view. Quite recently, E. Claparede indicated this difficulty: "If emotion is only consciousness of peripheral changes in the organism, why is it perceived as emotion and not as an organic sensation? If I am frightened, why do I experience fright instead of simply being conscious of certain organic impressions: shivering, heart rate, etc.? I do not recall that anyone has attempted to respond to this objection." "However," according to Claparede, "such a response presents no great difficulties. Emotion is nothing other than being conscious of the form or structure of these varied organic impressions. In other words, emotion is consciousness of a global attitude of the organism" (E. Claparede. 1928, p. 28). This kind of general and vague perception of a whole representing the most primitive form of perception, Claparede terms syncretic perception. But specifically this response discloses the whole insupportability of the perceptive theory of emotions considered from the phenomenal aspect. The whole matter consists of the fact that, according to the James-Lange theory, emotion is, from the psychological point of view, a completely structure less formation consisting of an aggregate of sensations, mentally extremely various, made up according to laws of physiological mechanics. We are inclined to maintain that the James-Lange theory is a theoretically structureless theory of emotions. Actually, how can fear arise as a unique, mental messenger structure, as an integral experience, from a sensation of stopping the breath, beating of the heart, cold sweat, raising of the hair, shivering, dryness of the mouth, yawning, and other bodily manifestations which James, following Darwin, lists as examples of the best descriptions of emotions? Of course, the very sense of this theory consists of the fact that fear, anger, and other emotions as whole, indivisible structures are a simple illusion, that, if we subtract elements of bodily sensations step by step from these structures, the structures will cease existing. Thus, constructing emotions from separate atoms, from elements of bodily sensations, is typical for this theory and makes it like the anti-structural atomistic theories that treated perceptions as sums of sensations. This theory could be called structural only if it was based on an admission of phenomenal and objective preeminence of fear and anger as such and if it found its place and meaning for separate organic sensations only within the composition of the whole experience. It recognizes phenomenal and objective reality and primariness only of elements and tries to construct from them a completely structureless whole that arises actually

140

The Teaching about Emotions

by a syncretic path, that is, a path of arbitrary combination of everything with everything else. Of course, James considered most emotional reactions as random, neither biological nor especially psychologically united by an internal, essential connection. He said that in such a complex organism as the nervous system is, there must exist many random reactions. Thus, Claparede's appeal to an all-powerful uniting principle as widespread in contemporary psychology as the structural principle is fatal for a theory that he is inclined to defend if only partially. The equals sign placed between emotions and perceptions in turn equates the James-Lange theory with Cartesian teaching. Since this point is of central significance for the whole James-Lange theory, the similarity of the two teachings cannot be a simple accident: of course, scientific theories, in contrast to emotion in the representation of James, cannot arise purely by chance, as chaotic uniting of alien elements; and if the two teachings coincide in any point central to both, that must be evidence of structural similarity if not of structural identity of the two. James insists that there are no special centers in which emotional processes might be localized, that they occur in general motor and sensory centers of the cortex, and that, consequently, in principle, they are identical with ordinary sensory processes that elicit sensations or perceptions. Dunlap, following James, insists on the possibility of explaining emotions on the basis of the mechanism that is the basis for ordinary perception. Directly dependent on this is the feature of this theory to which McDougall called attention, reproaching the creators of the theory with the fact that they consider only the sensory aspect of emotion and pay no attention to its impUlsive character. And for Descartes, t4is point has a central significance. For him, emotions are the essence of perception or sensation, that is, states passive in nature, and he calls them passions for this reason. Descartes teaches that passions arise in the soul in the same way as the objects of external organs of feelings and are sensed by it in exactly the same way. As long as agitation of the heart, blood, and vital spirits does not stop, passions are represented in our thinking in the same way as sensed objects acting on organs of our external feelings. Because of this, Sergi notes: we will find nothing more definite, nothing more clear in unintentional imitators of Descartes. He laid down the bases of the visceral theory of emotions with full consciousness of what he was doing. If Descartes, thus, is the true founder of the visceral theory since he reduced emotion to sensation of visceral changes, then he deserves equal recognition as the real founder of the theory also from the point of view of understanding the visceral aspect itself of emotion. Sergi calls attention to the fact that Descartes ascribes the appearance, maintenance, and reinforcement of emotion to the special movement of vital spirits. The organic theory of passions is hidden behind these puzzling words. The uniqueness of emotion obviously has as its source the uniqueness of corresponding vital processes. We are able to perceive objects owing to a certain movement of spirits. In exactly the same way, we owe the genesis of recollection to a special movement of spirits. Of what does the uniqueness of this movement of spirits that determines the genesis of passions consist? For Descartes, uniqueness consists of the fact that this movement has a visceral origin and visceral dependence. The uniqueness of Descartes' method of studying passions consists, as is known, in the fact that from the beginning he tries to consider the mechanism of passions as it would have acted in a robot or in a soulless machine. Passions, it is understood, would be reduced exclusively to movements characteristic for them, would not contain anything mental, and would have to be called by some other name. Only after explaining the automatic, emotional mechanism does Descartes join a soul capable of experiencing passions to the imagined soulless robot.

The Teaching about Emotions

141

This method of investigation is something much more important for the whole conception than the simply methodical device of analysis and segmentation of the complex problem. It has methodological and theoretical significance. For this reason, it is of primary significance for evaluating the Cartesian teaching on passions as a whole. But what is most instructive for our purposes, what we learn from the consideration of the unique analysis that separates the dual nature of passions into an automatic mechanism and into mental perceptions of the functions of this mechanism is the following: we see for ourselves how intimately and inseparably the theoretical aspect of the teaching is connected with its factual physical aspect. Physiological analysis of the passions of a soulless robot thus acquires a deeper theoretical meaning. Fischer says: "Such an explanation of passions of a soul-body nature of man is most typical of the teaching of Descartes both in the assumption he makes and in his theoretical tendency. With the help of vital spirits and organs of the soul, of which the cerebral gland is one, the philosopher tries to establish a purely mechanical base for the genesis of passions. This is the center of the burden and the innovation of his endeavor" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 1, p. 381). In order to study this point of the problem, the point central to the whole teaching, we must briefly recall the more important of Descartes' psychophysical assumptions. He considers the human body as a complex machine, parts of which are found in a complex interaction with each other and for this reason, form a single and indivisible whole in a certain sense. For this reason, for Descartes, the organism is nothing other than a segmented machine, a special kind of complex mechanism. In this complex machine, there is one part that has a completely exceptional significance. It is the habitat of the soul, that is, that organ which is preeminently connected with the soul and through which the soul communicates with the whole organism. Descartes believes the organ of the soul to be the cerebral gland located in the middle of the central organs of nerves, which is the place in which the actual interaction between the soul and the body occurs. Here, the movements of the vital spirits are converted into the soul's sensations and perceptions. The reverse conversion of the movements of the spirit into bodily movements of the gland occurs here also, and the movements spread from here to all organs. The vital spirits are factors of sensation and movement mediating communication between the soul and the body. Descartes explains the natural mechanical genesis of passions with the help of this psychophysiological mechanism localized in the cerebral gland, on the strength of its central position and uniqueness as an unpaired part of the brain. Imagine that a robot perceives some kind of frightening figure and the vital spirits activate the gland; in turn, it determines the direction of their reverse flow, which results in the well-known picture of movements characteristic of fright and flight. Simultaneously with the movements, the flow of vital spirits causes a series of movements in the internal organs which in the aggregate characterize a robot in a state of being threatened and fleeing. Such is the passion of a machine-such is the visceral state. For each passion, there is a corresponding special and definite picture of changes in internal organs-in the heart, stomach, lungs, etc. Sergi summarizes this position of Descartes in the following words: such is passion-such is the visceral formula-such is the formula of the blood-such is the tendency of the vital spirits; or, translated into our language: such is emotion-such is the formula of the blood-such is the cortical formula. Descartes, however, was not satisfied with such a significant conclusion. Before '.miting a soul to his machine and considering passions from the psychological as-

142

The Teaching about Emotions

pect, he had to develop still another stage in his physiological conception. He said: such is passion, such is the tendency of the spirits. He should have said: such is the tendency of spirits, such is passion. Descartes actually took this further and decisive step. The dependence between a certain kind of passion and a certain organic state is reversible. A complete circuit of passion seems possible. In an example considered earlier, the cerebral gland was activated from outside; an external object acted on the spirits when they exited from the gland. Now when the spirits enter the gland, not when they leave it, they push it now here now there. The cause of its movement is no longer the object that acts on the spirits, but blood which determines these movements and-even earlier-the general state of the organism. Perceptions of danger created an organic state of fright in the machine, and the spirits resulting from this state maintain and reinforce it. If we translate this into language of more contemporary physiology and replace spirits and the gland with corresponding terms, we obtain the following picture: the image of a threatening object on the retina calls forth reflexes of flight and certain visceral reflexes. This kind of Cartesian idea, Sergi concludes, has lost its obsolete external expression. In the presence of emotion, through visceral sensory paths, a certain visceral state activates reflexes which prolong and sustain this state. Yet this is how the mechanism of passion considered exclusively from the physical aspect appears. It is still passion played out in a completely soulless robot according to purely mechanical laws. Following Descartes, we must consider what will become of the activity of this mechanism if a soul is united with it capable of experiencing sensations of visceral changes and emotions. Here, at the decisive point of Cartesian analysis, we are confronted by an unheard of thing, unexpected and capable of confusing any teacher ready to reverse all teaching. It seems that, having considered the physical aspect of passion, we exhausted by this almost all of its content. Adding the soul does not introduce anything substantially new, as might have been expected, into the circuit of passions, into the activity of the emotional mechanism. It is surprising, but the investigator must ascertain that the difference between the passions of the machine without a soul and the passions of the soul itself is not always adequately clear in Descartes. Descartes, it seems, remains true to his initial intention, which he states in the foreword to the treatise: "My intention is to treat passions not like the orator and not like the moral philosopher, but like the physicist" (Descartes, 1914, p. XIV). This physicalistic, mechanical approach to passions obviously constituted from the very beginning the dominant idea of Descartes, which he maintained over almost the whole course of his study. Specifically this idea compelled him to write about his subject as if no one before him had touched it, and to contrast his study with the teachings of the ancients on the passions. Human passions were considered earlier from the psychological aspect. Their bodily, mechanical nature remained unrecognized. Descartes concentrated all attention specifically on this aspect of the problem, but in a strange way, it almost entirely exhausted the whole problem. If we analyze the example Descartes gives of how passions are aroused in the soul, we will see that in the picture of fright and flight that we considered above, little changes. In speaking of the equation between emotion and perception, we have already virtually touched upon what is new that appears in this case. The new consists only in that the soul senses and perceives the changes that are occurring in the body. In this case, vital spirits activating the cerebral gland, being an organ of the soul, activate not only certain motor and visceral changes, of which we spoke earlier, but also certain sensations of the soul. His own position that passions arise in the soul in the same way as objects are perceived by external feelings and are

The Teaching about Emotions

143

recognized by the soul through exactly the same process remains basic for Descartes. In analyzing his own example, Descartes reaches the conclusion that vital spirits in a situation of fear activate a certain movement in the gland that by its nature is assigned to move the soul to feeling this passion.
