Something similar happens with all other passions caused by movement of vital spirits which alone are capable of eliciting bodily and mental aspects of emotion. The tendency of the spirits flowing toward the nerves of the heart is sufficient to communicate movement to the gland through which fear arises in the soul. Actually, it would be hard to expect a greater correspondence with the visceral theory of emotions. Descartes sees the source of psychical passion in the same movement of the vital spirits that elicit certain changes in the internal organs for each passion. Thus, we return to the original point of the whole teaching-to defining passions as sensations or perceptions of the soul elicited by the activity of vital spirits that simultaneously elicit a series of changes of a visceral character represented by the soul exactly the same way it represents objects perceived through external organs of feeling. Passion appears to be nothing other than perception of visceral changes. Thus, if we find a striking correspondence between the theoretical positions of the Cartesian theory and the peripheral theory of emotions, we must expect that even the difficulties which confronted the latter, those irreconcilable contradictions in which it is entangled, those incongruities in it carried to the absurd, which dominate it from its very beginning, will be extremely close also to the teaching of Descartes. And this actually appears to be so. Dumas correctly showed that Lange's theory, with all its strong and weak aspects, is indebted to Cartesian teaching. Even in his mistakes, in the words of Dumas, Lange resembles the Cartesians. Dumas connects the anti-evolutionist tendency of this theory with the aversion that every follower of the mechanistic worldview, including Descartes, naturally feels toward historical explanations. We shall briefly consider explanations of the errors, contradictions, and incongruities in which both theories are involved in a remarkably similar way. First, we will refer to the factual sterility of both theories with respect to any real elucidation and description of the passions using this method of their investigation, which we can obviously describe as mainly mechanistic. Actually both theories exhibit completely identical and total impotence if it is necessary to bring forward concrete scientific knowledge of human passions and enrich it from the factual aspect. C. G. Lange, as we know, analyzed seven basic emotions. However, he assumed that this was only a brilliant beginning which must be followed by scientific investigation of the whole enormous variety of emotional reactions. It seemed that, taking step after step along the path he had marked, with the key he provided, we would be able to disclose the whole area of human feelings. The possibilities of the new theory seemed to Lange to be boundless and inexhaustible. His argument is that the old theory, completely arbitrarily classifying emotions, violates facts, establishing certain forms where there is an infinite number of imperceptible transidons. Using the old hypothesis, we frequently find it difficult to determine under which ordinary rubric to place a given momentary attitude. We frequently are satisfied with completely indefinite statements that some (~motion is experienced in the soul without being able to classify what we feel as any of the emotions for which we have a name. Lange hoped to lead investigation out of this sterile state, placing before it the real scientific task for this series of phenomena which consists in a precise determination of emotional reactions of the vasomotor system to various kinds of influences. He understood that achieving this

144

The Teaching about Emotions

goal is still far in the future and saw his problem only in indicating where its solution is to be found. More than a half century has passed since that time. Investigators of human emotions looked to solve the problem mainly in the direction indicated by Lange. The result of the search was provided in experiments of Sherrington and Cannon and in clinical observations of Wilson, Dana, and Head. Cannon formulated the result thus: it may be thought that bodily conditions which can make it possible to distinguish some emotions from others, as some psychologists proposed, are not suitable for this purpose, that these conditions must be found somewhere, but not in the internal organs (w. B. Cannon, 1927). The variety of bodily changes with various emotions seemed to Lange to be truly enormous. He assumed that a number of different combinations represented by various emotions would be derived from this. "Since we are dealing with three different muscle systems of which each can probably be excited in various ways, and sometimes only one or two of these can represent innervation disturbances, evidently we can count 127 different combinations for physical expression of emotion if we take into consideration even just innervation disturbances alone" (c. Lange, 1896, p. 46). As we have seen, expectations were not fated to be realized. Continuous 60year efforts directed toward finding various combinations not only did not disclose the 127 whose existence Lange assumed, but showed that for the basic lower emotions he described, there was evidently a uniform, standard, stereotypic formula of bodily emotional manifestations. Emotions most opposite from the psychological aspect have strikingly similar bodily manifestations. As recent investigators of the old theory, for example, Dunlap, have said, it seems that this might have been expected from the very beginning. Uniformity of organic changes could have been predicted on the basis of the James-Lange theory itself with the help of purely analytical scrutiny. The difference between emotions would have to be less significant than the similarity. According to the new variant of the organic theory, emotion does not at all display the infinite variety of form and transitions of which the creators of the theory spoke. It seems to be no more and no less than a simple, dynamic background, a uniform background, against which mental processes are played out. Precisely the same fate overtook the James theory: the same great hopes and the same complete sterility. James was not modest in his expectations. He assumed that he had caught the firebird with the golden feathers or, in the language of the English fairy tale, the goose that laid the golden egg. None of his predecessors had grasped what was most important: the fruitful guiding beginning, the basic point of view, the logical generalization. The new theory yielded this in one formula. The recipe for the appearance of emotion was the same in all cases and simple to the same degree. It seemed that the factual discoveries in the area of emotions had to follow one after the other, and of what was found, had to make the key to an ultimately fruitful guiding beginning. But the productive beginning developed to be as unproductive as the biblical fig tree. James did not ascribe great significance to establishing the difference between emotions and their classification. In his eyes, this was a simple auxiliary means that must become clear in itself once the common principle was discovered. He even laughed over the respectable compilation of catalogues of various features of emotions, their degrees, and the actions they elicited: all of this was necessary before we had at hand the common recipe for all emotions. James, let us repeat, was not modest in his expectations. He assumed that in the teaching on emotions, his theory would playa role like the role played by the idea of evolution in biology since both

The Teaching about Emotions

145

considered difference in species as a product of more general principles. For him, his own hypothesis seemed to be of Darwinian scale. For this reason, it is natural that concrete analysis of emotions and factual description of their features could not interest him. This was a matter for the future. The main thing was the principle. "If we already have the goose that lays the golden eggs, then describing separately each egg that is laid is a matter of secondary importance" (w. James, 1902, p. 303). About the gold-bearing principle of James, we might repeat literally what we have said about expectations connected with the theory of Lange. Continuous 60year efforts led to nothing. 10 describe each separately laid egg was an impossible task. We would find it difficult to name another hypothesis equally fruitless factually which was sustained in science for so many years. Without speaking of the higher, complex, fine specifically human affects, or even in recognizing such more obvious forms of emotion as anger, fear, love, envy, joy, sorrow, shame, and pride (if only to present the list compiled by James himself), we would not have taken even a step forward with the help of the new gold-bearing principle. Thus far, everything turns on consideration of the principle itself. The path from the goose that lays the golden eggs to describing each egg laid was impossible. Actually, there were no eggs. Thus far, the merits and advantages of this most unusual goose are described by various methods. James promised that with the help of his hypothesis, we would be able to rise above the level of concrete descriptions in analyzing emotions. He assumed that the point of view he developed would explain the remarkable variety of emotions and that it would make it possible to find a way out of the area of simple description and classification. Instead of describing external traits, scientific investigation would be able to take on explaining the causes of emotions. He said: "from a superficial analysis of emotions, we will thus pass on to a more profound study, to a study of a higher order. Classification and description are the lower steps in the development of science. As soon as the question of the causal connections in a given scientific area of investigation appears on the stage, classification and description move to a secondary plane and retain their significance only to the extent that they help us in the study of the causal connection" (ibid., p. 314). Probably now there is not one follower of the theory who would defend the idea that over the course of the 60 years since the day this theory was published, we advanced at all significantly in the analysis of causal connections in the sphere of emotional life, that we actually passed on to investigation of a higher order and were able to explain even a little of the infinite variety of emotions, discovering the nature of which James expected,-more briefly, the proverbial goose had not laid even one golden egg. Worse still, even in the area of investigation of a lower order, in the area, so scorned by James, of concrete description and explanation of special features and specific action of each emotion, in the area of classification and nomenclature, there was no possibility of movement in the development of scientific knowledge with the help of the new principle. This cannot but have a common cause. We think that it should be sought in the theoretical lack of structure and the inadequacy of the gold-bearing principle. Actually, from the very beginning, it proposes as an explanation something so foreign with respect to the mental nature of emotions, something that lies on a completely different methodological plane and, consequently, something incapable under any circumstances of serving as an answer to the question of the causal connection of emotional processes. The principle developed by Lange and James is not capable in its essence of discovering any kind of sensible connection between the mental nature of a given emotion and the organic sensations that it elicits. The hasic spirit of the teaching consists of acknowledging the complete and basic mean-

146

The Teaching about Emotions

inglessness of human emotions, the basic impossibility not only of capturing and understanding the structure corresponding to its experience, its functional connection with all the rest of the life of consciousness, its mental nature, but even of formulating the question of what a given emotion represents as a certain mental state. Here we have touched on the most essential basic question in all the criticism of the James-Lange theory, and thus, also of Cartesian teaching, the question that thus far has been left without attention. This is also the fundamental question of our investigation and the fundamental question in all of the teaching of Spinoza on the passions. For this reason, we must take time to explain it. James said: "If we do not experience bodily excitation at the sight of a fair or generous deed, then our mental state can scarcely be termed an emotion. De facto here we have simply an intellectual perception of a phenomenon that we classify as the fair and the generous, etc. Similar states involving a simple judgment should be referred to cognitive rather than to emotional, mental processes" (ibid., p. 317). It would be difficult to present in a clearer form the idea of the complete meaninglessness of all feeling. Of course, according to the theory of James, peripheral bodily excitation perceived by our consciousness does constitute the essence of emotion. Without it, feeling stops being feeling and is converted into a simple judgment. One might ask: what does it mean to maintain that since a feeling of fairness and generosity is specifically a feeling and not a simple judgment, it is apparently nothing other than a sensation of peripheral bodily excitation of a certain type and in a certain combination of elements if not to make a feeling of fairness and generosity essentially meaningless? In a feeling of moral fairness, what can explain for us the fact that it, in the words of James, is reflected in the sound of the voice or in the expression of the eyes? James promised that his hypothesis would bring us to an investigation of a higher order. From the old point of view, the only possible problems in the analysis of emotions were classification (to what family or species does a given emotion belong) or description (what kind of external manifestations characterize a given emotion). Now, however, we are concerned with explaining the causes of emotions: specifically what kind of modification does one object or another elicit in us and why does it elicit specifically those and not other modifications in us. Leaving aside the finer emotions such as fairness and generosity, we will consider those lower forms of which James is speaking all the time. We will ask ourselves what can be the psychological value of a causal explanation which we construct from James' own words that corresponds to the completely precise meaning of his example: "Why do we experience a feeling of horror at the thought of the death of a being dear to us?"-"Because we sense a feeling connected with an increased heart rate, shortness of breath, trembling of the lips, weakness of the extremities, goose bumps, and with the excitation of our internal organs." No one has yet gone into the matter of how this leads to the philosophical nature of the renowned formula of James, which presents a classical prototype of every causal explanation of human feelings. Otherwise its enormous incongruity would have been noted long ago. Actually, what does the following position mean from the point of view of a causal explanation: we cry because we are sad; we fight because we become furious; we tremble because we experience fright? Is it not completely clear that from the point of view of a real explanation of psychological facts, this formula has the same cognitive value as the statement: Socrates sat in prison because the muscles of his legs contracted and extended and thus brought him there?

The Teaching about Emotions

147

E. Spranger,82 an eminent representative of contemporary descriptive psychology, cites this famous Platonic example of the enormous incongruity of the causal explanation in order to disclose the untenability of the so-called natural-science, causal, explanatory psychology. Like the whole psychological tendency that he represents, he proceeds from the point that explanatory psychology itself, better than any of its opponents, showed the impossibility of causal explanations in psychological investigation since it forgot the basic position: psychology must be developed with a psychological method. No matter how obvious the complete untenability of the purely idealistic conclusion made by descriptive psychology on the basis of more fundamental fallacies of explanatory psychological analysis is, it cannot minimize in our eyes the significance and correctness of the basic critical objection of Spranger addressed to explanatory psychology of the type presented in the James theory, an objection relative to the logical impossibility of causal explanations, examples of which are presented above. We shall return again to the problem of explanatory and descriptive psychology of feelings-the problem, central in a certain sense, to all of our investigation-and we shall consider what kind of basic solution to this problem we find in the teaching of Spinoza on the passions. But even now we cannot but reach certain substantial conclusions. We cannot but agree with the fact that a causal explanation of psychological facts, as it was usually developed in psychology, as it is represented in the James theory, as it flows directly from the very sense of Cartesian teaching on the passions, can be brought to nothing other than to the admission of the complete untenability and impossibility of such explanatory psychology. If a causal explanation different from the explanation given above is impossible in psychology, then explanatory psychology as a science is itself impossible. W. Dilthey,83 one of the first to recognize the enormous absurdity of this kind of explanatory psychology and one of the first to take a stand on the path of purely idealistic psychology as a science of self-originated phenomena, correctly said: "By this, however, the bankruptcy of independent explanatory psychology is proclaimed. Its concerns pass into the hands of physiology" (1924, p. 34). But to the same extent that Dilthey, Spranger, and other partisans of descriptive teleological psychology are undoubtedly right in criticizing explanatory psychology of this kind as the psychology of emotions of James, they expose by not an iota less the whole shakiness and untenability of the idea they are defending of a purely descriptive psychology devoid of any causal explanations. They show not only with full clarity, but even with some ideational cynicism and shamelessness, that descriptive psychology lives only through the failures of explanatory psychology. Teleological consideration of psychological facts arises as a logical conclusion from errors of causal analysis. Idealistic psychology is indispen~able primarily because materialistic psychology was not equal to the tasks confronting it, went bankrupt, and yielded its concerns to physiology. Thus, the partisans of descriptive psychology, seemingly standing at a diametrically opposite point of view and justifiably ridiculing the inconsistencies of causal analysis of the I~ssentially explanatory Cartesian psychology, themselves not only have not moved '1ery far from those theoretical assumptions that inevitably lead to these absurdities, hut wholly and completely share and adopt the same assumptions. Essentially, in its explanatory assumptions, descriptive psychology is much more similar to the old explanatory psychology than is apparent at first glance and more than Dilthey and Spranger would probably have wanted. Moreover, their psychology is wholly based on the same theoretical positions as is the causal psychology they rejected. They are not in the least opponents, but rather, twins. Of course, descrip-

148

The Teaching about Emotions

tive psychology also proceeds from the idea that in psychology, the only possible explanation is an explanation that sees the cause for Socrates sitting in prison to be the muscular contractions of his legs. uue, representatives of descriptive psychology even admit to a certain degree the normality of this kind of explanation for a more limited area of elementary mental phenomena. They only require a natural amplification of this kind of explanation, specifically a teleological, descriptive analysis of higher manifestations of the human spirit. They not only do not reject the right to existence of this kind of explanatory psychology, but even admit that it is necessary together with descriptive psychology. "Nature we explain; mental life we understand" (W Dilthey, 1924, p. 8)-this position of Dilthey, basic for all cognitive psychology, determines the necessary delimitation of the spheres of influence and the area of mutual cooperation of causal and teleological, explanatory and descriptive psychology.84 Mental life has a natural aspect and is subject to natural-science study and causal analysis. This is the task of explanatory or physiological psychology. But not one explanatory psychology that exists at present can form the basis for sciences of the spirit. It is not in a state to give either a sufficient explanation or even a description of complex higher mental processes specific to man. For this reason, together with it, there must be cognitive, structural, teleological, and descriptive psychology. Explanatory psychology as a system cannot now nor in the future lead to an objective knowledge of the connections of mental phenomena. Specifically the absence of any intelligent, understood connection between feeling, reduced to sensing goose bumps and extended nostrils, and all the rest of mental life comprises the most distinctive trait of the explanatory psychology of emotions developed by James, as we have seen. For this reason, recognizing the connections of these phenomena must be the subject of a special science. But this special science not only implies eliminating the old explanatory psychology, but even, according to Dilthey's idea, providing for it the possibility of further productive development. Thus, a close cooperation on the basis of division of labor and sphere of knowledge between explanatory and descriptive psychology is established. The internal relationship of the two seemingly opposite conceptions is far from accidental. Of necessity, the one infers the other. The one cannot exist without the other. Only together do they form a complete whole. Who said "a" must say "b" as well. Who admits only the one possibility of an anecdotal, causal explanation of psychology inevitably must come to rejecting causal psychology and to creating teleological psychology. The one and the other grow from the same root: from the philosophy of Descartes. It is constructed with complete symmetry, with complete ideational equilibrium of mechanistic and spiritualistic principles. Nowhere is this duality disclosed as distinctly as in the teaching on the passions considered as the only manifestation of the joint life of the spirit and the body, consequently, as phenomena that are subject to explanation from the point of view of laws of mechanics and principles of teleology. The body is nothing other than a complex machine, and since passions reflect the bodily nature of man, they must be explained according to laws of mechanics. The soul, however, is a divine thing and for this reason, its life must be treated teleologically: to Caesar what is Caesar's, and to God what is God's. Thus, the idea of explanatory and descriptive psychology is already contained a priori in the teachings of Descartes on passions. Admitting complete lack of structure and absolute meaninglessness of passion considered purely mechanically, as we have seen, inevitably brought the James-Lange theory to a series of insurmountable difficulties and incongruities, but they are in essence also contained in Cartesian teaching. Naturally, if the new and the old theories correspond in the

The Teaching about Emotions

149

most important theoretical point, they inevitably must be confronted by completely identical difficulties to the extent that their development is logical. Actually, it is most instructive to recognize that history repeated itself after two centuries and, in this respect, with surprising precision. The Treatise on Passions . .. is full of descriptions of various movements of spirits and organs: expansion and contraction of the heart, differences in size, number, and velocity of blood particles and spirits, change in the stomach and lungs-in the expression of Sergi, Descartes juggles all of this precisely as James juggles the description of goose bumps and extension of the nostrils. Thue, Descartes recognizes the difficulty of the task. He shares his doubts with Princess Elizabeth. 85 He says it is not easy to study organic phenomena that correspond to every passion because they are usually mixed. We must separate facts and look for precise results based on statistics, comparisons, and elimination. For example, if we turn to cases in which love is coupled with joy, we can recognize neither the one nor the other of these passions. But if we compare love-joy with love-sorrow, the differences appear distinctly. Thus, the treatise directly proposes an experimental continuation that Descartes himself tried and could not accomplish because of the lack of laboratory equipment and workers. He was forced to use facts accessible to his observation. As Sergi delicately notes, regardless of the fact that Descartes introduced a number of refinements into our knowledge of the pulse that were somewhat enriched by the contemporary sphygmogram, it would be better to remain silent about what Descartes says with respect to the visceral picture corresponding to every emotion. But the point is not in these pictures. Descartes plows more deeply. The point is the theoretical direction of the investigation. He had to discover the cause of the movements of the spirits that he described for the basic passions. This cause turns out to be very simple. With love and sorrow, the stomach exhibits significant activity in digestion. With hatred and joy, on the contrary, this activity decreases. Why? Because our first passions have an alimentary origin. They are passions connected with feeding and they were formed around the digestive tract. Their further sublimation and their history is only an added structure over this immobile physiological base of the first days of our existence: the mechanism of the passions of the adult person has its prototype in the structure and functioning of the machine of the fetus. This is, perhaps, the only place in the teaching on passions where Descartes appears on the path that leads to a historical explanation. As naive as they might seem to the contemporary view, the theoretical significance of this turning to the history of development as the source of an explanation, together with physiological mechanics, deserves concentrated attention. We shall consider this aspect of the matter later, but we must note at the very outset that Descartes formulates the problem of causality with respect to passions in completely the same way as does James. James says that we must involve ourselves in explaining how one expression of fear or anger or another could appear and what constitutes, on the one hand, the problem of physiological mechanics, and on the other, the problem of the history of the human mind. We might have thought that James, like Sergi, simply states the solution of the problem of causality he found ready in Des,~artes.

But as with the laws of physiological mechanics, so with the history of the human mind, the matter appears to be identically hopeless. Now, however, randomness, lack of structure, and meaninglessness of connections between emotions and organic changes appear in the foreground and call attention to themselves whenever we are concerned with the methodological possibility itself of a factual investigation arising from these principles. Descartes connects hunger with sadness

150

The Teaching about Emotions

and anorexia with joy. Elizabeth protests. Descartes yields and agrees to a completely opposite grouping: a full stomach produces sadness, an empty stomach, joy. The pity is not that the factual representations of Descartes were devoid of any empirical basis and for this reason could, at the first whim of the princess, be replaced with the opposite, the pity is that methodologically, with the admission of complete meaninglessness of the connection between emotion and its organic expression, any connection becomes identically possible. The one is no more understandable than the other. The second is just as probable as the first. Sergi understands this. He notes pensively that if joy is connected with either anorexia or with hunger, we can no longer say: this kind of visceral state means this kind of passion. The whole structure appears compromised. It is not our intention to study the strong and weak aspects of the visceral theory of passions. We shall note only that James, Lange, and Cannon are also confronted by difficulties of this kind, difficulties that are met only along the path of very well-developed theories and not of intuitions of forecasters. Thus, the laws of mechanics seem to serve the new theory. They betray it at the first attempt to entrust them with guiding the course of factual investigation. They do not even make it possible to construct any kind of factual hypothesis. They can with equal ease and arbitrariness explain the one thing and the other. The laws of physiological mechanics, to which both Descartes and James appeal identically, seem to be completely the same as the rules of law about which the saying is, roughly: the law is like a tow bar, wherever you turn it, there it will go. But in this sterility of mechanistic laws in explaining human passions, it is not difficult to foresee the sterility that will, after more than 200 years, overtake the goose that promises to lay golden eggs and has laid not even one. But, perhaps, the laws of the history of the human mind will prove to be kinder with respect to the visceral theory? Perhaps here we shall find a more firmly based causal explanation that will not be turning, like the tow bar, in opposite directions? From the words of Dumas, we already know that Lange was developing his theory as a counterweight to evolutionary psychology and that he, like Descartes, had an aversion to historical explanations; nevertheless, we find in him a most instructive attempt to answer at least theoretically one of the basic problems of the whole teaching on the passions-the problem of development. Once again we have before us a question of greatest importance. C. G. Lange begins and ends the investigation with reference to I. Kant, thus putting the whole treatise in a seemingly philosophical frame. And actually, in this beginning and end, we find a distinct expression of the second philosophical idea of the visceral theory, which together with the first-with the principles of the mechanical scientific worldview-defines the philosophical basis of the whole theory. In a strange way, Lange begins with a sharp objection to Kant and closes with complete agreement with him (c. Lange, 1896, p. 80). It is indeed striking how Lange himself did not notice the screaming contradiction between the beginning and the end of his discussion. As if he himself did not notice it, he comes to rejecting what he affirmed when he began. The theory that he develops seemingly has its own logic independent of the logic of its author. It leads him in a direction exactly opposite the one he set out for. The story of the bear who leads the hunter who caught him where he, the bear, wants to go is repeated. The history of Descartes and the princess repeats itself. But this time, what must yield is not in the area of facts, flexible and pliable, obedient and subject more to laws of worldly courtesy than to the true laws of mechanics, but in the area of philosophical realization of these facts, in their basic interpretation.

The Teaching about Emotions

151

Lange begins his psychophysiological study On Movements of the Spirit with: "Kant, at one point in his Anthropology,86 defines affects as diseases of the soul" (ibid., p. 13). "It seems that to the great thinker, the soul is well only as long as it is under the unconditional and undisputed control of the power of the mind; everything that can shake that power is, in his eyes, something abnormal, harmful for man. '~ more real psychology which does not know the abstract, ideal man but 'takes him as he is' must consider such science of the soul as strange, and as pitiful the representation of man that considers joy and grief, compassion and anger, humility and pride as mental states foreign to the sound man, abnormal states, to which attention should not be given when the goal is to become familiar with the true essence of human nature" (ibid.). C. G. Lange assumes that to agree with Kant means to limit the sphere of the life of our spirit, if the talent for bowing to the great, being moved by the beautiful, feeling compassion for the unfortunate is to be considered a symptom of illness, then for us, the healthy, normal person would be only a passionless accounting clerk for whom every new impression provides only a reason for a conclusion. To Lange astonishingly strange is a view of the mutual relationship of mental powers that wants to see something random in a phenomenon that plays a much more significant role in the mental life of people than sound judgment and guides the fate not only of individuals, but also of nations and all of humanity to a greater degree than does sound judgment. If, as Kant assumed, affects actually are only diseases of the soul, who would want to cure his sick soul, asks Lange, if a cure would deprive man of all his emotions that make him capable of sympathizing with his neighbors, sharing their joy and grief, being elated by them or hating them? Lange presents as unquestionable that "we cannot consider a person as healthy, whole, and real if he knows only how to think, recognize, and judge, but cannot suffer, be joyful, and fight, although, perhaps, these passions in some way interfere with his ability to investigate and judge. "Emotions not only play the role of most important factors in the life of an individual, but are, in general, the most powerful of the natural forces known to us. Every page of history-of whole nations as well as of individuals-accords them invincible power. The storms of passions ruined more human lives and destroyed more countries than hurricanes. More cities were demolished by their torrents than by floods" (ibid., p. 14). For this reason, Lange refuses to see simply an abnormality and a sickness, as Kant does, in affects, those greatest of all forces that have at the same time an enormous significance for our internal life (ibid.). It would seem that after these clear, transparent, emotional, and, strictly speaking, beautiful words, we might expect that in his study, Lange would disclose for us the essence of these most powerful of all natural forces known to us that have such an enormous significance in the history of whole nations and personalties, guiding not only the fate of separate individuals, but of all of humanity, and thereby would show, first, by virtue of what and how specifically passions can have such a ?aramount significance in the life of man, and, second, how in a sound and real nan, not only is the capacity to suffer, be joyful, and fight not eliminated as a random and abnormal phenomenon, but how it grows and develops together with the history of humanity and the development of the internal life of man. But Lange disappoints our expectations and hopes as unambiguously and as ardently as he aroused them earlier. As we know, the basic result of Lange's investigation is the position that all emotional moments of our mental life, our joys and sorrows, our happy and un-

152

The Teaching about Emotions

happy days, we owe to the vasomotor system, more precisely, to its peripheral changes. This basic idea is accompanied by two secondary ideas that disclose its methodological content. It seems that from the point of view of laws of physiological mechanics and from the point of view of the development of the human mind, there is an antagonism between the intellectual and the affective life of man. This antagonism allows us to explain in greater detail the fate of affect in the life and development of the personality. Intellectual life itself also is dependent on vasomotor functions, although in a somewhat different way than the life of feelings. Intellectual activity presupposes an increased supply of blood to the brain and is facilitated by it, and in this case, of course, blood enters parts of the brain other than those that are aroused mainly by emotions. In a certain sense, the nature of the connections between vasomotor activity and intellectual life on the one hand and affects on the other is opposite. The first affects the second in a literal sense of the word in a derivative, blood-diverting way, and when Herman von Bremen counts to 20, by this insignificant mental work, he takes so much blood away from the motor part of his brain that all desire to fight with his wife is lost. Such is the first idea, which amplifies the basic position of Lange (ibid., p. 79). The second establishes the same antagonism between intellectual and affective life in the course of development. Lange says: "Education acts in the same direction. The purpose of rearing, which must not be confused with education, is to train the personality to control, overcome, and eliminate those motives that are the result of direct action of our physical organization, but that are not in accord with accepted social relations. From the physiological point of view, education can be considered as the development of the capability of suppressing the simpler initial reflexes or of replacing them with the higher. Thus, we are trained from earliest childhood to control the other reflexes that are not acceptable in proper society" (ibid., p. 79). From this point of view, Lange compares the fate of emotional reaction to the fate of reflexes of the urinary bladder: the rod trains a child not to cry from disappointment as a result of emotional spasm of the vessels in the same way that it trains him not to behave sloppily as a result of involuntary functions of reflexes (ibid.). In the opposition between the intellect and affect and in the gradual displacement of feelings as intellectual development progresses, Lange sees the basic law that is confirmed not only in ontogenesis, but also in the development of humanity as a whole. "History itself condemns the life of feeling to gradual fading away and to almost complete demise. Emotions are a dying breed that is gradually being displaced on the stage of history as civilization and culture grow." Excitability of the vasomotor apparatus differs greatly in different people. In this respect, there are not only individual, frequently hereditary differences. Conditions of a more general character not infrequently play an extremely important role here. Women have much readier emotions than the stronger gender owing to a. strong excitability of the nervous system, particularly its vasomotor section. The same thing is noted in children compared to adults. The general rule is that, like individuals, whole nations are more subject to emotions the lower their level of education. The so-called wild peoples are more irascible and indomitable, more unbridled in their joy, more depressed by their grief than civilized peoples. The same kind of difference is noted between different generations of one and the same tribe. We are more peaceful and gentle as compared to our barbaric predecessors who got greater pleasure in yielding to reckless outbursts and bellicose fury, but who fell so easily into dejection at any failure that they killed themselves because of trifles (ibid., pp. 77-78).

The Teaching about Emotions

153

Among people of a single generation, Lange finds the same law manifested: the most obvious trait of being educated is the calm self-control with which blows of fate are borne that in uneducated people evoke unbridled outbursts of passion. And as if to leave not the slightest doubt that historical development of the human mind leads to extinction of emotion, Lange formulates the law of causal relation between the one and the other: "This suppression of the affective aspect of life under the influence of increasing education, both in individual personalities and in whole generations, not only goes hand in hand with increasing development of the intellectual aspect of life, but is, for the most part, the result of this development" (ibid., p. 78). In addition to establishing this position, Lange finds himself unexpectedly confronted by a definitive result that is in irreconcilable contradiction to his original point. In truth, he started with being for health and ends by being for burial. He began with sharp objections to the thesis of Kant, whose views of affects as a sickness of the soul Lange called a pitiful representation of man; he ends with a full capitulation to this thesis, to the view of mutual relations of mental forces that wants to see something random in the phenomenon which plays a much more significant role than sound judgment in the mental life of the basic mass of people, and which guides the fate not only of individual persons, but also of whole nations and all of humanity to a much greater degree than does sound judgment. The logic of the investigation was stronger than the logic of the investigator. The bear stubbornly leads the hunter. For Lange, it remains only to admit this and to proceed to full capitulation to Kant, which he does in the concluding lines of his study. He says: "With the passage of time, as a result of constant restraint and inadequate harnessing, the vasomotor centers continuously lose the energy of their emotional activity. This result of training the intellectual life is conveyed through heredity to subsequent generations. New generations appear in the world with an ever more slack emotional innervation of vessels and with a stronger innervation of organs of intellectual activity. "If our development will continue along the direction adopted, in the final analysis, we will attain Kant's ideal: a purely rational man will appear for whom all emotions: joy and sorrow, anxiety and fear-should he be subject to such enticements-will become only illnesses or mental disturbances equally unseemly for him" (ibid., pp. 77-80). The whole investigation of Lange concludes on this note of hopelessness. The result to which we have come is of no small importance in our eyes. Now ~e realize very clearly how the problem of development of affects is solved in the theory we have been considering. Lange had the courage to follow to the end the logic of the development of his basic idea, taking it to the limit and disclosing in chis way its true philosophical essence. James, more careful in this regard, leaves :lgreat lack of clarity at this point. However, as we have seen, even he appeals to the history of the human mind together with laws of physiological mechanics. Consequently, he not only cannot avoid the problem of development, but regards as a merit of his theory the fact that this problem is brought to the forefront there. As we recall, James equates the basic principle of his theory, that celebrated goose that lays golden eggs, with the evolutionary idea of Darwin. For this reason, the answer that James gives to the question of the origin of emotional reactions cannot he neutral for us. Considering this question, James cites the "surmises of Spencer ... that found confirmation on the part of other scientists." James writes, "He was also, as far as I know, the first scientist to formulate the hypothesis that many movements in fear c.nd anger can be considered as rudimentary vestiges of movements that were in-

154

The Teaching about Emotions

itially positive" (W James, 1902 p. 336). Explanations given by Darwin, P. Mantegazza,87 and W Wundt, which James cites, take the same tack (ibid., pp. 337-338). To the same extent, James cites another principle developed by Wundt and T. Piderit88 (1886), a principle to which Darwin, perhaps, did not do full justice. It consists of similar reaction to similar perceptible stimuli (W James, 1902, p. 338). According to the latter principle, we may, as Wundt does, for example, consider "many of the most expressive reactions to moral motives as symbolically used expressions of gustatory impressions. These are all the mental states that language metaphorically designates as bitter, sour, or sweet and that are characterized by mimetic movements of the mouth that represent a similarity to expressions corresponding to gustatory impressions" (ibid., p. 338). But both principles, even taken together, cannot satisfy James. From a somewhat different aspect, but in a surprising way, his own response to the question of the origin of emotional reactions coincides with the solution of the problem of development that we found in Lange. James says that if some of our emotional reactions "can be explained by the two principles we indicated (the reader has probably already had the opportunity to be convinced how problematical and artificial the explanation of very many cases is in this case), there are still, nevertheless, many emotional reactions that cannot at all be explained and must be considered by us at present as purely idiopathic reactions to external stimulations ... It is the opinion of Spencer and Mantegazza that shivering observed not only in fear but also with many other excitations is a purely pathological phenomenon. These and other strong symptoms of fear are harmful to the life of those experiencing them. In a complex organism like the nervous system, there must be many random reactions. These reactions could not have developed completely independently due to only one usefulness they might provide for the organism. Seasickness, sensitivity to tickling, embarrassment, love of music, and tendency toward various intoxicating drinks must have arisen by chance. It would be absurd to maintain that not one of the emotional reactions could have arisen by chance" (ibid., p. 339). No matter how the explanation of James differs from the theory of Lange on the antagonism between intellectual and affective life according to concrete content, the theories coincide with each other fully in two basic and decisive points like two equal geometric figures when superimposed on one another. The first coincidence consists of the contradiction between the basic assumption and the definitive conclusion to which both authors come in the same way and to the same extent. We have just taken the opportunity to explain the appearance of this contradiction in Lange. In James, it is disclosed and appears in the same unmasked form since it requires no effort to see it. On the contrary, a great deal of optimistic trust in this theory is required for the flagrant contradiction not to be noticed. As we recall, with his theory, James promised to raise scientific investigation of emotions to the level of profound investigation, investigation of the highest order. He disdainfully rejects classification and description as lower steps in the development of science. With his productive guiding principle, he promises to discover the way to explaining the causal dependence of emotional reactions. He believes physiological mechanics to be the source of this causal explanation. In this respect, he was seriously misled and was confronted by the full factual sterility of explaining emotions causally and distinguishing them from this aspect. Another source remains-the history of the human mind. James considers the problem of a historical causal explanation of emotions to be essentially solvable, but finding its solution to be difficult. However, we have just had the opportunity to see that as soon as

The Teaching about Emotions

155

the matter reaches factual realization of this hope, it bursts like a soap bubble. Explanations derived from the principles of Spencer, Darwin, and Wundt are problematical and artificial. But, the main thing is that there are many emotional reactions that simply cannot be explained. These are literally the words of James himself. . Thus, the problem of a historical explanation of emotions, despite optimistic expectations, seems to be unsolvable, as was already clear to James. The 60 years that have passed since his pessimistic conclusion wholly confirmed this. Scarcely at any other point did the James theory prove itself as it did specifically here. Emotional reactions must be considered, James says, as purely idiopathic reactions, that is, inexplicable as reactions to external stimulation. Their biological significance is not only problematical, but very frequently must be absolutely rejected. These are random reactions that generally do not yield to causal, historical explanation. One may ask: what then remains of the illusion of an investigation of a higher order? What remains except classification and description besides a superficial analysis of emotions, besides describing their external traits? The most clever theory, evidently, cannot yield more than it contains. The second coincidence of the two independently developed variants of a single theory pertains to the essence of solving the problem of development. Lange had to surrender to the mercy of his victor, Kant, and admit after him that affects are nothing other than sicknesses of the soul. James also is inclined to consider affects as pathological phenomena, harmful to the beings experiencing them. Lange gloomily tells of the fate of passions' dying breed. James is also forced to consider them as rudimentary vestiges that were initially useful, but deteriorated in the course of development and were converted into useless, meaningless adjuncts of our mental apparatus connected in no way with the rest of its activity. Whoever begins with theoretical meaninglessness of emotions will inevitably come to recognizing the absurdity of the only right they have to exist. But from beginning to end of the investigation, the absurdity of all emotional life grows and gradually increases with each new step in the development of the theory, reaching a truly bombastic force by the end-in admitting emotions to be rudimentary, pathological, random, and inexplicable phenomena. Whoever sows wind will inevitably reap a storm. It remains for us to explain the last circumstance connected with the problem of development of emotions as it is formulated and resolved by the James-Lange theory: it remains for us to explain only the internal necessity, the logical inevitability of specifically such a formulation and specifically such a solution. Of course, the point is not in the aversion to historical explanations which, in the words of Dumas, every representative of the mechanistic worldview must try, following the steps of Descartes. At the end of the nineteenth century, after Darwin, aversion could scarcely explain for us the inability of any kind of empirical theory to solve the problem of development. As we have seen, Lange and James wanted very much to find the key to a historical explanation of emotions. However, they could not do this just as the great Descartes, the founder of the visceral theory of emotions, wanted to answer this question and could not. Obviously, the anti-historical tendency that paralyzed all attempts of investigators moving in that direction was established in the logic of the theory itself. Their good intentions were shattered ~very time on the internal, impenetrable nucleus of their own theory. This nucleus is actually absolutely anti-historical. In its very essence, it excludes completely any possibility of the history of human emotions. As we know, the nudeus of the whole theory consists of the idea that the source and actual cause of ,~motions are reflexive, peripheral changes in internal organs and the muscle system. Thus, the nucleus of the theory is immediately overgrown by two dense and im-

156

The Teaching about Emotions

penetrable ideational envelopes inseparable from it. The first rises of itself from the immutable fact that bodily manifestations taken for the true cause, for the actual essence of the emotional reaction, are more perceptible the lower the emotion with which we are dealing. Consequently, the more primitive, the lower the stage of development at which the emotion is, the more archaic it is, the more it will exhibit traits of true passion. Thus, according to the sense of the basic position of the theory, emotions must be referred to the most remote, prehistorical, pre human period of mental evolution. In man, they appear only in the role of rudiments, meaningless vestiges of a dark legacy of animal ancestors. In the history of the human mind, not only is any prospect of the development of emotions impossible, but, on the contrary, they are condemned to a gradual regression and, in the final analysis, to dying. Bodily manifestations that form the essence of emotions are immeasurably richer, brighter, and more palpable in animals than in man; in primitive man-than in cultured man; in a child-than in an adult. Of what kind of development but of reverse development, of reduction, can we be speaking with respect to emotions? Their evolution is nothing other than involution. Their history is a history of their atrophy and loss. Thus, the very understanding of development is not applicable to emotions and is impossible in the area of their investigation if we accept the basic assumption of the visceral hypothesis. As we have seen, following the logic of their own theory, both Lange and James are forced to come to this in the same way. The second envelope that covers the nucleus of their theory arises from the separation of emotion from all of our consciousness; this is part of the very nucleus of the theory. By separating emotions from the brain, taking them out to the periphery, reducing them to peripheral changes in the internal organs and muscles, the theory thereby hypothetically creates for them a substrate of residual consciousness. Of course, the internal organs-the heart, stomach, lungs-are that part of the human organism that cannot in any way, from the point of view of its participation in the historical development of man, be compared with the central nervous system, particularly with the cortex of the brain. The historical development of human consciousness is connected primarily with the development of the cortex of the brain. It is understood that this does not in any way mean that the organism as a whole and all its separate parts did not participate at all in evolution. However, there can scarcely be any doubt that when we speak of the historical development of human consciousness, we have in mind, primarily and mainly, specifically the cortex of the brain as the material basis of development, which in this respect qualitatively stands apart from all the other parts of the organism, being in the closest and most direct way connected with the mental development of man. In any case, this position is universally accepted for all higher, specifically human functions of consciousness. The peripheral theory of emotions, seeing their source in the activity of internal organs-those parts of the organism historically most immutable, unchanging, most removed from the direct organic basis of the historical development of consciousness-thus tears emotions away from the general context of the mental development of man and places them in an isolated position. They appear to be an island separated from the basic continent and surrounded on all sides by a sea of purely vegetative and animal, purely organic processes in the context of which they attain their true significance. Is it surprising after this that bodily manifestations comprising, according to the visceral theory, the very essence of emotional reactions, seem to be more similar to vegetative disturbances such as we observe in cold, fever, or asphyxia than to emotional states such as fear and anger? The localization itself of

The Teaching about Emotions

157

the source of emotions, from which the specific quality of feeling originates, outside the brain at the periphery, already assumes excluding affects from all of that complex of connections, from all of that system of relations, from all of that functional structure that comprise the true subject of the mental development of man. Thus, this position, contained in the nucleus itself of the theory, is covered by a new envelope to the same extent as the first, separating the theory from the problem of development. It is most strange that very little attention is given to this circumstance. The biological semblance of the theory gave rise to the illusion that it not only does not contradict the evolutionary idea in psychology, but directly suggests it. In contemporary psychology, only isolated voices are heard criticizing the theory from this aspect. Thus, Brett correctly states that "in all of literature about emotions, the most attention is paid to the endosomatic reactions and, thus, obviously, only one aspect of emotion as a whole is detected. "When we turn from experimental investigation to clinical, it seems to us that we have fallen into an entirely different world. We get the impression that a strict distinction must be drawn between emotions as they are usually portrayed and the kind of experiences that clinical psychology describes. The influence of zoopsychology and physiological schools intensely clouds the question of the possibility of development of emotions. There are no a priori data that would explain for us why emotions must not develop. This assumption seems to be a simple misunderstanding. If emotions develop, then the most obvious error consists in not differentiating separate levels of development. And this is the worst result of the interpretation that is usually given in theories like the James-Lange theory. Specifically because they are indisputably right to a certain point, they fall into error as soon as they go beyond this point. Many authors use the word biological incorrectly. It is completely true that instincts and their visceral accompaniment are what they are due to their biological meaning. But strictly speaking, this meaning is known only to the theoretician. Of course, an animal does not exhibit fright or fury for the same reason that self-preservation is the first law of life. The word biological, if we want to use it in its special meaning, signifies the relation between any act and its consequences for the individual or the genus. This relation does not yet make up a part of behavior until we accept that the behavior is controlled by memory or drive. Behavior is biological only for a trained observer. For the acting animal itself, it is psychological. "From this point of view, it would be necessary to try to develop a comparative study of emotions. Possibly all theories are correct, but they must be taken from the point of view of the evolutionary principle. At one end of this comparative scale, the type of reaction will approach the type of a complex reflex response. Instinct and emotion will still be undifferentiated to the extent that the argument about these terms will drop away. A general diffuse excitation will be identically characteristic for all forms of behavior at this stage of development. Emotion as a differentiated factor appears only at the point of this scale of development where it is possible to establish that the situation has some sense, if we use this word for signifying any form of connection between a given situation and other situations, regardless of whether they are remembered or anticipated. At a higher level, defined in the last analysis by development of the brain, there must be modified forms of a primitive reaction. Here bodily manifestations and mental stress must be emotional in the true sense of the word. The relation between ideas must come to the forefront and the nature of emotions must change because of this ... •~ historical review of theories of emotions concludes with a confused picture that urgently requires a full reconstruction of the psychology of emotions. All the

158

The Teaching about Emotions

latest efforts in this direction lead us to the path indicated by the evolutionary method. Man attained the contemporary state of development by the slow process of growth and integration in which we see the explanation of special cognitive functions. There is no basis for separating these functions from general organic states, but neither is there any sense in ignoring the possibility of great differences that depend on the degree of brain development and integration. In this, evidently, is contained the true element of the theory of James that captures the differences between lower and finer emotions. But in this form, this difference does not fully respond to the spirit of scientific psychology. In addition, in order to compare one class of emotions to another, we must assume that every emotion can have different forms as, for example, the fury of an animal differs from legitimate indignation. Since one form develops from another, depending on the general development of man, it can easily preserve a connection with a more primitive type of emotions or with other reactions connected with it. "In any case, the relation between emotion and its expression becomes less fixed and immobile to the extent that the organism develops and moves away from instinctive and stereotypic forms of reactions. More complex (finer) emotions that do not have a specific reaction connected with them (typical for the behavior of an animal) allow various expressions, and expression loses its direct connections with the conscious element of emotion-a circumstance that may help theory explain why we cry in the same way from joy as well as from grief" (D. S. Brett, 1928, pp. 393-396). We deliberately quoted the somewhat long conclusion of the historical outline of the development of the psychological ideas on the nature of emotions in order to lean on the objective evidence of a historian of science concerning the state of the problem of development in contemporary psychology of emotions. A brief idea of this long statement is that the psychology of emotions does not now have available even the most elementary rudiments of a theory of development, that it is a confused picture in which a distinction was made between higher and lower, animal and human, instincts and conscious emotions, a picture that takes us, in Brett's expression, in one sweep from one world to another. From the first, timid attempts at genetic investigation of emotions in their ontogenesis, we learn something more than the simple establishment of the fact that the old theory excluded a priori the possibility of emotional development. From these attempts, we also learn the content of the a priori anti-historical nucleus of the theory. In the light of the investigation cited, it can be characterized by two basic traits: the assumption of the sensory-reflex nature of the emotional reaction and rejection of its connection with intellectual states. The first assumption excludes development because reflex reactions are the most stable, the most unchangeable elements of all of behavior and because reducing emotions to simple sensations within organic changes deprives emotion of any real, active role in human consciousness. If the essence of fear consists of sensing trembling and goose bumps, there is no basis for assuming that these phenomena will be substantially different in a child than in an adult. In the same way, rejection of a connection between emotional and intellectual states excluded in advance all participation of emotions in the general development of consciousness in which changes in intellect occupy the central and most significant place. This rejection assumed in advance a formulation of the problem of the nature of emotions that would wholly and completely exclude the possibility itself of the problem of human emotion as distinct from emotion of the animal. Animal and man, the animal and the human in man himself, were torn apart; the lower and finer emotions, in Brett's expression, proved to be two different

The Teaching about Emotions

159

worlds (ibid., p. 393), and only the blind would not see in this the most immediate, the most direct, the most open realization of the old ideas that form the basis of Cartesian teaching. Descartes appears as if invisibly on every page of the psychological works on emotions that were written in the last 60 years. If it is true that we are now witnesses of a radical turn in the 6O-year course of the psychology of emotions, full of bankrupt ideas that determined its direction and outcome, then the current crisis in the teaching on emotions and the radical turn toward a scientific investigation that we have noted and the very understanding of the nature of emotions cannot have a sense other than anti-Cartesian. This conclusion follows of necessity from each step of scientific thought taken in the new direction. Every concrete problem of the new theory of emotions is based on the need to overcome Cartesian principles weighing heavily over this whole area of psychology. We will limit ourselves to only one example of this kind. In the new psychology of emotions, the problem of the dynamic nature of affect moves more and more to the forefront. In the investigations of K. Lewin and M. Prince,89 the dynamic, active, energetic aspect of emotion is advanced as the only method of understanding affect that allows a truly scientific, deterministic, and truly causal explanation of the whole system of mental processes. Of logical necessity, such an understanding assumes overcoming the dualistic approach to affective life and advances the understanding of affect as an integral psychophysiological reaction that includes in itself experience and behavior of a certain type and represents a unity of phenomenal and objective aspects. A study of the dynamic aspect of emotions could not help but come to a confrontation with the James-Lange theory, which, in its essence, excludes a monistic understanding of emotions as energetic and motivational stimulation that determines experience and behavior. "Considering emotions as dynamic processes in itself excludes understanding their role as 'simple sensory perceptions' of visceral functions ... as proposed by the James-Lange theory, according to which their discharges must in themselves determine behavior of a certain type. For this reason, emotion cannot be considered as an epiphenomenon connected with reflexes as has been done by behaviorists, but must itself be understood as an indispensable moment participating in nervous discharges that determine in one way or another the character of reactions. "Consequently, emotion cannot play the passive role of an epiphenomenon. It must do something. From this point of view, the function of emotion with respect to the organism becomes more understandable than if emotion represents nothing other than consciousness of a nervous discharge of energy or a passive sensory realization of visceral activity. From the latter point of view, we might completely do without emotion, without anger or fear, or any other feeling, since we would act like robots" (M. Prince, 1928, pp. 161-162). As evidence for this position, Prince gives several reasons. "First, everyday ob,ervation convinces us that emotion is internally connected with the discharge of energy directed to the internal organs and to the voluntary musculature. But how lS it connected? There is a basis for assuming that emotion occurs synchronically with the discharge which lasts as long as the emotion itself lasts in the consciousness. From the point of view of the James-Lange theory, which considered emotion as a passive sensory perception, emotion must follow visceral reaction. If emotion is such a superfluous, pure epiphenomenon incapable of determining anything in our reaction to a situation, the fact of synchronicity, which is an important problem that requires its own solution, becomes incomprehensible. This is the second argument.

160

The Teaching about Emotions

"The third consists of the fact that emotion as an epiphenomenon would be a completely useless effect in the biological sense, an effect that evolution also would not tolerate as 'nature a vacuum.' Finally, the last argument is evidence of direct experience that convinces us that passion moves us, that it provides energy for our thoughts and our actions. We realize that emotion and feeling activate us. This realization is an immediate fact that does not depend on a further interpretation. All of these ideas taken together compel the author to consider emotion in an internal, unbreakable connection with energy processes occurring in the organism for without the concept of energy, behavior in general cannot be explained" (ibid., pp. 162-164). As we might have expected, Prince in this connection mentions the name of Descartes and refers to the Cartesian teaching on the passions because the problem actually passes from the area of specific study to the plane of a philosophical problem of the psychophysical nature of emotions. But actually, what is unexpected is Prince's statement that the concept he defends against the James-Lange theory is essentially Cartesian. For the second time, we encounter an attempt to consider the James-Lange theory as an antithesis of the Cartesian teaching. We met this idea for the first time in Dunlap, who contrasted the centripetal hypothesis of the peripheral theory with the centrifugal theory of the emergence of emotions developed by Descartes. Thus, the comparison of the two theories pertained to the concrete problem of the physiological mechanism that is the substrate of emotion. We shall return to this problem again. Now we are dealing with something greater. The Cartesian teaching is compared with the James-Lange theory from the point of view of a theoretical understanding of the relation that exists in emotion between the bodily and the mental processes. Thus, both theories appear to be in contradiction not just from the point of view of the specific practical description, but from the point of view of their theoretical basis. This problem deserves the most fundamental explanation. For this reason, we will follow Prince further. In his conception, which considers emotion as energy, contrasting this point of view with the James-Lange theory and connecting it with the Cartesian teaching, Prince uses the point of view of emergent evolution90_ a new idealistic teaching that tries to find an escape from the dead-end of the alternatives-mechanism or vitalism-into which all of contemporary natural science is thrust. Emergent evolution is based on the assumption of sudden, seemingly dialectical leaps in development, sudden appearance of new qualities and an inexplicable conversion of some qualities into others. From this point of view, Prince explains two possibilities that exist for his conception. On the one hand, we must assume that emotional discharges of energy are connected with electrons in the most complex atomic structure of the nervous system. These discharges emerge as emotions since of themselves they contain energy not established objectively, but being the result of a most complex organization of an enormous number of units of nervous energy. On the other hand, we must assume that the kinetic centripetal nervous energy, being immaterial, is converted into the same kind of immaterial, mental energy that, with reverse flow, as a link in the chain of the whole process, is again converted into immaterial, centrifugal energy similarly to the physical law of conversion of energy. What is unknowable with objective methods emerges as accessible to psychological perception as a state of consciousness (ibid., p. 166). It is enough to cite these positions for the idea to become clear that a connection exists between the theory of Prince and Cartesian teaching. Assuming immaterial, mental energy that nevertheless acts completely like material, physical energy and is found in it in a simple mechanical interrelation is, as we shall consider

The Teaching about Emotions

161

in detail subsequently, an essential component of Cartesian teaching on the passions, dual in its very basis. Thus, Prince contrasts one principle of the Cartesian teaching-the spiritualistic-with another principle of the same teaching-the mechanistic. We have seen this earlier. Noting the accumulation of problems that contemporary investigation has brought forth on the relation of the James-Lange theory and Cartesian teaching, we mentioned this problem also, citing Dumas. Dumas called it the theological principle taken by Malebranche from Descartes that divides the old teaching from the new. In Prince's battle against James, we thus have as if two irreconcilable, internal, contradicting parts of Cartesian teaching that were polarized by contemporary psychology and stood one against the other, in turn spiritualistic and in turn mechanistic conceptions of emotion. This is indisputable. We cannot but agree with this. The spirit of the Cartesian teaching shows itself not only in mechanistic theories similar to the James theory, but also in new theories that try to overcome the imperfection of previous hypotheses with the aid of another aspect of the same teaching that gave rise to the ideas of their opponents. They do not suspect in this case that they are ignoring the devil Beelzebub and not only are not going beyond the limits of that closed circle in which all of contemporary psychology of emotions is turning, but close even more firmly the circle that tries to fully realize the old-fashioned Cartesian teaching. Their merit consists in that they, fully consciously, struggle for the triumph of the Cartesian principles of contemporary psychology. They just supplement the somewhat old-fashioned Descartes with the most contemporary theory of emergent evolution. But, as we shall see subsequently, it is not only not foreign to the spirit of Cartesian teaching, but is directly connected with it, something, moreover, that Prince himself admits. With the establishment of this, the whole picture of the ideational struggle in contemporary psychology of emotion is definitively cleared up. It is, of course, monism, says Prince of his conception; the only alternative hypothesis is dualism and parallelism, that is, epiphenomenalism and human automatism (ibid., pp. 166-167). We cannot but agree with this. The whole error consists only in that both hypotheses are considered as alternatives. Actually, in the teaching of Descartes, they mutually anticipate each other and only jointly form the true nucleus of his theory of passions. Of course, there is a certain logical inconsistency here, but only of a type that inevitably confronts all idealistic teaching that attempts to turn into a scientific explanation of real facts that occur in material reality and does not want to break with those facts. Such monism (spiritualistic) and such dualism (parallelistic) not only do not represent true alternatives, but more likely mutually presuppose each other in the teaching of Descartes and his followers, James and Prince, in any case. We recall that precisely such was the method of investigation applied by Descartes in studying the nature of passions. At first, he considers man as a soulless robot and studies the mechanism of passions as it acts in this complex machine, completely irrespective of its consciousness. In this, Descartes anticipated the theory of James. Next, he joins a soul to the robot, previously predetermining that its perceptions arising from the automatic activity of the soulless mechanism cannot be anything other than epiphenomena, and introducing the spiritualistic principle of reverse action of the soul on the bodily robot, thus establishing a mechanistic interaction between the soul and the body; in this way, he anticipated the theory of Prince. It is not difficult to see that what Prince proposed, the emergence of the mental from the physical and the reverse instant conversion of mental energy into bodily energy, is accomplished in the unnatural aggregate made up of pure spirit and the complex machine that Descartes constructed in his theory. Only he

162

The Teaching about Emotions

did not call this instantly occurring miracle emergence and frankly recognized that it is the most dark, unclear, and difficult point of his teaching. Everything develops sequentially and logically in this dualistic theory as long as spirit and body are considered separately. For Descartes, they are two substances that exclude each other. But as soon as the problem arises of uniting both substances in a human being and at the same point at which duality of human nature has a direct effect-in passion-the darkness of inexplicability encompasses the elegant, rationalistic teaching that had been permeated by light. As we recall, the first to attack this point in the teaching of Descartes was Spinoza, calling the hypothesis of uniting soul and body in a pine-cone-like, dark gland, "darker than any dark attribute ... It would be most desirable," Spinoza said, "if he would explain this connection through its immediate cause. But Descartes admitted that the soul was so different from the body that he could not show any single cause for either this connection or for the soul itself, and he had to turn to the cause of the whole universe, that is, to God" (1933, p. 199). In this is contained the theological principle in explaining passions, of which Dumas spoke. Descartes himself, in responding to the question of Princess Elizabeth as to how the combination of soul and body can be explained, referred to the unknowability of this combination. But is this not the very same thing that emergent evolution has in view? Descartes referred to an unknowable miracle. The new theory refers to inexplicable emergence. In 300 years, only the word has changed, but not the idea. But what is a word? Empty sound. In response to the fateful question, Descartes says that the human spirit is incapable of comprehending clearly the difference in the substance of the soul and the body or their combination since the human spirit would have to understand them as a single substance as well as two different substances, and this is self-contradictory. For this reason, we may assert that the problem of passions was the only stumbling block for the whole system of Descartes. If it were not for this cursed problem, if man did not exist in nature with his passions (for Descartes animals were just robots), the teaching on two mutually exclusive substances - the spiritual and the material-would develop sequentially, elegantly, and logically. But passions, this basic phenomenon of the human soul, are the primary manifestations of the dual human nature combining the spirit and the body in one being. Moreover, passions are the only phenomenon of joint life of spirit and body in the whole universe.
