For this reason, for their explanation, they necessarily require a combination of spiritualistic and mechanistic, theological and naturalistic principles. Descartes, despite his own words, had to refer to passions not only as a physicist, but also as a theologian. But specifically in this point of the system, a mixing of two opposed principles had to occur and the whole system not only had to lose its purity, but had to move along the path of further mutual penetration of two of its polar bases. In the graphic expression of K. Fischer, space was obtrusive. Figuratively speaking, if the soul gives it a finger, it takes the whole hand. If the thinking substance has its habitat anywhere, then its independence and distinctness from the bodily substance are already lost and not in only one respect, but in all respects. If the soul is localized, then it becomes materialistic and mechanistic in this way. The spiritualistic principle itself begins to require the addition of a mechanistic principle since the soul is involved in the mechanistic circuit of the passions and since it cannot participate in activity of this mechanism without entering as a mechanical force (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 1, p. 446). But at the same time, the opposite must occur. Not only space is obtrusive, but so is the spirit. If only the most insignificant bodily organ alone-the pineal gland-is capable of being activated by purely spiritual forces, then the human robot

The Teaching about Emotions

163

is inevitably a simple weapon of the thinking substance, a plaything of spiritualistic energy. Thus, the Cartesian teaching is not inadvertently, but basically dualistic, and the dualism in the teaching on passions is only a manifestation of Descartes' general ontological and gnosiological dualism. As Fischer correctly notes, in Descartes' teaching, the theological and naturalistic systems are united (1906, Vol. 1, p. 439). Skipping ahead, we will say that the duality of the Cartesian teaching already contains, wholly and completely, the dualism of explanatory and descriptive psychology. Here we cannot take up the question of the extent to which both parts of the system are firmly and elegantly united logically; we shall consider this union only in the teaching on the passions. Here it is manifested with greatest force and fully discloses its nature. On the one hand, Descartes transfers in full to human passions his own general position that thought and space differ substantially. He says that this is specifically the essence of the matter, that they exclude each other. Fischer says: "In reality, the spirit and the body are completely separate from each other, there is no contact between them: I know that in the light of reason" (ibid., p. 443). The body acts as a soulless robot wholly subject to laws of mechanics. The soul has absolute and unlimited freedom of will that forms our being god-like. The will, or freedom of the will, says Descartes, is the only one of all capabilities that, according to my experience, is so great that I cannot imagine a greater. This capability is also mainly why I consider myself god-like. Fischer's words, in which he spoke about the ontological conception of Descartes, are wholly applicable to both of these positions. The first statement expresses the naturalistic character of the system; the second, the theological. The dualistic character of the system arises from theory and then appears to be fundamental. Everything is successful in proceeding with this dualistic principle as long as Descartes is not confronted by the indisputable fact of the union of both mutually exclusive substances in one phenomenon, human passions. As we have seen, they undoubtedly reveal the indisputable fact of unity of spirit and body in one phenomenon, in one being. Here the logic of the dualistic system must of necessity suffer definitive collapse. Descartes says: "Nature teaches me nothing as clearly as the fact that I have a body that suffers when I feel pain and that requires food and water when I experience hunger or thirst. I cannot doubt that there is something real in these sensations. My affects and instincts make it clear to me that I am in my own body, not like a swimmer in a boat, but connected with it in the closest way and as if mixed in so that in a certain way we form as if one being. Otherwise, due to my spiritual nature, I would not feel pain when my body was harmed, but would only realize this harm as an object of cognition the way a shipbuilder sees when something in the ship breaks. When the body needs food and water, I would know about these states without having indefinite sensations of hunger and thirst. These sensations are actually vague representations originating from the union and seeming mixing of the spirit and the body" (ibid., p. 371).91 According to the perfect and transparent clarity and energy of the positions expressed here by Descartes, they could truly agree with his famous cogito, ergo sum and have pretensions to becoming the Archimedean point, the only firm point of support of all philosophical knowledge. As we know, Descartes' philosophy begins from the doubt principle and from searching for the certainty principle. He says: 'fuchirnedes required only one immovable point of support to move the earth. We can also hope for much if only even the smallest, firmly established, and immovable could be found" (ibid., p. 305).92 As we know, Descartes finds this im-

164

The Teaching about Emotions

movable point of support in the position: "I think, therefore I am," in the position that at the moment when I say it or think it, it is of necessity true. Why should I create other fantasies, Descartes asks himself, I am not that organism that is called a human body, neither am I that fine, ethereal substance that permeates my members, neither wind nor fire, neither vapor nor breath, nothing of any of that which I am in my imagination. 93 But, as we have seen, Descartes himself is forced to admit that nature teaches us nothing as clearly as that we have a body. We cannot have any doubt as to the reality of the pain, hunger, or thirst we experience. Our affects make it clear to us that we, together with our body, are one being. It is specifically passions that form the basic phenomenon of human nature. In them, man appears most fully since thinking is possible only in the spiritual nature, and movement, only in the bodily nature. Evidently, if the teaching on passions stood at the end and not at the beginning of Cartesian philosophy, its Archimedes point would have to consist in direct evidence and certainty manifested in affects of unity of the bodily and spiritual nature. Thus, in itself, the indisputable truth contained in Descartes' words quoted above illuminates better than any apologia and better than any critical objections the radical fallacy of his system. Thus, for Descartes, passions are not only the basic phenomenon of human nature, but also are completely impossible, meaningless, from the point of view of his system, and for this reason, an inexplicable phenomenon-a uniting of mutually exclusive, opposite substances in one being. Human passions are impossible from the point of view of Descartes' system: this is the fact, radical and central in significance, from which inevitably follows the position that no psychology of passions as a science is possible. But if the oppositeness or separation between spirit and body in the teaching of Descartes is clearly and distinctly conceivable, then uniting both must seem inconceivable and impossible in the natural light of reason, and if this factually exists, then it contradicts the principles of the system and its explanation imposes the most difficult test for the teaching of Descartes. A study must be made to determine whether the philosopher can withstand this test without rejecting his principles. The result of such study shows that the test is fatal for Descartes' whole system and that its dualism breaks down on the concept and fact of the existence of man. The contradiction is so obvious that the philosopher himself admits it. We shall not quote the statements of Descartes in which the contradiction of his views appears, statements in which he at one time admits this union of soul and body in man as a substantial unity and transfers the basic property of the one to the other, once considering the soul extended, another time, considering the body indivisible, maintaining that the soul and body considered in themselves also do not constitute the whole just as the hand does not constitute the whole human body, and for this reason, being incomplete substances, they need each other to be complete; then again, he denies that their uniting forms a unit of nature, seeing in it only a unity of composition and preserving wholly the dualism of his system. Something else is of interest to us: the necessary and forced mutual penetration of theological and naturalistic principles in the teaching on the passions, principles that infer each other just as the left infers the right and that also cannot exist one without the other, like the top without the bottom. It is important that we show that the separation of both principles that contemporary scientific psychology tries to implement in the independent existence of explanatory and descriptive psychology, in the opposition of the emergent theory of emotions of Prince and in the mechanistic hypothesis of James, is nothing more than an illusion. Both principles appear to be indivisible in Cartesian teaching and in psychological science.

The Teaching about Emotions

165

As we have seen, human passions are impossible in the world Descartes constructed in his system. For them to appear, he would have to change his own principles and admit a mixing of thought and space. This is where the fall of his philosophy begins, here is where a mixing of theology and naturalism begins. Descartes was forced to admit that the soul must engage with the body; he finds the place for this contact in the cerebral glands through which the body acts on the soul and the soul on the body. The point at which it engages with the body or enters with it into a connection must be spatial, local, bodily: now the soul is localized and becomes in this respect spatial. It is not clear in what respect it remains nonspatial or nonmaterial. The Cartesian position that only bodies are capable of moving and, independently of the first moving cause, can be set in motion only by bodies takes on a different meaning. Judging by this position (moving and brought into motion by a body), the soul must itself be bodily, and it shares the material entity regardless of all assertions that it is a thinking substance completely different from the body. The mechanical influence and connection that occur only between bodies now extend over both the soul and the body. As Princess Elizabeth correctly noted, adding the two substances cannot be conceivable without space and materiality of the soul. Cartesian anthropology contradicts not only dualistic principles of metaphysics, but also mechanical principles of natural philosophy that the amount of movement in the world is constant, that action and reaction, action and counteraction are equal. These fundamental positions of teaching on movement lose validity as soon as movements in bodies can be generated by other than material causes. Whatever we might think about combining both substances in human nature, as a unity or as a compound, in the one understanding and in the other, it contradicts theoretical dualism and inevitably leads to its polarity. It is impossible to say to which side the basic positions of the system are more inclined: toward pure theology or toward pure naturalism. Assuming a pure interaction of soul and body in a small segment of the cerebral gland, Descartes involves the soul in the mechanistic circuit of the passions and subjects the body to spiritualistic influence of immaterial energy equally. Just as in ontological teaching, it sometimes seems that in Descartes' teaching on the passions, the theological element reaches such an exclusive, dominant significance that here Augustinianism conquers naturalism; sometimes, conversely, it appears that there is no doubt that naturalistic principles wholly permeate the area of teaching on the soul. The one and the other are implemented in equal measure because the one and the other represent only two consequences of one and the same position on the possibility of mechanical interaction of the soul and the body in human passions. For this reason, we must admit as an error of investigators, especially Fischer, that they overestimate the victory of the naturalistic principle over the theological in the teaching of Descartes. Considering the struggle of these principles in the ontological teaching of Descartes, Fischer says: the more the naturalistic element yields and diminishes before the theological, "the more the independence of things is dissolved in the independence of God, the more the naturalistic appears anew in the theological element, the more the Cartesian God ceases being a supernatural being, and the more this conception of God is naturalized and moves away from the Augustinian, even being converted into its complete opposite. The dualistic formula, God and nature, becomes the monistic: God or nature. Descartes only touches on it, Spinoza gives it a preponderant significance. Evidently, approaching Augustine,94 Descartes actually approaches Spinoza. He goes out to meet him and goes so far as to express the formula that includes Spinozism in itself.

166

The Teaching about Emotions

"Feeling himself drawn by his personal inclination toward the church father and the Augustinian harmony constructed by the theologian, and rejoicing that agreement with Augustinianism was noted in his teaching, Descartes, with the spirit of his teaching, prepares trends that naturalism completes and contrasts it in the most extreme form to the theological system. The fortunes of philosophy are stronger than persons who are its bearers and weapons. Descartes stands on the path leading to Spinoza, thinking, moreover, that he substantiated the religious teaching of the church. The basic position of his system is the naturalistic trend" (K. Fischer, Vol. 1, pp. 439-440). The indisputable correctness of these positions of Fischer consists of the fact that the naturalistic and Augustinian principles in Descartes' system are so mutually connected into the single contradiction which they form that they continuously and uninterruptedly compel all basic concepts of the system to swing before our eyes like the well-known images that are presented to us from a direct and reverse perspective. The fallacy of Fischer's positions consists in a one-sided emphasis on the triumphs of the naturalistic principle and in underestimation of the force and viability of the theological system in Descartes' teaching. The error arises from the fact that Fischer limits consideration of this problem to a very short historical perspective. It is true that the objective development of philosophical thought brought to the forefront not the theological system of Descartes turned toward the middle ages, but his naturalistic system, and brought to life a direction that completes naturalism and contrasts it in the most extreme form to a theological system. But it is completely incorrect that further development of philosophical thought is predetermined by Descartes' teaching itself. The historical victory of the naturalistic direction occurred not only independently of Descartes, but despite him. Within his system, this victory is not noted at all. Within his system, the naturalistic tendency does not penetrate at all and does not subordinate itself to the theological system. In the Cartesian system, the latter is not a simple theological appendage as it is in Spinoza's system. In this respect, between Spinoza and Descartes there is not so much succession as a break. The direction that naturalism realizes and contrasts most sharply with the theological system simultaneously contrasts just as sharply with the theological system of Descartes himself. But here we return again to a point we have already investigated, where our path separated markedly from investigators who, like Fischer, wished to see in Spinoza a thinker who was and would always be Cartesian. Depending on this basic error, Fischer unilaterally evaluates the results of the struggle of mechanistic and theological principles in the Cartesian teaching on the passions. He points to the fact that, owing to the mixing of spirit and body in human passion, the soul is localized and becomes mechanized. But as we recall, Spinoza did not direct his basic objection against this point in Cartesian teaching. He tried to overcome the spiritualistic principle in the Cartesian teaching on the passions. True, Descartes tries to soften the sharpness of this contradiction into which he falls with the principles of his natural philosophy, maintaining that movements of the cerebral gland, and through them also the movement of the whole body, may be elicited by direct action of the will on this privileged and unique organ of our body. He tries to reduce to almost zero the action of the spirit on the automatic activity of the body, to weaken, soften, qualitatively diminish it. It seems to him that in this way, the basic significance of his hypothesis of interaction will be solidified. He begins with what limits territorially the mixing of spiritual and bodily substance in man. Thus, it seems to him that he changes his own principles only in a limited segment, retaining their significance for all the rest of the enormous

The Teaching about Emotions

167

territory of the human body. In the graphic expression of Fischer, he gives the body only the little finger of the soul, forgetting at the same time that if the anatomical territory where this mixing takes place is a spatially extremely insignificant and limited point, the principal significance of his assumption preserves all of its universal and absolute size (ibid., p. 446). In the expression of H(i1ffding, if we assume that thought as such is capable of shifting even one brain atom by one millionth of a millimeter, all laws of nature will already be broken. Descartes tries to present the matter in such a way that in that insignificant spatial point, the soul communicates to the body movements that are also insignificant in strength. In his opinion, the cerebral gland, as Spinoza says, "is suspended in the middle of the brain in such a way that it can be activated with the slightest movement of vital spirits" (Spinoza, 1933, p. 197). The gland can rotate easily and variously since it is in a hanging position. Moreover, Descartes assumes that the soul changes only the direction of physical movement, but does not elicit the movement itself. This idea of Descartes, known to physicists and adopted recently by Maxwell,95 allows coordination of the idea of mechanical action of the soul on the body with the law of conservation of energy, which "teaches that when a force acts perpendicularly to the direction of movement of a body, it does not do work, but only changes the direction of movement, but not the velocity. For this reason, the real energy, measured by the square of velocity, remains as before. But H(i1ffding says that only those can use this conclusion who can find meaning in the statement that the soul acts in a direction perpendicular to the movement of the brain particles and, in any case, we cannot avoid the difficulty caused by the law of inertia, if we understand by it that for every change in direction of movement, there must be an external, that is, a bodily reason. In the final analysis, the whole task is reduced to the question of whether the law of inertia, in the sense in which we understand it here, has meaning also for the processes in the brain with which the phenomena of consciousness are Gonnected. Accepting one hypothesis or the other depends on the solution of this problem. And when people think that it is possible to avoid this problem, then by the same token, they will also reject the whole problem of soul and body" (1904, p. 60).

Finally, going in the same direction, Descartes tries to soften the contradiction arising from his placing the soul in the middle of the brain into the ';onarium, where it both perceives and moves vital spirits and from where it activates the body and is itself activated by them. The soul and the body are not mixed in reality, but only in a certain way. They are simply put together, but not united in the true sense of the word. Their difference is much greater than their connection. It is not difficult to see that all of these attempts to keep in the background the true significance of the position on the mechanical interaction of the soul and the body speak only of the profound anxiety that this point of his own teaching impressed upon Descartes and of his complete inability to deal with it in any satisfactory way, of the inexcusable naivete of this great thinker with which he tries by quantitative modification of his statements to reduce to zero their principal meaning and of the complete impossibility of reconciling this point with the basic principles of his whole system. For Descartes, it remains only to admit (as he did in his answer to Elizabeth's question on how the union of soul and body can be explained) that we cannot grasp distinctly the difference in the substance of soul and body nor their combination since the one contradicts the other.

168

The Teaching about Emotions 13

We can now consider as answered two of the four questions we noted relative to the connections between Cartesian teaching on the passions and the peripheral theory of emotions: (1) the question of the almost complete identity of the factual description of the plan of the mechanism itself of emotional reaction in both teachings, and (2) the question of the common mechanistic principle as the basic explanatory principle of both theories. But in the course of answering these questions, we inevitably had to touch upon a third question directly connected with the second question, specifically, the question of the extent to which the spiritualistic principle, directly connected in the Cartesian teaching with the mechanistic, unites or divides the two theories. To answer this question, we must explain more precisely the relation presented in the one teaching and the other between emotion and other mental processes. We will begin with the teaching of Descartes, in which the problem of the relation between passions and the will occupies a central place. Descartes assumes the existence of absolute and unlimited freedom of the will as a purely spiritual force that serves our being god-like. Descartes formulates this basic position in the form of a thesis stating that, for this reason, the will is greater than the mind; this basic position is, as we shall see subsequently, the point of opposition of Spinozist teaching to Cartesian teaching. For Descartes, the mind seems to be limited since much is inaccessible to its understanding, and it comprehends much vaguely and unclearly. But there is nothing to which the will could not turn affirmatively or negatively or indifferently. Therefore, the sphere of its action is not limited by anything. It covers both what is known and what is not known, determining by its decisions the whole fate of the spiritual and corporal life of man. It represents an absolute value absolutely without natural limits, forming the final and real cause of everything that is accomplished in our soul. From the idea of the will being primordial, absolute, unlimited, and subordinate to no natural laws, follows its relation to the passions. As we have seen, Descartes bases the genesis of passions purely mechanically. He contrasts his teaching to the old fallacies that considered passions as mental phenomena and could not see any corporal nature in them. Only with the establishment of a dual, spiritual-corporal nature of passions does it become understandable why passions can control the spirit and subjugate its freedom. Thus, passions contradict the very essence of our spirit. Usually to explain this fact, the soul itself was divided into two parts: "into the intelligent part and the unintelligent part, into the higher and the lower, and passions were ascribed only to the latter. Here, the unity of the soul, its indivisibility, was lost and the soul seemingly split into different parts and was made up of various personalities or souls, and its very essence was denied by this" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 1, p. 381). Descartes poses the question of the struggle of the mind or will with the passions in a different way. He admits the central significance of this fact, but assumes, according to Fischer, that this struggle does not take place in the spiritual nature of man, which seemingly rises against itself. Actually, the struggle occurs between two movements going in opposite directions that communicate to the cerebral gland, the organ of the soul: the one, by the body through the vital spirits, and the other, through the will; the first movement is involuntary and is determined exclusively by bodily impressions, and the second movement is voluntary and is motivated by intention established by the will. Bodily impressions stimulated by vital spirits in the organ of the soul, and through it in the soul itself, are converted in it into representations of feelings. If they pertain to a class of ordinary perceptions, they

The Teaching about Emotions

169

leave the will in peace and, therefore, the soul has no reason to struggle with them. If, however, they disturb and excite our will with their direct relation to our mode of life, they are passions that impinge upon the will and evoke counteraction on its part. Action flows from bodily causes. It originates with a naturally essential force and is accomplished according to mechanical laws; its intensity contains the force of passions; counteraction is free; it acts with a force that is spiritual and passionless in itself. For this reason, it can struggle with and overcome passions: the strength of this force is due to its power over the passions. A soul beleaguered by impressions of vital spirits can begin to experience fear, but reassured by its own will, it can remain courageous and overcome fear instilled initially by passion. It can reverse the organ of the soul and with it also the vital spirits, so that the members are stimulated to fight, whereas fear stimulated them to flee. Now it is clear what kind of forces struggle with each other in the passions. That which was taken to be a struggle between the lower and the higher nature of the soul is actually a conflict between the body and the soul, between passion and will, between natural necessity and rational freedom, between nature (material) and spirit. Through action on the organ of the soul, even the weakest souls can master the movement of the vital spirits, and in doing this, can direct the passions in such a way as to attain full control over them. The dual nature of man stipulates the dual nature of passions. They arise and act on the will as mechanical forces, but they may be overcome by the spiritual energy of the will pointed in the opposite direction. Now the foundation on which the theory of passions in the Cartesian system is based is completely understandable (ibid., pp. 282-283). It is completely clear that the naturalistic and theological principles for explaining passions in Descartes are not in contradiction, that they supplement each other, and that only when taken together can they serve as a basis for his theory of interaction between the soul and the body, in which passions are the mediating link that translates mechanical energy to spiritual and spiritual energy to mechanical. In this respect, in Descartes' teaching, passion plays the same role in the system of mental forces as the cerebral gland does in the system of organs. As the gland is a representative of the soul in the body, precisely so passion is a representative of the body in the soul. Descartes' basic idea that provides the tone of all the music of his teaching on the passions thus consists in recognizing the absolute power of our will over the passions. This alone is quite enough for rejecting forever the idea defended by Fischer that the naturalistic principle in Descartes's system subordinates to itself the theological system. The position on the unconditional and absolute rule of the will over the passions speaks for exactly the opposite, for the fact that the naturalistic principle in explaining the passions is wholly subordinate to the absolute godlike arbitrariness of the soul. According to this alone, the laws of nature prove to be broken once and for all in the life of the human being. The supernatural is in charge of the natural, and the principle of naturalism proves to be definitively compromised. Specifically against this point, Spinoza directs all the force of his criticism and, what is most remarkable for a correct understanding of his teaching, begins with a refutation of the idea of the absolute power of the will over the passions by referring to experience. '~though the Stoics thought that affects depend absolutely on our will and that we can have unlimited control of them, experience, glaringly opposed to this, compelled them to confess, despite their principles, that considerable habit and effort is required for limiting and restraining affects" (Spinoza, 1933, p. 197). Descartes' opinion agrees completely with this teaching of the Stoics. He

170

The Teaching about Emotions

admits that because of joining with the pineal gland, the soul perceives through it all the movements that arise in the body and can activate the body only with the help of the will. "Finally, Descartes maintains that although each movement of this gland is by nature evidently connected from the very beginning of our life with separate acts of our thinking, habit may connect them with other acts .... From this, Descartes reaches the conclusion that there is no soul so weak that with proper direction it would not be in a state to acquire absolute power over its passions. For in his definition, these passions consist in perceptions, sensations, or movements of the soul, referring especially to it and produced, preserved, and augmented by some kind of movement of vital spirits. And since we can join any kind of movement of the gland with any desire, and consequently that of vital spirits also, the determination of the will depends on our power alone; determining our will with certain firm judgments according to which we want to direct the actions of our life and combining with these judgments the movements of the desired passions, we acquire absolute power over our passions" (ibid., pp. 197-198). Spinoza objects to Descartes' example relative to the will over passions given above. He says: "Further, I would like very much to know how many degrees of movement the soul can communicate to this cerebral gland and with what force the soul can maintain it in its suspended position since I do not know if this gland moves more slowly or more rapidly than do the vital spirits; and may not the movements of the passions, closely united with firm judgments, be separated from them anew by bodily causes? It would follow from this that although the soul firmly intends to move against dangers and to unite with this decision the movements of courage, at the sight of danger, the gland comes to a position where the soul will be in a state of thinking only of flight. Actually, if there is no relation of the will to movement, then neither is there any correlation between power or forces of the soul and the body, and, consequently, forces of the body can in no way be determined by forces of the soul" (ibid., p. 193). The force of Spinoza's objection seems to be indisputable. If we assume that the will, acting as a mechanical force, overcomes passions, naturally the question arises as to this force being able to overcome the force of vital spirits and to communicate to the gland movement in the opposite direction only if-specifically as mechanical force-it proves to be greater than the force of the vital spirits. There is nothing to be done: if the soul is involved in the mechanical circuit of passions and acts as a mechanical force, it must be subject to the basic laws of mechanics. Consequently we must assume that, always and under all circumstances, the will of even the weakest soul will act energetically, exceeding the force of the vital spirits. But here another objection arises that is just as incontrovertible as the first. Of course, the will itself is roused to struggle with the passions by the vital spirits and, consequently, at the sight of danger, the gland may reach a position where the soul will be able to think only of flight. Again nothing can be done: if passions arise in the soul purely mechanically, they must, consequently, determine the action of the soul itself and deprive it of its absolute freedom to make one set of determinations and decisions or another of the will. But no matter how irrefutable these objections may be, they really miss the target. They remain valid only as long as we, retaining the Spinozist formulation of the question, remain within the framework of natural and logical explanation. But as soon as we, like Descartes, base explanations of passions on the supernatural and irrational, then the enormous incongruity of his explanation becomes a natural attribute of the god-like miracle that our soul exhibits whenever it overcomes passions.

The Teaching about Emotions

171

That Descartes consciously resorts to miracle in explaining the absolute power of the will over the passions, that he consciously avoids all natural and rational explanation of this question, that, in this way, he consciously subjects the naturalistic principle to the theological, is obvious from the distinction that he made between a possible natural explanation and the supernatural explanation he accepted of the power of the will over passions. The remote and vague possibility of a natural explanation glimmers in different parts of Cartesian teaching. Undoubtedly it occurred to Descartes repeatedly, but he rejected it each time. A vague possibility of such a natural explanation is really contained in the example we gave in which the will, aroused by fear to flee, moves the organ of the soul in the opposite direction, rousing the body to fight, whereas fear roused it to flee. Let us recall the point in the teaching on passions in which Descartes leaves consideration of the passions as they would occur in a soulless robot and goes on to consider real passions of man, adding to the complex machine that produces passions a soul capable of experiencing sensations or perceptions of movement of vital spirits. As we recall, movements of vital spirits in perception of danger act in a dual way: on the one hand, they cause a turn of the spine and movement of the legs that serve flight, and on the other hand, they cause changes in the heart that, in their turn with the help of the vital spirits, evoke in the gland the emotion of fear and cause movement corresponding to this emotion intended by its very nature to produce this passion in the soul. Thus, in rousing any emotion, the soul seems to be involved in its circuit. In perception of danger, simultaneously with a representation of the object, a representation of danger arises. Involuntarily, the will strives to protect the body by flight or by fight; for this reason, involuntarily the organ of the soul is activated and the impulse is conveyed to the flow of the vital spirits that incites the members to either fight or flee. The will to fight is bravery; the wish to flee is cowardice. Bravery and cowardice are not simple sensations but arousals of the will. They are not simply a representation, but a movement of the soul or passion (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 1, pp. 380-381). Thus, the will participates in all emotion. It is natural, therefore, to assume that in the case being examined, when the will overcomes fear brought on by passion and incites the body to fight, whereas fear incited it to flee, we are dealing simply with a struggle of two passions: of course, bravery and cowardice are identically passions that can be identically aroused by perception of danger. The will seemingly encounters two passions, bravery and cowardice, together, the one overcoming the other by force. In another part of the teaching, Descartes comes even closer to the possibility of a natural explanation. As we know, he distinguishes six primary or primitive passions from which all the other special or particular passions can be produced as derivative or combined forms. The six primitive passions that are the base of all other passions are: astonishment, love, hatred, desire, joy, and sorrow. In this list of primordial passions, one passion, specifically astonishment, occupies a completely exclusive place. All primary passions are positive or negative since, according to Descartes' teaching, passion is aroused not by an object in itself, but by its value, that is, by the good or the harm that it does us. But there are objects that irrepressibly attract our soul by the power and novelty of their impression without in the least arousing our desire. These objects arouse astonishment, which is thus the only passion that is neither positive nor negative. "Of all our passions not one is as theoretical and as easy to recognize as astonishment. Descartes agrees with Aristotle96 that philosophy begins with astonishment, which directs our will toward cognition. Astonishment involuntarily gives the will a theoretical direction and inclines it to cognition. For this reason, in the eyes of our philosopher, it is not only the

172

The Teaching about Emotions

first among the primitive passions, but the most important of all" (ibid., pp. 394395). Descartes says: "Other passions may serve to compel us to turn our attention to useful or harmful objects, but only astonishment turns our attention to rare objects."97 Thus, Descartes comes extremely close to a natural explanation of the higher, not mechanical, aspect in the life of the passions. He not only admits that the will itself is directed initially to cognition by astonishment, that is, by passion, and, consequently, is determined to act not of itself, not by virtue of its absolute freedom, but according to indispensable laws of the spirit-body nature of man to which all passions, including astonishment, are subject. Moreover, in explaining the higher nature of man, he admits the vague and unclear possibility of a path that Spinoza will follow later. Some investigators more perspicacious than Fischer note specifically this point in the Cartesian teaching on the passions as a real internal uniting link between the theory of Descartes and that of Spinoza, which to a much greater degree than external classification of passions draws the two teachings together. These investigators go to another extreme, erroneously assuming that at this point, both teachings fully agree, losing sight of the fact, first, that, according to Descartes, the idea itself of a natural explanation of the action of the will on passions still is one of a number of vague and unclear ideas, and, second, that Descartes himself resolutely avoided the possibility of a natural explanation and openly took the side of the theological principle. Thus, S. F. Kechek'yan 98 sees direct continuity between Descartes and Spinoza specifically at the point where the teaching on passions naturally approaches explaining the higher aspect of the life of our feelings and where psychology meets ethics directly. Studying the solution to the problem in Cartesian teaching that we are considering, the investigator says: "To study the mechanism of human passions and to explain their significance for liberating the spirit means to carry out the task of ethics. Specifically at the point where ethics meets psychology, the task arises of finding the mental property, the passion, that would in itself determine a moral form of life. Just as Spinoza will subsequently teach that affects can be suppressed only by other affects, so Descartes maintains that in the mechanism itself of passions it is possible to find a passion that will result in a greater good-freedom of the human will. It is important to note that in Descartes, morals have the meaning of science, and like all science, follow the only right method, the method of deduction, which is recognized as the method of natural cognition" (S. F. Kechek'an, 1914, pp. 8-9). 'fiue, this author cannot but see that in the teaching on freedom of the will, Spinoza's position is opposite that of Descartes, but in his opinion, this is only an inconsistency of Descartes, nothing more. "Spinoza necessarily comes to rejecting free will, and here again Spinoza is more consistent than Descartes. The idea of identity of the will with accepting and rejecting belongs to Descartes. But he did not reach any conclusions from it that are dangerous to freedom of the will and retained for the will its independence of cognition and limitless arbitrariness of its determinations. Spinoza, on the contrary, interpreting Descartes' idea, found it necessary to merge will and cognition into one and saw in this a new argument for defense of the determinism that he was vindicating. So there can be no question of the freedom of the will in Spinoza's system. Freedom, as the opposite of nature, cannot find a place in it. Freedom may be only an element of that nature, not an opposite to natural necessity but only one of the forms of this necessity. "Freedom does not destroy necessity, but assumes it," said Spinoza99 (ibid., p. 111).

The Teaching about Emotions

173

Thus, the agreement of the two teachings seems more than doubtful because they differ radically at the central point as only determinism and indeterminism, spiritualism and materialism, a natural and a supernatural explanation of the control of affect by will can differ. In the final analysis, the question is: does what is higher in man, his free and rational will and his control over his own passions, allow a natural explanation that does not reduce the higher to the lower, the rational to the automatic, the free to the mechanical, but preserves all the meaning of this higher aspect of our mental life in all its fullness, or to explain the higher, do we inevitably have to resort to rejecting the laws of nature, to introducing a theological and spiritualistic principle of an absolutely free will not subject to natural necessity? In other words, the question is: is scientific knowledge of higher forms of conscious activity possible or impossible, is human psychology as a science, not as applied metaphysics as it is in all consistent idealists, beginning with Descartes, continuing with Lotze, and ending with Bergson, possible or impossible? It is indisputable that Descartes thought such a possibility of a scientific, natural explanation of the higher nature of man at least very vague and unclear, but on the whole, he did not reject it and finally adopted the second part of our alternative. Spinoza developed the first. Thus, while coming close to a certain degree at one point of the path, the two thinkers drew farther apart in opposite directions, making up in classical form two poles of human thought, striving to know their own nature. For this reason, we must consider the further development of the thesis on the continuity between the teaching of Spinoza and Descartes an error. Considering the solution of the problem of freedom in Spinoza's teaching, Kechek'yan comes to the conclusion that "the path marked out by Spinoza is a path not from slavery to freedom, but from his point of view, from one kind of slavery to another" (ibid., p. 146). Here, in a surprising way, our investigator repeats almost word for word the thought of Descartes himself, identifying all natural necessity with slavery and permitting only a metaphysical solution of the problem in the sense of recognizing the absolutely opposite natural necessity of freedom of the will. "In this respect, Spinoza repeats the error of Descartes. According to Descartes, the higher good must, in a certain sense, become the subject of our desire and for this reason, a passion must exist that in itself determines a moral form of life. This is the point at which psychology and morals are closely intertwined with each other. As we have seen, it is the same thing in Spinoza. The mind must act as affect in order to ensure a moral life. According to Descartes, magnanimity is the passion that holds in its hands the reins of moral life. As long as the soul submits to desire, it is the plaything of the passions and can overcome some passions in no other way than by submitting to others. Thus, of necessity, any passion dominates in the soul. Magnanimity builds freedom. Descartes seems to forget that perhaps magnanimity is a passion, of a different kind, it is true, than the other passions, but nevertheless, a passion. For this reason, instead of freedom, in essence, we fall into a new slavery, from the frying pan into the fire: we do not free ourselves definitively, but only replace the master" (ibid., pp. 146-147). We shall return again to the attempt to compromise Spinoza's teaching on freedom and demonstrate that, in this thinker, freedom is nothing other than slavery of a different kind, and to the attempt based on admitting the psychophysical parallelism of the basic point of view of the Spinoza teaching and on a purely Cartesian definition of the concept of freedom and slavery. For the time being, we shall put it aside. Something else must be our concern now: Descartes himself developed his idea in precisely the same way as contemporary Cartesian critics of Spinoza. For Descartes, this attempt to approach the natural explanation of human passions was

174

The Teaching about Emotions

no more than a simple error that he immediately tried to correct, remaining faithful to the spirit of his teaching. The basis of moral life, according to Descartes, consists of controlling our desires. Since passions nudge us toward action by means of the desire they arouse, we must control our desires-this is the main use of morals. Let us recall that Descartes admits two means against our vain desires, of which the first consists in a high and true self-regard, and the second, in reasoning about the everlasting determinacy of the course of things by divine Providence. The first of these means pertains to the area of passions, the second, to cognition. Thus, the illusory possibility arises of a natural explanation of freedom of the will as a product of higher development of the mind and passion. Descartes closes his work on the passions of the soul by pointing to the means for curbing our passions and for converting them into a source of the joy of life. This single means is wisdom. But the path to wisdom lies through the dark and dangerous valley of the passions. As we remember, among all the primitive passions, Descartes noted astonishment as the very first passion, superior to the others. This emotion, theoretical in nature, is the natural impulse that compels us to follow the path whose goal is wisdom. 1oo '~s long as we are aroused by the force of a new and unusual impression, we are completely unaware of the usefulness or harmfulness of an object, which is the basic theme of all other passions. For this reason, astonishment precedes them; it is the first of passions and has nothing in common with the nature of the others, a nature that allows the opposite" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. 1, p. 389). Among the derivative or particular forms of passion, Descartes distinguishes different kinds of astonishment depending on the object, whose rarity strikes us, determining whether its character is outstanding as to greatness or nothingness, depending on whether through this object we or others will be free beings. Thus, astonishment leads to an evaluation of others, resulting in respect or contempt, and to self-evaluation exhibited as magnanimity and pride or pusillanimity and submissiveness. Descartes ascribes a very special significance to personal self-valuation. "Nothing is so evident in a man's behavior, in the expression of his face, in gestures and in his gait as an unusually elated or depressed feeling of his personality. Elation or depression may be true and false. nue self-respect is magnanimity, false selfrespect, the opposite, pride. Descartes calls true humility, faint-heartedness, false humility, submissiveness. The criterion that makes separation of the true from the false in our self-valuation is laid down exclusively in the object of these passions. Only free beings can serve as objects of respect or contempt, and there is only one object genuinely worthy of respect: it is our free will due to which reason is dominant in our nature and passions are subordinate. Whoever has attained this freedom of will and in this way mastered himself, has magnanimity of the soul, and from this follows the truly great and only sure self-regard-an attitude of magnanimity. . . . Every respect for oneself that does not follow from a feeling of magnanimity and freedom is false as is every submissiveness that is based on other sensations and not on a feeling of the impotence of one's own will" (ibid., pp. 389-390). Thus, freedom of the will, resulting in control of the passions, is the only object that can evoke in us that exalted passion of magnanimity that is a derivative form of astonishment and a special case of our self-valuation. But Descartes also admits the reverse dependence. If only freedom of the will were recognized as the only source and cause of the most exalted passion, then immediately after this, Descartes is ready to admit that this exalted passion itself is the source and cause of our freedom. The vicious logical circle that circumscribes his idea here is resolved in a completely unexpected way through a sudden abandonment of a natural expla-

The Teaching about Emotions

175

nation of the relation between the will and passions and the return to a supernatural explanation with the aid of the theological principle. We remember that astonishment, according to Descartes, is a purely theoretical passion that compels us to take the path that ends at wisdom. This passion frees the instinct of cognition from bonds and compels it proceed to true self-knowledge and to true self-valuation. Thus, from the instinct of astonishment is born an attraction toward knowledge, from this, doubt of self-trust, and from this in the light of reason-that astonishment whose object is the greatest and most exalted of all properties: freedom of the will. From this springs the movement of the soul that Descartes called magnanimity of the soul, which holds in its hands the reins of moral life. The vicious logical circle is perfectly obvious: on the one hand, from astonishment springs attraction toward knowledge, self-knowledge, and self-valuation, which lays the path to freedom of the will; on the other hand, from freedom of the will springs magnanimity, that most exalted of the passions. Astonishment lays the path for freedom of the will and freedom of the will calls forth the special form of astonishment that is called magnanimity. In other words, at one time, passion lays the path toward freedom of the will; at another time, freedom of the will engenders passion. It remains only to break this vicious circle with a single stroke to get out of it. Descartes does this in the teaching on the weapons of the soul itself with which it overcomes passions. So long as the soul gives itself up to passions, it is their plaything; it can overcome some passions by giving in to others and in this way, it changes one master for another. This illusory triumph is celebrated not by the soul, but by one of its passions; the soul itself remains captive. On the other hand, if by the force of its will and freedom, with the mediation of clear and definite cognition, the soul has risen above the level of these desires, it will conquer with its own weapons and then its victory will be a true victory. Such victory is a triumph of the freedom of the spirit. "What I call its own weapons, Descartes explains, are the unshakable and reliable judgments of good and evil according to which the soul decides how to act. Only the very weakest souls pay no dues to cognition and allow their will to follow various passions in one direction, then in the opposite direction. These passions turn the will against itself and lead the soul to the poorest state in which it can possibly be. Thus, on the one hand, to us, fear presents death as the greatest evil, which we can avoid only by flight, while, on the other hand, ambition compels us to regard such cowardly flight as an even greater evil than death. The two passions draw the will along different paths and it falls under the influence first of one and then the other and constantly struggles with itself, and, in this way, makes the position of the soul servile and disastrous" (ibid., pp. 397-398; cf. R. Descartes, Passions of the Soul, Vol. 1, §48). In this philosophical principle, which affirms that the will overcomes passions with its own weapons without pitting them one against the other, without the help of the passion of magnanimity, which Descartes called a kind of key to all other virtues and the principal means against intoxication by the passions, Descartes, as correctly noted by Fischer, "returns to his most profound basic principles" (1906, Vol. 1, p. 398), that is, to the teaching on the complete oppositeness of the spiritual and corporal nature of man and to the idea of an absolutely independent will. The victory over passions Descartes again considers to be a victory of the spirit over nature; he could repeat again the thesis that Spinoza attacked: there is no soul so weak that it would not be in a state, with proper direction, to acquire absolute power over its passions; even the weakest souls can master the movement of the

176

The Teaching about Emotions

vital spirits by action on the organ of the soul and, in this way, control passions so as to be in a position to attain full control over them. The possibility of a natural explanation of the higher in man, human freedom, was actually illUSOry. Like the finest spider web, like an incorporeal shadow of his naturalistic principle, it shines through the firm, basic threads of his system and is broken before coming to the end. This is why Descartes, as we have seen, cannot always make a clear distinction between passions of the soul and passions of a soulless machine. For this reason, as Fischer has correctly noted, the victory of the will over passions appears to be not a victory of a higher natilre of the soul over a lower, of exalted passions over base, but a victory of the will over passion, of freedom over necessity, of the spirit over nature (ibid., pp. 398-399).

14

The relation between passions and the will as it is sketched in Cartesian teaching now appears to us in its true and real light. 10 explain the whole problem, we still have to consider the relation between passions and thinking and between cognitive and emotional elements of our mental life. For a long time, the most various investigators saw the Cartesian teaching on passions as the greatest triumph of intellectualism, reducing feelings to a purely cognitive process. In the teaching on passions, Descartes actually assigns such a place to the role of the intellectual elements; according to Sergi's correct observation, an investigator like Lange might notice nothing except these elements in the Treatise on the Passions. . . and might completely conscientiously consider himself the inventor of the visceral theory. In an effort to present Descartes as the true founder of this theory, Sergi is ready even to regret slightly that Descartes in his Treatise scatters, a little here and there, ideas that perceptions, recollections, opinions, the idea of some kind of loved, hated, or frightening object, are the reasons for love, hatred, anger, or fear. Thus, according to Descartes, joy is derived from the opinion that we possess some kind of good. G. Sergi tries to calm his anxiety and notes: a reader mature in physiological thinking can have nothing against Descartes' statement that opinion is a cause of emotion. But Sergi must admit that everything was simple and direct as long as we saw in man, consisting of a body and a soul, only a machine capable of experiencing passions due only to the play of the internal organs. The path of the visceral theory becomes burdensome and difficult as soon as it must take into account all the other parts of the machine and all the other aspects of the soul, especially when, together with passions, it must consider the relation of passions to other psychological phenomena. Actually, here our theory encounters unprecedented difficulties. The theory clearly begins to swing between two possible causal explanations of emotions. On the one hand, the cause of emotion is considered in a unique organic state which, through vital spirits and the cerebral gland, is perceived by the soul as passion. On the other hand, sensation, perception, opinion, and idea are perceived as causes of emotion. The visceral and intellectual interpretation of emotion seemingly balance each other on the pans of the Cartesian scales. But this is only a seeming equilibrium. Actually, the pan with the visceral explanation is clearly preponderan t. Descartes introduces the distinction between proximate or final causes and remote or first causes. The final (proximate) cause of passions of the soul, Descartes says, is exclusively the movement that the spirits produce in the small gland located

The Teaching about Emotions

177

in the center of the brain. The sources of passions must be studied and their first causes considered. First causes seem to be sensations and ideas. As Sergi discovers, in this distinction, the visceral theory does not yield even an inch of its territory to intellectualism. He sees evidence for this in that the theory is capable of managing completely without remote causes, which might be absent in cases due only to proximate causes: the play of spirits determined by the general state of the organism. When we are completely well, we experience a feeling of happiness which is not evoked by any function, but exclusively by impressions produced in the brain by movement of the spirits. In precisely the same way, we feel sad when we are not well, although we may not yet know completely what is wrong. Thus, Descartes preserves the purity of his initial explanation and marks a sharp boundary between sensory and intellectual states that precede emotions on the one hand and passions on the other. Sensation and feeling seem to him to be separable to the extent that even where they are so indivisibly merged that, as we have seen, they cause many investigators, following Stumpf, to see a special class of experiences of sensations of feeling (for example, the feeling of pain), Descartes finds no internal connection between the one element of consciousness and the other. TIue to his principle that ascribes complete meaninglessness to emotional experience, Descartes finds no understandable, explicable, in general possible and psychologically survivable connection between emotion as such and a sensory or intellectual state that would phenomenally be experienced by us as moments merging directly with the feeling that accompanies them. Working with lifeless, formallogical delimited abstractions, Descartes considers any mathematical combination of sensations and feelings, any transposition in one experience of combinations of sensation and feeling that we know from direct experience, as equally possible and equally understandable to consciousness. Thus, Descartes makes precise distinctions between joy and sadness on the one hand and satisfaction and pain on the other. As passions, the first differ from the second not only as sensations, but may be completely separate from them. It is easy to imagine that the most vivid pain would be experienced with the same emotional indifference as the most banal sensation. If, in thought, we follow the Cartesian method to the end, we might even be surprised that pain is so often accompanied by sadness, and satisfaction, by joy, that a sensation of hunger and desire, apparent in appetite, also represents accompanying internal phenomena connected with each other. It would be impossible to express more clearly and strongly the idea of the complete meaninglessness, absolute chance, complete lack of structure and unconnectedness that rule in the area of relations between passions and the cognitive processes. Any combination is equally meaningless and for this reason, equally possible. Even the connection between hunger and appetite seems incomprehensible and meaningless, evoking our astonishment just like every connection between sen;;ation and desire, between perception and feeling. Here, where confirmation of the nonsense of passions reaches apogee, where any uniting of everything with everything becomes the only guiding principle of psychological explanation, where an llgebraic combination of dead abstractions celebrates a great triumph, where the :ast breath of living mental life has been extinguished, where the music of the pas~ions, speaking in the language of Pushkin's Salieri, has been dissected like a l:orpse 101_ here, strictly speaking, Descartes brings to a logical end the basic idea 'Jf the mechanical origin of passions. TIue, as we have noted, he does not succeed in this case in avoiding what he fears most of all-an explanation of emotions that is essentially sensualistic. Here,

178

The Teaching about Emotions

we must agree with Sergi, who strives as hard as he can to show the triumph of the visceral theory in Cartesian teaching on the passions. He even assumes that at this critical point of the path, Descartes and James go opposite ways. This critical point of the whole teaching is disclosed for us at the moment when, having distinguished passions from perception and sensation of external objects in the sharpest way, whether we want to or not, we must admit that in the final analysis, passion is nothing other than a vague, undifferentiated, global sensation of the general state of the organism. Then it seems that passions or emotions no longer exist, but only sensations alone exist. In his discussion, Sergi maintains that James, fearing this result, falls into the Spinozist theory and deviates from the path marked out by Descartes. We have seen earlier that this is not so when we established, following Clap are de, that the theory of James also inevitably brings us to the same dissolution of emotions in sensations. Trying to save his position, Claparecte brings forth the concept he created of syncretic perception. If emotion is only consciousness of peripheral organic changes, why is it perceived as emotion and not as organic sensation? When I am frightened, why do I experience a feeling of fear and not a simple organic sensation of heart beat, trembling, etc.? Thus, against this sensualistic, submerged rock, the theories of both James and Descartes break up. Claparecte tries to save the position with the help of the now fashionable structural principle-this new goose that lays golden eggs. Emotion is a structure that unites various organic sensations. It is nothing other than a vague and general perception of a series of united sensations, which the author designates as syncretic perception. In other words, emotion is consciousness of the general state of the organism. As we see, the interpretations of Descartes and James differ in only one thing: in the admission of a structural or a structureless character of these sensations, to which both thinkers are forced, against their wills, to bring emotion. If we recall that James himself managed splendidly without the structural corrective that Claparede added to his theory and that in all the rest, Sergi and Claparecte, interpreting every theory of their predecessor, agree completely right up to the literal and word-for-word coincidence of the resulting formula that reduces emotion to a global sensation of the general organic state, we can consider the agreement between James and Descartes, which Sergi tried to shake at this point, reestablished once again. They follow the same path and there is nothing surprising in their being confronted by exactly the same difficulties. There is actually a disagreement between James and Descartes, but it does not occur where Sergi wants to see it: not with respect to the sensualistic explanation of emotions, but in a certain detail, a substantial detail, in the factual description of the emotional mechanism itself. At this point of the theory, Lange maintains a more strict and sequential line of Cartesian teaching than the James hypothesis does. As we know, James ascribes motor changes together with visceral changes to bodily manifestations of emotion that serve as its source and true cause: mimic, pantomimic, and exhibiting emotion in actions and behavior. As Sergi correctly notes, Descartes in this respect is close to James. In movements in which emotion is exhibited, Descartes always distinguishes internal movement (it causes the most emotional experience) and external movement (it is an expression of emotion or serves the interests of the machine that experiences passions). For Descartes, as for the ordinary view, flight is not the cause of fear, and aggressiveness is not the cause of anger. We can continue to experience fear and anger having voluntarily stopped the movement of flight or aggression. Passions continue to be represented in the soul until the organic state that evokes them ceases, and the only thing that the will can do is to disagree with the actions that emanate from these passions. As we

The Teaching about Emotions

179

recall, the will can give the organ of the soul direction opposite to that which is determined by fear and, because of this, the body is incited to fight, whereas fear incited it to flee. According to Descartes, mimicry that accompanies our emotions arises by chance due to the connection between the nerves that control it and the alimentary and respiratory apparatus because the facial nerve and the sixth pair of nerves have their origin in adjacent parts of the brain and are activated simultaneously by the vital spirits. Sergi notes with satisfaction that in explaining mimicry, Descartes remains true to his basic idea to the end, finding in it neither expression, nor cause, nor positive accompaniment to emotion, not finding any sense in it at all, and seeing in it only an accidental and neutral accompaniment to the play of emotional reactions. He sees merit in Descartes in that he remains more true to the point of view of the physiologist and physicist than Darwin, Wundt, or Spencer.

15 James and Lange radically differ with Cartesian teaching in only one point evidently. The question of the possibility of emotion with complete absence of peripheral changes, specifically visceral, separates the two theories. This question is directly connected with the possibility of the existence of centrifugal sensations. As we know, James spoke sharply against the theory of Wundt that allows such a possibility in the form of the presence of innervation sensations. Descartes also allows this possibility. As Sergi says, in Descartes' teaching, the physiological direction intersects with other directions at each step and presents no difficulty in extracting from his Treatise an intellectualistic or finalistic theory, that is, a teleological theory of emotions. If these departures from the basic path cannot shake the general conception of Descartes on the whole, then in one point, the intersection of the visceral theory with the intellectualistic appears so distinctly that it cannot be avoided in any way. Both theories intersect exactly in the question of the possibility of emotional states that do not arise along the visceral path. Is it possible for intellectual emotions free of any mixing with the bodily state to exist together with emotions-feelings? Can passions be experienced with complete silence of the internal organs? Descartes gives a positive answer to this question. He assumes that all perception, including emotions, may arise not just centripetally, but also centrifugally. He repeats many times that the final material cause of perception is specific movement of the spirits with their exit from the gland in the direction from the center to the nerves. The proximate material cause of perception is not the centripetal, but the centrifugal movement. Thus, in the Cartesian teaching there is the most general form of the centrifugal theory of mental phenomena. Usually, in order to move the spirits to exit the gland, as is necessary for perception, a change in the retina, in the ear, in the skin, or in the internal organs is needed. But hallucinations, dreams, and illusions of amputees show that this can occur in another way and that spirits may evoke sensations that are not aroused by any object. Descartes sometimes indicates this and compels us to think that movement of the spirits, which evoke all perceptions, may have causes other than those that facilitate them under normal conditions. The ideas of the movement of our members, says Descartes, consist only of the fact that the spirits exit the gland, being directed outward in a certain way. Movements of the members and ideas of the movements may mutually cause each

180

The Teaching about Emotions

other. It is enough for the spirits to exit the gland and be directed toward the optic nerve for us to perceive some object. It is enough for the spirits to be directed to a motor nerve for us to sense movement. It is enough for the spirits to be directed toward the cardiac nerve and to be capable of taking hold of it for us to experience a feeling of sadness. Passions are caused by spirits to the extent that they are directed toward the sixth pair. In order for a movement of the members to occur, it is necessary for the spirits to reach the muscles, but for the development of a perception of this movement, it is enough for the spirits to exit from the gland in an appropriate manner. For this reason, for the development of emotion, it is not necessary for the spirits to induce a corresponding visceral storm at the periphery, in the thorax, and in the abdominal cavity. It is enough for the spirits to leave the gland as they are supposed to. The result of this is the possibility of passions with complete silence of the visceral organs. However, Descartes himself did not realize the whole significance and the whole importance of the centrifugal theory of emotions. He did not reach the necessary conclusions from it. According to the observation of Sergi, Descartes' own harsh words spoken about Aristotle might be applied to him: he manages to say something close to the truth only by accident. The most substantial consequence of the centrifugal theory-the possibility of emotions with absence of visceral changes-was noted only by Descartes' successors. He himself bypassed this conclusion. Nevertheless, he seems to have sensed it at one time. We meet as if in passing this note of Descartes: when an object of love, desire, hatred, or joy occupies the soul to the extent that all spirits that are in the gland participate in the process of representing it in the soul and cannot therefore serve any motor manifestation, the body remains inert. This is ecstasy, these are the greatest emotions with no internal or external manifestations. This one note is enough to explode the whole previous construction. It acts like a spark on the powder locked in the centrifugal theory of emotions. It creates a catastrophe. "What does it mean?" Sergi asks in dismay. We have before us emotion that is unfolding in all its splendor not only without participation of the visceral organs, but even without intervention of the motor neurons, without the possibility even of resorting to the centrifugal theory. This is a complete disruption of the visceral theory that casts us into pure sensualism or intellectualism. We are returning to the classical distinction between passions as feelings and intellectual emotions that do not depend on the body. Here new horizons open, new points of view, and the road becomes more tortuous and difficult. The centrifugal theory of emotions, opening the possibility of a complete rejection of the opposite centripetal theory which constitutes the nucleus of Cartesian teaching on the passions, also found its heirs and successors in contemporary experimental psychology, which has thus far been so permeated with the ideas of Descartes and so kept alive by them that it can fully and justifiably be considered his own offspring. It is possible to show-and this is the task of the present section of our investigation-that all the principal contradictions of contemporary psychology, both those lying at the base of its crisis and those pertaining to its separate and particular problems, are contradictions embedded in the Cartesian teaching on the passions. In this sense, we do not know another book the study of which would be as central in its significance to understanding the real historical sense of the whole past of psychological science and its contemporary crisis as the last, concluding work of Descartes- his Treatise on the Passions . ... We are justified in asserting that Descartes' Treatise, little known today and far from central among all his works,

The Teaching about Emotions

181

stands at the very beginning of all contemporary psychology and all the contradictions that tear it apart. All the contradictions of the Cartesian system, collected as if into a focal point, in his teaching on the passions are-to use a musical term-the basic theme with respect to which all contemporary psychology is nothing other than a variation that carries and develops this basic theme. In the course of the development of psychological science, Cartesian teaching disintegrated into a series of separate conceptions and directions that, as they are developed by separate investigators or in separate psychological systems, are, in external form, seemingly independent, logically complete, isolated streams of scientific thought, sharply contrasted with other streams that have their beginning in the same Cartesian source and are often engaged in an irreconcilable struggle. Internally contradictory in its own base, the Cartesian system of Descartes' psychological ideas could not but fall apart in the course of scientific development into separate, independent, and mutually inimical theoretical directions of psychological thought. This is why we do not find in any of the contemporary psychological systems a complete and entire embodiment of Cartesian teaching. Everywhere there are pieces, everywhere, only parts of the internally split, grandiose construction of this teaching. But if we go beyond the separate psychological trends and separate scientists, if we rise above them and study the sources and roots of conflicting systems on the plane of historical investigation, if, with the help of theoretical, essentially philosophical study of the basic problems of contemporary psychology, we discover their internal unity and connection and show that behind the conflict of opinions there are contradictions embedded in the Cartesian teaching itself, then theories that are frequently polar will be not so much enemies as twins, not so much opposite positions that exclude each other on the plane of empirical knowledge as correlative concepts that presuppose each other and cannot exist one without the other just as the right cannot exist without the left. We have already seen this in the example of basic contradiction of the whole contemporary psychological crisis-in the problem of explanatory and descriptive psychology. Now we shall have the opportunity to once again be convinced of this in connection with the analysis of the teaching of Descartes on the first and last, proximate and remote causes of passions cited above, because the problem of a causal explanation is the basic problem of the possibility of psychology as a science engendering the historical division into explanatory and descriptive psychology. In this respect, the problem of causality is the cornerstone of the whole psychological crisis. "frue knowledge is possible only as causal knowledge. Is psychology as a causal science possible, and is it possible generally in principle to apply to the world of the higher mental life of man a causal explanation that is the basis itself of scientific knowledge of the patterns and determinacy of everything that happens? Therefore, is scientific knowledge of the higher in man possible? In general, is human psychology as a science possible, or is it possible only as applied metaphysics? This question, and exclusively this question, is the subject of the argument between explanatory and descriptive psychology. In this sense, we may say that the apparent oppositeness of the twins of contemporary psychological knowledge is already present in the seed, in the unity of the contradictory teaching of Descartes on the first cause and proximate causes of passions. But just as behind this general theoretical problem, on which the whole existence and the whole fate of psychology as a science depend, there are unresolved contradictions in the Cartesian teaching, advancing, on the one hand, a strictly consistent, absolute, mathematical principle of explanation and, on the other hand, overturning the same principle at only one insignificant point of endless extent-in

182

The Teaching about Emotions

the cerebral gland of man-exactly like the unresolved contradictions of the Cartesian teaching on the passions, this time, contradictions of a factual character, such as the contradiction between centripetal and centrifugal theories of passions that we just discussed, stand behind the concrete and particular psychological controversy between teachings that allow only a peripheral origin of sensations arising from effects of the external world on our organs of feeling, and teachings that allow a central origin of sensations together with the peripheral, teachings that allow the spirit to know directly what kind of activity originates in its main organ, in the brain of man. In this respect, we believe that in the historical investigation of the theoretical aspect of psychology, Dunlap reaches a conclusion that is incontrovertible and fundamental in significance. This investigator says that Descartes, who is recognized by everyone as the first among the founders of modern psychological theory, creates with his right hand an object of psychology while with his left hand, perhaps inadvertently, he destroys its bases and gives the whole structure of that science a decided list to the right which has been retained over the long course of subsequent development. In the Treatise on Passions of the Soul, he lays the cornerstone of physiological psychology and all of contemporary reactological theory, although his personal, reactological theory was discarded.
