In a discussion of method, however, as in his Principles . .. , he is inclined toward basing the objective in psychology in accordance with common sense and so prepares in advance the way for the pernicious teachings of psychophysical parallelism and epistemological dualism that were directly developed by Malebranche and passed on from him to J. Locke, and became the architectural plan for the development of psychology for the next three centuries. We have a surprising coincidence of two investigations coming from two opposite ends that lead to establishing the fact that the whole argument about the fate of the peripheral theory of emotions is really generated by the contradiction between the peripheral and central theories of passions that, by no means only through an oversight of the great author, are peacefully united in the Cartesian teaching. We have already had the opportunity to cite one of these investigations. As we have seen, it shows convincingly that in a certain respect, the old and contemporary argument between the peripheral and central theories of origin of sensations and emotions, that is, the old argument between James and Wundt and the new argument between followers of James and the founders of the modern central theory of emotions, like Cannon, Dana, Head, and others, is actually a revival in a new form and at a new stage of development of the psychological science of the same controversy that is contained in the teachings of Descartes on the passions. G. Sergi came close to establishing this fact by tracing the fate of Cartesian teaching in contemporary psychology of emotions. Speaking on the centrifugal theory of passions contained in the Cartesian teaching together with the centripetal theory, Sergi correctly cites Wundt and his remarkable theory of innervational sensations. According to this theory, in addition to peripheral motor sensations, also recognized is the existence of motor sensations of central origin that arise due to interaction of motor and sensory centers and allow consciousness to sense motor impulses directly at the moment of their generation. This direct knowledge of the generation of motor impulses is the sensation of innervation. In cases of paralysis and in the absence of any arousal of sensitive nerves of our musculature, consequently with complete absence and even the impossibility of any bodily movement, we can nevertheless retain a sensation of our motor impulses, our motor intentions. Because of this, a hemiplegic can have a motor hallucination.

The Teaching about Emotions

183

The theoretical significance of the theory of innervational sensations was not definitively understood by Wundt himself nor by his opponents. It only aroused consciousness of a completely new principle in the author who introduced it into physiological psychology, a principle that stands in sharp contradiction to generally accepted teaching on the peripheral origin of all sensation that found incontrovertible factual confirmation, and finally the principle that allowed presentation in a more complex form of the relation between mental and physiological processes in the act of volitional intention. This theory opened new, although quite obscure horizons. It allowed a vague hope that, with its help, complex volitional processes would be given a natural, causal, psychophysiological explanation, that they would not be reduced, on the one hand, to a simple mechanism of habit constructed on the circuit of central and peripheral moments of some motor act, and on the other hand, would not be given over wholly to the discretion of spiritualistic theories that would exclude any possibility of a natural explanation. Even its opponents felt this. For this reason, on their part, the theory met a criticism so fierce that it could not be compared to the criticism addressed to the peripheral theory of emotions of James. The opponents were no more conscious than was the author of the theory of its theoretical significance and joined the battle with arguments more of a factual than of a philosophical order. Only later did it become completely clear that the theory of innervational sensations, nowhere visibly and openly opposed to the peripheral theory of emotions, is essentially its principal antagonist since it expresses an opposite philosophical trend that is manifested in it. Behind Wundt and his theory of innervational sensations stood Descartes with his centrifugal theory of passions just exactly as behind James and his peripheral theory of emotions stood the same Descartes with his centripetal theory of the mechanical origin of movements of the soul as sensations and perceptions of visceral changes. Here, something that we have spoken of earlier occurred: the contradictory system of the Cartesian teaching broke into component elements and to each of the opponents was apportioned the honor of developing and defending one part against another. James fought against Wundt and, what seemed to him, the fantastic theory of central sensations. Wundt repudiated the peripheral theory of emotions of James. In both cases, one part of Cartesian teaching rose against another and the Cartesian system was developed from contradictions that tore it apart, but in neither case did physiological psychology emerge from the vicious circle of the system that seemed to be the fateful limit set to the development of psychological thought; it still has not been crossed. G. Sergi always tries to prove that the James-Lange theory in its theoretical and factual content is contained wholly in the teaching of Descartes. This succeeds as long as Sergi considers only one side of the teaching. As soon as he approaches the other side, which contains the theory of centrifugal origin of emotions, by his own admission, he has to forget James and Lange for a certain time and turn to the thesis that is the opposite of the whole spirit of their theory (a thesis that James did not like and against which he fought) in order to find traces of the half-forgotten part of Cartesian teaching lost in contemporary psychology. Sergi says that this thesis was subjected to severe criticism, but the author developed it in an improved form and it could be revived. As we shall see later, the prediction was actually realized. But before we speak of this, we must look more closely at the connection of the Wundt theory and Cartesian teaching. Sergi says that what we find in Descartes concerning the theory of innervational sensations has a much more general significance and includes the whole class of

184

The Teaching about Emotions

perceptions that also encompass passions together with motor sensations. Descartes repeats that the latter material cause of perception is always a special movement of spirits when they exit, as they flow out of the gland, as they leave it and move from the center to the nerves. He admits that the proximate material cause of all perception is not centripetal movement, but centrifugal, and the centrifugal theory of psychological phenomena is thus contained wholly in his teaching on the passions and, moreover, in the most general form. In striving for a full justification of Cartesian teaching, so typical for a certain direction of contemporary psychology that wants to see in this teaching the possibility of applying naturalistic and theological approaches to the human soul, Sergi defends the following idea. Where James appears as an opponent of Descartes, where he differs with the centrifugal theory of passions, there truth seems to be on the side of the great philosopher, those continuing his work, that is, the creators of the innervational theory of sensations-this later augmentation of Cartesian ideas. Sergi even finds factual confirmation for this. It seems to him that a natural consequence of the idea of the central origin of sensations is admitting the fact that the existence of passion is possible with absolute silence of the internal organs, since spirits evoke passion even without reaching these organs in some cases and do not cause any changes in them. This assertion leads us directly and immediately to the experiments of Sherrington: when a dog was separated in a certain way from visceral organs, it retained the ability to experience and exhibit emotion nevertheless. The theory of James cannot avoid the objections raised by Sherrington in any other way than to refer to the possibility of affective hallucinations. Just as hallucinatory perception that is absolutely not peripheral in origin is possible, so hallucinatory emotion is possible, that is, hallucinatory perception of bodily changes in which emotion is usually exhibited when in reality such changes are absent. Descartes decidedly rejects such a possibility, although it seems that his centrifugal theory of emotions leads us directly to the necessity of admitting affective hallucinations. Without referring to them, remaining faithful to the internal logic of its system, the theory of the seventeenth century adjusts less painfully to contemporary experimental knowledge than do its younger brothers-in-progress. It would, however, be a great fallacy to consider that in this case, James is at the pole opposite the Cartesian teaching. It is easy to be convinced of exactly the opposite: of the fact that in disagreeing with a certain part of that teaching, he remains wholly within the limits of its system. As evidence, we would like to refer to two circumstances. The first is connected with James' teaching on affective hallucinations, which we mentioned, which is seemingly a theoretical equivalent of the centrifugal theory of passions, seemingly another variant of solving the same problem that compelled Descartes himself to give up consistent pursuit of the mechanistic peripheral theory of the origin of emotions. The second is connected with the methodological significance of the centrifugal theory of Descartes. We shall consider both circumstances. G. Dumas, studying the history of the development of the teaching on affective hallucinations, says that the physiological experiment only fails to confirm the James-Lange theory, but does not serve as its direct refutation; however, psychological and clinical observation is a basis for taking a more negative position with respect to this theory, since it has at its disposal a number of facts that the peripheral theory cannot explain at all. Here we must give the first place to the problem of higher, or finer, emotions from which James tried to separate himself with the unsubstantiated statement that they can be reduced either to physical satisfaction

The Teaching about Emotions

185

or suffering or to judgments, while, because of the absence or insignificance of peripheral manifestations, they appear to us as emotions of central origin. In the second place, we must put pathological manifestations of joy, which are not like either a joyful excitement or even like a calm experience of a joyful feeling, because the first are completely passive: it is an ecstatic joy, the bliss of saints. From these facts, we can draw a serious argument against the peripheral theory of joy. Self-delusion is usually noted in these cases of a seemingly catalytic state. Saint Thresa 102 describes her state thus: "Even at moments of greatest rapture, the body frequently seems to be dead and completely impotent: it remains in the position in which this state finds it, standing or sitting with hands open or folded. Sometimes in these cases, I almost completely lost my pulse. At least the sisters who were around me at these times assure me of this." p. Janet103 described in great detail the pathological state of ecstasy in which mental experience of joy was accompanied by a slowing of all vital functions. Movements are absent, respiration is attenuated, blood circulation is slowed, and the body is absolutely immobile. Mignard investigated clinically the state of passive joy that he observed even in idiots, patients suffering from senile dementia, and in progressive paralytics. As a result of the investigation, he concluded that in terms of mental changes, joy can be accompanied by an attenuation of all cognitive functions, intellectual, affective, and active, sometimes with complete inertia, and in terms of physical changes, joy can be combined with all the symptoms that are usually considered as characteristic signs of depression: with attenuated breathing and blood circulation, a drop in arterial pressure and temperature, and an inhibition of digestion. Finally, in an even more general form, joy can combine with distinctly manifested states of cachexia and dementia, that is, states of physical and moral decline. Mignard presents a very probable explanation for these states that connects them, just like active joy, with absence of inhibition and full realization of a tendency. There are drives toward peacefulness just as there is drive toward activity, and sleep is a need, the realization of which never stops being pleasant. Passive bliss described and explained by Mignard comes very close to the goals of epicureans. Seeing all the difficulties with which his theory was confronted, James could not bypass with silence the objection based on the presence of ecstatic joy. He himself cites other states of joy, evidently even richer though less characteristic from the organic point of view. He says: "If there is actually a purely spiritual emotion, I would be inclined to limit it to a cerebral sensation of the fullness and lightness of the spirit, a sensation of activity of thought confronted by no impediments. If there is a case of independent emotions, I would assume that the place to look for them would be in these raptures of pure thought" (1902, p. 317). This position of James contains the same explanation of independent emotions aided by a full realization of tendencies, but in addition, it also contains the conception of cerebral synesthesia, which essentially speaks against a purely peripheral theory of emotions. We may confidently assume that in these kinds of cases, we are dealing with hallucinatory phenomena. James advances this hypothesis in order to explain the exception that, from the point of view of his theory, represents a state of ecstasy, but since this hypothesis has no basis other than defending a preconceived theoretical opinion, it may have only the significance of a simple reference. It is not difficult to see that in the teaching on independent emotions, on purely spiritual affects, whether we consider them as affective hallucinations or as completely real affects in the fullness of their psychological nature, James really comes

186

The Teaching about Emotions

to literally the same thing that Descartes came to: specifically, to admitting a purely central origin for emotions. Obviously, the logic of the well-known system, the logic of the facts that survived the system itself, has its own internal development. Whoever accepts one of its parts must unavoidably accept the other no matter how much effort he might make to destroy any trace of it in scientific psychology and no matter how brilliant a psychologist he might be. Now we are fully justified in asserting that consistent carrying through of the mechanical principle in the peripheral theory of passions compelled Descartes to develop the opposite spiritualistic principle in the teaching on the central origin of emotions. Similarly, in the theory of James, if we take it in all its fullness, the laws of physiological mechanics, to which he appeals as to the ultimate source for explaining the nature of human feelings, necessitate assuming, as a complement to the idea of independent emotions, the idea of cerebral, that is, central, sensations, the idea of the purely spiritual innervational sensations of Wundt. Mechanism and spiritualism once again seem to be reunited into one teaching as they were once united in the teaching of Descartes on the passions of the soul. For this reason, it seems to us that Dunlap was not right when, in discussing the controversy among contemporary psychologists relative to the central or peripheral conception of the origin of the sensation of emotions, he connects only one part of this alternative with Descartes. Dunlap, the faithful follower of James, says: I consider emotion as process, and when I try to learn how it arises, the only thing I can find is a change in the visceral organs. Probably James never adopted his own theory fully and to its end. He not only was a partisan of psychological parallelism, but also retained many spiritualistic feelings that he could not subordinate to crude, bodily dependence. You know, we usually perceive our stomach and bowels as something base and vulgar. It is curious that we do not assume that the brain, which is biologically not very significantly above them, is something base in relation to our feelings. The old theory proposed by Descartes according to which afferent currents cause an intellectual state and efferent currents, passions of the soul, seemed to James to be definitively refuted by Miinsterberg. Obviously, James was mistaken (K. Dunlap, 1928, p. 159). Dunlap sees the refutation of the old theory of Descartes in the contemporary thalamic hypothesis on the origin of emotions. Thus, he believes that the current controversy between the visceral and the thalamic theories goes back in only one of its parts to Cartesian teaching. The criterion that allows Dunlap to separate Cartesian theory, which allows innervational feeling on the one hand, from the James-Lange theory on the other, is the question of the mechanism of the origin of emotions-centrifugal or centripetal. The mistake of such a representation arises for two reasons. First, Dunlap ignores the circumstance that Descartes' theory contains not only a centrifugal, but also a centripetal origin of emotions. Of this, we could be adequately convinced through the course of all the preceding consideration of the problem. Thus, the alternatives-central or peripheral origin of emotions-are already wholly included in Cartesian teaching. Some investigators, like Sergi, place the peripheral theory of emotions in the forefront in Cartesian teaching. Others, like Dunlap and Prince, give the first place in this teaching to the central hypothesis. Finally, a third group, like Spearman, see Malebranche as a direct successor in the Cartesian line, a general forefather of all contemporary theories of emotional life: McDougall, A. Bain,l04 and Wundt (c. E. Spearman, 1928, p. 40). Actually, as we have seen, in the eyes of Lange himself, Malebranche was the only person in 200 years who succeeded in creating a vasomotor theory with bodily manifestations of emotions because, with the astuteness of a genius, he discovered the true connection between

The Teaching about Emotions

187

phenomena. Then, in his second investigation, with complete justification, Dunlap sees in Malebranche the real father of all of contemporary introspective psychology. We already know that Malebranche inherited this duality from Descartes. Thus, a necessary correction to the analysis of Dunlap presented above must be the position that both members of the antithesis-the central and peripheral theory of emotions-are equally indebted to Descartes for parentage; in his teaching they are not at all presented as alternatives, as antithesis, as opposite conceptions that exclude each other. The second correction was actually made by Dunlap himself. As we have seen, he must agree with the fact that James never fully accepted his own theory, that together with the peripheral theory of emotions, he retained fully and purely the spiritualistic conception with respect to spiritual feelings, admitting their central origin, as well as the possibility of central sensations. Thus, James, so decisively rejecting the theory of innervational sensations, not knowing how to reconcile them, actually united the two opposite parts of Cartesian teaching. The agreement of James with Descartes is remarkable not only where Sergi so brilliantly proved and substantiated it, that is, not only in the visceral hypothesis of the origin of emotions, but also where Sergi sees-this time, together with Dunlap-the opposition between the two investigations. As we recall, Descartes tries to reconcile the opposite views on the origin of emotions contained in his teaching with the help of the difference between passions-feelings, which arise as a result of changes in the internal organs, and intellectual emotions, which are completely independent of the body. In almost the same words, James speaks of independent emotions that arise from the sensation of the activity of pure, unimpeded thought. With these two essential corrections, we may admit that the picture drawn by Dunlap and his historical analysis are basically correct. His achievement consists in that, proceeding from a point opposite that from which Sergi started his investigation, he was able to reach the same results. Sergi proceeded from Descartes and came to contemporary psychology with the contradictions between opposite conceptions of emotions that split it. Dunlap proceeded from the contemporary scientific controversy and came to Descartes with his contradictory theory of the passions. Thus, both investigations, proceeding from opposite ends, meet at one point and coincide in their definitive results and conclusions. This may serve as yet another proof of how much the main Cartesian problems are not incidental and peripheral reminiscences in contemporary psychology of emotions, but its only genuine and real bases, how much all of contemporary psychology of emotions with all of its achievements and contradictions is Cartesian in the true sense of the word, not only because of a historical connection with the teaching of Descartes, but also because of the fact that it lives and breathes, struggles and suffers thus far in the vicious circle of this teaching. For contemporary psychology of emotions, Descartes is not the distant past, but the very living reality of today. As Moliere's well-known hero, without suspecting it himself, spoke in real prose, contemporary psychology of emotions, without itself realizing it, speaks in the classical and pure prose of the Cartesian 1i'eatise on the Passions of the Soul. But both investigations-that of Sergi and that of Dunlap-agree in still another point of paramount importance. As we have seen, Dunlap was forced to admit that James, in assuming the existence of purely spiritual and independent emotions arid supplementing his peripheral theory with spiritualistic conceptions, actually held to psychophysical parallelism. Irrespective of James, Sergi establishes the same connection between the centrifugal theory of emotions in the teachings of Descartes and parallelism. Thus, the factual coincidence of Descartes and James grows into a most profound philosophical kinship of the two thinkers. Sergi says that the cen-

188

The Teaching about Emotions

trifugal theory of perceptions occupies a most definite place in the history of Cartesian parallelism. The Cartesian teaching on the passions and the theory of James are united not only by the presence of the peripheral and central hypotheses on the origin of emotions, but also by something much more important, specifically, the common solution of the psychophysical problem, the common answer that they give to the question on the relation of thought and space, the soul and the body in human feeling. Without this point, our analysis of the two teachings would be incomplete. For this reason, in the conclusion of the chapter, we shall consider this last point of the present part of our investigation. Thus far in the course of our investigation, we stressed unilaterally only one aspect of the Cartesian solution of the psychophysical problem as applied to the theory of passions. We tried to study the hypothesis of the interaction between the spirit and the body originating in the cerebral gland and the consequences following from that hypothesis. But, as we have said, assuming a direct action of the spirit on the body and the body on the spirit is the exception rather than the rule in Descartes' system. It is in irreconcilable contradiction to the basic thesis of his whole system, according to which thought and space represent opposite substances that exclude each other. The true basis of Cartesian psychology for this reason is not the hypothesis of interaction, but the theory of psychophysical parallelism. The oppositeness of the spirit and the body are for Descartes the basic point of his whole system. Nothing that thinks is spatial. Nothing spatial thinks. Thought and space are different as Descartes expresses himself in the argument with Hobbes,lo5 But if oppositeness or separation between spirit and body is clearly and distinctly conceivable, then, in the natural light of reason, uniting the two must seem inconceivable and impossible; and if such a union actually exists, it contradicts the bases of the system and its explanation subjects Descartes' teaching to the most difficult test. We must investigate whether the philosopher will stand this test without denying his principles. We have already seen that Descartes' system does not stand the test and is forced to change itself in the hypothesis of interactions and to resort to denying its own bases. We shall not repeat everything here that was said in this connection above. Let us recall only this: specifically because of the fact that the psychophysical problem was not at all resolved from the point of view of the absolute dualism of the Cartesian system, Descartes was forced to admit an interaction, trying in every way possible to limit it to only one point in the universe, the insignificant territory of the cerebral gland, and keeping the principle of dualism in full force for all the rest of the infinite universe. Thus, the hypothesis of interaction is not just a basic principle of the system, but its stumbling block, not its basis, but the place of its complete downfall and demise. There are no stronger objections to this system than the incontrovertible facts of nature itself. Man is the negative instance of complete dualism of a spiritual and bodily nature since he is the one and the other together. The philosopher explains: in reality, the spirit and the body are completely separate from each other, there is no communication between them; I see this in the light of reason. Human nature is convinced of the opposite because it represents such communication. According to the concepts of the dualist, natural things are either spirits or bodies. Man is living proof of the opposite: he is a natural being that is simultaneously the one and the other. The voice of his self-confidence says to man: you are spirit. The voice of his natural inclinations and needs says just as clearly: you are body. The substantiality of the spiritual and bodily nature and, at the same time, their dualism

The Teaching about Emotions

189

break down on the concept and the fact of man's existence. The contradiction is so obvious that the philosopher himself admits it. We have seen that the hypothesis of interaction is found in sharp contradiction with all the bases of the Cartesian system. Correctly understood, it leads to their complete denial. The soul is localized and, thus, it is materialized and mechanized. Moving and activated by the body, the soul must itself be bodily; it is made of material substance regardless of all protestations that it is a thinking substance completely different from the body. Cartesian anthropology contradicts not only the dualistic principles of metaphysics, but also the mechanistic principles of natural philosophy. That the amount of movement in the world remains constant, that action and reaction, act and counteract are equal-these fundamental positions of the teaching on movement become invalid as soon as movements in a body are generated by nonmaterial causes. No matter how we thought of the union of the two substances in human nature-as a unity or as a compound-in the one concept and in the other, it contradicts theoretical dualism and leads unavoidably to its opposite. We recalled these positions, which we considered before, only to show the extent to which the hypothesis of interaction contradicts the whole Cartesian system, and consequently cannot be considered as a basic solution of the psychophysical problem for this system. We repeat, this is nothing more than a unique object, the only exception in the world to the general law on relations between thOUght and space. What is the general law? The answer can leave no doubt at all. This general law is the law of parallel and independent existence of absolutely opposite, mutually exclusive substances of thought and space that do not meet anywhere and do not communicate one with other. Actually, what else is parallelism if not the assertion contained in the formula of Descartes that, in reality, the spirit and the body are completely separate from each other and that there is no communication between them? What else can the parallelistic hypothesis mean if not absolute dualism of mental and physical processes? Now we are interested in two points directly connected with the teaching on passions. It might have been thought that Descartes keeps the hypothesis of parallelism for the whole system with the exception of the psychological part in which he rejects parallelism and remains wholly committed to the hypothesis of interaction. But to think that would mean to fall into serious fallacy. As has already been said, to prove this, we shall limit ourselves to considering two points connected with applying the hypothesis of parallelism to an explanation of human passions. The first was already noted by Sergi and is connected with the centrifugal theory of the origin of emotions. The second is directly connected with the problem of sensations in Descartes' teaching and with determining their nature. We shall consider these. If, as the Cartesian centrifugal theory admits, passions of a purely spiritual nature that have no relation to bodily states are possible, if there are intellectual emotions, pure ecstasies of the spirit, sublime feelings without any external or internal manifestations on the one hand, and on the other, sensual passions are possible that arise purely mechanically just as they would arise in a soulless robot, passions of a purely bodily nature that, as we have seen, Descartes himself could not consistently and precisely distinguish from passions of the soul, we can reach no other conclusion from this except a conclusion on absolute independence and parallelism of the spiritual and bodily aspects of human passions. In sensual passions, played out according to mechanical laws completely identically in a soulless robot gifted with the consciousness of man and in intellectual emotions absolutely

190

The Teaching about Emotions

independent of the body, speaking in the words of the Cartesian formula, the spirit and the body would in reality be completely separated from each other and there would be no communication between them. This is what pure parallelism is. But in the Cartesian teaching, parallelism stretches much further. The interaction between the spirit and the body allowed by this teaching is only an instantaneous infraction of the law of parallelism at the moment when vital spirits compel the soul to experience passion, an instantaneous Fall of the soul entering into a bond with the body. Before this insignificant instant and after it, the body and the spirit, experiencing passion, live completely self-sufficient, independent lives subject to opposite laws. In order to be convinced of this, it is necessary to recall three concrete examples considered above, which Descartes uses to develop his idea. The first pertains to emotional reaction as it is played out in a soulless machine. As we recall, Descartes considers a frightening figure acting on a soulless robot and evoking in him a number of motor changes in the muscles and in the internal organs. A picture is created of a frightened living machine preparing to flee. Thus, passion does not yet have anything mental in it. It is played out according to purely mechanical laws and is explained exclusively by the naturalistic principle. Everything occurs as if the soul were completely unnecessary and the body, a perfectly acting robot. This example leads us directly to the second example, in which Descartes returns the soul previously taken away from him to the living robot. Joining mental activity to bodily activity does not essentially change anything in the automatism of passion. To the series of phenomena occurring in the body, another series occurring in the soul is simply joined, which consists of bodily sensations arising in precisely the same way as sensations of external objects. Reducing passions to sensations and perceptions converts them into passive states of the soul that change nothing in the course of automatic passion. With all clarity, Descartes develops the idea of dual effects, maintaining that the movement of vital spirits evoked by perception of the frightening figure results in two mutually independent effects: on the one hand, it activates bodily automatism of passion, and on the other, it evokes, maintains, and reinforces emotion in the soul. Here parallelism reaches its full and definitive form. Finally, from these two examples in which epiphenomenalism of mental emotion appears with maximal distinctness, in which spiritual and bodily effects are so separated from each other that they can be considered completely apart, we move to the third example: to the struggle of the will with passions. We already know that spiritual and bodily passions arise at the sight of a frightening figure as two self-sufficient, independent, and parallel series of phenomena. Tho parallel lines meet, intersect at one point in a completely inexplicable way, and continue to develop completely independently, each according to its own laws. If a living robot were completely without a soul and capable of experiencing passions, nothing would change in the appearance and fate of emotion. This is why Descartes, it seems, is not able to make a precise delimitation between the passions of a robot and the passions of the soul. In the third example, Descartes joins to the robot not only the passive aspect of our soul capable of experiencing perceptions and emotions, but also its active aspect, the will. Here we have the same situation, only in reverse form. The soul, spurred by movement of vital spirits, experiences fear, but the will can induce it to overcome fear and make the organ of the soul reverse direction and, together with it and the vital spirits that prompt movements of the body, cause actions opposite to those that were initially suggested by passion. Instead of flight, the body is incited to fight. Again parallel lines intersect for one instant only to take a parallel course

The Teaching about Emotions

191

again and develop according their own laws. In the teaching on the power of the will over passions, the bodily array, which characterizes passion, is itself converted into an epiphenomenon. As we recall, for Descartes, the struggle of the will with passions occurs not in the spiritual nature of man-no struggle occurs in the soul, but exclusively between the spiritual and bodily aspects of human nature. Actually, there is a conflict between two opposite directions of movements which are communicated to the organ of the soul: one by the body through vital spirits, the other by the soul through the will. The first movement is involuntary and determined exclusively by bodily impressions, the second voluntary and motivated by intention established by the will. The soul overcomes passions with its own weapons and, according to its determination, directs the movement of the body. All three examples culminate in the teaching on purely spiritual emotions that can arise and take their course independently of the body. So, according to Descartes, in the two extreme cases, we can consider passions as either the pure product of bodily automatism or as the pure result of spiritual activity. In the two middle cases, the one and the other meet for an instant only to return once again to the original, independent position. It is enough just to glance at these examples taken together to be convinced that parallelism is the basis not only of the whole system of Descartes, but also of that part of it that comprises the teaching on passions. And in passion, as in the whole universe, space and thought are completely independent of each other and there is no communication between them except for an instantaneous intersection of parallel lines. And in passion, nothing spatial thinks, and nothing that thinks is spatial. The parallels intersected for an instant and again became parallel until the next instantaneous intersection. The hypothesis of interaction is introduced only for explaining this instant; it is nothing other than a forced concession with respect to the incontrovertible fact of combining space and thought in man. But this concession is a momentary weakness of the system itself that is a considerable embarrassment to its author as a betrayal of itself and not at all as its base. Parallelism remains the true base. In Descartes' teaching on the relation between passions and sensations, we find even more serious evidence of the parallelism that is the basis of the teaching on passions. A consideration of this problem is the last task of the present part of our investigation. Two points in Descartes' teaching on the passions are of paramount importance in this respect: first, reducing passion to sensation and perception of intraorganic changes, and, second, recognizing passions as the exclusive property of human nature and rejectipg it in animals. As we recall, Descartes refers passions of the soul to a passive state of our consciousness, considering them as a special case of perception. He speaks of passions as of perceptions and sensations of the soul evoked by sensation of vital spirits; in other places of the 7reatise, he returns many times to this idea, maintaining that passions arise in the soul precisely as they do in the sensing of objects represented by external organs of feelings and are perceived by the soul in precisely the same way. Passions are sensations, but sensations of a special type that represent changes to consciousness occurring not in the external world, but changes of one's own organism. According to Descartes, animals are also living robots. Descartes strictly separates the concept of life and the concept of animateness. A living body is not an animated body, the soul is not a physical principle. The body lives not because the soul moves and warms it, and dies not because the soul leaves it. Life does not consist of the connection of soul and body; death is not their separation. Life and death is an inevitable result of physical causes. Life is a simple mechanism; death

192

The Teaching about Emotions

a destruction of that mechanism. As Descartes himself says, death never follows as a consequence of one of the body's main organs being destroyed. For this reason, we can say that the body of a living man differs from the body of the dead just as a clock (or a robot of a different kind, that is, any kind of self-moving machine), having in it the bodily principle of the movements that it must make, together with all conditions necessary for their working, differs from the complex clock mechanism in which the moving principle has stopped working. Animals are living, but not animated bodies. They are pure robots. However, they possess sensual sensations and instincts that must be considered as bodily movements that originate and can be explained according to mechanical laws. For this reason, everything that is common to man and to animals must inevitably be considered as a phenomenon of a purely bodily nature. For this reason, sensations, attractions in general (so also with respect to man) must be considered as mechanical phenomena that do not have anything in common with mental activity. Dualism between animals and man moves Descartes toward the inevitable conclusion that animals are devoid of passions, since he considers passions as movements of the soul. Here arises one of the most insoluble contradictions of the whole system which encompasses the problem of sensation. Descartes' teaching vacillates with respect to sensations and, because of dualistic and anthropological principles, goes in three completely different directions. Initial reflections treat sensations and sensual perceptions as mental facts and refer them only to the spirit. The next reflections consider them to be anthropological facts and refer them to the connections of spirit and body, and the work on the passions ascribes to them the significance only of bodily mental facts and refers sensations and instincts exclusively to the body. The attempt to explain the problem tangles us in snares of antinomy and dilemma. Sensation is once recognized as a purely bodily fact subject to mechanical explanation, and again as purely spiritual requiring spiritualistic consideration. Remaining within the system, it is just as impossible to accept sensation as it is impossible to deny it. More briefly stated, from the point of Descartes' teaching, the fact of sensation is not explained and is inexplicable. We are now interested in the contradiction exclusively in connection with the teaching on the passions. Here the contradiction is even more monstrous. On the one hand, as we have seen, the soulless robot is fully capable of experiencing passions, and on the other, animals are devoid of passions. On the one hand, passion is nothing other than a sensation arising in the soul, and on the other, sensation is nothing other than a purely bodily phenomenon. The only conclusion that can be reached is the following: since he is guided in his teaching on the passions by the naturalistic principle, Descartes inevitably comes to admitting a pure epiphenomenalism and human automatism in the rise and development of passions because, defining passion as sensation, and sensation as a bodily phenomenon, without noticing it himself, he maintains that passion cannot exist as a basic phenomenon of human, that is, dual, spirit-body nature. On the one hand, all passions that refer to the body remain purely bodily phenomena because even sensation, which passion is essentially, considered from the mental aspect, is inherent in the animal and is a mechanical phenomenon arising in a moving machine. On the other hand, there are purely spiritual passions independent of the body. The dualism of thought and space, pure and consistent parallelism, spoke its final and decisive word at this point. There are bodily passions and there are spiritual passions. No passion is possible that would be simultaneously bodily and spiritual, in which an actual communication, an actual connection between spirit and body would be possible, just as it is impossible that anything spatial would think and anything thinking would be spatial.

The Teaching about Emotions

193

Thus, we approach the last point of the whole Cartesian teaching on the passions, the point that finished with a grandiose catastrophe, a complete destruction, all of this heroic attempt to explain the nature of human passions proceeding from the dualistic principles of the system. The end of the teaching is a full and entire negation of its beginning. Passions seem to be divided between the spiritual and the bodily nature of man, and each nature acts completely independently of the other. Where in this teaching is there a place for passion as a basic phenomenon of a dual, spirit-body nature of man that would be the only real basis of passions? In the teaching on passions, spiritualism and naturalism are two opposite poles. Dualism and parallelism appear as the actual base of the teaching on passions. Epiphenomenalism and human automatism are the beginning and the end, the first and the last words of all the psychology of passions.

16

We can be convinced from the last work of Descartes on this problem-his letter on love-that parallelism was actually the final thought of all his reflection on the nature of human passions. In this letter, he tells the Swedish queen lO6 what the essence of love is and which is worse: excessive love or excessive hatred. The letter is a short exposition of the last thoughts of Descartes on the essence of love as well as on the essence of all human passion. "This letter is a small masterpiece in which every expert in philosophy, not knowing about the author and the reasons for the letter, but paying attention only to the course of the study, to the nature of the ideas, the selection of expressions, would say immediately: this is real Descartes. There is no other work this short (it stays within the framework of a letter) that would make it possible to know this thinker better" (K. Fischer, 1906, Vol. I, p. 258). In the letter, Descartes begins directly to distinguish intellectual and affective love, thus making the point of departure the distinction of spiritual and corporal passions that in the Treatise on the Passions . .. was only the concluding point. There is love as spiritual passion and there is love as sensual passion. The first arises from the fact that we know an object, the presence and possession of which gives us joy and the absence or loss of which causes suffering. For this reason, we strive toward this object with the full energy of our will. We want to unite with it or form a single whole with it and be only a part of that whole. Love is necessarily connected with joy, with sorrow, and with desire. These four tendencies of the will have their roots in the nature of the spirit and are inherent in the mental bond with the body. They are contained in the cognitive need of the thinking being. The joy and suffering of intellectual love are for this reason not passions, but clear ideas. Even if an analysis of the Treatise on the Passions . .. did not bring us to the conclusion on the presence in Descartes' teaching of admitting purely spiritual passions, the letter would convince us of this. Developing the idea of the purely spiritual nature of intellectual passion sequentially, Descartes establishes that only by obscuring it can affective and sensual love appear based on the union of the soul with the body. These are the corporal states and changes to which that certain striving in our soul corresponds, but here the similarity and connection between them are not obvious to us. In this way, vague, sensual affective desires arise that have certain objectives that bring joy from possession of some objects and painful suffering from the presence of others, that cause loving the objects of striving and hating the objects of aversion: joy and sorrow, love and hatred-such are the basic

194

The Teaching about Emotions

forms of sensual desires, the elementary and principal passions from the mixing and changing of which all the others are created; they are the only ones that we had even before birth because they are already manifested during feeding in embryonal life. Intellectual love coincides with the need for cognition in our thinking nature; sensual love is rooted in the needs for nourishment of the organic nature. There are representations of objects worthy of striving (intellectual love) without sensual arousal and sensual desire, just as the latter may occur without cognition. There is love without passion and there is passion without love. In the ordinary sense, in human love, the two elements are united. The body and the soul are united in such a way that certain states of representation will accompany certain states of bodily organs and elicit each other mutually, like thought and word. In the same way, love finds its involuntary bodily expression in the excitation of the heart with accelerated movement of the blood. This spiritual-sensual love, this union of comprehension and striving, forms the sensation about whose essence the queen was asking. Thus, the initial independence of intellectual and sensual, of spiritual and corporal passion, incomprehensible and inexplicable, the parallel accompanying development of the one and the other, the full possibility of their separate existence, the accidental quality of their uniting, which leads only to obscuring intellectual love, in short, all of the basic theses of Cartesian parallelism in the teaching on the passions, of the parallelism that leads to a complete separation of spiritualistic and naturalistic principles that the philosopher carefully tried to unite in his Treatise, appear here with a clarity that leaves no doubt about the true nature of Descartes' teaching on the passions. But whoever takes the Cartesian path must inevitably come to his end point, the Cartesian conclusions. We have already seen that James not only started on this path, but inevitably had to supplement his automatic theory of emotions with the teaching on intellectual feelings independent of the body. In order to explain ecstatic joy, he had to admit the presence of emotions and sensations of a purely central origin from the beginning. But, as Dumas correctly notes, the problem must be formulated in relation not only to passive joy, but also to active joy. If once we admit the possibility of cerebral synesthesia to explain ecstasy, it is impossible for us to maintain that it does not play any role in any other emotions. Dumas says that if we follow to the end the theory of Lange and James, we can assert that active joy is reduced to consciousness of muscular tonus and all peripheral reactions. But we have just seen that the presence of cerebral synesthesia that elicits joy was admitted to explain ecstasy, and for this reason, we must distinguish in active joy the consciousness of organic excitation and the consciousness of this cerebral synesthesia. This pertains also to passive and active sorrow. Continuing in essence the idea of James, depending on Sherrington's experiments and on facts of passive joy and melancholic stupor, Dumas assumes the concept of cerebral feelings of satisfaction and suffering that are not subject to peripheral explanation. The fact of absence of conscious sensitivity during electrical and traumatic stimulation of the brain, firmly established in physiology, speaks against this assumption. But there is a great difference between these rough and nonspecific and specific functional excitations, and, without falling into a contradiction with physiology, we can postulate a synesthesia of the brain. As we have seen, this conclusion is also reached in the contemporary thalamic theory of emotions, which sees the thalamus as the real source for the origin of specific emotional tone. The same point of view is supported by a number of other investigators with

The Teaching about Emotions

195

data of chronaxie, who construct a hypothetical mechanism for this central synesthesia. Dumas himself believes that in postulating emotions of a purely central origin, he only develops and supplements the theory of James. Challenging the place of the James-Lange theory in the difficult problem of the nature of emotions, we are trying to introduce more precise definitions into this theory that might make it more complex and more flexible, and in this way, reject the paradoxical and oversimplified formulae with which James wanted to startle our imagination. It remains for us only to show that, according to the reliable note of Dunlap, James, remaining a consistent parallelist, not accepting to its end his own peripheral theory and assuming the existence of purely spiritualistic feelings, at the same time, preserved in his second spiritualistic theory all the theoretical bases of his main hypothesis. We recall that in this hypothesis, James considers exclusively the sensory, passive aspect of emotional reactions and arrives at epiphenomenalism and automatism. In his eyes, emotions represent unnecessary rudiments of long extinct animal adaptations or random reactions of a pathological or idiopathic character that do not admit any kind of sensible explanation. In his teaching on purely spiritual emotions, James not only openly takes the point of view of pure spiritualism, but even brings to unnatural proportions the thesis of epiphenomenalism of the mental aspect of our emotions. Developing the basic hypothesis, James maintained that human emotion, devoid of any corporal backing, is empty sound. In his opinion, all higher feelings which he cannot finally subject to the laws of physiological mechanics must naturally be such an empty sound. To a still greater degree, the most spiritual emotions which arise from the activity of pure thought, and which James considers as not depending on the body, must also be such an empty sound. Defending his basic hypothesis, James does not maintain that such emotion is something that contradicts the nature of things and that pure spirits are sentenced to a passionless and intellectual existence. He wants only to say that emotion estranged from all corporal feelings is something unimaginable. The more we analyze our spiritual state, the more we are convinced that base passions and inclinations that we experience are essentially created and elicited by the bodily changes that we usually call their manifestations or results; all the more, it seems probable to us that if our organism becomes anesthetic, the life of affects, both pleasant and unpleasant, would become completely foreign to us and we would have to eke out an existence of a purely cognitive or intellectual character. Although such existence might seem ideal to ancient sages, for us who are several generations removed from the philosophical epoch that brought sensitivity to the forefront, it must seem somewhat apathetic and spiritless for us to strive toward it so stubbornly (w. James, 1902, pp. 316-317). Three points may interest us as definitive results of comparing these two theories of James-the mechanistic and the spiritualistic. The first consists of the fact that the basic hypothesis of James assumes the existence of emotions, devoid of any corporal backing, only in the passionless intellectual existence of pure spirits, and excludes it from human existence. But if in the second theory, James considers higher emotions of the soul as arising from activity of pure, unimpeded thought, as independent of the body, by the same token, he takes the position of pure spiritualism, accepting the presence in human consciousness of emotions inherent exclusively absolutely in spiritual existence. The second point pertains to evaluating the role that the spiritual aspect of our emotions plays. If in the basic hypothesis of James, it appears as an unnecessary adjunct, as a secondary phenomenon, which does not in any way participate in the real life of man, as a simple consciousness of peripheral changes, it must be de-

196

The Teaching about Emotions

preciated to such an absolute degree that all human feeling, representing nothing other than simple consciousness of trembling and the sensation of distension of the nostrils, must seem an empty sound, the apathetic and spiritless activity of consciousness sentenced to eking out a wretched existence of a purely cognitive nature. This epiphenomenalism in the teaching on emotions reaches Homeric proportions in the hypothesis of James according to which all higher, purely spiritual feelings of central origin must be considered simply as affective hallucinations. To declare the higher life of feeling a simple merging of hallucinatory experiences, to declare higher emotion a hallucination, means to bring to an extreme the epiphenomenalism that is already contained in the James hypothesis. If higher joy accessible to man is nothing other than hallucination, then this is the last word in making human feeling meaningless. Finally, the third point that appears from the method of examination we have adopted, in which one theory of James illuminates another, is the surprising coincidence between him and Lange in determining the definitive fate of feeling in the history of human development. We have already tried to show that the theory of James excludes any possibility of development of emotions. Now we might supplement that position by showing that, in reality, it assumes their full demise as the only real outcome of the historical development of man. James, like Lange, begins with a hymn in honor of sensual emotions. An individual without feelings seems to him to be apathetic and spiritless and existence without feelings to be pathetic and impossible for man. But if we ask what these feelings are that are satiating our lives with sense and animating them, it develops that they are only pathetic rudiments of animal life, pale metaphors of our sensual reactions, problematic and artificial, idiopathic and pathological habits of the human species, simply accidental reactions of the organism, passively reflected in consciousness, like seasickness and ticklishness, shyness and love. Like someone drowning, James grasps at the straw of the first bodily experience that comes to hand, as if it were genuine, vital, and indubitable. It was he who generated in all later psychology the increased attention to the least, scarcely noticeable, insignificant bodily manifestations. He is the one who inspired the idea that in the tension of the vocal chords and language we should see the true basis of human thought. As G. Stout 107 correctly notes, James equates spiritual activity with certain muscular sensations. li'ying to show what is the central nucleus of activity that we call ours, James again, as in the teaching on emotions, tries to grasp at the straw that might have saved him from ruin. This nucleus, in his words, he found in certain intracephalic movements, which we usually contrast, as subjective, to the activity of the supercorporeal world. As justification, James says that he raised only the question of what kind of activity deserves to be called ours. Since we are personalities, as opposed to the environment, movements that originate in our bodies figure as our activity, and James cannot point to any other activities that would be ours in the strict facial (Russian Edition Editor: personal) sense of the word. James proposes the completely correct position: since thoughts and feelings in themselves cannot be active, they become such only by inciting activity of the body. The body is the center of the cyclone, the origin of the coordinates. Everything turns on the body, everything is felt from its point of view. All of this is incontrovertible. All of this is indisputably true. The tragic element of James' position, and with his, that of all of contemporary psychology, consists only in that they cannot find any way to understanding a truly sensible connection between our thoughts and feelings on the one hand, and the activity of our body

The Teaching about Emotions

197

on the other. In the end, speaking in the language of James, the whole question might have been contained in a nutshell: how are our consciousness and our real vital life connected? Unless we understand this connection, consciousness will inevitably seem to be an epiphenomenon, a pathetic and useless adjunct to the automatic activity of our body, a passive reflection of changes occurring in it, and, in the best case, a chain of hallucinations of a dreaming spirit. But the misfortune is that the question of consciousness, and especially of emotions, is posed and resolved by James and all of contemporary psychology in such a way that the complete impossibility of finding any sensible connection between passions of the soul and real life of man is predetermined. This is why James cannot find the path from our thoughts and feelings, from our spiritual activity, to our real human life in all the inexhaustible richness of its actual content. Like a drowning man clutching at a straw, he must clutch at evidence of internal experience of the fact that spiritual activity consists of movements occurring in the head. Actually, in the interpretation of James, no sensible and intelligent path leads us from emotions-with the sensations of excitation in internal organs, weakness of the limbs, shallow breathing lying at their base-to acts and actions of man, to his internal and external struggle, to his lively love and hatred, to his suffering and celebration. If, as James says, the essence of the emotion of fear actually consists of a sensation of accelerated heart rate, shallow breathing, trembling of the lips, weakness of the limbs, goose bumps and internal agitation, then no psychology except one believing in miracles will ever be able to explain for us the terrible weakness of the human spirit that we call repudiation and treachery from fright. If, as James says, the feeling of moral rectitude consists of changes in the sound of the voice and expression of the eyes, etc., then psychology will never be able to explain why Socrates sat in the dungeon and did not flee as his friends advised him to, and did not experience a feeling of fright with weakness of the limbs and shallow breathing. The terrible result to which contemporary psychology of emotions brings us is to completely deprive the passions of the soul of meaning and to completely eliminate any hope of our ever understanding the vital significance of passion and with it, all of human consciousness. In reality, this result is already wholly and fully contained in Cartesian teaching, which we have just considered. Basic and most terrible in this teaching is the fact that the soul from beginning to end is assumed to be outside of life. It does not at all participate in the life of the body. Life-common to us and to animals-belongs wholly to space and in this way excludes any possibility of the intimacy of which James speaks, the possibility of any interest and participation of the soul in real existence and in the real fate of the robot that our body is. Although, according to Descartes' statement, affects make it clear to us that we are in our own body not like a swimmer in a boat, but connected with it in the closest way, as if mixed in so that we form a single being, 108 with all his teaching on the passions, he maintains the opposite. His will controls passions similarly to the way a sea captain navigates when something in the ship breaks. Using the example of intellectual and sensual love, Descartes shows the whole meaninglessness, unnaturalness, incomprehensibility, and inexplicability of the connection between the one form of passion and the other. What can there be that is common between the basic forms of sensual passions, which we had even before birth and which are rooted in the needs for nourishment during the period of embryonal life, and the sublime passions of the spirit, the source of which are the needs of cognition rooted in our thinking nature? We understand the statement of Descartes that they can and must exist separately, but we absolutely cannot under-

198

The Teaching about Emotions

stand how they can exist together. Descartes himself states that the similarity and connection between them cannot be obvious to us. Thus, the parallelistic solution of the psychophysical problem-dualism of the body and spirit, dualism between animals and man-inevitably leads to the same terrible idea of Descartes, to the complete separation of consciousness and life. Life, according to Descartes, not only does not include the possibility and necessity of consciousness, but completely excludes this possibility. From the very beginning, the soul is placed outside life. This fact leads to the situation that the psychophysical problem in Descartes' solution, that most abstract of all problems, it would seem, turns here into a problem of the role and meaning of passions of the soul in the life of man, a problem that gets the most hopeless solution of all the problems that confront humanity at any time: life seems to be absolutely meaningless, passions, absolutely lifeless. But does not the contemporary psychology of emotions bring us to the same thing? On the one hand, there is the theory of innate emotions that exist even before birth, rooted in the animal nature of man, of archaic, rudimentary, random, absolutely meaningless and unnecessary organic sensations, and on the other hand, the theory of independent emotions, those lifeless hallucinations of the spirit. M. Prince is right when he says that contemporary psychology is confronted by a dilemma: either to break through to some kind of understanding, even the most remote and vague, of the unity of emotional consciousness and life, or to adopt an alternative hypothesis of dualism and parallelism, that is, epiphenomenalism and human automatism (M. Prince, 1928, p. 167). Contemporary psychology, like Buridan's ass, stands between these hypotheses incapable of selecting one of them, incapable of solving the most basic question, the problem of problems that McDougall formulates in two words: man or robot. All contemporary psychology of emotions, without exaggeration and to the same extent as the Cartesian, is either a psychology of passions of a soulless robot or a psychology of independent emotions of lifeless spirits. All contemporary psychology of emotions can, for this reason, be recognized as whatever one wants, but not as a psychology of man. What has occurred in contemporary psychology of emotions might best be expressed by the hopeless cry of one of the heroes of a Chekhov drama, a senile old man left in an abandoned house in which the windows are being boarded up: "Man they killed!"t09 The pathos of the absolute meaninglessness of organic feeling and the absolute lifelessness of independent, hallucinatory emotions may nourish only the psychology of soulless robots or the psychology of purely rational man, who was the ideal of Kant and whose advent Lange predicted on the basis of his theory, the psychology of those pure spirits sentenced to passionless, intellectual existence, who seem to be, in the words of James, the ideal of the ancient sages. Death is the last word of all contemporary psychology of emotions that, following Descartes, from the very beginning assumes a soul outside of life. It remains only to board up more firmly the windows of the empty house in which, according to the assertion of all real experts of the human spirit, there was always the sound of lively and passionate life and which is now only the last habitat of slain man. But in this final act of the drama, with the spectacle of such a hopeless finale of the classical psychology of emotions, we are enveloped by a feeling of astonishment from which, according to the assertion of Aristotle and, following him, Descartes, philosophy begins. This feeling of astonishment, fraught with philosophical reflection, was demonstrated by one of the Russian poets. He caught sight of a leper eating grains of henbane, poisoned by their venom and experiencing the same state of great bliss that Mignard observed in idiots, in the feeble-minded, in

The Teaching about Emotions

199

patients suffering from cachexia and dementia, in paralytics and the insane in a state of complete mental and moral decrepitude. If a fate of sublime joy is to be the lot of idiots and the insane, then involuntarily the idea arises of the complete meaninglessness of human passions as well as of all the life of consciousness.

17 It remains for us to sum up this protracted examination of the philosophical nature of contemporary psychology of emotions and, in concise conclusions, to define the fate of the Cartesian teaching on the passions in the development of psychological knowledge and the basic problems that confront the psychology of the future in connection with the final disintegration of the Cartesian thought dominant in this area of scientific experimental knowledge. We think that the whole course of the preceding investigation has demonstrated the correctness of our principal thesis with sufficient clarity: the James-Lange theory and the whole system of critical studies developed around it, which led before our eyes to the construction of a new theory of emotions, is directly connected, if we explain its ideological genesis and methodological basis, not with the teaching of Spinoza on the passions, but with the ideas of Descartes and Malebranche. It remains for us only to reach conclusions from this position and to evaluate theoretically the significance of this fact for further development of psychology as a science. We will evaluate primarily the theoretical metaphysics of all of that philosophical and scientific trend of thought that unites and combines into one current the theories of Descartes and James. We have already had the opportunity to explain in detail that the theory of James and the theory of Lange, no matter how they seem at first glance to be strictly and consistently biological theories, even in a certain sense, a great triumph of the biological idea in psychology, actually are essentially antibiological theories since they are characterized by two basic traits: (1) the complete absence and basic impossibility of applying the idea of development to the sphere of activity that they study; (2) the epiphenomenalistic representation of the meaning of emotions that is the basis of this. We have only to trace the roots of this metaphysical conception that excludes any possibility of development in the area of emotions and any rational and comprehensible connection between emotions and consciousness as a whole, between emotions and the real life of man. It is easy to see that these roots are fixed in the Cartesian teaching on passions of the soul. As we know, the idea of development was not at all foreign to Descartes. Regardless of the fact that the cosmology and physics of Descartes, as Y. F. Asmus correctly notes, were immutably mechanistic, that Descartes understood interaction not as a dynamic counteraction, but as a mechanistic transmission of movement from body to body, nevertheless Descartes introduced the idea of development into cosmology and his cosmology is still cosmology. "He actually was the first in a new time to construct a cosmological theory grandiose in the extent of its synthetic scope" (Y. F. Asmus, 1929, p. 35). However, even in the area of the cosmological problem, Descartes remains to a much greater degree a consistent adherent of the theological and mechanistic principles of his system than of the principle of development. He remains in complete agreement with the church teaching on the creation of the world, which for him was unshakable truth. He says: "There is no doubt that the world from the beginning was created in all its perfection in such a way that the sun, the earth, the moon, and the stars existed in it. On the earth there were not only embryos of plants, but plants themselves. Adam and Eve were created

200

The Teaching about Emotions

not as children, but as adults. Christian faith and natural intellect convince us of this" (Descartes, 1914, p. 7). Just as precisely, Descartes preserves complete faith for the principle of explaining the world which he represents as an infinite aggregate of bodies mechanically acting on each other. For him, the basic law of nature remains the law of inertia. All bodies are infinitely divisible. As Fischer correctly notes, Descartes' explanation of nature rests wholly on mathematical-mechanical principles. The explanation of life and consciousness in Descartes' philosophy is even more metaphysical, even more subject to mechanistic and theological principles. True, here we also find timid hints of the problem of development, which was itself knocking at the door but against which the doors of the Cartesian teaching seemed to be tightly shut. For a better understanding of the nature of plants or animals, says Descartes, it is much preferable to reason as if they were gradually generated from seeds and not created by God at the beginning of the world. In this way, we can discover certain simply and easily understood principles. From these principles, as from a seed, we can demonstrate the origin of stars, the earth, and everything that we can grasp in the visible world. As we know, Descartes was concerned with the problem of embryonal development. For him, the solution of the anthropological problem encompassed physiological, psychological, and ethical problems. For him, the physiological problem pertaining to organs and functions of the human body was closely connected with the zoological. The organization of the human body must be learned from its beginning, from the history of the development of the embryo, but the latter, however, from the history of the formation of animal organisms. The philosopher constantly and zealously strives to penetrate into this area with the aid of anatomical studies of a comparative nature. Here, he draws directly from the spring of nature. He dissected animals and tried in this way to find a solution to the physiological problem experimentally. That Descartes sought a resolution of the secrets of life with the aid of comparative anatomy and embryology is remarkable evidence of his methodical thinking, which, in opinions of his biological work, must be placed above the great or small value of the results of this work. He was interested very early in the beginning of animals and wrote a work on the human body. This is his treatise on man, considering digestion, blood circulation, respiration, muscle movement, actions of organs of feeling and sensation, internal movements and functions of the brain. In a reworking of the treatise, a new work covering animals and man appeared: a description of the functions of the human organism and an explanation of the formation of the animal, or, as the title states, a Treatise on the Formation of the Embryo .110 That the idea of the origin of animals and embryonal development was important to Descartes is clear from his letter to Elizabeth in which he touches on the question of the reworking of the Treatise that was just mentioned. He writes that in describing animal and human functions, he even dared to introduce and develop the history of the formation of the animal from its very beginning. He speaks about the animal in general since, as far as man in particular is concerned, he cannot elaborate because of lack of appropriate experience. In the letter, Descartes notes with complete clarity the limits of his idea of development. There can be no doubt that the problem of the development of animals and the embryo was vaguely present in his consciousness. However, he worked on this problem with respect to animals in general and not with respect to man in particular. Not only lack of experience thwarted this, as Descartes explains, but also something much more basic and essential. The foundation itself of Cartesian psychology completely excludes the possibility of the origin of even a vague idea of development. The

The Teaching about Emotions

201

animal is another matter. We know that Descartes considers the organism as a self-moving mechanism, wholly subject to laws of nature and, for this reason, requiring a naturalistic explanation. In the psychological problem, on the other hand, Descartes is bound to the theological principle. From the fact that from the very beginning, Descartes assumes a soul outside of life and considers the problem of life on an exclusively mechanistic plane, from the fact that he establishes absolute dualism of man and animal, and finally, from the fact that he considers thought and space in man as completely separate and mutually exclusive substances, it follows that he is faced not only with the basic impossibility of introducing a soul into his teaching on the passions and even the most vague rudiments and premonitions of a historical explanation, but he is forced to develop this teaching on a plane of basic anithistoricism, as a purely metaphysical teaching. As we have seen, Descartes teaches that our primitive passions are rooted in the history of embryonal development. These are passions that originate from the embryo's vital need of nourishment. They develop around the digestive tract. No matter how complex they become subsequently, their whole history is only a superstructure over an unchangeable physiological base of the first days. The mechanism of passions of the adult person has its source in the structure and functioning of the embryonal machine. As we recall, specifically this circumstance caused Descartes to solve the problem of the causal explanation of the visceral nature of our passions. In this respect, Descartes was simply more consistent than his later pupils. If passion is nothing other than a change in the internal organs (the stomach, intestines, the heart), then the problem arises of making the connection between them rational and comprehensible, of finding its proximate cause. Descartes solves the problem by finding the cause and sense of this connection in the embryonal origin of passions. As distinct from later investigators, he is not satisfied with a simple description of the mechanism of passions, but wants to explain why in love and sorrow, the stomach exhibits great visceral activity, and in hatred and joy, this activity decreases. It is immaterial that Descartes was so ready to admit an opposite connection between digestion and the passions also. Now the limitless pliancy of thOUght allows the assumption that the embryo experiences sorrow because it obtains inappropriate nourishment and is overjoyed because his stomach is being prepared for the intake of good food. No matter how firm the explanation and its factual certainty is, the basic status of the matter does not change by even an iota. It is important, and actually most important, that Descartes tries to give a causal explanation of the gastric origin of love and hatred, joy and sorrow, and that he finds this cause of basic passions of the soul in embryonal life. In this position, full of theoretical significance, the visceral theory of passions finds completion and discloses the deepest and final bases. We cannot agree with Sergi, who believes that the shakiness of Descartes' factual explanations, his easy compliance, his readiness to change established connections between passions and the nourishment needs of the embryo compromise his whole structure. On the contrary, only at this point does the system acquire its true significance and sense. Specifically here Descartes appears as the physicist he wanted to be in the Treatise on the Passions of the Soul. Only by taking into account the teaching on the embryonal origin of our basic passions of the soul can we accurately evaluate the whole conception of Descartes and understand its true relation to contemporary visceral theories of emotions. The basic passions of the soul are already established in the embryonal life of man, that is, they exist before birth. All later, complex, and derivative passions are nothing

202

The Teaching about Emotions

other than variations or modifications of the embryonal states. In the letter on love, Descartes says directly that the basic forms of sensual desires, the elementary and principal passions from whose mixing and changing all the rest are created, appear during the periods of nourishment in embryonal life and are rooted in the nourishment needs of the organism.
