For this reason, these passions are a simple reflection in the consciousness of complex organic states. It is impossible to develop more fully and clearly the theory of innate passions than does Descartes. Discussions pertaining to this question are a direct analogy to his teaching on innate ideas. As we know, the idea of God is presented in Descartes' teaching as a primordial given, not mediated by feelings nor in any other way, that is, as an innate idea that God imprinted in us, as an artist imprints his work. The basic passions are innate features of the corporal nature of man to the same extent that basic ideas are innate features of his spiritual nature. Descartes says: I do not maintain that the spirit of the infant in the mother's womb reflects on metaphysical problems, but he has an idea of God, of himself, and of all the truths that are known of themselves as they are in adults when they do not think at all about these truths. Just as Descartes admits basic ideas that are known of themselves as innate and already present in the spirit of the infant in the womb of its mother, he admits that the spirit of the infant in the womb of its mother already experiences the basic passions of the soul-love and hatred, joy and sorrow-as adults do. The teaching on the fact that basic passions of the soul are rooted in needs for nourishment in embryonal life and, as a result of this, are innate not only does not divert Descartes from his original path, not only does not represent a change in his whole basic conception, but, on the contrary, forms the concluding point of his investigation. He came to the point toward which he directed his steps. The idea of innate passions at first gives the whole teaching the significance of a basic conception, or a complete philosophical construction true to the basic principles of Descartes' whole system, and raises it above the level of simple physiological representations and surmises that inevitably become obsolete in time. No matter how vague Descartes' ideas on the real physiology of the human organism were, his philosophical idea on the nature of passions was for him transparent and clear to the end and remains so for us also. Only for this reason could it survive the centuries and be preserved as a living part of all of recent psychology. As we have said, this idea gives the only possible meaning to the whole visceral theory of emotions. Only it makes understanding the rational connection that exists between passions of the soul and the reality of the internal organs possible. For this reason, it is the last word of the visceral theory without which, as we shall see, all other variants of this hypothesis remain incomplete. The innateness of passions is the final base of the visceral theory. Here we disagree decisively with Sergi, who bypasses this idea without understanding its theoretical significance and, for this reason, is inclined to see in it rather a deviation from the general conception than its completion. He calls Descartes the true founder of the visceral theory of emotions, depending exclusively on the purely physiological ideas of the philosopher. Moreover, only the teaching on innate passions, crowning the whole construction, gives us the right to consider Descartes as the real father of all of contemporary psychology of emotions since it revolves around the organic hypothesis on the nature of human feeling as around its axis. According to Sergi's recapitulation of the content of Cartesian teaching, behind the finalistic appearance, behind the teaching on the intellectual emotions, almost foreign to man, behind sensations, recollections, and judgments, operates a mechanism that might be schematically portrayed in several words. External stimulation

The Teaching about Emotions

203

and psychological and intellectual phenomena cannot arise without a very high degree of various centrifugal nerve currents; some of these provide more or less successful adaptation of the human machine to external circumstances, others are directed toward deep organs, and changes elicited there are transmitted in consciousness or into the language of visceral sensations or into the language of original emotions that are not reducible to anything else, Sergi says, completely bypassing the problem of innate passions. Sergi continues, the road Descartes chose, which he continued and made passable over such a great expanse, was later followed by numerous investigators and remains up-to-date now more than ever. It is frequently said condescendingly that he was the precursor, but if his archaisms are translated into our language, if the idea that animated him is freed from factual errors that now seem completely harmless, he would have to be accorded his real title as the founder of the theory, Sergi maintains. We cannot but agree with this if we keep in mind not only what Sergi says over the course of his investigation, but also the philosophical significance of the theory founded by Descartes culminating in the teaching on innate passions. Specifically that part of the teaching survived the rest of the theory and formed the base of contemporary variants of the visceral hypothesis. Strange as it may seem, thus far the fact has not been noted that the JamesLange theory is a theory of innate emotions. Bodily manifestations-that source and essence of emotional experience-arise purely reflexively. Like all other reflexes, they are innate reactions of the organism pre-established and prepared in advance by the whole course of zoological and embryonal development. They are inherent in man due to the organization of his organism and, strictly speaking, they exclude any possibility of development. As we know, James saw a special advantage of his theory in the fact that it makes possible a causal explanation of emotions (which he finds in reflex acts), and in the fact that this explanation makes the amazing variety of emotions comprehensible for us. We know that James, if he were living now, would be deeply disappointed with the expectations connected with the possibility of explaining the amazing variability of emotions on the basis of his theory. He would have to admit that the bodily manifestations of emotions are extremely uniform and stereotypic, and because of this alone, they cannot serve as a source of an explanation of all the variety of emotional reactions. But this is not of interest to us now. It is much more important for us to establish that James, seeing the cause of emotions in innumerable reflex acts, affirmed in contemporary psychology the Cartesian teaching on innate passions. It is true that the most various reflex actions are possible and that these actions vary endlessly. But it is even more true that the reflex act is an innate reaction of the organism, the most common in all the individuals of a given species, that it is the most changeless and absolute of all other forms of human behavior. If reflex acts are the causes of emotions, we have no other possibility of explaining emotions except for admitting that they are innate passions. With all changes, emotions remain eternal, inviolable essences, like species that were at one time considered changeless in biology. As we see, James comes to rejecting what he started with. As species were at one time considered changeless essences due to the absence of the idea of development of a theory of evolution, so a theory of emotions that excludes the possibility of development inevitably leads us to admitting emotions to be eternal, inviolable, changeless essences. We have tried to show above that in a radical way this theory excludes all possibility of development. Not evolution, but involution; not development, but convolution; not a complication and a conversion into higher forms, but a dying off and conversion into rudimentary

204

The Teaching about Emotions

vestiges; not progenetic functions with a future, but degenerating, archaic functions, survivors of a long past function-this is what the James theory represents. Its final word announces that emotion is a chance pathological reaction, a useless and unnecessary survivor of antiquity incapable of any development. This is even more distinct in the theory of Lange, who settles on the fact that the emotions are elicited mainly not by a simple impression from some organ of feeling, but by mental causes, recollection, and association of ideas, even if association itself is evoked by a sensual impression. To explain this, Lange develops the theory completely in the spirit of the teaching on conditioned reflexes, showing that the reflex act is initially connected with direct sensual impression and later is connected with other stimuli due to combination of the impression with new conditioned stimuli that become capable of eliciting it because of this. Lange writes: ';<\s an example of the simplest case, I want to present a fact, the truth of which every mother will confirm. The child cries when he sees a spoon from which he was several times compelled to take a medicine he did not like. How does this happen? Similar cases were quite frequently analyzed from the psychological point of view and we can find very different answers to our question. Some say: he cries because he believes the spoon is the cause of his previous suffering; but the matter is not at all explained in this way. Others say: because the spoon stimulates recollection of past suffering; this may be completely correct, but it does not transfer the problem to the realm of physiology. And yet another answer may be given: because the spoon elicits fear of a future unpleasant feeling; the problem consists specifically of how the sight of the spoon, because of its previous use, is capable of producing fear, that is, of eliciting a certain kind of activity in the vasomotor center" (1896, p. 70). Lange's explanation consists in that "each time the child takes the medication, his feelings, gustatory and visual, are simultaneously impressed-the first impression from the medication and the second, from the spoon. The two impressions are connected, linked, and due to this, recollections can elicit emotions ... If the child is shown a spoon that is not suspect for him, with which he did not previously experience the bitterness of medication it might contain, then instead of crying, he will try to grab the spoon. However, if the child has seen the spoon with the medication several times and noticed that this involves a sensation of a repulsive taste each time, then seeing only the spoon (in itself) will compel the child to cry; in other words, it activates his vasomotor center" (ibid., pp. 70-71). C. G. Lange develops a hypothesis on the establishment of a new, functional connection between two centers, a connection that did not previously exist, by creating a new brain pathway. Pavlov's best student could not have explained more logically the origin of mental emotions through the conditioned reflex path. But Lange is more logical than contemporary physiologists and is bold enough to understand definitively that admitting a conditioned reflex emotional reaction changes nothing essentially in the nature of emotion itself. The whole matter consists only in "a much longer, circuitous path which the impulse generated externally must take before it reaches the vasomotor center. But as far as I can judge," Lange says, "the basic traits of the physiological process remain constantly the same: moving the stimulation from central organs of feeling to the cells of the cortical substance, and from the latter to the vasomotor cells of the medulla oblongata" (ibid., p. 74). In other words, the conditioned reflex remains a reflex in full and absolute measure, although it is elicited by new stimuli. "Therefore I was justified in saying that the difference between emotions of physical origin and emotions elicited by mental causes do not, from the physiological point of view, contain anything favorable, anything substantial. The main thing is

The Teaching about Emotions

205

that the determining factor in the origin of both emotions is one and the same thing: excitation of the vasomotor center. The difference is only in the path along which the impulse reaches this center. Another circumstance is added to this: in indirect mental emotions, the force of the impulse is increased over previously aroused and not yet extinguished brain activity which is combined with the impulse of the external impression" (ibid., pp. 74-75). Actually, if we accept emotion as an innate, reflex reaction of the organism, the possibility for its development or increased complexity has a purely illusory character. Of what does development of a conditioned reflex consist? Exclusively of the fact that stimuli that elicit it and activate the reflex mechanism change. In Pavlov's experiments, the dog secretes saliva in a specific amount and of a specific quality when food is introduced. Then, when the conditioned reflex is established, it begins to respond with the same reaction to a new, previously neutral and indifferent stimulus, blue light, for example. But the salivation reaction itself remains completely unchanged in this case. The dog continues to secrete saliva in the same amount and of the same quality, but only for a different reason. The same thing is wholly applicable to all other reflex acts, particularly to emotional reactions. The emotional reaction of fear is elicited initially by the direct action of a frightening cause. Later, it can be elicited by some other stimulus that is combined several times with the initial cause. The child initially reacts with crying and fear when he is given the bitter medicine. Later just the sight of the spoon will elicit the same reaction in him. The direct cause of the reaction has undoubtedly changed, but the reaction as such remained unchanged. In a general form, we might express this idea as follows: if, according to James, numerous reflex acts are the essence of emotion, then the only possible change in emotions is that the stimuli eliciting them can change, replacing one another as conditioned stimuli, but the emotion itself, the feeling experienced by the person remains always the same, always equal to itself, so that in the history of the development of emotions, the concrete causes for their appearance may change, but emotions themselves cannot change. For this reason, Lange maintains with complete justification: " ... actually, the difference between the fury of those poisoned by amanita mushrooms or maniacs and the anger of those dealt a deadly injury consists only in a difference in causes and in the presence of consciousness of the corresponding causes or in the absence of such consciousness" (1896, p. 65). Thus, for Descartes, its founder, and for his involuntary followers, the visceral theory not only bypasses the problem of development, but factually resolves the problem in the sense of a full and complete denial of any possibility of emotional development in man. This is the inevitable conclusion from the teaching on innate passions.

18 Directly connected with the problem of development of emotions and central to our investigation is the problem of the uniqueness of human emotions in comparison with the emotions of animals. It is a question of the extent to which teaching on emotions can become a chapter in human psychology. In solving this problem, the involuntary followers of Descartes evidently differ sharply with their teacher. Descartes establishes a sharp impassable difference between animals and man. He separates by an abyss the human organism capable of experiencing emotions from the animal organism absolutely devoid of passions. All passion is the distinctive

206

The Teaching about Emotions

privilege of man. In animal nature there is nothing at all like the passions of the soul because in it the soul itself does not exist. Thus, the Cartesian teaching on passions refers wholly and completely to man and to him alone. At first glance, the teaching on the passions appears to us to be developed from the point of view of human psychology. With James and Lange, the theory of emotions refers to man only to the extent to which he represents a higher animal. Their theory is essentially a zoopsychological theory of emotions pertaining to man only to the extent that he himself is a biological being. This is undoubtedly apparent from the teaching on the animal origin of human passions, from the assertion of commonality of basic emotions in animals and man, and, finally, from the basic ideas of the whole theory on the innate, reflex, animal nature of emotions. Little attention has been given to this aspect of the problem because the problem of man did not at all arise before contemporary psychology. But from the very beginning, even the authors of the theory and their critics understood that in the visceral hypothesis, they were speaking in essence of the animal nature of human emotions. We will cite Chabrier, who advanced this idea in the most complete form. Chabrier says that with this problem, we penetrate into the heart of the problem and touch on the major objection that rises against the peripheral theory. When we are speaking about instincts, we have before us an absolutely and invariably established mechanism, which is activated automatically as soon as an appropriate stimulation appears. It is possible that this is true also with respect to the primitive emotions of the child, but it cannot be the same with respect to the usual emotions of adults. We are not concerned here with the fact that in themselves, organic states that elicit one emotion or another depend directly on the organization of consciousness, on the number and systematization of ideas by means of which external impressions are processed. It is not just that our emotions express states of the body, and the states of the body themselves are an expression of the order of our perceptions. In the first place, and primarily, the matter pertains to the problem of emotions specific to man. James himself was inclined toward limiting his hypothesis to the sphere of lower emotions and not toward extending it to the finer and higher feelings. It seems, however, that all human emotions must be classified as finer emotions because, if we disregard idiots, the most limited man is always bound by some kind of more or less vague ideal, some kind of more or less perceptible consciousness. The basest feelings arose under the influence of tradition, creeds, or religious presumptions. They are not of the sort that might be considered instinctive reactions to stimulation, reactions that do not depend on an established system of ideas. For this reason, if we put some pressure on the formula of our author, we could compel him to admit that his theory cannot explain anything in the feelings of man. At least he himself was not concerned with vindicating the differences he noted and overturns them with his own examples. James refers in the same way to the fear of a man confronted by a bear and the grief of a mother learning of the death of a son as examples to which his theory can apply. But if the first case pertains to a group of lower emotions, the same cannot be said of the second case, and we cannot but be surprised that the author does not refer it to the class of finer feelings. If James does not draw a demarcation line, perhaps it is because for him it does not exist. It seems that he accepts the classical, traditional differentiation between higher moral feelings pertaining to such ideal objects as good and beauty, arising from purely spiritual activity, and lower, physical feelings, whose beginning and end is connected with the body and which, for this reason, are subject to physiological explanation.

The Teaching about Emotions

207

Chabrier completely justifiably refers to the fact that a feeling of hunger, usually considered in the group of lower bodily feelings in civilized man, is already a fine feeling from the point of view of the nomenclature of James, that the simple need of food can acquire a religious sense when it leads to the appearance of a symbolic rite of mystical communication between man and God. And conversely, a religious feeling, usually considered as a purely spiritual emotion, in pious cannibals bringing human sacrifices to the gods, can scarcely be referred to the group of higher emotions. Consequently, there is no emotion that by nature would be independent of the body and not connected with it. James' book, The Uzrieties of Religious Experience, shows incontrovertibly the extent to which higher feelings are closely connected with all the fibers of our body. For this reason, we must not cut the enormous sphere of emotions into two parts, one to which the peripheral hypothesis would apply and another, to which it would not apply. No feelings exist which, because of privilege of origin, would belong to a higher class while others, by their nature, would be placed in the lower class. The natural difference is a difference in richness and complexity, and all of our emotions are capable of rising along all the degrees of sentimental evolution. Every emotion can be qualified not otherwise than from the point of view of the degree of its development. For this reason, only that theory of emotions can be considered satisfactory which can be applied to all degrees of development of feeling. Separating emotions from the development of a system of ideas and establishing their dependence exclusively on organic structures, James inevitably comes to the fatalistic conception of emotions which encompasses animals and man equally. The serious differences that human emotions display depending on the era, the degree of civilization, the difference between mystical adoration of a knight for his lady and the noble gallantry of the seventeenth century, remain unexplained from the point of view of this theory. Chabrier says, if we imagine the infinitely rich nature of the poorest emotion, if we pay less attention to the imaginary psychology of single-celled organisms than to the remarkable analysis of novelists and writers, if we simply make use of valuable data supplied by observations of people around us, we cannot but admit the complete failure of the peripheral theory. Actually, it is impossible to admit that simple perception of a female silhouette automatically evoked an endless series of organic reactions of which could be born love such as the love of Dante for Beatrice if we do not previously assume the whole ensemble of theological, political, esthetic, and scientific ideas that comprised the consciousness of the genius, AIighieri. Partisans of the organic theory have forgotten in their hypothesis nothing more than the human spirit. All emotion is a function of personality: this is specifically what the peripheral theory loses sight of. Thus, a purely naturalistic theory of emotions requires as a supplement a real and adequate theory of human emotions. Thus arises the problem of a descriptive human psychology that contrasts itself with an explanatory, physiological psychology of emotions. It seeks a scientific path to those problems of the human spirit that are solved by great artists in novels and tragedies. It wants to make accessible to a study in concepts what these writers made the subject of an artistic representation. The problem of higher feelings, connected with the teaching on values, is usually considered as an area completely inaccessible to a psychology occupied with a psychophysical and psychophysiological investigation of elementary processes of consciousness and their bodily substrate. Thus arises a teleological, descriptive psychology of higher feelings directly generated by the complete failure of the contemporary explanatory psychology of emotions. If it is true, as one of the more

208

The Teaching about Emotions

prominent investigators in contemporary comparative psychology maintains, that in man, emotions attain a higher development in complexity, fineness, and variability of manifestation, but that their genesis, their evolution, and psychological nature remain the same as that of higher animals, then the need for some kind of different, non explanatory psychology is really inevitable. Even from the point of view of the most complex affect of a human-like simian closest to man, it is impossible to explain the most elementary human passions. For this reason, great psychology must break sharply with natural-science, causal psychology and seek its own path somewhere outside it and apart from it. As S. Freud 1l1 says, a completely different approach to the problem of feelings is needed than that which was formed in the official school of psychology, particularly in medical psychology. Freud says that there they are evidently interested most of all in the anatomical paths along which the state of fear develops. Speaking of the fact that he devoted much time and effort to the study of fear, Freud notes that he knows of nothing that would be more indifferent for the psychological understanding of fear than knowing the nerve path along which its excitation travels. He continues: what is affect with respect to dynamics? First, affect includes certain motor innervations or outflows of energy, and, second, a certain sensation of a dual type: perception of motor effects that are occurring and a direct sensation of delight or dissatisfaction that give the affect its basic tone, so to speak. But it does not follow from this that all that was listed comprised the essence of affect. With other affects it seems that we might look deeper and discover a nucleus that unites the specified ensemble. In this way a "deep" psychology of affects arises that tries to disclose the internal nucleus and to make a heroic attempt to preserve a strictly deterministic, causal psychology of affects through a complete self-containment in the sphere of pure mental causality. This special and unique branch of pure psychology, which goes into the depths, arises as a necessary reaction of scientific thought to the failure of academic psychology that treats only the surface of phenomena. Naturally, it does not find a common language with physiological psychology. Freud says, do not think that what has just been said about affect is a generally recognized property of normal psychology. On the contrary, these are views that have grown in the soil of psychoanalysis and are recognized only there. What you can know about affects in psychology, for example, the James-Lange theory, is for us psychoanalysts something simply incomprehensible and not subject to discussion. Thus, the attempt to preserve a strictly causal consideration of psychological facts, and, at the same time, not to bring psychology as an independent science to bankruptcy and not to turn its work over into the hands of physiology, will make depth psychology admit the full substantial independence of mental processes and autonomy of mental causality. Another trend in contemporary psychology of emotions that developed as a reaction to the failed reflex theory of emotions solves the same problem of adequate psychological knowledge of affects in a different way. Mainly, it rejects a causal consideration of feelings and develops as a purely descriptive phenomenology of emotional life. In the words of M. Scheler ll2 (M. Scheler, 1923), one of the conspicuous representatives of this trend, long forgotten was the fact that together with causal laws and psychophysical dependence of emotional life on bodily processes, there are also independent laws of meaning of the so-called higher emotional acts and functions distinct from the sensation of feeling. The intentional and value-cognitive nature of the life of our higher feelings was established anew first by Lotze, who did not develop it, since he maintained only in the most general form this logic of the heart and did not consider it in detail. It was his idea and dictum that

The Teaching about Emotions

209

in feeling, our mind holds the values of things and their relations in as serious and significant a manner of revelation as there is in the bases of rational investigation using the irreplaceable weapons of an experiment. Even in his first works, Scheler himself perceived, developed, and made the old and great idea of B. Pascal on the order of the heart, the logic of the heart, and the mind of the heart the foundation of his own ethics. From this point of view, he understood an analysis of ethical, social, and religious feelings in which, in his opinion, the true and deep thought of Pascal found strong evidence for itself. Going further in the same direction, he believes it necessary to undertake the same kind of phenomenological analysis of the essence and form of the feeling of shame, fear, and horror, feelings of honor, etc. He anticipates in his system a study of the more important derivatives of the generic feelings indicated above so that together with psychological and value-theoretical consideration of them, there would also be a place for the problem of the order of development of these feelings on the individual and species planes and an explanation of their significance for the construction and preservation, shaping and formation of various forms of contemporary human life. Thus, together with the mechanistic theory of lower emotions, constructed according to laws of physiological mechanics, contemporary psychology creates a purely descriptive teaching on the higher, specifically human, historically developed feelings, teaching that is developing into a completely independent branch of knowledge constructed on a foundation opposite the physiological theory. As Scheler also notes, this teaching is connected through its latest roots with metaphysics and is itself being converted into a certain metaphysical system that places at its base the admission of the genetic indelibility of the truly spiritual manifestations of feeling theoretically distinct from its vital manifestations. Since Scheler applies this teaching to the theory of human love, he returns in essence to the Cartesian division of spiritual and sensual passion. Such are the two basic answers that contemporary psychology gives to the problem of the nature of human feelings not resolved from the point of view of the reflex theory. Contemporary psychology seeks elucidation of the mystery either in the metaphysical depths of the human psyche, in the Schopenhauer Will,l13 or at the metaphysical heights on which passion seems to be completely separated from vital functions and finds its real basis in supra-terrestrial spheres. But whether metaphysics looks for the final basis of passions in underground or supra-terrestrial spheres, whether, with Freud, it will willingly use images of the underground kingdom, of hell and the extreme depths of the human spirit, or, with Scheler, turn its eyes toward the music of the stars in the heavenly spheres, it will, nevertheless, remain metaphysics, which in its theistic form and in its pandemonistic form seems to be the inevitable supplement to the superficial psychology of emotions that reduces them to sensation of visceral and motor reactions. The intentionality of higher feelings, the understandable connection of feeling with an object, without which, according to Froebes, higher feeling no longer deserves the name, the sense of human feeling, accessible to our understanding just as is the development of conclusions from premises, the voice of human feeling, all require explanation and find it in teleological, descriptive psychology. Thus, if we take contemporary psychology of feelings in all its fullness, if we understand the necessity with which the mechanistic theory of lower feelings assumes the teleological theory of higher feelings, and how inevitably the teaching on the animal nature of emotions requires as its supplement the teaching on extra-vital feelings, feelings outside life in man, it becomes clear that contemporary

210

The Teaching about Emotions

psychology of feelings, taken as a whole, cannot in any way be accused of divergence from Cartesian teaching. On the contrary, it is its living embodiment, the continuation and development in a science-like form. It doesn't matter that it fell to JamesLange to develop only one of two principles of this teaching, that their theory was limited to applying the naturalistic point of view to explaining emotions. Just as in the system of Descartes himself, the naturalistic explanation for passions of the soul leads to the spiritualistic teaching on intellectual feelings, so the most consistent and naturalistic theory of emotions in contemporary psychology creates at the other pole, as a counterweight, the teleological teaching on the logic of the revelation of higher feelings. The equilibrium to which the Cartesian system holds is again reestablished in contemporary psychology of emotions, in which the naturalistic and teleological principles balance each other. If we add that James not only was not hostile to the second method of considering human feelings, but came very close to it in the teaching on emotions not dependent on the body and in investigating the variety of religious experience, it is easy to be convinced of the fact that the author of the physiological theory of emotions himself essentially accepted the Cartesian teaching in all its fullness, although he did develop one of its aspects predominantly. Thus, if we speak of the theoretical aspect of the matter, then this divergence of James from Descartes also becomes illusory. Returning again to the Cartesian teaching, we can be definitively convinced of this. As we established earlier, its seeming divergence with the theory of James begins with the problem of man. Descartes ascribes passions only to man and denies them in animals. James, on the other hand, considers human emotions as a manifestation of man's purely animal life. The real, and not imaginary divergence consists only in the fact that James, together with all of contemporary science, rejects the Cartesian view of the absolute separateness of man and animals. But if we recall of what the essence of Descartes' teaching on passions consists, it is easy to see that he solves the problem of human passions completely in the same spirit and on the same theoretical plane as James does. Illusory also is the idea that Descartes, accepting passions as a basic phenomenon of human nature belonging exclusively to it alone, does not solve the problem of human feelings in all their specificity to any extent, but only poses it. The dualism between higher and lower feelings, as we tried to establish above, inevitably leads to man, with his living and intelligent passions, forgetting himself and firmly locking himself into a lifeless psychology of incorporeal spirits and into a senseless psychology of soulless robots. Thus, the words of Chabrier, which he said about the theory of James, are fully applicable to Descartes: if we put some pressure on the formula of the author, it might compel him to admit that his theory can explain nothing in human feelings. The dualistic solution of the problem of human passions in Cartesian teaching, the insolubility, from the point of this teaching, of the problem of development, problems of man and his life, already contains in itself in essence the break-up of contemporary psychology of emotions into explanatory and descriptive theories of human feeling. Behind the James-Lange theory, which resorts to the laws of physiological mechanics as to a final explanatory instance, and behind the theory of Scheler, which resorts to the metaphysics of teleological intentional bonds as this instance, again arises in all its magnitude the grandiose contradiction that the great philosopher introduced at the base of his teaching on the passions of the soul.

The Teaching about Emotions

211 19

The second most common problem from the point of view of which we must sum up our investigation of the ultimate bases of the old and the contemporary Cartesian psychology of passions is the problem of connections, dependencies, and relations between passions and the rest of the bodily and spiritual life of man. This problem is connected directly with the problem we have just considered, the problem of development and specific features of human feelings. As we have seen, in it, the question of a causal explanation of emotions is brought to the forefront. Real knowledge is possible only as causal knowledge. Without it, no science is possible. As James notes, explaining causes belongs to investigation of a higher order and forms a higher degree in the development of science. It is natural for this reason that in the psychology of passions, beginning with Descartes and ending with James and contemporary investigators, the problem of causal explanation of human feelings is considered the central and basic problem of the teaching on the passions. How can a causal consideration of facts of the emotional life of man be possible? We have already mentioned the caustic remark of Spranger, one of the more prominent representatives of descriptive psychology, that causal explanation given by explanatory psychology is extremely suggestive of the celebrated parody of Socrates on inadequate explanation. 114 This example may serve as a paradigm in our consideration of the problem of causality in Cartesian and Spinozist psychology of passions and in their contemporary branches. As we have tried to show above, the possibility of a causal explanation of emotions is purchased by James and Lange at a very high price-the price of complete rejection of any intelligible connections of emotions with the rest of the mental life of man. In the opinion of its authors, what the theory wins in this way in establishing a real causal connection between physiological manifestations and emotional experience, it loses in the possibility of establishing any kind of understandable and intelligible connection between feeling as a function of personality and all the rest of the life of consciousness. For this reason, it is not surprising that the causal explanation cited by this theory sharply contradicts our direct experience and the actual connection of emotions with all the internal content of our personality. The directly experienced connection advanced by the founders of descriptive psychology as a basis for all comprehension of the facts of a spiritual, historical, and social order actually inevitably must become the subject of a completely special science if a causal explanation of a type contained in the James-Lange theory is the only one possible in explanatory psychology. Dilthey states: "We can be delivered from all the difficulties cited above only by the development of the science which I propose to call descriptive and analytical psychology in contrast to explanatory and constructive psychology. As descriptive psychology, I understand a representation of the component parts and connections uniformly manifested in all developed human emotional life that is neither devised nor derived, but experienced. Thus, psychology of this kind would be a description and analysis of the connection that we are given at the outset and always in the form of life itself. An important consequence follows from this. The subject of this kind of psychology is the systematic development of the connection of developed emotional life. It represents the connection of internal life in a certain kind of typical man" (1924, pp. 17-18). "The uniformity comprising the main subject of psychology of our time pertains to formulas of the internal process. The reality of emotional life, powerful in content, is beyond the limits of this psychology. The works of poets, the reflections on

212

The Teaching about Emotions

life expressed by great writers like Seneca, Marcus Aurelius, Saint Augustine, Machiavelli, Montaigne, and Pascal, include an understanding of man in all his reality that leaves all of explanatory psychology far behind" (ibid., p. 18). In its essence, the possibility of causal explanation of emotions discovered by explanatory psychology excludes the possibility of investigating the internal spiritual connection of emotions and thus closes the door to investigating their content and leaves only the possibility of admitting direct evidence of internal experience, which every man experiences incessantly, as illusion without any scientific meaning, or developing another psychology constructed on completely opposite principles, which, at the cost of rejecting a causal explanation, would be able to comprehend the internal connection between the "powerful in content" reality of our feelings and all the rest of the internal life of the personality. The authors themselves, proud of the possibility of causal explanation that they discovered, could not help noting this circumstance. Lange said: "I have no doubt that the mother mourning the death of her child will be disturbed, perhaps even be indignant, if she is told that that which she is experiencing is fatigue and muscular weakness, the chill of blood-deprived skin, lack of brain power for clear and quick thinking-but all of this is explained by remembering the cause of the indicated manifestations. But the grieving mother has no basis for being disturbed: her feeling is just as strong, deep and pure, no matter from what its source is" (1896,

p.57). These words are, perhaps, the simplest, the most human, the deepest of all that is contained in this small study. Regardless of the fact that they deal with a banal example from schoolbooks of psychology, they contain a most profound problem. At its base is a fact, which Lange himself did not doubt, that requires a scientific explanation. The mother's being indignant and disturbed is due to the most undoubted, the most obvious consciousness of her sorrowful experience. Is it possible that this direct experience of grief must be recognized as wholly and completely false? In this case, why does the mother crying over the death of the child feel grief and not "fatigue and muscular weakness and the chill of blood-deprived skin?" We have considered this banal example in such detail because, in our view, it has a major significance, the equal of which we could not detect in any other example of the theory under consideration. Practically speaking, all of descriptive psychology is already continuing the hypothetical suit against the mechanistic psychology of the mother who lost her son. Its beginning and end points, the whole sense of its existence, the only basis for its legitimacy, which not one psychologist, perhaps not even one man can dispute who has ever experienced real grief, are comprised of a fact which Lange bypasses so easily and with such a feeling of superiority. Here, indeed, if facts do not agree with theory, so much the worse for the facts. The experience of grief is a fact, living and sensible. Lange himself understands that it must not be counted as a really nonexistent trait, as a delusion of a disturbed imagination. Of course, he has no doubt that the mother, learning what kind of interpretation her grief is given in light of the peripheral theory, will be disturbed and indignant, that is, will react emotionally. The disturbed state and indignation are the same kind of undoubted emotions as grief, although they are manifested in completely different muscle and skin sensations. Emotions arising from mental causes, according to Lange, do not differ in anything substantial from real emotions elicited by physical influence. Consequently, the experience of grief, capable of eliciting in the mother real emotions of anger and indignation, is the most authentic, the most real, the most indisputable fact of psychological life.

The Teaching about Emotions

213

The scientific task consists of giving a causal explanation of this directly experienced connection. Apparent here specifically is the bankruptcy of the contemporary psychology of emotions, which disintegrates at the first confrontation with the most banal, simple case of human feeling into two parts, completely oblivious of each other, of which one finds nothing better than to repeat the Socratic parody for causal explanation. Before the grief of the mother, the other helplessly spreads its hands, not being able to understand scientifically the directly experienced connection of feeling with the rest of the life of consciousness that gives it sense and meaning, and revealing that this connection is beyond the limits of scientific knowledge. In the first case, following explanatory psychology, we would have to tear all evidence of direct internal experience out by the roots and-"contrary to reason, counter to the elements" -consider the weeping being, according to Cartesian rule, as a soulless robot, measuring the force, depth, and purity of its muscle and skin sensations and, comforting ourselves on the ruins of a living psychological life with doubtful consolation that these sensations may be as strong, deep, and pure as the most limitless sorrow. In the second case, following descriptive psychology, we have no possibility other than to reject the proud desire for scientific knowledge and explanation and directly merge with the weeping mother, and fully transferring into her mental state, empathize with her sorrow and explain this as the simple sympathy of a passerby with the help of the new psychology, which is finally able to convert our knowledge of mental life into a science of the spirit. In the first case, in order to preserve the life of feeling, we must abandon its sense. In the second case, in order to preserve the experience and its sense, we must similarly abandon life. In both cases, we have to abandon in the same way all hope of ever scientifically comprehending man and the real meaning of his internallife. We have already studied, carefully and in detail, the path of explanatory psychology of emotions that brings us to the blind alley of a meaningless causal explanation. We know it in all its points, and we no longer need to return to it. We shall briefly trace the path that leads to the other blind alley-to the rejection of all causal explanation and admission of absolute lifelessness of feeling, that is, to the descriptive psychology of emotions. Descriptive psychology of emotions begins with the question of the nature of higher feelings. Do higher emotions represent complex combinations and modifications of elementary emotions or something new that requires a completely separate scientific approach? Descriptive psychology takes as a basic premise the second part of the dilemma, advancing intentionality of higher feelings, their tendency, their considered, understood connection with their object as the principal distinguishing property. Without the understood connection with the object, which we experience directly, higher feeling ceases being itself. In one of his early works, Scheler bases the distinction he makes between higher and lower feelings specifically on this. The connection of lower feelings with objects is always mediated, established by successive acts of referral. Sometimes we even have to try to fmd the subject of our sadness. Higher feeling, on the other hand, is always directed toward something just exactly as an idea. This is an intelligible process mainly available only to comprehension, while elementary, sensual states allow only their establishment and a causal explanation. When I am happy or sad, experiences with a value elicit certain feelings. As F. Brentano ll5 has shown, love and hatred are intentional, in the strict sense of the word. We love not about something, but something. Thus, higher feelings require not establishment and causal-explanatory investigation, but only comprehending psychology that has no other purpose except to

214

The Teaching about Emotions

comprehend directly experienced connections. The experience with a value does not elicit certain higher feelings according to logical connection between the one and the other like connections uniting premises and conclusions in a syllogism. Here the connection is teleological. The nature of conscious life is organized in such a way that I respond with joy to all that I experience as having a certain value, thereby my will is aroused to appropriate inclinations. This connection permits only understanding coupled with the experience of its expediency; On the other hand, incomprehensible for us still is the connection according to which something sweet elicits satisfaction and something bitter, dissatisfaction. I can only accept these connections as facts that in themselves remain incomprehensible to me. The principal incomprehensibility of basic or primitive feelings, as we have said, comprises one of the cornerstones of Cartesian teaching on the passions. Descartes maintains that sorrow and joy as passions are not only different from pain and satisfaction as sensations, but can be completely separate from them. We can imagine that pain can be experienced with the same emotional indifference as the most usual sensation. We can even be surprised that pain is so frequently accompanied by sadness, and satisfaction, by joy. We can be surprised that hunger, that simple sensation, and appetite, that desire, are so intimately connected with each other that they always accompany each other. Thus, contemporary descriptive psychology of emotions only repeats through Scheler's lips the old Cartesian thesis on the complete lack of meaning of elementary emotions, principally excluding any possibility of understanding them, maintaining the privilege only with respect to higher feelings. The teaching on the intentional nature of higher feelings, developed by Brentano, Scheler, A. Pfander,116 M. Geiger,117 and others, laid the bases for contemporary psychology of emotions. With this teaching, descriptive psychology of emotions attempts to overcome the dead-ended naturalistic theory of feeling that is inclined to consider higher feelings as a complex or product of the development of simpler mental elements. Scheler sees the error not in that it incorrectly explains facts of the life of higher feelings, but in that it simply does not see these phenomena and is blind with respect to them. If the naturalistic theory were simply to see the phenomena of holy or spiritual love, it would at the same time see from any facts pertaining to the sphere of vital love, that it could not in any way fail to understand them nor could it escape them. But this is the basic inadequacy of this and other opinions of the naturalistic theory: its whole system blinds it to the fact that in the course of the development of the life of man, completely new acts and qualities develop, that these can constantly develop, that these qualities seem to us to have arisen spasmodically in essential content, and can never be considered as simple, gradual branching of old forms, as is assumed with respect to the bodily organization of a living being. The system of the naturalistic theory blinds it with respect to the fact that in the course of life development, basically new and deeper degrees of existence and values may appear and new areas of objects and values for self-evolving life may develop on their base, and that only as life develops do these new areas of existence and values begin to disclose and include in themselves all the fullness of the qualities that determine them. For this theory, every new quality signifies a new illusion. It, like all naturalistic philosophy, is a major speculation betting on diminishment. In the life of feelings, descriptive psychology finds the deepest and most vital object. "Here we see before us the genuine center of spiritual life. The poetry of all time finds its subject matter here. The interests of humanity constantly turn toward the life of feelings. The fortune and misfortune of human existence depends on it, the psychology of the seventeenth century, seriously turning its attention to

The Teaching about Emotions

215

the content of the life of the spirit, concentrated on the teaching on sensual statesbecause these were its affects" (w. Dilthey, 1924, p. 56). Dilthey proceeds from the fact that sensual states are as firmly resistant to analysis as they are important and central. For the most part, our feelings merge into general states in which separate component parts are no longer distinct. Our feelings, like motives, cannot be voluntarily reproduced or made conscious. We can only regenerate states the way we experimentally bring into consciousness conditions in which corresponding states arise. "From this it follows that our determinations of mental states do not break up their content, but only indicate the conditions under which given mental states occur. Such is the nature of all definitions of mental states in Spinoza and Hobbes. For this reason, we must first of all improve on the methods of these thinkers. Definition, precise nomenclature, and classification are the first task of descriptive psychology in this area. 1tue, in the study of expressive movements and symbols of ideas for the mental states, new, auxiliary means are being discovered: specifically, the comparative method which introduces simpler relations into feelings and motives of animals and primitive peoples and frees us to go beyond the limits of anthropology of the seventeenth century. But even using these auxiliary means does not provide firm points of support for the explanatory method which strives to deduce phenomena in the given area from a limited number of unequivocally determined elements" (ibid., p. 57). . Here Dilthey allows a logically totally unjustifiable mixture of three positions, which coincide in practical conclusions, but which, from the theoretical aspect, not only cannot be united, but, on the contrary, are the sharpest example of internally contradictory anecdotal logic. He establishes that factual attempts to explain the life of our feelings are in a state of conflict with each other from which there is definitely no escape. Even the basic problems of the relations of feelings to motives and will and of the relations of qualitative sensual states to the ideas that merge with them do not admit a convincing solution. Thus, the explanatory psychology of feelings seems to be factually unfounded and not attainable in practice. Dilthey immediately makes the factual failure of explanatory psychology of feelings the basis for the conclusion that it is unnecessary and impossible to explain feeling. He says that if we glance at the literature, remarkably rich among all nations, that pertains to mental states and human passions, we cannot but see that none of the positions that are fruitful and illuminate this area require explanatory assumptions of this kind. That literature describes only complex and prominent forms of processes in which various aspects connected with each other are mentioned, and it is necessary only to penetrate deeply enough into the apparent facts in this area to be convinced of the uselessness of such explanatory hypotheses here. Dilthey cites as evidence of this idea the example of aesthetic pleasure elicited by an artistic work, which is characterized by most psychologists as a state of satisfaction. He says that the aesthete studying work of various kinds of styles in different artistic works will, nevertheless, be forced to admit the inadequacy of this understanding. The style of any Michelangelo fresco or Bach fugue flows from the frame of mind of a great soul, and understanding these works of art communicates to the soul an enjoyable specific form of mood in which it expands, is ennobled, and is as if amplified (ibid., pp. 57-58). If Dilthey confounds the factual failure of explanatory psychology of emotions with the theoretical uselessness of explanatory hypotheses in this area and with the theoretical impossibility of a causal explanation of higher forms of mood in which the soul is extended, ennobled, and as if amplified, then immediately after this, he returns again to the factual position of things and is ready to admit that explanatory psychology still simply has not matured enough to solve the problem of feelings

216

The Teaching about Emotions

and, consequently, descriptive psychology must prepare and clear a way for it. This is his third position. Dilthey says that for this reason the area of mental life actually has not matured enough for full analytical treatment. First it is necessary for descriptive and analytical psychology to conclude their work on the details. Thus, the mixing of three claims different in content resembles remarkably the logic of the anecdote cited by Freud on his studies of wit. A woman whose neighbor accuses her of breaking a borrowed pot, to be more convincing, presents three arguments at once, she says: first, I did not take any pot from the neighbor; second, when I took it, it was already broken; third, I returned it completely whole. Dilthey says: first, explanatory psychology thus far has not given a satisfactory explanation of the life of our feelings; second, such an explanation is completely useless, is not necessary, and cannot be given in general; third, explanatory psychology will be able to give this explanation after descriptive psychology concludes its task of separation and analysis. This kind of mixing of positions different in content also includes a positive program of investigation, which Dilthey plans for descriptive psychology of feelings. The investigation must move predominantly in three directions. It reflects the basic types of trends of mental processes. What great poets, especially Shakespeare, gave us in images, it tries to make accessible for concept analysis. It isolates certain basic relations that occur through the life of feelings and motives of man, and tries to establish separate component parts of the states of feelings and motives (ibid., p. 58). Dilthey sees an advantage of the descriptive and analytical method over the explanatory in that it is limited to considering problems that have been solved. Obviously, the task of explanatory psychology of feelings seems to him to be insoluble. In general, there was no pot, neither a broken one nor a whole one, regardless of the fact that we have just maintained that the pot was broken when taken and whole when returned. Another investigator, Miinsterberg, avoids this contradiction; just as clearly as Dilthey and many others, he makes a distinction between causal and teleological psychology as two independent sciences with equal rights. This idea, suggested by the whole historical course of the development of contemporary psychology, matured simultaneously with various investigators: in the words of Goethe, just as apples fall simultaneously in different orchards. But more consistently than Dilthey, Miinsterberg, although he dedicated his concrete work to solving the problems of explanatory psychology, nevertheless developed most fully a program and plan for research in descriptive psychology. "The impoverished state of contemporary psychology is expressed by the fact that we know incomparably more about psychological facts than ever before, but much less about what psychology really is ... The psychology of our time fights against the prejudice that there is only one type of psychology ... The concept of psychology includes two completely different scientific tasks which must be essentially distinguished and for which it is best to use special designations. Actually there is psychology of two kinds, but if prejudice rules that one of them is enough for science, it is natural that some psychologists will cultivate only the first form of psychology and leave the second aside, and others will be concerned immediately with the second form and neglect the first, or perhaps, finally, both forms will be mixed together into a seeming unity and the material will be arbitrarily divided between them, or one of them will more or less merge with the other. All of these possibilities are represented in contemporary scientific psychology. "It is self-evident that forms of psychology that are so dissimilar would not be able to exist one beside the other and be bound in spirit if they had nothing in common. What is common consists primarily in that all psychology has to do with

The Teaching about Emotions

217

the experiences of the individual. This distinguishes it from sciences of a bodily nature and from normative sciences. The personality is thus the decisive point of departure for all of psychology" (H. Miinsterberg, 1924, pp. 7-8). Beyond this common point of departure begins the essential divergence of the two possible psychologies. With each beat of the pulse of our life experience, it becomes obvious to us that we can understand our own inner life in two ways, thus acquiring a double knowledge of it. Specifically: in one case, we grasp the sense of our feeling and desire, our attention and thinking, our remembering and ideas. We try to comprehend all of this and retain it as it is present in each experience, that is, as activity of our "I," as directed toward a certain goal of our personality. We can then trace how one desire includes in itself another, how one idea points to another, how in our spirit a world of internal relations opens up. But we can also look very differently at our experiences. We can present our experiences to ourselves not as a mentally acting personality, but as a simple spectator; then the experiences will become for us the content of our perception. Of course, these contents will differ from the contents of nature. We will separate them as contents of our consciousness from external contents, but will be as interested in them just as we are interested in external things and processes. We will consider the contents of consciousness only from the point of view of a spectator who describes their course and comprehends their necessary connection, that is, tries to explain them. Through this description, the content of consciousness becomes a combination of elements; through explaining these elements, a chain of causes and actions is formed. Thus we come to a completely different understanding of that same mental life: in one case, to comprehending the internal relations and to grasping the internal intentions connected with them, and in the other case, to describing and explaining the elements and their actions. If we follow to the end and in both directions these different methods of understanding our internal life and give them a scientifically completed form, we will actually have to arrive at two basically different theoretical disciplines. One of them describes mental life as an aggregate of the content of consciousness and explains it. The other is interested in and understands the same mental life as an aggregate of relations of intention and meaning. One is causal psychology and the other teleological and intentional psychology. Since all material must be considered from both points of view, there is no delimitation of material between the one and the other psychology. Every feeling, every memory, every desire can be understood in a category of causality, as content of consciousness, just as well as from the intentional point of view, as mental activity (ibid., pp. 8-9). In historical and contemporary psychology, both forms are mixed into a seeming unity, but each of them is seldom actually pure and consistent. For the most part, teleological psychology merges in some way with elements of causal psychology. In this case, the processes of memory, for example, are presented as causal, but the processes of feeling and will, as intentional-a mixing that arises easily under the influence of the naive ideas of daily life .... Thus, together with causal psychology, we can speak of intentional psychology, or of psychology of the spirit together with psychology of consciousness, or of comprehending psychology together with explanatory psychology (ibid., pp. 9-10). In this delimitation of the tasks of the dual type of psychology, Miinsterberg consistently develops the idea to its logical conclusion. He completely excludes any need for or possibility of a causal explanation in descriptive psychology which assumes understanding and comprehending system and meaning relations between experiences and, consequently, requires consideration of mental activity as a completely autonomic area of activity that is outside nature and outside life, which,

218

The Teaching about Emotions

speaking in the language of Spinoza, is not a natural thing that follows the general laws of nature, but a thing that is outside the bounds of nature, as if a realm within a realm. But we have only to look into and consider carefully the argumentation of Dilthey and Miinsterberg to see immediately its strength and its weakness, its positive and negative poles, its unconditional correctness and its equally unconditional fallibility. The strength and correctness of their argumentation lies exclusively in the admission of the failure, deficiency, and inadequacy of those explanations that were advanced thus far by physiological psychology with respect to higher manifestations of the mental life of man. Its correctness and its strength lie exclusively in that it places at the forefront problems of prime importance, problems of what is higher in man, and in this way, in general, places first and at full length the problem of the psychology of real, living man. But this point also includes the weakness and fallacy of the argumentation under consideration. Speaking practically, the new psychology is not that different from the old. To some degree and even, perhaps, in what is most central and essential, they completely coincide with each other, regardless of the apparent oppositeness. Specifically, descriptive psychology wholly and fully accepts the basic idea of explanatory psychology (causal explanation can be nothing other than mechanical reduction of complex and higher processes to automatically differentiated elements of mental life ). In this way, the new psychology fully takes the same positions from which the development of the old psychology always proceeded. Admitting mechanistic causality as the only possible category of explaining mental life, limiting the causal explanation of psychology to the narrow limits of the Socratic parody-this is the common point in which the new and the old psychologies meet and coincide. Thus, the only legitimate argument in favor of the development of an independent descriptive psychology is the failure of explanatory psychology, which does not know how to escape the bounds of mechanistic causality in explaining mental life. In the broken pot of the neighbor, the new psychology sees the only reason for having to cook the meat in its own, completely separate pot. Argumentation from the broken pot comprises simultaneously the strength and the weakness of the partisans of the new psychology. It is completely incontrovertible that explanatory psychology, according to the correct remark of Scheler, did not give a false explanation to real problems of human psychology, but simply that it did not notice these problems and was blind with respect to them. It is just as incontrovertible that these problems must be placed before scientific psychology as primary and central problems urgently requiring solution. But from what has been said, reasoning logically, we can reach no other conclusion except the necessity of radically reconstructing the bases on which contemporary psychology rests. Deducing from these premises the need to transfer the solution of the given problems to some kind of new and completely separate science that totally and theoretically excludes the possibility of a causal explanation means to wholly and completely justify the contemporary state of explanatory psychology with all its errors and to share with it its fallacies, not to rise above it and not to overcome it, but simply to ask it to make room and build on this same rotten foundation, on which nothing can stand except a castle in the air or a house of cards, an illusory building of the psychology of the spirit. For this reason, the James-Lange theory, with its parody of a causal explanation of human feelings, inevitably generates the theory of Scheler with its complete rejection of any explanation of higher feelings, replaced by the insight of a teleological connection. But Scheler also moved no farther from James than the whole new psychology moved from the old. Together with James, he evidently admits that the only explanation allowed to psychology is an explanation from the laws of physi-

The Teaching about Emotions

219

ological mechanics. For this reason, he, like all of descriptive psychology, does not solve the problem, but bypasses it. 10 the question placed before contemporary psychology, which we consider as the prototype of all basic problems that require a causal explanation, the question as to why Socrates sat in the Athenian dungeon, the James-Lange theory answers with a reference to the strain and weakness of the muscles that flex the extremities, and the theory of Scheler answers by indicating that remaining in the dungeon had the purpose of satisfying a feeling of higher value .... The one and the other is just as incontrovertible and just as obvious as it is fruitless. The one and the other is just as far from a real scientific answer to the question. The one and the other does not pay attention to the real cause. The real grief of the mother weeping over the death of her child, if we recall the example of Lange, is directly and closely bound with her tears, although it might have occurred in her soul without being accompanied by tears, and the tears might have been a manifestation of an opposite feeling, for example, joy. All of this is incontrovertible, but to see in this a cause would be, in the words of Plato, lengthwise and crosswise stupid. Just as completely incontrovertible and obvious is it that the decision of Socrates to remain in the dungeon was connected with the pursuit of a certain vital goal and a satisfaction of a certain sense of value. But an event opposite in meaning-his fleeing-would have the same system and value character. In essence, rejection of any causal explanation and the attempt to bypass the problem, depending on teleological analysis, not only do not move us forward in comparison with the explanatory psychology of feelings with all its undoubted defects, but, on the contrary, they take us back a long way. Dilthey maintained that definition, precise nomenclature, and classification comprise the first task of descriptive psychology in this area (1924, p. 57). He forgets here that the path for definition and classification, which psychology opened over the course of several centuries, led to the fact that the psychology of feelings was the least fruitful and most tedious of all the chapters of science, as James justifiably wrote. Dilthey consistently asks us to tum to the anthropology of the seventeenth century and to improve its methods. It is remarkable that from the thinkers of that century, he selects specifically what is Spinoza's oldest, most obsolete, and lifeless: his nomenclature, classification, and definition, which do not disclose the content of our affects, but only point to the conditions in which a given mental state occurs.' 18 Thus, from the teaching of Spinoza on passions, descriptive psychology draws to its side not the living part turned toward the future, but the dead part turned toward the past. Dilthey sees the only possibility that allows the new psychology to go beyond the limits of seventeenth century anthropology in applying the comparative method in the study of expressive movements and symbols of mental states (1924, p. 57). But the one and the other leave at our disposal only a new auxiliary means for solving the old problem and basically do not take us beyond the limits of the psychology of passions of the seventeenth century. Thus, one flourish of the pen deletes almost 300 years of development of psychological thought and knowledge, and the movement backwards, back to the seventeenth century, into the depths of history, is the only path of scientific progress of psychology. In a certain sense, descriptive psychology, advancing a teleological and spiritualistic consideration of mental phenomena in place of a causal explanation, takes us back to the era of philosophical thought that was dominant before Spinoza. It was specifically Spinoza who fought for a natural, deterministic, materialistic, causal explanation of human passions. It was specifically he who was the thinker who first formed a philosophical base for the possibility of explanatory psychology of man

220

The Teaching about Emotions

as a science in the true sense of that word and marked the paths for its further development. In this sense, Spinoza resists all of contemporary descriptive psychology as its irreconcilable opponent. It is he who fought against the Cartesian dualism, spiritualism, and teleologism resurrected in contemporary descriptive psychology. In this respect, we will have to oppose our understanding of the real connection of Spinoza's teaching on passions with the contemporary psychology of emotions to the opinion of Dilthey. It is remarkable that, advancing the basic problems of human psychology, the new trend had to turn to the psychology of the seventeenth century, seriously turning its attention to the true center of mental life, to the content of our affects, and to naming Spinoza as the beacon lighting the way for new investigations. In Spinoza, the partisans of the new trend find not only the nomenclature and classification of passions, but also certain basic relations that go across the whole life of feelings and motivations, that have a decisive significance for understanding man and the subjects that are the components of a precise descriptive method. Such, for example, is the basic relation consisting in the fact that both Hobbes and Spinoza designated self-preservation as instinct or growth of the "I": a striving toward the fullness of mental states, toward overcoming self, toward development of strength and motives. Thus, not just the method, but also the content of Spinoza's teaching on passions is advanced as a guiding source for developing investigations in a new direction, in the direction of understanding man. In this statement, in this turning to Spinoza, the truth is mixed with falsehood to such an extent that it is difficult to separate it from fallacy. In order to do this, it is necessary to recall that we were confronted at one time with a similar statement, a mixture of truth and fallacy concerning the connection of Spinozist teaching on passions with contemporary psychology of emotions. In order to understand the significance of Dilthey's idea that descriptive psychology of feelings must be the successor of Spinoza's psychology, we must recall that Lange also called Spinoza the thinker who came closest to the physiological theory of emotions, which Lange developed, because Spinoza "not only does not consider bodily manifestations dependent on mental movements, but puts them next to each other, even brings them almost to the forefront" (c. Lange, 1896, p. 89). Thus, Lange and Dilthey, descriptive psychology and explanatory psychology of emotions, forming two opposite poles of contemporary scientific knowledge on human feelings, turn in the same way to their sources, to the Spinozist teaching on the passions. The coincidence cannot be accidental. It includes a very deep historical and theoretical idea. Even now, we must extract something significant to our purposes from the fact of coincidence of two opposite teachings in one tendency toward Spinozist thought as to their ideological beginning. We have already spoken about the connection of the theory of Lange with the teaching of Spinoza on passions. We were able to establish that to a significant degree, admitting direct and indirect historical and ideological connections between the teaching of Spinoza about affects and the James-Lange theory is based on illusion. Lange himself vaguely understood the fallacy of his pointing to the closeness of Spinozist teaching and his theory. With a feeling of admiration, he finds a complete vasomotor theory on bodily manifestations of emotions in Malebranche,119 who "with the astuteness of a genius, discovered the true connection between phenomena" (ibid., p. 86). We actually find the device of emotional mechanism expressed in vague language of the physiology of that day, which permits translation into the language of contemporary physiology and in this form may be close to the James-Lange hypothesis. The same factual coincidence was established very early by Irons, who showed that Descartes occupies the same position as James. We have

The Teaching about Emotions

221

seen that later investigations, especially the works of Sergi, fully confirmed this opinion. But this is not enough. In the course of our investigation, we tried to explain that not only factual description of the mechanism of emotional reaction brings together these theories, separated by almost three centuries, but also that the factual coincidence is evidence of a deeper methodological relationship between them, a relationship based on the fact that contemporary physiological psychology wholly inherited from Descartes the naturalistic and mechanistic principles of interpreting emotions. Cartesian parallelism, automatism, and epiphenomenalism are the true bases of the hypotheses of Lange and James, and this gave Dunlap full right to call the great philosopher the father of all of contemporary reactological psychology. Thus we see that the theory of Lange actually goes back not to Spinozist, but to Cartesian teaching on the passions of the soul. In this sense, we might say that Lange, in the conclusive note to his study, calls on the name of Spinoza in vain. In short, this is the result at which we arrived in considering this question. Now we might amplify this conclusion with yet another new and most significant and important trait, which clearly appears in a comparison of descriptive and explanatory psychology of emotions: in a certain respect, Spinozist teaching actually is much more closely related to explanatory than to descriptive psychology, and this means that it is most likely closer to the hypothesis of Lange, in which the basic principle of explanatory psychology of emotions was expressed most clearly, than to the program of descriptive psychology of feelings outlined by Dilthey. In the argument between causal psychology and teleological psychology, in the argument between the deterministic and nondeterministic conceptions of feelings, in the confrontation between spiritualistic and materialistic hypotheses, Spinoza must be placed on the side of those who defend the scientific conception of human feelings against the metaphysical. Specifically in this point in which Spinozist teaching on passions approaches explanatory psychology of emotions, it diverges in the most irreconcilable way from descriptive psychology. This time Dilthey and not Lange calls in vain on the name of Spinoza at the very beginning of his program for future investigation. Actually, what can these investigators have in common who consciously revive the teleological and metaphysical conceptions of seventeenth century anthropology against which Spinoza always fought with the strict determinism, causality, and materialism of his system?
