As we have been indicating, Dilthey brings to the forefront in Spinoza's teaching the most obsolete, formal, and speculative part turned toward the past, his nomenclature, classification, and definition. Dilthey's principles are not only not on the path with the great principles of the Spinozist system, but their own path can be continued only through the most fierce struggle against these principles. After everything that has been said, there can scarcely remain any doubt that in reviving the spiritualistic and teleological principles of the seventeenth century, descriptive psychology in its basic nucleus goes back not to Spinoza, but to Descartes, in whose teaching on passions of the soul it finds its full and true program. Spinoza is, of course, not with Dilthey and Miinsterberg, not with their teaching on autonomous and independent mental life existing exclusively due to system connections and relations of meaning, but with Lange and James in their struggle against unchangeable mental essences, eternal and separate, against conceptions that consider emotions not as human emotions, but as essences, beings, powers, demons which possess man, which are outside the limits of nature. Of course, he never would agree to admitting that mental fear in itself can explain why people grow pale, tremble, etc., and in this Lange is unconditionally correct. He is with those who, like James, consider description and classification to be lower levels in

222

The Teaching about Emotions

the development of science, and explanation of a causal connection as deeper investigation, investigation of a higher order. But the complexity of the matter is increased by the fact that, no matter how obvious the fallacy of the attempt to base descriptive psychology of feelings on Spinozist teaching on passions is, in a certain respect, this attempt contains some portion of truth. We tried above to find it in the fact that the problems advanced in descriptive psychology of feelings-the problem of specific features of human feelings, the problem of the vital significance of feelings, the problem of the higher in the emotional life of man-all of these problems, to which explanatory psychology was blind and which, in their nature go beyond the limits of mechanistic interpretation, were actually first posed in full in Spinoza's teaching on the passions. In this point the Spinozist teaching is actually on the side of the new psychology against the old; it supports Dilthey against Lange. Thus, we face the final summary, which cannot but confuse us with the extreme complexity of the results it contains. We saw that the line of Spinozist thought finds its historical continuation to some extent in Lange and in Dilthey, that is, in both explanatory and in descriptive psychology of our time. Something of Spinozist teaching is contained in each of these theories that fight with each other. Penetrating to the causal, natural-science explanation of emotions, the James-Lange theory solves one of the central problems of Spinozist materialistic and deterministic psychology at the same time. But even descriptive psychology, as we have seen, advancing to the forefront the problem of the meaning and vital significance of human feelings, also tries to resolve the basic and central problems of Spinozist ethics in this way. In a few words, we can define the true relation of Spinozist teaching on passions to explanatory and descriptive psychology of emotions, saying that, practically speaking, this teaching on solving the one and only problem, the problem of a deterministic, causal explanation of what is higher in the life of human passions, also partially contains explanatory psychology, retaining the idea of causal explanation but rejecting the problem of the higher in human passions, and descriptive psychology, rejecting the idea of a causal explanation and retaining the problem of the higher in the life of human passions. Thus, forming its deepest and most internal nucleus, Spinoza's teaching contains specifically what is in neither of the two parts into which contemporary psychology of emotions has disintegrated: the unity of the causal explanation and the problem of the vital significance of human passions, the unity of descriptive and explanatory psychology of feelings. For this reason, Spinoza is closely connected with the most vital, the most critical news of the day for contemporary psychology of emotions, news of the day which prevails in it, determining the paroxysm of crisis that envelops it. The problems of Spinoza await their solution, without which tomorrow's day in our psychology is impossible. But in solving the problem of Spinoza, the explanatory and descriptive psychology of emotions, Lange and Dilthey move away completely from his teaching, and, as we have tried to show above, are wholly contained in Cartesian teaching on the passions of the soul. Thus, the crisis of contemporary psychology of emotions, having disintegrated into two irreconcilable parts that fight against each other, demonstrates for us the historical fate not of Spinozist but of Cartesian philosophical thought. This is apparent most clearly in the basic point that serves as a watershed between explanatory psychology and descriptive psychology on the question of the causal explanation of human emotions. Actually, we have seen that specifically the Cartesian teaching of Descartes on the passions of the soul contains two independent and equally legitimate parts ex-

The Teaching about Emotions

223

isting with each other, the strictly deterministic, mechanistic, causal teaching on emotions and the purely spiritualistic, nondeterministic, teleological teaching on intellectual passions. Spiritual and sensual love arise, each from its source: the first, from the free, cognitive need of the soul, and the second, from the nourishment needs of embryonal life. Their connection is so unclear that we understand much more plainly their initial separateness than their short-term coming together and communication. Since spiritual and sensual passions differ sharply from each other, naturally they must become the subject of two completely different kinds of scientific knowledge. The first must be studied as manifestations of independent, free, spiritual activity, the second, as manifestations of human automatism subject to the laws of mechanics. In this, the idea of the separation of explanatory and descriptive psychology of emotions is fully contained, an idea that is assumed by Cartesian teaching with the same inevitability with which the Spinozist teaching on passions assumes the opposite, specifically the unity of explanatory and descriptive psychology of feelings. Developing the visceral theory of passions, as a nonmediated and direct cause of emotions Descartes advances specific organic states that cause the soul to experience passions. Making use of any kind of good does not contain in itself the feeling of joy as such. But movement of vital spirits flowing from the brain to the muscles and nerves does take on a character from which this feeling must flow. The divergence between Descartes and later representatives of the visceral theory consists only in details. As direct cause of emotion, Descartes considers only changes in internal organs, but not external movements. As Sergi says, one can imagine him speaking with James: our heart does not contract because we are sad, but we experience sadness because our heart contracts. However, he could never say: we experience fear because we flee, we are angry because we hit someone. In this sense, Descartes' theory agrees more closely with the variant that Lange gave it subsequently, and deviates somewhat from the variant that James developed. But the basic idea of a causal, automatic explanation of passions appears in Descartes' teaching in all its grandiose dimensions. As we have shown above, this does not exhaust Descartes' teaching on the causes of passions. It must be supplemented with two ideas connected with each other that create a bridge from his teaching to contemporary descriptive psychology of emotions. Together with visceral changes, Descartes repeatedly names perceptions, memories, the thought of a loved one, and a hateful or frightening object as causes of emotions. As much as Sergi tries to eliminate the contradiction contained here, he succeeds poorly. 1l'ue, differentiating between proximate and remote causes seems to make it possible to eliminate the contradiction. The last and most proximate cause of passions of the soul is the movement of the cerebral gland that is elicited by vital spirits. But the remote and first causes of passions may be sensations, memories, ideas. This was fully perceived by later successors of Descartes. For James and Lange, in just this way, the proximate and last cause of emotions is its bodily manifestation. But these investigators are also ready to consider perceptions, memories, and thoughts as remote causes of emotion. Practically speaking, the confusion on the question of causal explanation of emotions conceals a problem of enormous importance. On the one hand, phenomena arising from human automatism and implemented according to purely mechanical laws are recognized as the last and direct causes of emotions. As fits mechanical laws, the phenomena subjected to them are devoid of any meaning. Posing the question itself on the intelligibility or meaning of causal connections in this plan is as absurd as looking for the meaning of why the impact of a rolling ball hitting one that is not moving causes the latter to move. Here, bare and absolute lack of

224

The Teaching about Emotions

meaning of mechanical relations is dominant. Maintaining that we must wonder why a sensation of hunger is internally connected with appetite sounds somewhat odd, but, on the other hand, it is completely consistent. Thus far, everything is clear. But beginning with a certain point, it seems that the pure lack of meaning of mechanical relations does not exhaust completely the possible causal consideration of emotions. However strangely, the most neutral, the most remote, the very first causes of emotions, which are far from necessary for the development of these states and in the absence of which emotions can develop just as freely as in their presence, are directly connected to their consequences in a certain intelligible relation. If a judgment is a cause of emotions, if it is true that the idea of a loved object is a cause of love and the idea of a hated object is a cause of hatred, if Descartes' statement is true that joy originates with judgment, as if we were in possession of some kind of good, then it seems that emotions not only allow, but require an evaluating, intentional (that is, connected with a certain tendency toward the object), immanently meaningful consideration and explanation. These brief determinations contain completely the whole methodology of differentiation of the higher and lower emotions in Scheler's teaching. The two methods of considering emotional life do not meet anywhere and, like two parallel lines, do not intersect with each other. Neither of them is needed to supplement the other. They cannot at all be placed in any kind of theoretical relation to each other. Each emotion, as Miinsterberg might say, can be understood as mental activity as much in the category of causality as from the point of view of intentionality. Each emotion must be considered from both points of view, whiC;h, being developed to the end, will bring us to two different methods of understanding our internal life, to two basically different theoretical disciplines, one of which describes mental life as an aggregate of the content of consciousness and explains it, and the other of which interprets and understands the same mental life as an aggregate of intelligible system relations. One of the disciplines is causal psychology, the other, teleological and intentional psychology. Of course, there is another possibility which must also be traced to the very end. We may, perhaps, not agree with Miinsterberg that between the one psychology and the other, there is no demarcation of the material, that every feeling can be understood as much in the category of causality as from the intentional point of view. But then, together with Scheler, we will inevitably come to the same demarcation of material between two different methods of comprehending emotions in which lower feelings, connected only indirectly with an object, devoid of all immanent drive toward the object, completely inaccessible to an intelligible understanding and allowing only a factual establishment of the causal connections at their base, must be the subject of explanatory psychology, while higher feelings, in which an immanent drive toward an object is present from the beginning, require teleological consideration of their intelligible connections and dependencies, comprising in this way the direct subject of descriptive psychology of the spirit. Both of these possibilities, later realized in various trends in descriptive psychology, remain open, but both are wholly contained as logical conclusions in Descartes' teaching on causal dependence of emotions of a dual kind. According to this teaching, emotions can at one time be considered as causally determined, automatically occurring bodily changes, and at another time, as intelligibly dependent on value experiences. Both methods of consideration are theoretically absolutely independent and contain truth wholly and completely. Contemporary psychology of emotions comes to precisely the same conclusion; it stands and falls together with admitting these two equally legitimate and equally possible, mutually independent methods of considering the emotional life of man.

The Teaching about Emotions

225

As we have seen, neither Dilthey nor Miinsterberg nor any of the partisans of descriptive psychology reject the first Cartesian principle, the strict mechanical causal explanation of emotional life. Some, like Miinsterberg, admit that all feeling must be the object of investigation in categories of causality and in categories of purpose. Others, like Scheler, rendering to God what is God's and to Caesar what is Caesar's, attach to explanatory psychology as its legitimate sphere, the whole domain of lower feelings, and to descriptive psychology, the spirit, the higher sphere of human feelings. This differentiation does not change the essence of the matter and does not change the basic idea of the inevitability of dualistic consideration of emotions. C. G. Lange assumes that, together with the causal emotions that he establishes in his investigation, there may be a study of emotion that will consider memories of former suffering as their causes. It is true that this does not transfer the question to the sphere of physiology. This is the task of another science of emotions. But despite the admission itself that memory can be named as a cause of emotion at one time, and vasomotor reaction, at another time, the position itself that both methods of causal explanation are equally legitimate and independent, not mutually dependent, returns us completely and fully to the Cartesian teaching on the possibility of a dual view of emotions-from the angle of an intelligible connection with memories and ideas and from the angle of mechanical dependence on bodily causes. But this scarcely differs in any way from the idea of Miinsterberg that all feeling can be understood as much in the category of causality as from the intentional point of view. C. G. Lange would probably be very surprised if he found out that many years later, P. Natorp120 would repeat essentially the same differentiation of two possible methods of considering mental phenomena. Since we are speaking of disclosing causal patterns of phenomena that are called mental, the consideration can be nothing other than a conscious, methodically consistent, not connected with any metaphysical prejudice, natural-scientific, predominantly physiological study of organs of feelings and the brain. It is simply a branch of natural science that might better be called physiology instead of psychology. But, together with this study, there is another method of knowing mental life, the inherent task of which is not description and not explanation of causal connection, but reconstruction, reestablishment, recreation of the whole concreteness of what is experienced. To the extent that mental phenomena require causal explanation and allow it, they constitute the subject of physiological knowledge. To the extent that they are comprehended in all their internal uniqueness, they do not require and do not allow any kind of explanation of causal dependence and can be known only with the help of a complete reproduction of experiences in all their concreteness, the ideal of which is not explanation, but tautology. Scientific knowledge is essentially incapable of adding anything new to what is directly disclosed in experience itself. It can only tautologically affirm that the only explanation of the connection of experiences is the experienced connection. C. G. Lange understands that differentiation of mental and physical causes of emotion must have far-reaching consequences. Different mental causes even of identical emotions do not elicit essentially identical phenomena. The fear of a ghost, for example, does not assume the same form as the fear of an enemy bullet (Lange, pp. 60-61). If we think through to the end these statements of Lange, we must conclude that the real scientific task for the given series of phenomena is not only the precise determination of emotional reactions of the vasomotor system to different kinds of effects, but also a completely systematic explanation of forms or shades of emotion depending on the causes that produce it. If the task of causal explanation cannot be admitted as secondary for natural-science knowledge, then,

226

The Teaching about Emotions

obviously, fear of ghosts and fear before an enemy bullet, as completely special forms of fear, can be explained and scientifically known not otherwise than in connection with their causes. It is obvious that together with a physiological explanation of emotions there must also be purely psychological explanations. But does this not return us to the Cartesian teaching on the possibility of a dual causal consideration of passion caused one time by movement of low spirits and another time, by thoughts of a loved or of a hated object? Between mental and physical emotions, there is a difference in causes as there is in presence or absence of consciousness of the corresponding causes. As we have seen, causes and consciousness of them do not remain neutral with respect to the experienced emotion, but give it a completely determined and distinct form different each time from other forms. If one should want to differentiate emotions on the basis of such a principle (ibid., p. 66), then, of course, there can be no objection to this. It is completely natural that leaving everything in the sphere of physiology, Lange does not see the possibility of drawing a precise boundary between mental and physical causes of emotions. For this reason, for him, the similarity between emotions of different origin (mental and physical) is so vividly apparent that it appears much more distinctly than the difference between them. But this is so only as long as we stay in the sphere of physiology. Obviously, if we begin to study emotion from another, a psychological aspect, the difference will appear much more substantial than the similarity. Since they go beyond the limits of pure physiology and develop their investigation on the plane of physiological psychology, W. James and C. G. Lange attempt to explain emotional experience as such in a causal way, and vaguely sense that they cannot avoid the mixing of causal and teleological points of view that, according to the correct statement of Miinsterberg, comprises the distinctive mark of all of the old psychology. When, describing the essence of fear, together with the pulse and color of the face Lange names the force of speech and clarity of thought, ascribing them all to the same order of physical symptoms of fear, he assumes, in a most obvious way, a mixing of two dissimilar aspects in the study of emotions. In completely the same way, in presenting a state of anger that he reduces to agitation in the breast, flow of blood to the face, distention of nostrils, clenching of the teeth, and a drive toward energetic acts, James clearly mixes intentional and causal approaches to the study of emotions. For, strictly speaking, what do clenching of teeth, and a drive toward energetic acts, disturbance of the pulse and blurring of thoughts have in common? The James-Lange theory cryptically contains the requirement for supplementing the method of considering emotions with another method implemented by descriptive psychology of feelings. This is clear from the following: as we have seen, James, thinking through his theory again and again, comes to the conviction that in emotional manifestations, we have not simple reflexes, but that the manifestations always imply the individual's consciousness of the special meaning and sense that he contributes to the given external impression. Fear, anger, and other reactions and the impUlsive actions develop because the external impression is understood by the individual and is for him a subject of fear or anger. Scheler would have willingly subscribed to these words since they wholly contain the idea of intentional tendency of emotion toward an object and a need for explaining the intelligible connections and dependencies that determine and promote our concrete feelings all the time. So that no doubt should remain that the idea of descriptive psychology of feelings is contained as an internally indispensable link in the chain of Cartesian reasoning and in the most consistent of all explanatory theories of emotions, in the

The Teaching about Emotions

227

James-Lange hypothesis, let us recall Descartes' teaching on purely mental, intellectual passions, on emotions that can develop in all their magnificence regardless of the body, the teaching in which Sergi sees the ruins of the visceral theory. He says that emotion, according to that teaching, is directly connected with ideas and their play. In this point of Cartesian teaching, Sergi correctly sees a transition of the physiological trend into an intellectualistic and finalistic trend, a transition to absolutely new points of view that open new horizons. We have already traced this new direction, these new points of view, these new horizons in sufficient detail and know them quite well. They are nothing other than the methodological system of the descriptive psychology of feelings. In the same way, James' teaching on independent emotions originating from pure activity of our thoughts cannot assume anything different as its further development except the consistent descriptive psychology of feelings that considers emotion not in the categories of causality, but from the intentional point of view-as mental activity aided by disclosure of a world of internal relations that determine the life of our spirit. Except for an intuitive understanding of sensible connections and relations that are disclosed directly in experiences, what remains for scientific understanding of these purely spiritualistic sensations? With this, we can conclude the investigation of the problem of causal explanation in contemporary psychology of emotions and summarize the results to which it brings us. We have seen that the naturalistic theory of emotions tempted the scientific thought included in this theory with the possibility of a genuine, that is, causal, knowledge of the nature of human feelings. This was the higher point toward which the hypotheses of Lange and James were directed, and in the attainment of which they saw great triumph. The creation of the psychology of emotions as a science in the real sense of the word and overturning the metaphysical teachings in this area seemed to them to be directly connected with evidence of the possibility of a strict causal explanation of emotional life. But the naturalistic theory suffered a breathtaking catastrophe specifically on the problem of causality. The higher point toward which it was directed was the point of collapse and demise. The problem of causality split contemporary psychology of emotions into two irreconcilable parts internally presuming each other. Causal explanation needed a consideration of the teleological as a supplement. In an imperceptible way, the explanation grew into an intuitive understanding. Instead of subverting the metaphysical teachings, psychology had to resort to them as to its last and only basis. The pillar and confirmation of truth in the teaching on emotions were found in the metaphysics of the seventeenth century. James stated that purely descriptive literature on this question, beginning with Descartes even to our day, represents the dullest section of psychology in order that Dilthey might tum to metaphysical anthropology of the seventeenth century as to the only source of living psychology, the path to which lies through improving methods of the old spiritualism. It seems to us that in this sense, Ribot, who in general had a condescending attitude toward the theory of Lange and James, understood better than others the internal dependence of their theory on Cartesian teaching, indicating the fact that the theory led to retracting the unjust attack on the thought of Descartes, which he expressed in the Treatise on Passions of the Soul; he also had a better understanding of the internal failure of this theory, which is exhibited most clearly in the formulation and solution of the problem of causality. Ribot says: "The only point on which I diverge from the James-Lange theory, which seems to me closest to truth in its attempt to explain facts from the aspect of those who do not admit psychological substances, pertains to the set-up of the theory, not to its basis. It is

228

The Teaching about Emotions

obvious that both our authors, consciously or not, take the same dualistic point of view as the representatives of the dominant opinion to which they object. The whole difference between them is in the view of causes and effects: some see cause in emotions, others, in physical manifestations. "However, I believe that understanding of cause and effect, any relation in general of causality, must be excluded from this problem and the dualistic position must be replaced by the unitary and monistic. The teaching of Aristotle on matter and form seems to me to be more correct if as matter, we understand somatic facts and as form, mental states corresponding to them. Nevertheless, both of these terms are closely connected and can be separated only by abstraction. Due to tradition in ancient psychology, the behavior of the soul and the behavior of the body were studied separately. The newer psychology does not look at it in this way. Actually, if the problem were given a metaphysical coloring, then we would not be dealing with psychology. However, if it remains within the sphere of experimental psychology, then there is no reason to separate them because they go hand in hand. Consciousness must not be separated from its physical conditions: they comprise one natural whole that must be studied as such. "Considering a separate emotion, we find that movement of the face and the body, vasomotor agitation, respiratory and secretory changes express objectively what is expressed subjectively by the state of consciousness that corresponds to them, classiHed according to qualities on the basis of internal observation. It is one and the same phenomenon expressed in a dual form. This unitary point of view, corresponding more closely to the nature of things and to contemporary trends in psychology, saves us in practice, in my view, from many objections and difficulties" (1: Ribot, 1897, pp. 107-108). Most remarkable in the criticism of Ribot is the exposure of the true essence of the theory of James and Lange. Ribot shows that their theory is what it is, that is, the classical theory of the cause-effect dependence between emotional experiences and manifestations turned inside out. All the paradoxical quality of this theory consists only of its showing us the back of the classical theory. But essentially, the new theory wholly retains the dualistic basis of the old. The one and the other considers emotional experiences and manifestations from the point of view of the cause-effect dependence. The whole difference between them is in the view of cause and effect. Some see cause in emotions, others, in physical phenomena. Cause and effect change places, but the members of the cause-effect dependence remain the same. 1: Ribot is also right when he sees the only means of overcoming the dualistic and metaphysical quality of the James-Lange theory in a complete elimination of the causality relationship, the concept of cause and effect, from the explanation of this problem. He proposes replacing dualistic understanding with a monistic hypothesis of parallelism and interaction-a hypothesis of psychophysical identity. But in this way, the problem of causality in contemporary psychology of emotions grows directly into a psychophysical problem; its analysis must also be the concluding link in our consideration of the results to which we were brought by the investigation of the old and the new Cartesian psychology of passions in their internal relations to each other.

20 First, the most naive and direct impression that inevitably arises when one becomes familiar with the James-Lange theory (from the moment of its appearance

The Teaching about Emotions

229

even to our day) is the notion that it is directly connected with some kind of definite solution to the psychological problem in the area of the teaching on emotions. For this reason, this theory first of all forces the illusion of being materialistic. The illusion has been exposed repeatedly, but it firmly persists and remains intact, renewing itself with each new investigator to the most recent time. James himself had to accompany his theory with a justifying thesis: "My point of view cannot be called materialistic." Obviously, it was clear to him that this problem requires explanation, that at first glance, this theory may seem to the reader to be a theory that leads to an unchangeable, materialistic interpretation of the phenomena of emotions. About his theory, James says: "In it, there is no more and no less materialism than in any view according to which our emotions are facilitated by nerve processes" (1902, p. 313). In a general form, this position does not disturb anyone, but it is easy to find materialism in it as soon as we speak about one or another particular kind of emotion. "Such processes were always considered by psychologists who tend toward Platonizing as phenomena connected with something exceptionally low. But no matter what the physiological conditions of the formation of our emotions might be, in themselves, as mental phenomena, they must, nevertheless, remain what they are. If they are deep and pure mental facts valuable in significance, then from the point of view of any theory of origin, they will remain just as deep, pure, and significant in meaning for us as they appear from the point of view of our theory. They include in themselves an internal measure of their meaning, and to prove with the proposed theory of emotions that sensual processes do not necessarily have to be distinguished by a low, material character is just as logically absurd as to argue against the proposed theory by citing the fact that it leads to a low, materialistic interpretation of the phenomena of emotions" (ibid., p. 313). James was, of course, completely right when he tried from the very beginning to explain in this way the relation of his theory to materialism. Of course, only to a naive view can it appear that this theory necessarily contains in itself a materialistic explanation of the nature of our feelings. The dependence of mental processes on nerve processes is an indisputable fact for all scientific psychology, and any physiological theory, no matter in what it might see a material cause of nerve processes, leaves open the question of a material or idealistic interpretation of the relation between nervous and mental processes. In this sense, the peripheral theory of emotions actually contains in itself no more and no less materialism than the central or any other theory. For this reason, the point of view of contemporary reactological psychology and behaviorism, according to which the James theory must be considered as a living embodiment of materialistic, natural-science thought, can seem to us to be nothing other than illusion. If James had to defend his theory against enemies who accused him of materialism, then investigators of our time must defend this theory from its friends and successors who praise it for its materialistic quality. Thus far, this theory has been considered as revolutionary, clearly and prominently emphasizing material, purely physiological roots of mental states. Thus far, there is a tendency to see in it a manifestation of exceptional boldness. In this way, behavioral psychology of our day accords the James theory undeserved honor in the same way that its opponents contemporary to James raised equally undeserved charges against it. This seems so obvious and clear after the explanation of James and what we have said above on the materialistic and idealistic character of the theory under consideration that the problem seems completely exhausted from the very beginning by the simple uncovering of the widespread illusion. Illusion remains illusion. The theory of James contains in itself no more and no less materialism due to the fact

230

The Teaching about Emotions

that it develops a hypothesis on the peripheral origin of emotions than the theory opposite to it which insists on their central origin. Nevertheless, the problem is much more confused and complex than it may seem at first glance. It is not at all exhausted by a simple uncovering of the illusion. It urgently requires investigation. We believe that one fact has a paramount significance for elucidating the problem: regardless of the explanation of James himself on the seeming materialistic quality of his theory, it still became a part of the history of psychology as a materialistic interpretation of emotional life and, in this respect, shared the fate of many explanatory theories which, as Dilthey correctly notes, were connected time and again with materialism. The last is explanatory psychology in all its variants. Every theory that assumes as a base a connection of physical processes and includes mental facts in them is materialism (W Dilthey, 1924, p. 30). The historical fate of the James theory was expressed primarily in that it not only was accepted by the most radical wing of contemporary natural-science psychology, but also produced in its image a similarly influential and powerful direction that was called the psychology of reaction or behavior. In essence, the James theory, as we have shown above, anticipated the teaching on conditioned reflexes as the basis of behavior. We have already expressed the opinion on conditioned reflexes of one of the investigators that all of contemporary psychology of reactions is constructed according to the pattern of and much like the James-Lange visceral hypothesis. Thus, uncontrollably striving toward a natural-scientific materialism, biological and mechanistic psychology was the direct continuation of James' work. The James theory, however, proved capable of entering into contact with the spiritualistic trends in psychology. If the connection of this theory with natural-science psychology becomes self-evident in light of the naturalistic and materialistic principles that unite them, the connection with spiritualistic trends requires explanation. This connection becomes comprehensible only when we recall a fact we have mentioned repeatedly: that the opposite poles of contemporary psychology are internally united with each other and presume each other, that their combination goes back to Descartes, who, as we have explained, can be considered the father of mechanistic psychology and spiritualistic psychology, which do not exclude, but complement each other. Time and again, we have seen how the spiritualistic theory found a primary basis for its own structure in a consistent mechanistic explanation of some question. The James-Lange theory plays the same role in contemporary spiritualistic psychology, the clearest example of which may be the psychology of H. Bergson. Before explaining the relation of Bergson to the James-Lange theory, and the connection through which he includes this theory in his psychology of feeling, in keeping with the psychophysical problem that interests us now, we must emphasize specifically this aspect of the theory. We will use the well-known theses of Bergson on psychophysical parallelism. These contain in a concise form the basic view of this great contemporary philosopher on the metaphysical basis of psychology. 1. Bergson says, if psychophysical parallelism is not distinguished by either strictness or completeness, if there is no absolute correspondence between each specific thought and a specific state of the brain, then the concern of the experiment is to note with increasing approximation specifically those points where parallelism begins and ends. 2. If such experimental study is possible, it will measure more and more precisely the deviation between thought and the physical conditions in which this thought operates. In other words, it will elucidate ever better the relation between man-thinking substance and man-living substance, and in this way, elucidate what might be called the meaning of life.

The Teaching about Emotions

231

3. If the meaning of life can be determined empirically with more and more precision and fullness, then positive metaphysics is also possible, that is, incontrovertible and capable of rectilinear and infinite progress. The theses cited express not only the basic goal of metaphysical psychology, but also the method with which it attempts to reach the goal as well as the prerequisites of this method. The prerequisites must interest us primarily because they disclose the significance of the psychophysical problem for all of spiritualistic psychology and the place it occupies in the system of applied metaphysics. It is reduced to empirical determination of the meaning of life and comprises the basic task of metaphysical psychology. Thus, the assumption is that the meaning of life will increase as we succeed in noting and establishing more and more completely the mutual divergence of the mental and the corporal in man. The undoubted legitimacy of such formulation of the question on psychophysical parallelism, as G. Bellot 121 noted in his discussion of the theses of Bergson, not only returns us to the unresolved problems of Cartesian metaphysics, but tries to place them on the solid, scientific ground of factual investigation. The trend in contemporary philosophy-transferring the solution of a number of central philosophical problems to the area of concrete scientific knowledge as well as the converging trend of contemporary scientific psychology-is a trend to include consciously a number of philosophical problems directly contained in empirical investigation in the circle of psychological studies; we have already noted this trend as one of the most remarkable trends of our science, leading steadily to convergence of philosophy with psychology and a very deep transformation of the whole structure and content of contemporary philosophical and psychological investigation. We repeat, in a certain sense, the present investigation was also produced by this trend and tries to find its internal justification in it. In connection with the theses cited, Bellot says that Bergson's method is new more because of the original and ingenious use the author makes of it than in itself. It is scarcely necessary to recall here that the greatest part of Cartesian metaphysics was attributable to the problem of the relation between the soul and the body. The Cartesians considered the main task to be making these relations comprehensible, while Bergson remains alone on the ground of facts and, specifically on establishing a certain irregularity in mutual psychophysical relations; he wants to bring out the necessity of a spiritualistic hypothesis. In response to the objections raised by Bellot, Bergson not only did not consider it necessary to repudiate a connection between the method he proposed for defending the spiritualistic hypothesis and the Cartesian method, but objected to contrasting the method he proposed for defending the spiritualistic hypothesis with the method of Descartes. Bergson believes that the criterion of comprehensibility for Cartesian philosophers was much more empirical than they themselves thought. He was in full accord with intensifying their own experience. But our experience is much more extensive. It has become so extensive that we had to give up-for almost a century-the hope of universal mathematics. Thus, comprehensibility extends, little by little, to new concepts, themselves suggested by experience. "If the Cartesians returned to life now, would they have the same idea of comprehensibility?" asked Bergson, assuming that it would become false to the method of Descartes and would require a review of the Cartesian teaching, including specifically of the trends which, without a doubt, the philosopher-Cartesian would need if he had a more flexible science and assumed a complexity of organization in natural phenomena that would be difficult to convert into a mathematical mechanism. If a certain position of the mind relative to its object, a certain adaptation of forms of investigation to their material is called a method, then this does not mean re-

232

The Teaching about Emotions

maining true to the method and preselVing its devices while the materials with which this method operates change radically. To remain true to a certain method means to constantly transform its form according to the material so as always to preselVe the same precision of adaptation. Thus, Bergson proceeds completely consciously in defense of the spiritualistic hypothesis along the path marked out by Descartes. The difference between the metaphysics of Bergson and that of Descartes consists only of the fact that he tries to improve the method and extend the boundaries of comprehensibility in keeping with the richer scientific experience, and, rejecting the specific devices of investigation introduced by Descartes, he remains faithful to his method, adapting it to contemporary scientific knowledge. In his own words, Bergson only accepts science in its contemporary complexity, and having this new science as material, renews his efforts, similar to those that the old metaphysicians made, depending on a more simple science. He breaks with the old mathematical framework, he takes into account biological, psychological, and sociological sciences, and on this broad base constructs a new metaphysics. This is the only difference between his and Cartesian spiritualism. The basic method of Descartes, the method of clear comprehension of the complete separation of the spirit and the body, transferred to the ground of contemporary scientific knowledge and converted into the method of experimental investigation of the divergence of thought from the physical conditions in which it operates, this is the method of Bergson. After this, there should be no special difficulties in understanding the method with which Bergson includes the JamesLange theory in his spiritualistic concept. On the contrary, we should be surprised rather by the precision and the coincidence, even in the details, with which the logical structure of Cartesian teaching on the passions of the soul, in which the spiritualistic principle is balanced by the mechanistic, is reestablished in all its fullness in the new historical setting, in the new scientific expression. Bergson achieves this logical balance by supplementing his spiritualistic conception with the mechanistic theory of emotions, In studying the intensity of psychological states, which Bergson makes a prerequisite as an introduction to the analysis of the problem of freedom of the will, he wholly accepts the theory of James on the centripetal origin of sensations of stress. On the one hand, he applies the theory of organic sensations as bases for experiencing the intensity of mental states to attention and the intellectual effort that accompanies it, and, on the other hand, to the violent or keen emotions (anger, fear, certain varieties of joy, grief, passions, and desires). Physiological movements accompanying attention are not the cause and not the result of the phenomena, but a part of it, seemingly expressing attention extended over space. When attention is demanded, after its work is finished, we seem to be conscious of increasing pressure of the soul, increasing nonmaterial effort. Analyze this impression, and you will find in it only the feeling of muscle tension that extends spatially or changes its essence; for example, stress is transformed into pressure, fatigue, and pain. Bergson sees no substantial difference between the stress of attention and what might be called effort of mental stress, for example, keen desire, furious anger, passionate love, mad hatred. For this reason, the intensity of strong emotions is nothing other than the muscular tension that accompanies them. Bergson cites as remarkable the description of physiological symptoms of fear given by Darwin and cited by James. He says: "Of course, we do not agree with James that the emotion of fear can be reduced to the sum of organic sensations; an irreducible element always enters into the feeling of anger, even if it were only the idea of hitting or fighting of which Darwin speaks and which gives so many different movements a common direction. But if this idea determines the direction of the emotional state

The Teaching about Emotions

233

and orientation of accompanying movements, it seems to us that the increasing intensity of the state itself is nothing other than an ever more profound jolting of the organism. Exclude all traces of jolting of the organism, all weak attempts of muscle contraction, and you will have nothing but only the idea or, if you do not want to deny emotion, emotion devoid of intensity." These words of Bergson leave no doubt that his disagreement with James is purely illusory (Bergson sees this disagreement in the presence of a mental element that is not reducible to peripheral sensations-the idea of hitting or fighting): and James always admitted the presence of such an idea in emotions, but only denied it, precisely as Bergson does, as the specific quality of an experienced feeling, retaining for it only the right to be called a purely intellectual state. But, of course, Bergson does exactly the same thing. His statement: exclude all traces of jolting of the organism, all weak attempts of muscle contraction, and of the feeling of anger, you will have only the idea-he repeats the statement of James word for word: suppress in yourself external manifestation of passion and it will be extinguished, subtract, one after another, all sensations of bodily symptoms connected with it from the state of our consciousness, and in the last analysis, nothing will be left; anger will be completely absent, and as a residue, there will be only calm, passionless judgment, wholly belonging to the intellectual area, that is, the idea of hitting or fighting, about which Bergson is speaking. Even in syntactic structure, Bergson's position is completely analogous to a similar statement of Lange: eliminate in a frightened person all physical symptoms of fear-what then will remain of his fear? Bergson cites Spencer, who says that intensive fear is expressed in a scream, in an attempt to hide or to conceal oneself, in twitching or trembling. Bergson says that we go even further and maintain that these movements are a part of the feeling itself of fear; they convert the feeling of fear into an emotion capable of passing through various stages of intensity. If you suppress these movements, the intense fear will be replaced, more or less, by the idea of fear, an intellectual representation of danger that must be avoided. The same thing can be said about the keen feeling of joy, grief, desire, repulsion, even of shame, the cause of the intensity of which is rooted in automatic reactive movements produced by the organism and perceived by consciousness. From this point of view, Bergson does not see a substantial difference between deep feelings, for example, the feeling of compassion, aesthetic feeling, and others, and the keen, strong emotions that were just listed. To say that love, hatred, or desire increase in strength is to say that they are projected outward. that they radiate to the surface, that peripheral sensations replace the internal elements. But regardless of what kind of feelings these are, superficial or deep, sudden or deliberate, their intensity always consists of a multitude of simple states that our consciousness vaguely differentiates. In this way, Bergson, in his own words, refusing to see in an affective state anything other than a mental expression of the jolting of the organism or an internal echo of external causes, fully assumes the point of view of James. The James-Lange theory finds for itself just as firm a place in the spiritualistic psychology of pure spirit as in the natural-science psychology of behavior. If we ask what function this theory can serve in the spiritualistic psychological system, how it can reinforce the psychology of the spirit, what kind of auxiliary problem it can resolve in the common defense of the metaphysical hypothesis, what its role in this system is-stated more briefly, why is it necessary to the restored Cartesian method, we will be able to give no answer other than indicating that the naturalistic theory of emotions fulfills in the neo-Cartesian teaching exactly the same role that it fulfilled in the teaching of Descartes himself: lowering passions-that basic phe-

234

The Teaching about Emotions

nomenon of human nature-to simple manifestations of the soulless robot of our body, and in this way, clearing the way for admitting the absolutely indeterminate, free, mental will, independent of the body. For this reason, Bergson is completely right when he says that in defending the spiritualistic hypothesis, he lays claim to continuing the work of the Cartesians, but takes into account the greater complexity of contemporary science. This is why we are justified in considering the fact that he includes the James-Lange theory in the psychology of feelings as very important in a symptomatically theoretical respect. The fact indicates that the James-Lange theory, representing, as we have seen, a development of one of the only nonindependent parts of Cartesian teaching, acquires its sense and true significance only by being again restored to the structure of this whole. Thus, Cartesian teaching, including in itself a prototype of all mechanical theories of emotional life on the one hand, and the teaching of Bergson, which reunites naturalistic and spiritualistic parts of the old conception on the scientific ground of contemporary natural science on the other, illuminate for us the philosophical nature of the theory under consideration. They show what it was at the time it was founded before it became differentiated and branched from the complex ideological whole within whose structure it appeared, and what it inevitably had to become in its full development, being again included in a whole system, an organic and separate part of which it comprises. As we have noted, it is most remarkable that this theory retains the same role in the new spiritualistic system that it fulfilled in Cartesian teaching. It lowers our passions to the level of simple automatism in order to raise above them the free activity of the spirit. We can be convinced that this is really so from the investigation of Bergson of the relations between the spirit and the body. Here, Bergson fully accepts and brings to a logical limit the purely mechanistic view of the activity of the brain. He tries to show that the brain is only the weapon of action, an organ capable of creating motor automatisms but not having in itself any other possibilities. Completely in accord with mechanistic natural science, Bergson tries to trace step by step the progress of external perception, beginning with the amoeba and ending with higher vertebrates. He finds that even at the stage of a simple cell cluster of protoplasm, the living material exhibits irritability and contraction, that it responds to external influences, reacts to them mechanically, physically, and chemically. Moving up the series of organisms, we note a physiological division of labor, the appearance of differentiation and combination into a system of nerve cells. Together with this, the animals begin to react to external stimulation with more various movements, but always what is involved here is automatic motor reaction. In higher vertebrates, there is, without a doubt, a radical difference between purely automatic acts, which are always controlled by the brain stem, and conscious activity, which requires involvement of the brain. It would be possible to imagine that, in this case, an impression coming from outside, instead of spreading in the form of movements, might be converted into cognition. But Bergson assumes that it is enough to compare the structure of the brain and that of the brain stem to be convinced that between functions of the brain and reflex activity of the brain stem there is only a difference in complexity and not in essence. This is the basic idea of Bergson's philosophy. There is virtually no difference between functions of the brain and the reflex activity of the brain stem. This means that the whole development of perception, beginning with the amoeba and ending with higher vertebrates and man, does not result in the appearance of anything essentially new, if we consider it from the point of view of organization of physiological conditions. Development is only an increase in complexity of automatism that was already established in the organism of the amoeba, and the difference

235

The Teaching about Emotions

between the functions of the brain in man and irritability and contraction of the simple cluster of cells of protoplasm is only in complexity, but not in essence. Not to see a major difference between the activity of the brain and the brain stem, and to bring the reflex activity of the brain stem to the greater variability of motor automatisms in comparison with the activity of the amoeba means to reject development as a process of continuous appearance of neoformations, it means to reduce all of the higher cerebral activity to the automatism of a simple reflex or even lower-to the irritability of protoplasm.

••• In no way does analysis of the Bergsonian teaching on the relation of the body to the spirit enter into the subject matter of the present investigation. In concluding a review of the fate of Cartesian teaching on the passions in contemporary psychology, it was important for us only to show that this teaching was polarized with respect to the contradictory principles included in it and found its embodiment in the extreme mechanistic and spiritualistic conceptions of contemporary psychology.

ON THE PROBLEM OF THE PSYCHOLOGY OF THE ACTOR'S CREATIVE WORK! The problem of the psychology of the actor and theatrical creative work is at one and the same time old and completely new. On the one hand, it seems there never was one even somewhat great theatrical pedagogue or critic, not one man of the theater in general, who might pose this question one way or another, and who, in practical activity, performance, teaching, or evaluations, would not proceed from one or another understanding of the psychology of the actor. Many who were active in the theater produced extremely complex systems of the actor's performance in which they found a concrete expression not only of purely artistic aspirations of their authors, not only the canons of style, but also systems of practical psychology of the actors' creative work. Such, for example, is the famous system of K. S. Stanislavsky, full of theoretical staging, which, unfortunately, we do not have to this day. If we try to trace the sources of theatrical psychology, they will take us far back, and we will see the great and very difficult problems of this area, which over the course of a century and in various forms, agitated the minds of the best representatives of the theater. This problem, which D. Diderot 2 poses in the remarkable "Paradox of the Actor," already anticipates the sharpest arguments between various contemporary theatrical systems, but he, in his turn, was anticipated by a number of thinkers of the theater who long before Diderot put the question in a somewhat different form, but on the same plane as Diderot sets it. There is something basic in this formulation of the question, and when one begins to study attentively its historical development, one is inevitably convinced that, obviously, it is rooted in the very essence of the creative work of the actor as it opens up to direct understanding, which is still wholly guided by a naive amazement before a new psychological phenomenon. Thus, in theatrical systems, if the problem of the psychology of the actor with all its changes retained as central the paradox of the actor's emotion, then in the new time, investigations of a different type opened paths applicable to the same problem. New investigations begin to involve the actor's profession in the common circle of investigations of the psychology of the profession, bringing to the forefront the psychotechnical approach to the actor's craft. The problem usually at the center of attention is how certain general qualities and traits of human giftedness should be developed to ensure the person who has them success in the area of theatrical creative work. Tests are created to study fantasy, the motor system, verbal memory, and excitability of the actors, and on that basis, a "professiogram" of the actor's work is constructed according to exactly the same principle as analogous psychograms for any other professions are constructed; then, according to the register of established qualities, people are selected for this profession who best correspond to this register.

237

238

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

Only very recently did we note an attempt to overcome the inadequacies of this and another approach to this problem and formulate it differently. In this respect, works of a new type have come into view; in this respect also, we have called the problem of the psychology of the actor a problem that is completely new and almost not studied at all. It is easiest to define the new approach to the old problem by comparing it to two former trends. They had a common inadequacy over and above the unique, radical methodological flaws that characterize each of them separately and which to a certain degree is opposite in the one and the other system of investigation. The common inadequacy of former trends is the complete empiricism, the attempt to proceed from what is on the surface, to establish facts that are directly grasped and to elevate them to the rank of a scientifically discovered pattern. Although the empiricism with which people of the theater work is frequently an area of phenomena profoundly unique and extremely significant in the general sphere of cultural life, although facts such as the specific works of great masters are dealt with here, the scientific significance of these materials is not beyond the limits of the collection of factual data and general deliberation over the formulation of the problem. Also marked by the same radical empiricism are the psychotechnical investigations of the actor's craft which cannot rise to any extent above the directly factual data and include them in the general, previously determined methodological and theoretical understanding of the subject. Moreover, as has been indicated, each of these trends has a specific inadequacy. Stage systems, from the actor, from theatrical pedagogy, from observations of rehearsals, and during performance, which are usually enormous generalizations of the producer's or actor's experience, set specific, unique, features of experience, inherent only in the actor, as being of paramount importance, forgetting that these features must be understood against a background of general psychological patterns, that the actor's psychology comprises only a part of the total psychology in both the abstract-scientific and the concrete-life significance of this word. However, when these systems reach to general psychology for support, the attempts prove to be a more or less accidental connection in the manner of that which exists between the Stanislavsky system and the psychological system of T. Ribot. Psychotechnical investigations, on the other hand, lose sight of all specificity, all the uniqueness of the actor's psychology, seeing in the creative work of the actor only a special combination of the same mental qualities that are found in a different combination in any profession. Forgetting that the activity of the actor is itself a unique, creative work of psychophysiological conditions, and not analyzing these specific conditions in all the variety of their psychological nature, the investigatorspsychotechnicians dissolve the problem of the actor's creative work in general, and at the same time, banal test psychology, paying no attention to the actor and all the uniqueness of his psychology. The new approach to the psychology of the creative work of the actor is characterized, first of all, by the attempt to overcome the radical empiricism of the two theories and to comprehend the psychology of the actor in all the qualitative uniqueness of its nature, but in the light of more general psychological patterns. At the same time, the factual aspect of the problem assumes a completely different character-from abstract, it becomes concrete. If formerly the testimony of one actor or another or one epoch or another was always considered from the point of view of the eternal and unchanging nature of the theater, now investigators approach a given fact mainly as a historical fact which is finished and which must be understood most of all in the full complexity

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

239

of its historical condition. The psychology of the actor is formulated as a problem of concrete psychology, and many irreconcilable points of view of formal logic, abstract contradictions of various systems, identically reinforced by factual data, are explained as a living and concrete historical contradiction of different forms of the actor's creative work, which changes from epoch to epoch and from theater to theater. For example, Diderot's paradox of the actor consisted of the fact that the actor portraying strong heartfelt passions and agitation on the stage and bringing the audience to a high emotional pitch, himself remains devoid of even a shade of the passion that he portrays and that shocks the viewer. The absolute formulation of the question by Diderot is as follows: must the actor experience what he portrays, or is his acting a higher form of "aping," an imitation of an ideal prototype? The question of the internal states of the actor during a stage play is the central node of the whole problem. Must the actor experience the role or not? This question was subjected to serious discussion, but the very formulation of the question suggested that it would allow only one answer. Moreover, contrasting the work of two actresses, Clairon3 and Dumenil,4 Diderot knew that they were representatives of two different systems of the actor's craft, equally possible, although opposite in a certain sense. In the new formulation of the question that we are considering, the paradox and the contradiction contained in it find a resolution in the historical approach to the psychology of the actor. In the beautiful words of Diderot, "first of all, pronouncing the words, 'Zaira, you are crying,' or 'you will remain there, my daughter,' the actor hears himself for a long time and he hears himself at the moment when he touches you, and all his talent is not in feeling, as you might think, but in transmitting most subtly the external signs of feeling and thus deceiving you. The cries of his grief are distinctly revealed in his ears, his gestures of despair are imprinted in his memory and were preliminarily learned in front of a mirror. He knows completely precisely at which moment to take out his handkerchief and when his tears will flow. Expect them at a certain word, at a certain syllable, not sooner and not later. The trembling voice, the interrupted words, the muffled or drawn-out sounds, the shuddering body, buckling knees, the swooning, the impetuous outbursts-all of this is the purest imitation, a lesson learned by heart in advance, a passionate grimace, a splendid 'aping'" (D. Diderot, 1936, pp. 576-577). As Diderot says, all the passions of the actor and their expression enter as a component part into the system of declamation, are subject to no law of unity, and are selected and harmoniously disposed in a certain way. In essence, two things that are very close to each other, but never completely merged, are mixed in Diderot's paradox. First, Diderot has in mind the most superpersonal, ideal character of the passions that the actor projects from the stage. These are idealized passions and movements of the soul; they are not natural, live feelings of one actor or another, they are artificial, they are created by the creative force of man and to that extent must be considered as artificial creations, like a novel, a sonata, or a statue. Because of this, they differ in content from corresponding feelings of the actor himself. Diderot says: '~ gladiator of ancient times is like a great actor, and a great actor is like an ancient gladiator; they do not die as people die in bed. They must portray before us a different death so as to please us, and the viewer feels that the bare, unadorned truth of movement would be shallow and contrary to the poetry of the whole" (ibid., p. 581). Not just from the point of view of content, but also from the aspect of formal connections and couplings that determine their course, the feelings of the actor

240

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

differ from real, live feelings. Diderot says: "But I want very much to tell you, as an example, how an actor and his wife, hating each other, played a scene of tender and passionate lovers in the theater. Never have two actors seemed so strong in their roles, never have they aroused such long applause from the orchestra and the loges. We have interrupted this scene dozens of times with applause and shouts of delight. This is in scene three, act four of Moliere's Le Depit Amoureux" (ibid., p. 586). Later, Diderot brings in the dialogue of the two actors, which he calls a dual scene, a scene of the lovers and a scene of the husband and wife. The scene of the declaration of love is interwoven here with the scene of a family quarrel, and in this interweaving, Diderot sees the best evidence of his being correct (ibid., pp. 586-588). As we have said, Diderot's view is based on facts, and this is the source of his strength, his unsurpassable significance for a future scientific theory of the actor's creative work. But there are also facts of an opposite character which do not in the least refute Diderot. These facts consist in the fact that there is still another system of performance and another nature of artistic experiences of the actor on the stage. If we take an example close at hand, the evidence is all the stagecraft of the Stanislavsky school. This contradiction, which cannot be resolved in abstract psychology with the metaphysical formulation of the question, has a possibility of being resolved if we approach it from the dialectical point of view. We have said that the new trend poses the problem of actor psychology as a problem of concrete psychology. Not eternal and unchangeable laws of the nature of actors' experiences on the stage, but historical laws of various forms and systems of theatrical plays are in this case the controlling direction for the investigator. For this reason, in the refutations of the paradox of Diderot that we find among many psychologists, there is still the attempt to solve the problem on the absolute plane regardless of the historical, concrete form of the theater whose psychology we are considering. Moreover, the basic prerequisite of any historically directed investigation in this area is the idea that the psychology of the actor expresses the social ideology of his epoch and that it also changes in the process of the historical development of man just as external forms of the theater and its style and content change. The psychology of the actor of the Stanislavsky theater differs much more from the psychology of the actor of the Sophocles epoch than the contemporary building differs from the ancient amphitheater. The psychology of the actor is a historical and class category, not a biological category. The idea central to all new investigations that determines the approach to the concrete psychology of the actor is expressed in this one aspect alone. Consequently it is not biological patterns primarily that determine the character of the actor's stage experiences. These experiences comprise a part of the complex function of the artistic work that has a definite social, class function historically established by the whole state of the mental development of the epoch and class, and, consequently, the laws of coupling passions and the laws of interpreting and interweaving feelings of a role with feelings of the actor must be resolved primarily on the historical plane and not on the plane of naturalistic (biological) psychology. Only after this resolution can the question arise of how, from the point of view of the biological patterns of the mind, one or another historical form of the actor's work is possible. Thus, it is not the nature of human passions that determines directly the experiences of the actor on the stage; it only contains the possibility of the development of many most varied and changeable forms of the stage implementation of the artistic forms.

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

241

1bgether with recognizing the historical nature of the problem that interests us, we come to the conclusion that we have before us a problem based in a dual respect on the sociological prerequisite in the study of the theater. First, like any concrete mental phenomenon, the actor's work represents a part of the social-psychological activity that must be studied and defined primarily in the context of that whole to which it belongs. The function of a stage performance in a given epoch for a given class must be revealed as must the basic trends on which the actor's effect on the viewer depends, and, consequently, it is necessary to determine the social nature of the theatrical form in the context in which the given stage experiences will have a concrete explanation. Second, admitting the historical character of this problem and at the same time touching on the experiences of the actor, we will begin to speak not so much of the individual psychological context as of the social-psychological context in which the experiences are included. In the happy German expression, the experiences of the actor are not so much a feeling, of "I" as a feeling of ''we.'' The actor creates on the stage infinite sensations, feelings, or emotions that become the emotions of the whole theatrical audience. Before they became the subject of the actor's embodiment, they were given a literary formulation, they were borne in the air, in social consciousness. The melancholy of Chekhov's Three Sisters, presented on the stage by actresses of the Arts Theater,5 becomes the emotion of the whole audience because it was to a large degree a crystallized formulation of the attitude of large social circles for whom its stage expression was a kind of means of realization and artistic interpretation of themselves. In the light of the aspects indicated, the significance of actors' awareness of their work becomes clear. The first thing we come to is the establishment of the limited significance of this material. From this point of view, the actor's awareness of his sensations, the data of self-observation of his acting and his general state do not lose their significance for studying the psychology of the actor, but cease being the only and universal source for making judgments on their nature. They show how the actor recognizes his own emotions and what their relation to the structure of his personality is, but they do not disclose for us the nature of these emotions in all their actual fullness. Before us, we have only partial, factual material that illuminates the problem from only one point of view-from the point of view of the actor's self-awareness. In order to extract from such material its whole scientific significance, we must understand the part it plays within the system of the whole. We must understand the psychology of one actor or another in all his concrete historical and social circumstances; then the normal connection of the given form of stage experience with the social content that is projected through this actor's experiences to the audience will become clear and understandable for us. We must not forget that the emotions of the actor, since they are a fact of art, go beyond the limits of his personality and make up a part of the emotional dialogue between the actor and the public. The actor's emotions experience what R Paulhan6 felicitously called a "fortunate transformation of feelings." They become understandable only if they are included in the broader social-psychological system of which they are a part. In this sense, one must not separate the character of the stage experience of the actor, taken from the formal aspect, from the concrete content that includes the stage image, and relation to and interest in that image from the social-psychological significance and from the function that it fulfills in the given case of the actor's experience. Let us say that the experience of the actor trying

242

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

to laugh at a certain structure of psychological and life types and of the actor trying to give an apologia for the same images will, naturally, be different. Here we are approaching close to an extremely important psychological moment, the inexplicability of which was the cause, in our opinion, of a series of misunderstandings of the problem that interests us. For example, most of those writing about the system of Stanislavsky identified this system in its psychological part with the stylistic tasks that it initially served; in other words, they identified the system of Stanislavsky with his theatrical practical work. nue, all theatrical practical work is a concrete expression of a given system, but does not exhaust the whole content of the system, which can have many other concrete expressions; theatrical practical work does not present the system in all its range. A step toward separating the system from its concrete expression was taken by E. B. Vakhtangov,7 whose stylistic aspirations were so very different from the initial naturalism of the Arts Theater, but who, nevertheless, was aware that his own system was an application to new stylistic tasks of the basic ideas of Stanislavsky. This can be demonstrated with the example of Vakhtangov's work on the staging of Princess Turandot. 8 Wishing to project from the stage not simply the content of the tale, but his own contemporary relation to the tale, his irony, a smile "addressed to the tragic content of the tale," Vakhtangov creates a new content for the play. B. E. Zakhava 9 tells of a remarkable case from the history of the staging of this play: "At the first rehearsals, Vakhtangov used this device. He proposed the actors play not the roles indicated in the text of the play, but Italian actors playing these roles ... For example, he proposed that the actress playing the role of Adelma play not Adelma, but an Italian actress playing Adelma. He improvised on the theme, supposing she were the wife of the director of the troupe and the mistress at the opening, that she is wearing broken shoes, that they are too big for her and when she walks, they flap at the heel, slap the floor, etc. Another actress playing Zelima is an idler who does not want to act, and she does not at all hide this from the public (she wants to sleep)" (1930, pp. 143-144). Thus, we see that Vakhtangov directly changes the content of the play he is given, but in the form of its presentation, he depended on the same foundation that was put in place in the system of Stanislavsky: Stanislavsky taught that finding the truth of feelings on the stage is an internal justification of each stage form of behavior. Zakhava says: "Internal justification, the basic requirement of Stanislavsky, remains as before one of the basic requirements of Vakhtangov, only the content itself of these feelings is entirely different with Vakhtangov than with Stanislavsky ... If the feelings now become different, if they require different theatrical means of expression, still the truth of these feelings is as it was and will always be unchangeably the basis of the soil on which only the flowers of genuine great art can grow" (ibid., p. 133). We see how the internal technique of Stanislavsky and his mental naturalism come to serve completely different stylistic tasks, opposite in a certain sense to the one that they served at the very beginning of development. We see how certain content dictates a new theatrical form, how a system proves to be much broader than the concrete application it is given. For this reason, the declarations of the actors on their work, especially summary declarations, are in themselves incapable of explaining their character and their nature; they are made up from generalizations of the actors' own and most various experience and do not take into account all of the content embodied in the actor's emotion. It is necessary to go beyond the limits of the direct experience

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

243

of the actor to explain it. Unfortunately, this real and remarkable paradox of all of psychology has not, thus far, been sufficiently assimilated by a number of trends. In order to explain and understand experience, it is necessary to go beyond its limits; it is necessary to forget about it for a minute and move away from it. The same thing is true also with respect to the psychology of the actor.
