If the actor's experience were a closed whole, a world existing within itself, then it would be natural to look for laws that govern it, exclusively in its sphere, in an analysis of its composition, a careful description of its contours. But if the actor's experience also differs from everyday life experience by the fact that it comprises a part of an entirely different system, then its explanation must be found in laws of the structure of that system. In conclusion, we would like to note briefly the conversion that the old paradox of the actor experiences in the new psychology. In the contemporary state of our science, we are still far from resolving this paradox, but we are already close to its correct formulation as a genuine scientific problem. As we have seen, the essence of the problem, which seemed paradoxical to all who wrote about it, consists in the relation of the artificially produced emotion of a role to the real, live, natural emotion of the actor playing the role. We think that solving this problem is possible if we take into account two points that are equally important for its correct interpretation. The first consists in that Stanislavsky expresses the involuntary quality of feeling in a certain situation. Stanislavsky says that feeling cannot be commanded. We have no direct power over feeling of this nature as we have over movement or over the associative process. But if feeling "cannot be evoked ... voluntarily and directly, then it may be enticed by resorting to what is more subject to our power, to ideas"lO (L. Ya. Gurevich, 1927, p. 58). Actually, all contemporary psychophysiological investigations of emotions show that the path to mastery of emotions, and, consequently, the path of voluntary arousal and artificial creation of new emotions, is not based on direct interference of our will in the sphere of sensations in the way that this occurs in the area of thinking and movement. This path is much more tortuous and, as Stanislavsky correctly notes, more like coaxing than direct arousal of the required feeling. Only indirectly, creating a complex system of ideas, concepts, and images of which emotion is a part, can we arouse the required feelings and, in this way, give a unique, psychological coloring to the entire given system as a whole and to its external expression. Stanislavsky says: "These feelings are not at all those that actors experience in life" (ibid.). They are more likely feelings and concepts that are purified of everything extraneous, are generalized, devoid of their aimless character. According to the justifiable expression of L. Ya. Gurevich,11 if they passed through the process of artistic shaping, they differ according to a number of traits from corresponding living emotions. In this sense, we agree with Gurevich 12 that the solution of the problem, as usually happens in very stubborn and long controversies, "lies not in the middle between two extremes, but on a different plane that makes it possible to see the subject from a new point of view" (ibid., p. 62). We are compelled to this new point of view both by the accumulated documents on the problem of stage creativity, the testimony of the creators-actors themselves, and by the investigations carried out in recent decades by scientific psychology (ibid., p.62). But this is just one aspect of the problem. The other is included in the fact that the transfer of the paradox of the actor to the ground of concrete psychology immediately eliminates a number of insoluble problems that were formerly its component parts and replaces them with new problems that are productive and resolv-

244

The Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work

able and place the investigator on new paths. From this point of view, there is not a biological-aesthetic and eternally given explanation, but each given system of the actor's performance is subject to a concrete-psychological and historical, changeable explanation, and instead of the once-and-for-all, given paradox of the actor of all times and peoples, we have before us from the historical aspect, a series of historical paradoxes of actors of given environments in given epochs. The paradox of the actor is converted into an investigation of historical development of human emotion and its concrete expression at different stages of social life. Psychology teaches that emotions are not an exception different from other manifestations of our mental life. Like all other mental functions, emotions do not remain in the connection in which they are given initially by virtue of the biological organization of the mind. In the process of social life, feelings develop and former connections disintegrate; emotions appear in new relations with other elements of mental life, new systems develop, new alloys of mental functions and unities of a higher order appear within which special patterns, interdependencies, special forms of connection and movement are dominant. To study the order and connection of affects is the principal task of scientific psychology because it is not in emotions taken in an isolated form, but in connections combining emotions with more complex psychological systems that the solution of the paradox of the actor lies. This solution, as might be expected even now, will bring the investigators to a position that has a fundamental significance for all of the psychology of the actor. The experience of the actor, his emotions, appear not as functions of his personal mental life, but as a phenomenon that has an objective, social sense and significance that serves as a transitional stage from psychology to ideology.

EPILOGUE 1 The basic content of this volume is comprised of two works of L. S. Vygotsky: "Tool and Sign in the Development of the Child" and "The Teaching about Emotions" ("The Teaching of Descartes and Spinoza on Passions"). Both are of great significance for understanding the wealth of ideas he left, for understanding the dynamics of his creative genius. These works were not published while the author lived. The second work was not completed. There is a basis for assuming that specifically here the philosophical-psychological quest of the remarkable Soviet investigator was interrupted, that this specifically marked the beginning of a new phase in the development of his theoretical thought. The works of Vygotsky must not be considered as something homogeneous that forms a completed, well-developed system. The reader is already familiar with his intensive and complex work on the search for new ways to solve problems that baffled the minds of psychologists of that time. We must not extract the conceptions he generated from the context of the time, which was full of controversy, critical phenomena, and attempts to transform the whole system of psychological knowledge from various positions. As far as the philosophical position of Vygotsky himself is concerned, it can be unequivocally evaluated. He was consciously and purposefully guided by the dialectical-materialistic methodology whose principles served as a compass for him in determining the general perspectives of psychological science. As we know, all Soviet psychologists were oriented toward this methodology. But applying its principles to the solution of concrete problems required special effort and the development of their own research programs from each of them. In fact, ready solutions to these problems were not to be found anywhere. The uniqueness of the "itinerary" that distinguished the quest of Vygotsky was determined by the individual style of his thinking and his special skill in correlating the situation in world psychology with the possibilities that its philosophical, Marxist interpretation was opening up before the investigators of mental regulation of activity; this interpretation contained a new understanding of the basic beginnings of a scientific explanation of the nature of man-social determinism, historicism, and the systemic approach. More than anyone, Vygotsky was sensitive to the problems combined by Western authors (Binswanger et al.) under the title "criticisms of the psychological mind" and of the special direction, the subject of which was psychological knowledge itself, its basic concepts, categorical structures, and methodological tools. Vygotsky was an unsurpassed master of concrete-methodological analysis of evolution and structure of psychological ideas. He made an important contribution to the development

245

246

Epilogue

of "self-knowledge" of psychology, to the understanding of the originality of its disciplinary status and its dependence on the needs of social practice. Vygotsky considered the union of psychology with this practice as a deciding factor in asserting the new methodological orientation of concrete psychological studies. As methodology, we must of course understand a system of general principles (methods) of organization and interpretation of knowledge and not just theoretical postulates on which it is based. For this reason, the problem of the relation of knowledge to the possibilities of its practical application in the sphere of interpersonal relations was for Vygotsky as urgent as the methodological problem. He understood very well that the methodology of concrete-scientific research is the general process of its formation, not limited by its logical-theoretical regulations. He regarded the connection with urgent needs of people and the need to meet practical social requirements as factors capable of inducing those radical shifts in the conceptual composition of psychology which were lacking in both the objective historical logic of the development of science and of the society which had just experienced a great revolution. The idea that the philosophy of Marxism and the practice of the new society serve as a pledge of psychology's escape from its jolting crisis passes through Vygotsky's thought like a red thread. Vygotsky was convinced that resorting to practice would inevitably lead to the triumph of explanatory, causal psychology similar in style to the thinking of natural sciences whose successes mankind not only prided itself on, but used everyday as well.

2

The results of the intense and productive methodological and concrete-scientific quest of L. S. Vygotsky were demonstrated in the manuscript "Tool and Sign in the Development of the Child," which may serve as eminent evidence of the achievement of foremost Soviet psychology abroad in the 1930s, and of its ideational-scientific merits over other trends of world psychological thought. The whole spectrum of these trends was invariably within the scope of Vygotsky's keen attention. The successes of the branches of genetic psychology (in the studies of the behavior of animals, of children of various ages, of culturally backward peoples, of developmental anomalies due to disintegration of higher functions) enriched the scientific knowledge of the mind with powerful layers of empirical material and advanced a number of problems of fundamental significance for theories of general psychology. Not one of the major schools of psychology (behaviorism, Freudianism, Gestaltism, sociologically oriented French psychology) could henceforward disregard them; these schools tested their conceptual apparatus on the analysis of these problems, claiming productivity for their own research program and its superiority over others. In this historical situation, in discussion with partisans of conflicting trends, Vygotsky's synthesizing mind, correlating various approaches and solutions, isolated ideas and phenomena that could serve to construct a theory based on a principally new methodological orientation, that would answer the cardinal requirements of logical development of scientific knowledge. Such a theory, according to Vygotsky's conception, was required for disclosing the patterns and mechanisms of development of higher mental functions that distinguish the vital activity of man from the behavior of all other living beings. Let us recall that the principles of determinism, systemic quality, and development act as basic explanatory principles of psychology, and that each of them

Epilogue

247

acquires a new.c~nte~t in the course of the evolution of psychological knowledge. Vygotsky was dlstmgUIshed not only by the fact that he proceeded from these principles. They also guided his scientific thought in the development of other concepts, in the polemics and confrontation in which his teaching was formed. The thought and structure of his teaching can be understood only by taking into account the essentially new traits that, in Vygotsky, enriched all three main explanatory principles. We have seen how high he placed the Pavlovian sequentially deterministic pattern of the formation of a conditioned reflex. But it (like the behaviorist conception of behavior that tested its influence) was biodeterministic and, for this reason, inadequate for disclosing factors that create the mental organization of man-primordially social in its genesis and subsequent development. The problem of the dependence of mental properties of the individual on his social connections was posed by a number of investigators, specifically, P. Janet and J. Piaget, whose views influenced Vygotsky. Both were guided by ideas of converting the "external into the internal," the interindividual (occurring in the sphere of microsocial relations) into the intraindividual (forming an internal plan of action). This idea, entering into the scientific-psychological dictionary as interiorization, made it possible to overcome the burden weighing on psychology of the dualistic representation of the nature of connections between external, objectively observable actions and contacts of the individual with other people and the internal operations of his mind, invisible processes, considered open only to the "internal gaze" of the subject experiencing them. This was a new method of deterministic analysis. Its important advantage was that phenomena whose beginning was assumed to be immanent spiritual activity of the individual appeared as derivatives of real (subject to study with the same positive means as any other science) processes of interaction of the subject with the external (social) environment. Proceeding from these processes accessible to reproduction and objective control, the investigator penetrated into the "darkness" of another's soul. The possibility of this was guaranteed by the fact that it itself was conceived and created through acts that are done in objective, spatial-temporal coordinates. Of course, with respect to mentally regulated behavior, these coordinates have specific characteristics. The individual acts in a specific environment, not physically or chemically identical, and the relations of people are qualitatively different from relations between natural objects. Human relations are mediated by two powerful determinants, independent of individual consciousness, but forming it-by tool and sign. Both, being products of cultural-historical development, transformed the prehuman forms of mental life into truly human forms. This is how the matter stood in phylogenesis. According to Vygotsky, to psychology falls the mission of discovering their determining role in ontogenesis, in the formation of those functions whose systemic interconnections form the higher level of mental development. 'furning for these purposes to the idea of interiorization, Vygotsky accepts as a decisive factor the conversion of external, objectively observable processes of interaction of the individual with his real-object and human-surroundings and the inclusiveness in these processes of the system of tool and cultural-sign as means. Specifically these means (tools and signs, and mainly speech signs) ensure the initial integration of the child into the microsocial community in the midst of which occurs the miracle of converting his natural, very simple functions into higher, culturalhistorical functions. These represent a qualitatively new formation, a kind of crown of the great tree of universal-historical development of the mind. Its features (voluntary control of behavior on the whole and of separate processes-perception,

248

Epilogue

attention, memory, etc. and initiation of these processes by the subject without direct stimulation from outside), absent at very low levels of evolution, served from primordial times as the main argument for the indeterministic version of the uniqueness of consciousness, its nondeducibility from real, earthly conditions of human existence. According to Vygotsky, at the level of ontogenesis, these conditions are hidden in the "symbolic activity of the child," organized by the adult. Initially, this activity is a "social form of cooperation." Social connections, being cooperation mediated by tools and signs, are converted from connections that develop during external, direct contact into deep layers of the personality, which, using means acquired in the practice of joint activity, is now capable of voluntarily controlling its own mental acts outside cooperative activity as well. In Vygotsky's interpretation, this history of conversion of means of social behavior into means of individual-psychological organization acts as the principal track of formation of higher mental functions. P. P. Blonsky was the first to stand up for affirming the historical point of view in psychology. But in promoting this requirement, he stood on the ground of reflexological representations. Even the teaching of I. P. Pavlov on conditioned reflexes was permeated with the idea that behavior is "historical": it changes, is modified, and is restructured, acquiring new forms in the process of individual development. Determinants of development were (a) the capability of the organism for a signal interaction with the environment (acquisition by external stimuli of the function of signals) and (b) the need of the organism to preserve his basic vital constants (homeostasis), realized by means of the indicated capability. As long as thought was limited by these determinants, it could not escape from being slave to biology. Obvious qualitative differences between reactions of animals and man remained outside the zone of causal explanation. In the search for an answer to the question on the specifics of higher nervous activity of man, Pavlov came to the idea of two signal systems. "Second signals" -speech signals-were treated as replacements of the first, introducing into the work of the cerebral hemispheres a new principle-abstraction, generalization, higher analysis, and synthesis-everything that is the privilege of the human intellect. It must be noted, however, that resorting to speech signals could bring about radical changes in the categorical structure of scientific knowledge only if these signals were interpreted as applying to a special level of interrelations of the organism with the environment different from the signal-homeostatic interrelation. The level of determination of which we are speaking was discovered by Marxist philosophy. This level is represented by the history of work in the course of which man, altering external nature, forms his essential mental strengths and subjects them to his own authority. The new historical view decisively changed the whole system of thought. The word as an integral component of behavior "at the stage of man" now meant something different in principle than the Pavlovian "second signal." It appeared with the status of an "instrument," and the individual operating with it controlled his own actions, which acquired traits of voluntariness, conscious control, and radical planning. In these traits, the old psychology found evidence that not even one of the other realia was comparable to consciousness. Moreover, consciousness was assumed to be something primordially given, not derived from anything, an essence of a special kind. It was assumed that there was only one alternative with this kind of view-reductionism: reducing mental facts to nerve processes or stimulus-reaction relations. The permanent view of Vygotsky is defined by his confirming a different alternative, and this required a transition from an orientation toward Pavlov's teaching on conditioned reflexes to an orientation toward the teaching of Marx on the social essence of man.

Epilogue

249

The natural-science content of the Pavlovian conception now entered in a derived form into the trend in psychological analysis projected by Vygotsky. The significance of the revolution precipitated by Pavlov consists in the development of a special scientific category-the category of behavior. Having assimilated it and depending on the Marxist explanation of factors in the formation of the internal mental world of man, Vygotsky introduces new variables into the pattern of individual behavior, giving each of the individual's acts an "instrumental" significance-in Vygotsky's terminology-and including the "natural" process of formation of the mind (perception, attention, memory, etc.) in the sociocultural series. In operating with signs and symbols, the individual transforms the mental structure given by nature into an apparatus of self-regulation of his acts, recorded as the history of the culture and as methods of social interaction. Considering Vygotsky's cultural-historical conception presented in its genesis in the manuscript "Tool and Sign ... ," we see that, reflecting the supraindividual needs of the development of psychology, this conception was generated due to the uniqueness of the individual scientific biography of its author. His work in physiology and aesthetics reflected the influence of A. A. Potebnya, the subjective, idealistic understanding of whose works (by Potebnya's partisans) structuralists (to whom Vygotsky was close at one time) contrasted with antipsychologism-a system that disconnected the product of culture from the activity of its creation, which required that the product be regarded as organized according to special laws that had nothing in common with psychology. They saw no possibility of treating the processes of creation other than from the point of view of traditional psychology that trapped them within the boundaries of the internal world of the subject. Pavlovian teaching led to a way different from the traditional; in its concepts, behavior was given a strictly objective explanation. This teaching, like structuralism, rejected turning toward the internal, SUbjective plane of vital activity. But if structuralism concentrated on the product as an objective creation constructed on bases independent of the actions of anyone whatever, Pavlov studied processes and mechanisms. They pertained to biologically based behavior and not to the interaction of a personality with the world of culture. Nevertheless, since they pertained not to results estranged from action, results in which processes of their generation are not evident, but to the real, living action, the Pavlovian teaching served as a point of support for Vygotsky in overcoming his earlier psychological tendencies. Moreover, in itself this teaching could not become a lever for the construction of psychology (since the subjective seemed to extend "this side" of behavior). Having passed through the school of Potebnya and then of Pavlov, Vygotsky turned to the Marxist explanation of the phylogenesis of consciousness and found his point of support there. Tools-signs represent cultural value independent of the individual. This is their basic difference from stimuli-signals that serve, according to Pavlov, as regulators of response reactions. But these cultural signs are not special essences foreign to everything subjective, as they appear in structuralistic conceptions. They work continuously in the consciousness of the subject socializing with other people, creating a complex architectonics of mental function. Thus we see that the transformations produced in psychology by Vygotsky became possible only in the ideological atmosphere of Soviet science. In the environment in which Vygotsky's talent grew, objectively and independently of this talent, ideas circulated that represented various lines of development of scientific thought. This was Russian philology, the teaching on conditioned reflexes, and the Marxist conception of man that entered the consciousness of Soviet scientists under the influence of the process of building a new society. The views of Vygotsky must be considered in the dynamics. They developed along a spiral-from culturological,

250

Epilogue

structuralistic systems that became a strong antidote to the subjective-idealistic picture of mental life, to a natural-science understanding of the mechanisms of that life developed by Pavlov. Later, the principle of cultural-historical determination of the origin of human consciousness was established, but on bases disclosed by Marxism, which made it possible to create a new variant of objective psychology. The trends that developed outside the boundaries of psychology as an independent science (progressive philology and aesthetics, the teaching on higher nervous activity, the historical-mathematical theory of society) were refined in the work of Vygotsky into a conception of higher mental functions, in which the investigation of the development of the child's mind became the empirical reference point. As has been noted, Vygotsky applied Marx' explanation of the phylogenesis of consciousness to the analysis of determinants of its ontogenesis. But in phylogenesis, sign systems appear and change in the crucible of social-historical practice, the nucleus of which is work. With respect to early childhood, however, productive work cannot be considered as a determinant of development. Nevertheless, real, practical actions of the child acquire, according to Vygotsky, a significance of the "greatest genetic instance" when they are united with the application of symbolic signs. Later, in critical notes on the cultural-historical theory, certain Soviet psychologists emphasized the fact that the theory is distinguished by the tendency to understand the social only as interaction of consciousnesses (of the child and the adult)-outside material practice: But in doing this, they lost sight of the fact that central for Vygotsky was the problem of the genesis of individual consciousness (higher mental functions), of the transition to a uniquely human type of regulation of behavior that is formed in the first years of the child's life when there can be no thought of "material practice" in the sense of social production. As far as the primary forms of practical actions ("practical intellect") are concerned, Vygotsky not only included processes of the child's manipulation of external objects in the category of factors most essential for his mental development, but also disclosed the qualitative difference between these processes and the "tool" actions of higher animals. As we assume, there is no basis for including Vygotsky in the ranks of the partisans of the formula "socialness without the material," which the French social-psychological school followed, which undoubtedly had an effect on him, specifically in connection with Vygotsky's including the principle of sociodeterminism in the explanation of the genesis of the human mind (from this also ~omes the idea of interiorization). Understanding socializing as symbolic activity, he believed its tool to be speech, a phenomenon of culture, therefore something independent not only of individual consciousness, but also of direct contacts of this consciousness with the consciousness of others. Reference to culture (language, art, science, and other forms of culture) as systems of values created by society, but not diluted in the processes of communication among its members, allowed the construction of the study of consciousness at the beginnings of historicism. Cultural signs (symbols) and methods of operation recorded in them (just as a tool may not be a physical thing but a material "cluster" of work operations accomplished by social man) become rooted, according to Vygotsky, in consciousness "from outside," and "grow" into it. But they bring with them not just the experience of interpersonal contacts. Owing to them, the individual becomes a co-participant in the great world of culture. + We must admit that even less well-founded than reproofs that Vygotsky ignored prac'See specifically: Psychological Science in the USSR, Moscow, 1960, Vol. 1, p. 433 and others. tWe note, by the way, that Vygotsky developed not a "cultural-historical theory of thinking," but a theory of the formation of higher mental functions as special regulators of behavior in ontogenesis.

Epilogue

251

tical forms of interrelations of the individual with the objective environment are assertions that he seemed to consider mental processes as a reflection of physiological processes in the brain. The whole essence of the cultural-historical conception can be reduced to disclosing the determinants external with respect to the bodily substrate, transforming it into a carrier of higher mental functions as a purely human regulator of activity. Of course, just as any other theory, the cultural-historical conception is a child of its time. But it should be judged by what is new that it introduced in comparison with former knowledge, by the shifts it caused in the general categorical apparatus of psychological thought, and by its influence on the subsequent development of science. Approaching the contribution of Vygotsky with such criteria, we have a basis for assuming that the development of the explanatory principles that guided the efforts of subsequent generations of Soviet psychologists can be traced to him.

3 The unfinished work of L. S. Vygotsky on the psychophysiology of emotions must be considered in the context of the general conceptual quest of the author. Its sense was determined by the task of constructing a new teaching on the mind of man as an integral and developing essence in all the fullness of his life. Vygotsky moved step by step toward this teaching. The movement of his thought was directed toward encompassing all the variety of mental manifestations in a single picture, the methodological framework of which was formed by the principles of determinism, development and systemic quality. These had long since guided psychological knowledge, giving it the dignity of a science. But their content changed from one epoch to another, transforming both the theoretical composition of the concepts of vital activity as well as its empirical investigation. Among the three indicated methodological principles, determinism led as the inherent idea of the causal connection of phenomena. "... fA] causal explanation is the basic problem of the possibility of psychology as a science ... " Vygotsky maintained decisively (p. 181 of the present volume). Vygotsky considered the central factor in the crisis being played out in psychology to be the sharp confrontation of two trends-the causal and the spiritualistic. In the plan to unlink the "two psychologies," some features of the development of knowledge of the mind during the period being considered took on a different aspect. If the so-called elementary processes (sensation, perception, association, reaction time) were successfully included in the orbit of deterministic analysis, others (thinking, will) continued not to be subject to it. Finally, overall mental activity of man was represented in the form of two heterogeneous orders of phenomena. Consciousness seemed to be divisible into lower and higher functions, into essences belonging to different worlds and subject to investigation in concepts with no connection among themselves. Vygotsky's thinking strained against this in order to overcome the version of "two psychologies." He decidedly rejected the path of their eclectic union, the path of compromise solutions. '~ in the legend, two trees united in their crowns tore into two the body of the ancient prince, so every scientific system will be torn into two if it attaches itself to different trunks" (Vol. 1). According to Vygotsky, the trunk that ensures progress for psychology is perhaps only the natural-science, causal study of psychological facts directed toward real causes hidden from the consciousness observing itself (no matter how penetrating the observation-whether it is traditional introspective psychology, or in-

252

Epilogue

sightful, phenomenological, or any other kind). Speaking of the new psychology capable of leading investigation of its subject out of the crisis controversies, Vygotsky assumed that resolving this problem would not at all mean that the builders of the future would all have to begin from the beginning, rejecting as fallacy the preceding "efforts of free thought to master the mind" (Vol. 1). Together with the works of G. Fechner, H. Helmholtz, A. Binet, and T. Ribot, he referred the James-Lange peripheral theory of emotions to the ranks of these efforts. Such was the initial relation of Vygotsky to this theory when, turning to the historical path in psychology, he isolated in it the idea that represented the trunk of a natural-science explanation of mental functions, the development of which must become the new science of these functions in the post-crisis epoch. The James-Lange peripheral theory, according to which the bodily reaction to a stimulus is primary and the emotional state coupled with it secondary, actually contained elements of deterministic explanation. Feeling, which for centuries was considered as a product of the soul (although accompanied by bodily shocks), appeared as an effect of processes within the organism. This gave Vygotsky a basis for assuming that the orientation of the camp that opposes idealism, indeterminism, and phenomenology to cognitive psychology was represented in the peripheral (visceral) theory of emotions. However, having been involved with the problem of emotions and having made a careful analysis of the history and methodology of its development over several years, Vygotsky reviewed his evaluation. He came to the conclusion that this theory reproduces the explanatory plan created by Descartes and that both the theory and the plan combine what is incompatible, specifically, causality with spiritualism. In this, both appeared "to bind themselves to two different trunks" and for this reason were "torn in two." Evidence for this position is the chapter on the broad panoramic study of emotions, which Vygotsky pondered and which he managed to finish. Thus, if in his first prognoses of the paths for overcoming the crisis situation, he saw the future of psychology in its gravitating toward being scientific and following the centuries-old tradition of the causal course, into this general view, an important corrective was now introduced: not every causality is capable of bringing psychology to new limits. The Cartesian variant of causal interpretation of the psyche does not simply oppose the spiritualistic as an antithesis, but is its unfailing twin, its inseparable sojourner. This led to a basically new understanding of the roots of the psychological crisis, which now appeared not only as a crisis of Cartesian introspective conception of consciousness (such an opinion was dominant in psychological literature), but as a crisis of Cartesian treatment of determinism, which was inherent in it. In its turn, this meant that the problem that the psychologist must solve by methodological thought to overcome the crisis situation in his science is not solved by transforming the view supplied to it by the 17th century, the view of consciousness as observing its own content and acts. Let us remember that the question of consciousness was at the epicenter of the psychological crisis. During the time when psychology was being isolated into an independent science, it was faced with defining its own subject that distinguished it from philosophy (as whose servant it was considered for ages) and from physiology (owing to the achievements of which experimentation was introduced into it). Phenomena of consciousness accorded the subject in his direct experience were isolated as unique realia not studied by any other sciences. It was assumed that these could be grasped through particular, specially organized internal observation. According to this kind of program, which at first inspired many young investigators, psychology was entrusted with the search for the threads from which the "material"

Epilogue

253

of consciousness is woven and the laws according to which they are "interwoven." Consciousness was represented by a kind of internal "field" (devoid, however, of spatial characteristics), where reflection finds its nonmaterial objects (phenomena, processes). Not one other science is interested in them. They remain the portion of the psychology. Although the version according to which the closed sphere of consciousness serves as the subject of psychology, where only self-observation can penetrate, held its ground for quite a long time, it was exploded at the beginning of the 20th century, mostly due to the successes of Freudianism and behaviorism. The former portrayed consciousness as an agent that maximizes the actions of powerful latent forces. The latter demanded an end to consciousness as a vestige of times of scholastics and alchemy. In this historical situation, for psychology oriented toward Marxism, the problem of primary importance was the struggle for consciousness which, however, represented not a return to the obsolete introspective version, but to overcoming it along the paths of developing a new theory of conscious control of

mental activity.

Vygotsky acted as an initiator of this struggle and this effort. We must not forget the uniqueness of this historical period. At this time, in the sciences pertaining to man, the materialistic approach was thought to be expressed by the formula, "psychology without consciousness." Vygotsky immediately attracted attention with his paper, "Consciousness as a Problem of the Psychology of Behavior" (Vol. 1). The struggle for consciousness at that time meant a struggle against its ancient treatment, arising before the time of Augustine, which continued to guide the minds of those who were engulfed not by speCUlative metaphysical meditations, but by everyday work in the laboratory surrounded by apparatus and protocols of experiments. A great deal of time and effort was given to criticism of introspection ism and attacks on the subjective method. It seemed that psychology, having finished with this method, would stand on firm ground. However, owing to a penetrating analysis of common methodological principles of psychology in their historical development, Vygotsky disclosed the internal connection between introspection and mechanodeterminism. In this way, it became evident that no matter how sharp the criticism of introspective representations was, introspection in itself was inadequate without a radical transformation of the deterministic method of thinking from which the classical introspective conception that caused so much difficulty in psychology was inseparable. Vygotsky concentrated his dagger-like blows of critical thought not against this conception, the weakness of which was demonstrated in many ways, but against determinism (in its mechanistic variant), which seemed to place psychology in the same rank as other natural sciences. Descartes was the father of mechanodeterminism in psychology, and his principal psychological work was the Ireatise on Passions of the Soul. According to Vygotsky, the ideas of this Ireatise hold the central place in the whole methodological structure of the psychology of the new times, including its experimental trend. '~1 the principal contradictions of contemporary psychology, both those lying at the base of its crisis and those pertaining to its separate and particular problems, are contradictions embedded in the Cartesian teaching on the passions. In this sense, we do not know another book the study of which would be as central in its significance to understanding the real historical sense of the whole past of psychological science and its contemporary crisis as the last, concluding work of Descartes-his Ireatise on the Passions . ... In a certain sense, we are justified in asserting that this Ireatise, little known to psychologists and far from central, stands

254

Epilogue

at the very beginning of all contemporary psychology and all the contradictions that are tearing it apart (pp. 180-181 of the present volume). The annals of psychology-from the 17th century to the period of the crisis inclusive-considered from this point of view, appeared as a verbatim translation of Descartes' teaching on the passions. Vygotsky's careful analysis of this work determined this. His analysis disclosed the historical limitedness of the mechanodeterministic approach not only with respect to the emotional states of man, but also with respect to the entire sphere of the mind as a whole. For this reason, for Vygotsky, the subject of the historical-methodological analysis is, together with emotions (feelings, affects), the broadest complex of radical psychological problems-the psychophysical (relation of consciousness to phenomena of the physical world that generate it), the psychophysiological (relation of these phenomena to the bodily mechanism), the psychognostic (pertaining to the cognitive role of mental processes), the psychopraxic (the problem of the influence of consciousness and will on the behavior of the individual). We have before us one of the main features of the thinking of Vygotsky-his skill in considering every single event in psychology through the "magic crystal" of the new methodology that allowed him to determine immediately a great number of conceptual threads that connect an event with the whole front of the movement of scientific knowledge.

4

The scheme of Descartes, fixing a historically limited form of determinism, was devised in a period when a new view of the causal connection of things was being examined in the forge of professional activity, the advantage of which over the natural philosophy of the middle ages became ever more evident. Descartes himself indicated that for explaining nature, he learned much from the example of certain bodies artificially created by man. Descartes imagined an organic body similar in form to a mechanism whose components and the principle of their interaction were accessible to direct experimental testing; before Descartes, the motive force and organizer of all actions of such an organic body was believed to be the soul. But in his doctrine, man appeared as a center of incompatible sources: of a consciousness that only thinks and a body that only moves. Dualism also permeated Descartes' views of emotional life, an analysis of which is also found at the center of Vygotsky's manuscript published in this volume. The Soviet psychologist turned toward the past with a historical-methodological purpose, and not with a purely historical investigation of the problems of his science. Here again we have before us one of the defining features of his work-the undeviating drive to realize the principle of historicism with respect to an explanation not only of the patterns of the mind, but also with respect to the activity of the mind in recognizing these patterns. Vygotsky invariably related psychological facts and theories and the subject content of knowledge of mental reality to an analysis of the means used for constructing this knowledge. His research proceeded synchronically as if on two levels, constantly relating analysis of fact, ideas, and hypothesis to explaining the principles of the work of the intellectual apparatus that generated these products. Scientific fact, idea, hypothesis-these are the thoughts of the scientist that reflect reality with varying degrees of adequacy. But there may also be "thoughts of a second order" that have as their subject thinking itself, its structure and dynamics, which are no less real

Epilogue

255

than the objects produced through them and independent of consciousness. Thrning to "thoughts of a second order," the investigator becomes a logician of science, its methodologist. Knowing how to apply the logical-methodological approach to what is concrete-scientific facilitated the major achievements of Vygotsky. Just in knowing the work of Vygotsky "The Historical Meaning of the Crisis in Psychology" (Vol. 1), we would be convinced that in working on logical-methodological problems, the author, educated in the philosophy of dialectical materialism, developed their innovative understanding that was realized with respect to the situation in psychology at the turn of the 19th century, at a time when this discipline was disintegrating into a multitude of schools and trends. In contrast to L. Binswanger and other Western authors who presented the problem of methodological investigation as a particularly logical "criticism of concepts," Vygotsky indicated that concepts are continuously criticized in the practice of scientific work itself and in the processes of their correlation with empirical data. From this it followed that the task of methodology is not to prescribe from outside rules and norms of activity corresponding to criteria of science in general for workers in a concrete area of knowledge, but to derive principles of effective organization of this activity from its real dynamics, from its history. The idea that logical-methodological study of science must be inseparably linked to the historical was basically new. To what extent Vygotsky was perspicacious in this respect, we can confirm in our time when the disintegration of traditional methodological conceptions in Western philosophy resulted in the appearance of the so-called historical school (K. Popper, 1. Lakatos, T. Kuhn et al.), representatives of which declared that the philosophy (logic, methodology) of science without the history of science is empty. This trend appeared during the epoch of scientific-technical revolution with its dynamism, when, before the eyes of a single generation, seemingly immovable scientific truths collapsed and radically new views were generated that, in this way, demonstrate graphically the historical essence of scientific knowledge. Long before these processes, Vygotsky was demonstrating that methodological investigation of scientific knowledge can be carried out only on a historical basis, and he himself left examples of such investigation, specifically, the critical analysis of the methodological sense of one of the basic chapters of psychology-the teaching on emotions. Attempts to reconstruct the past of this teaching were made even before Vygotsky. Thus, an interesting historical sketch pertained to the well-known Canadian historiographer G. Brett (Vygotsky used his abstract). But Vygotsky's attention was drawn not to relics, but to the ideas and principles that arose long before contemporary experimental psychology though they penetrated its body and continued to control every step of its workers.

5

The James-Lange peripheral conception, the Cartesian genealogy of which Vygotsky traced, set the approach inherent in mechanistic physiology. It did not even try to explain the vital sense of perturbation in internal organs, which, according to this conception, resulted in the subjective states pertaining to the discharge of emotions. Moreover, experimental investigation of bodily changes in emotions gave impetus to a theory that was an alternative to the peripheral. This was called the central theory since it moved the bodily substrate of feelings to the higher nerve centers. Its author was the American physiologist, W. Cannon,

256

Epilogue

whose conclusions Vygotsky analyzed in detail. Studying the reason for the inhibition of secretion of gastric juices in an experimental dog due to emotional excitation, Cannon concluded that to explain this phenomenon, it is necessary to consider the "adrenaline factor" (secretion of suprarenal adrenaline) internally connected with functions of the sympathetic nervous system. This system is responsible for autonomic "storms" observed in various affects (fear, rage, etc.). Did the James-Lange hypothesis influence Cannon when he approached his experiments? There can be scarcely any doubt that it did, if only because Cannon studied with James at Harvard and, for this reason could have, so to speak, firsthand knowledge of his views. James' key idea explained affects physiologically on the basis that their source is hidden in the depths of the organism. It seemed that Cannon followed this path. Emotions were correlated with changes in the internal organs (and not in the soul or in consciousness). In the introduction to the Russian edition of Cannon's work, Bodily Changes in Pain, Hunger, Fear, and Rage, the editor of the translation, the Soviet biologist, B. M. Zavadovsky, wrote that the Cannon book shows that the James hypothesis "in our eyes, is invested with real concrete forms of the biological experiment" (in: W. Cannon, 1927, p. 3). L. S. Vygotsky justifiably disputes this evaluation, turning attention to Cannon's works as a physiological basis for the peripheral theory of emotions. This theory had no empirical foundation. Its authors related their assumptions to imagined, not real experiments. They said, let us remove the internal modifications from emotion-then nothing will be left of it. Testing of the James-Lange theory not in the mind, but in the laboratory showed that it was unfounded. As Vygotsky correctly noted, Sherrington was the first to test it, but even stronger arguments against it were developed by the Cannon laboratory, where a new experimental model appeared at the beginning of the 20th century-the sympathectomized animal, that is, an animal in which the sympathetic section of the autonomic nervous system was completely removed. All the emotional reactions usually typical for these animals were fully retained. In the presence of a dog, the sympathectomized cat raised its paw with extended claws, made a threatening sound, and bared its teeth. Moreover, due to the removal of the sympathetic ganglia, no changes at the level of peripheral reactions (vascular and others) could occur in the animal. Using the method of extirpation of various segments of the brain, Cannon and his students developed a hypothesis according to which the main organ of emotional behavior is the thalamus. Contemporary physiologists assume that emotions cannot have any limited substrate as a neurohumoral base. The conception of interaction of the cortical-subcortical structures is given preference. Experimental and clinical data favor the decisive role of the hypothalamolimbic system. Vygotsky could not have known about these data. According to James, visceral sensations, and only these, give perception an emotional flavor, an intimate warmth and richness of experience. On the basis of the physiological experiment, Cannon concluded that James ascribed to visceral sensations a function that was completely foreign to them. Signals proceeding from internal organs, being very weak, cannot serve as a means of distinguishing such powerful emotions as fear and rage. In this case, what is the biological value of the diverse and remarkable manifestations of activity of the sympathetic system? The action that James and Lange ascribed to the visceral processes, taking them to be the basis of human passions, turned out to be fictive. What then is the real purpose of these vegetative reactions? The search for the answer to this question moved Cannon to develop one of the strongest and most productive theories in 20th century physiology-the teaching on homeostasis-the preservation of the internal environment of an organism de-

Epilogue

257

spite the influence of factors that disturb it. "Processes occurring in the internal organs as a result of the activity of the sympathetic system are indeed remarkable and diverse. Their meaning for the organism, however, consists not in giving experiences a certain coloration, but rather in an adaptation of the internal 'economics' that would not allow-despite a shift in external circumstances-a substantial disturbance of the uniform order of internal life" (w. B. Cannon, 1929, p. 358). This position, noted in the second edition of Cannon's book, was developed in subsequent works of the American physiologist, who was later called the "father of homeostasis." Having formulated the teaching on homeostasis, Cannon also included in it his treatment of affects as disturbances in the "internal management of the organism." '~t first glance, changes that occur with emotional confusion look like a significant disturbance of homeostasis. In themselves, this is what they seem to be. But I am convinced that they may be explained only as preparation for strong muscular stress. When stress occurs, changes in the internal environment immediately appear to be advantageous and are rapidly neutralized by effects of the stress itself" (w. B. Cannon, 1932, p. 216). After the work of Cannon on homeostasis, changes in the internal environment of the organism due to affect appeared not as primary, but as derivative (the authors of the peripheral theory ranked affect as an ultimate cause for which there were no biological bases). These changes were significant as phenomena connected with mobilization of bodily forces for possible "flight or fight," preparing the organism for reestablishing the dynamic equilibrium of the internal environment when faced with demands that inevitably disturb its equilibrium. Cannon treated the mechanisms of reestablishing homeostasis, with which bodily changes in emotion were now connected, as products of natural selection. Thus, we see that the biodeterministic method of analysis allowed Cannon to overcome the mechanodeterminism of his teacher, James, whose peripheral hypothesis, as Vygotsky demonstrated, mistook both bodily changes in emotions and emotions themselves. However, neither Cannon's "centralism" nor his including the bodily mechanism of emotions in the general pattern of homeostasis led research beyond the limits of physiology. The categories within the system of which he worked did not make it possible to disclose the specifics of emotion as a mental reality. For this reason, Vygotsky, placing Cannon's theory above the James-Lange theory, nevertheless assumes that the first is still incapable of overcoming dualism. In explaining the limitedness of the natural-science, causal view of the psyche, biological determinism made substantial progress in comparison with mechanistic determinism, its predecessor, but it could not master its central areas; however, the limitedness did give new impetus to the indeterministic movement in philosophy. This movement isolated more tangible features of human consciousness-its internal activity, individual sense of experience, orientation toward spiritual values, and others-as an indisputable indicator of its immanent inclusion in an entirely different order of existence than secretion of adrenaline or extension of the nostrils in anger. This direction repudiated the outmoded Cartesianism-not only the mechanistic picture of the external world, but also the introspectionistic picture of the internal world, which, as was noted, dominated experimental psychology, which had separated from philosophy and was striving to find the primary elements of consciousness. Sensory (or quasisensory) nature was assigned to these elements, and the establishment of regular connections between them was a problem to be solved by methods similar to those applied in the natural sciences. As to the problem of the relation of consciousness to its bodily substrate, psychophysical parallelism was con-

258

Epilogue

sidered to be a solution uniquely consistent with the principles of natural science (specifically the law of conservation of energy). For the first time in our psychology, Vygotsky's manuscript presented a penetrating analysis of a new, idealistic direction (it entered history as a philosophy of life), which opposed both determinism and classical introspectionism. It appeared with the thesis that together with physical nature and reflexive consciousness (reason, judgment), there is a third principle that is not reducible to them. It was designated with the ancient word life, but with traits coupled to it from biological conceptions that were successfully worked out in natural science on the basis of Darwinian teaching. Among these traits was a special vital, personal principle due to which, as was subsequently said, the individual realizes himself, his existence, in a unique, inimitable way. The cognitive psychology of Dilthey was one of the first branches of this trend. With respect to the sphere of emotions, this trend turned to the variables for which there were, in general, numerous physiologically oriented explanations of its phenomena, whether they were peripheral theories or central. Conflict of causality and spiritualism that permeated the whole historical path of psychology appeared here with a new foreshortening. The specifics of emotional experiences of man, the essences of the social-historical, primordially immersed in the world of cultural values, fell, as Vygotsky convincingly pointed out, into a trap of irrationalistic philosophy and were mystified there and separated from the real, practical connections of the human personality with objective reality, from its involvement in material and spiritual productivity, with the development of which, personality and the wealth of its feelings also develops. For this reason, although it would seem that the culturological orientation in understanding these phenomena, presented by W Dilthey, M. Scheler, and others, would have to be impressive for Vygotsky, who was concentrating on criticism of naturalism, mechanism, and dualism, he rejects it with the same decisiveness as the attempts to derive emotions from vascular reaction, the functions of the thalamus, or expressive movements that remind man that he is a refugee from the simian tribe. Having organically assimilated the historical-materialistic view of human consciousness, Vygotsky discerned as culturological conceptions of Dilthey, Scheler, and others, the whole spiritualistic approach, in overcoming which, psychology affirmed principles that allowed ascribing to cognition a sense of the scientific with respect to its realia. Moreover, comparing this approach to emotional processes with the mechanistic approach, Vygotsky shows that, with obvious oppositeness, they actually complement each other, and, more precisely, are inseparable companions. Thus, setting a goal of describing the independent, sensible laws of higher emotional acts and functions ("logic of the heart" according to H. Lotze), Scheler considers them as parallel, causal psychophysical dependencies of feelings on bodily processes. In its turn, distinguishing higher and lower emotions in the conception of James coincided with the articulation advanced by Scheler, the phenomenologist. It seems that both polar views have a common root, which, according to Vygotsky, is the root that also serves the Cartesian teaching on the passions. It is a child of the scientific revolution of the 17th century, which, shaking to its foundation the model of man of the middle ages and ensuring progress of knowledge, plunged the new psychology which was developing empirical-laboratory lines into a quagmire of deep crisis for more than two centuries. L. S. Vygotsky showed that historical reflection, in turning toward long obsolete conceptions (which may be more archaic than the Descartes hypothesis on animal spirits that move the brain gland!), because it discloses the methodological infrastructure of these conceptions, reveals the source of contemporary collisions. It can

Epilogue

259

correlate contemporaneity with the quests of earlier ages only because through concrete views and hypotheses it penetrates into the depth of their categorical patterns that connect hoary antiquity with the frontiers of science. Vygotsky was a master of such penetrating analysis. The methodology of science and its history were very closely connected in his thinking. Methodology was considered as a historically dependent form of organization of knowledge, and history as a realization in irreversible time, in layers of concrete events that change from epoch to epoch, of stable, methodological, regulators of the cognitive process. According to Vygotsky, historical-methodological analysis was called upon not just to trace the dependence of developments actually accomplished in science to their "genetic" roots. Disclosing the patterns of development of science, this analysis was turned toward the future. In addition, it made it possible to evaluate what was produced in past epochs not only from the point of view of influences that led to crisis phenomena, but also to evaluate the quests for solutions that were opposed to these influences but were not developed due to the dominance of other ideological forces that were antagonistic to the solutions. Such orientation determined the position, central to all of Vygotsky's research, on the idea that, together with the Descartes teaching on the passions, which led psychology into a cycle of inescapable contradictions, the 17th century developed another teaching whose potential remained unexploited although specifically this teaching would have made it possible to reconstruct contemporary psychological theory on bases that would have saved it from these contradictions, from dividing emotions into lower and higher, from subjecting some to the laws of nature and others to laws of the spirit, from contrasting causality, teleology, descriptive explanation, etc. According to Vygotsky, Spinoza produced such an anti-Cartesian trend. Developing a basis for this conclusion required careful consideration of the nature of the relation between the teachings of both great philosophers with the same persistence and consistency with which Vygotsky demonstrated the single Cartesian genealogy of the two only seemingly incompatible theories-the visceral (peripheral) theory of emotions on the one hand, and the phenomenological (intentional) on the other. To the methodological pivot of these theories, he contrasts the line marked out by Spinoza as leading the study of human motivations and experiences into a basically new channel. If in Descartes' Treatise on Passions of the Soul, Vygotsky saw the source of the contemporary psychological crisis, then in Spinoza's Ethics, he saw ideas that made it possible to overcome the whole complex of dualistic versions of the relation of the mind to its bodily substrate, intellectual and affective (motivating), "higher" and "lower" forms of emotional behavior, involuntary and voluntary. Vygotsky completed only that part of his investigation that envisioned explaining the methodological role of the Cartesian pattern in the construction of newer, concrete-scientific conceptions. Vygotsky does not miss the chance to remind the reader that the position of Spinoza is not comparable to this pattern. He subjects to resolute criticism the historians of philosophy who treat Spinoza as an heir of Descartes. In polemics with them, Vygotsky exaggerates the spiritualistic and undervalues the deterministic trend of Descartes' teaching on passions of the soul. Vygotsky speaks of the '~ugustinianism" of Descartes. It is true that the introspective concept of consciousness was generated in the depths of religious metaphysics-in the works of Plotinus and Augustine. In Descartes, however, it is purified of religious interpretation and becomes "secular." According to the Plotinus-Augustinian conception, man uses phenomena of his consciousness to enter into contact with the Most High as the only unshakable reality. For Descartes,

260

Epilogue

however, the only indisputable object of introspection is its own thought. There may be doubt about everything, natural or supernatural, but there can be no skepticism about the judgment, "I think," from which it inexorably follows that there is a carrier of this judgment-a thinking subject. Only a man of the new formulation could give individual consciousness such independent high status. Concentrating on disclosing growing contradictions in the psychological conceptions of Descartes, Vygotsky did not pay attention to this social-philosophical aspect. He believed that its appearance was determined by mechanism and dualism, that Descartes's determinism in treating passions of the soul was exhausted by the model of a mechanistically operating, non animated body (of course, participation of the soul was completely eliminated from the organization of actions of the organism as a natural body). As a counterweight to this, it must be stressed that Descartes did not at all assume a certain category of passions to be neutral with respect to active regulation of human behavior, specifically those that, according to contemporary terminology, carry the name of emotions. According to Descartes, under the influence of emotions, the organism strives toward what is beneficial to it and avoids external effects that are harmful to it. Descartes combined the concept of emotion with the idea of self-preservation of the living body, which, as we shall see, had a major significance for Spinoza also. Self-preservation is not realized automatically. It is provided by the apparatus of emotions that fulfill a unique signal function. Regardless of the ability to think (reflect) on the benefit or harm, emotions as states generated (inanimate) by the body orient it in the surrounding world, directing it toward some objects and diverting it from others according to requirements of self-preservation. In this way, in the Descartes conception, emotions were generated by representations that severed the net of mechanodeterministic categories not from the height of incorporeal and substantial activity of consciousness intruding from outside, but on the basis of natural properties of the organism. Among these properties were two connected concepts unfamiliar to the thinking of the physicist: the factor of demand or need of the organism to conduct itself with "partiality" with respect to the world for the purpose of surviving in it (subsequently, demand was included in the category of motivation), and the factor of value that characterizes not the state of the organism, but the properties of external objects from the point of view of their significance for it (their usefulness or harmfulness). Vygotsky did not note these innovations of Descartes. Neither did he consider the evolution of Descartes' views and a feature of these as important as the striving to overcome the initial treatment of the organism in strictly physical concepts and the view of the animal as a pure machine. Descartes' deviation from mechanicism did not attract the attention of Vygotsky; he was trying to reconstruct the Cartesian teaching in a clearly consistent expression of his principles since mechanicism and dualism specifically, and not the attempts to overcome them on the part of Descartes himself, affected the path of development of psychology. As we know, Vygotsky believed his central task to be proving that in all parameters, the Spinozist system of ideas was the antithesis of the Cartesian, and that specifically the latter plunged concrete teachings on emotional processes into a crisis situation. To overcome it, it was necessary to turn back to Spinoza, who, having passed the Cartesian school, produced a teaching on man opposite to its postulates, a teaching on the motivating forces of man's behavior. Vygotsky believed that the James-Lange conception of affects, as well as its seeming alternative, the conception of experiences of Dilthey-Scheler, being Cartesian in essence, were retained in psychology only because other conceptions capable of surpassing the Cartesian

Epilogue

261

~ere not being develope~ as a counterweight. Here, he had in mind not explana-

tions of concrete mechamsms of emotional behavior, nor classification of its separate manifestations, or other forms of empirical knowledge of human feelings, but general philosophical indicators on which the treatment of these mechanisms manifestations, etc. depends. ' According to Vygotsky, the main reference point for Spinoza was naturalism, consistent, uncompromising, allowing no supernatural essences or forces and for this reason rejecting the view of passion (feeling, affect) as the most direct expression of the duality of human nature. But the problem of duality of behavior (at the human level) was not limited to its emotional aspect. From the teaching on emotions extended invisible, but unbreakable threads to the most various problems: ontological (is being a single substance), gnosiological (how is this being known), ethical (how are actions of man as a free, moral personality possible), psychological (how are the various mental functions-thinking, feeling, will-related to each other). Vygotsky showed that for each of the problems, the solution of Spinoza contradicts the Cartesian, and from this a polarity of their explanations of emotions (passions, affects) inevitably followed.

6

In working on his manuscript, immediately following the critical part, Vygotsky planned to write a positive part with a well-developed analysis of the methodological principles that could be drawn from Spinoza's Ethics, setting up a basis for a radically new direction in the study of emotions as one of the central chapters in psychology. Vygotsky mentions this project frequently, referring the reader to a following section of the manuscript which he had not yet written. We do not know whether Vygotsky produced a draft of this section or whether the ideas that he meant to present in a systematized form were not yet formulated. Th understand Vygotsky's points of departure and the vector of the movement toward the goal for which all of his investigations were undertaken, reliable material can be found only where Vygotsky chooses Spinoza as an ally in criticism of Cartesianism. We shall try to reconstruct the possible courses of Vygotsky's thought in his analysis of Spinoza from the point of view of building on it the basis of a new theory of emotions. Entering into a discussion with Descartes, Spinoza cryptically inserted his own convictions in his contra-arguments. The selection of these contra-arguments that Vygotsky used allows us, within certain limits, to present the contours of the positions whose mutual connection could form a system of positive-anti-Cartesianideas capable, as Vygotsky trusted, of converting the section on emotions into an integral part of the whole teaching on human consciousness, free of the contradictions that prevented the development of deterministic psychology (and, according to Vygotsky, no other psychology can be scientific). The first crucial word in this system was monism. Spinoza-as a counterweight to Descartes-proclaimed that nature is the only substance. In this way, he deprived the mental in any of its forms (whether it be thinking, will, consciousness) of any substantial meaning. Nature was infinite. Space, with which Descartes identified it, was only one of its innumerable attributes. Another of its attributes was thinking. What relation does this general-philosophy postulate have to the special psychological teaching on the emotional reactions of man? We have already had occasion to note that behind one or another development and sense of this "local"

262

Epilogue

teaching is hidden a whole complex of global philosophical problems. From the ontological view of substance as a single nature, disclosed to man in two of its attributes, came the idea of indivisibility of the mental and the physical, and thereby also the impossibility of influence of one of them on the other as independent substances. As a result, the question that created many blind alleys and "epistemological barriers" was eliminated not only in philosophy, but also in the very thicket of research practice of the psychologist concerned particularly with explaining the determining dependencies between the emotional states experienced by the research subject and the processes taking place in the organism and in its various systems. According to Spinoza, man represents an integral natural being. His thinking and his spatial body are included in one and the same causal order. Determined by the same causes, they cannot enter among themselves in a determining relation. There is only one governing law and one need, one and the same order both for things (including such a thing as the body) and for ideas. Spinoza treats the question of interrelations between two attributes of substance (thought and space) as a psychophysical and not psychophysiological problem. The subject here is a single "ideocorporeal" thing-the human organism-in the general order of the universe and not of the correlations between the individual soul and the individual body outside the universal pattern to which both are irreversibly subject. Spinoza repudiates the fallacious course of the idea where indivisible elements are first split, its components hypostasized (mental and bodily) into independent essences (substances, things), and then an attempt is made to find connections among what, after this operation on the integral structure, is impossible to conceive as ontologically related or logically homogeneous. On the ontological plane, the idea of psychophysical unity required identifying the general principle to which any thing is subject as a whole (in both attributive characteristics open to the human mind-as mental and as physical). Spinoza holds the drive toward self-preservation-a powerful stimulating ("motivating") force-as a universal law of existence and change in any concrete thing, laid down by nature in each of its individual realia. We have already been introduced to the idea that passions of the soul are generated from the drive of the body toward self-preservation. Descartes expressed it. Spinoza developed the idea further, according to the psychophysical monism that he inflexibly upheld. From the principle of the existence of any concrete thing, it is transformed into a moving-in essence, sole-source of human behavior. This source (let us stress once again, emanating from the universal principle of self-preservation) acquires the status of a drive, which "is nothing other than the very essence of man" (Spinoza, 1957, p. 464). Drive is a psychophysiological phenomenon because it refers to the soul and to the body. Here the indivisibility of the attributes of a single substance appears not on a "cosmic" scale, not as applied to an inexhaustible universe as a whole, but as applied to the behavior of a concrete man in all the diversity of his passions, affects, and feelings. In contemporary terminology, the phenomenon that Spinoza calls a drive is referred to the category of motivations. Accordingly, translating the philosophical principle of psychophysical unity into working, scientific language, we might say that in the material of real investigation and explanation of the facts of the vital activity of man, motivation acquired a basically suppositional character. In it, the unity of the mental and the physical, the bodily and the spiritual acquired concrete embodiment. Specifically here, a direct thread extended from the general under-

Epilogue

263

standing of the place of man in nature to the analysis of each of the concrete acts of the psychophysiological regulation of his behavior. Spinoza termed these motivational acts affects. '~s affects, I understand states of the body that increase or decrease the ability of the body itself to act, favor it or limit it, and the ideas of these states as well" (ibid., p. 456). From Spinoza's point of view, the body is an integral, psychophysical, dynamic system that undergoes changes, passing from less perfect to more perfect and vice versa. The flow of spiritual states reflects not only the shifts in this system, but is also directed toward increasing its capability to act. From this it followed that the soul seems to serve the body. It tries to have images of what is favorable to it and, on the contrary, it "turns away from an image of what diminishes or limits the capability of its body" (ibid., p. 467). According to Spinoza, the drive for self-preservation is connected internally with two other basic affects-joy and sorrow. These affects reflect the transition of man from lesser to greater completeness. Positive affects increase the capability of the body to act and the soul to know; negative affects decrease this capability. For this reason, in the context of Spinoza's interpretation, the drive toward self-preservation (self-actualization) is a drive not to equilibrium (maintaining what has been attained), but toward "self-development," toward increase, toward ceaseless intensification of activity both with respect to the body and with respect to the soul. From this understanding of the principle of self-preservation, it followed that the primary motive of behavior is expressed not in striving to attain equilibrium with the environment, but in an attitude of overcoming its resistance, of increasing the capability of the human body for self-development, for extending its psychophysical potentials. Placing motivation at the center of his psychological system, Spinoza tried to overcome not only the dualism of the bodily and the mental in the structure of behavior, but also the dualism of the lower (generated by the body as a physical thing) and the higher (flowing from the sphere of pure will) stimulating forces. "The will and the mind (intellect) is one and the same" (ibid., p. 447). This conclusion should not be interpreted in the sense that Spinoza was placing intellectualism as a counterweight to libertarianism. For him, intellect is not at all an independent force or capability. It is inseparable from the body since the attribute of thinking is inseparable from the attribute of extent. Affects move these as they move all else in human behavior. Among these are emotional (psychophysical) acts of various levels, but no matter how various they are, their nature is unity. The idea of antagonism from earliest times between body and spirit led to dividing the sphere of motivation into opposed forces. As a counterweight to this conception, Spinoza demonstrated the bodily essence of motives (affects) of any order. In the eyes of opponents of materialism, this signified exalting the lower, material inclinations to the detriment of higher values and ideals. The actual intention of the Spinozist conception was expressed by the prospect of converting these values into regulators of real and, therefore, bodily behavior. Its author still knew nothing about nerve and humoral mechanisms of motivation, about the interaction of cortical and subcortical structures, etc. But not knowing the concrete psychophysiological characteristics of emotional behavior, he maintained the general principle without which these same characteristics could not be developed. On the basis of the postulate on the inseparability of the soul from the body, whose law of life is the drive for self-preservation and the experience of joy when capability to act increases and sorrow when it decreases, Spinoza assumed that behavior always has an affective tonality, is always motivated.

264

Epilogue
