For Spinoza, the principle of primacy of the mind, of the intellectual side in the sphere of cognition (he referred sensory experience to a lower level), did not convert man into a contemplative, inactive being in the sphere of behavior. According to Spinoza, a factor of development of the mind is activity of the body. "Whoever as a child or youngster has a body capable of very little and most likely is dependent on external causes, that person has a soul that, considered in itself, knows almost nothing about itself or about God: or about things; and, conversely, whoever has a body capable of very much, has a soul that, considered in itself, has great knowledge of itself and of God and things" (ibid., p. 615). Knowledge not only of other things, but of the soul itself was placed in direct dependence on the capability of the body to act. Thus, Spinoza combined the principle of causality with the principle of active functioning that extended the possibilities of both the physical body and the inseparable whole mental organization developing with it. Unfortunately, because of the incompleteness of Vygotsky's manuscript, it is difficult to determine the spectrum of ideas that were identified by the author in the teachings of Spinoza as a starting point for developing his own solutions pertaining to the problem of motivating forces in human behavior. Nevertheless, the main lines are distinctly drawn. Determinism is primary. For Descartes, this principle signified mechanodeterminism, which entailed treating emotions as states suffered by the soul (passions) elicited by changes in the "machine of the body." As far as truly human active states were concerned (thinking, will), according to the Cartesian conception, they were referred to nonbodily acts, the final causal instance of which was the individual subject. Overcoming mechanodeterminism and its inevitable accompaniments-epiphenomenalism (everything mental, generated by the body, can only be a consequence or result, but has no independent causal meaning), indeterminism in the treatment of higher functions (since they cannot be extracted from the interaction of physical bodies), dualism (contrasting the involuntary mental processes to the voluntary)such was the historical thinking of Spinoza as presented by Vygotsky (as seems apparent from the text available to us). Since Spinoza was an eminent determinist, it followed from Vygotsky's presentation that a new form of determinism was generated in the philosophy of Spinoza-as a methodological base of a new psychological theory free of the birthmarks of the Cartesian method of thinking. According to Vygotsky, this theory has as its subject man as an integral and active psychophysical being, striving toward self-development, motivated only by bodily-spiritual needs. Its key category is the concept of motivation. Uncompromisingly causal explanation (and not description or cognition) serves as its main method. Disclosing the historical meaning of the great step taken by Spinoza on the path that made it possible to eliminate the crisis phenomena in contemporary psychology (generated by Spinoza's overcoming the Cartesian tradition), Vygotsky, however, never speaks of the limitations of this theory, of the fact that it was constructed on the basis of naturalism, and not historicism, which served as a guiding star for Vygotsky. 7

It may be that the concluding part of the work not only would have disclosed the greatness of Spinoza's contribution, but would have provided a critical analysis *Spinoza considered God identical with nature.

Epilogue

265

of his teaching. The basis for this opinion is the general conception of the development of mental phenomena given by Vygotsky in his other works and the series of positions that he developed pertaining specifically to human feelings, particularly in the essay published in this volume on the psychology of the actor's creative work. The short essay pertaining to the special question showed the orientation of the author, his approach to the experience of the personality as a cultural-historical phenomenon. ltue, in this case, the subject is not the "natural life feelings," but idealized passions produced by the creative force of the actor (which in its time inspired Diderot to consider the problem of how these two orders of emotions are related in the activity of the actor). But Vygotsky proceeded from the fact that "the actor's psychology comprises only a part of the total psychology" (p. 238, this volume), and the qualitative uniqueness of its nature may be sensed only "in light of more general psychological patterns" (p. 238, this volume). For this reason, his judgments on the specifics of the experiences of the actor (where feelings of the role and feelings of the personality merge) reflect the approach he developed toward the psychology of emotions as a whole. We shall note several of the more substantial points. First of all, Vygotsky indicates the need to surmount the empiricism expressed by the situation where the investigator, limited by testimony of self-observation or evidence of the subject on the emotional states he is experiencing, cannot rise above these facts and grasp them with a general methodological understanding of the topic. Seemingly, nothing is given to man with such direct certainty and intimacy as the feeling he is experiencing. But real knowledge about it is acquired only with an explanation of its genesis and a sense of the basis of objective patterns that are acting behind the wings of internal observation. It is obvious that Vygotsky defended the correctness of explanatory psychology of experience as a counterweight to descriptive and cognitive psychology with this thesis. In evaluating explanatory psychology as the only scientific psychology, he stood firmly on the same positions as Descartes, the physicist, and of Spinoza. However, if for them, explanation meant separating emotional phenomena from laws of nature, then Vygotsky insists on the need to consider experience as a fact that requires resorting to laws of history. It is not the biological nature of man that serves as the source of his spiritual passions and agitation, but the historical-cultural context of his activity. For Descartes, the causal explanation of emotions was built on a mechanodeterministic basis, for Darwin, on a biodeterministic one. For Vygotsky, however, the point of departure is sociodeterminism, since the historical development of behavior is determined by the action of social forces. He emphasizes: "In the process of social life, feelings develop ... emotions appear in new relations with other elements of mental life, new systems develop, new alloys of mental functions" (p. 244, this volume). If in his preceding periods of thinking about psychological systems, Vygotsky concentrated on considering the cognitive processes (perception, memory, speech) as parts of a whole, then at this point, the problems of feelings, experiences, and affects moved to the center of analysis. Now, in the new phase of his creativity, he felt especially keenly the need to comprehend the unity of the cognitive and the affective-motivational in the activity of the personality. Working basically on problems of thinking and speech, he clearly saw the limitedness of the intellectual orientation inherent in the most interesting research in this area. He began to ascribe a decisive significance to problems of the connection between intellect and affect. He treated consciousness as a "dynamic system of meaning that represents the unity of affective and intellectual processes"

266

Epilogue

(Vol. 1). Now, for Vygotsky, it was not units of verbal thinking in themselves (signs as carriers of meaning), but their affective charge that became the main subject of contemplation of the specifics of psychological analysis in contrast to linguosemiotic analysis. He emphasizes that "in every idea, there is, in a processed form, an affective relation of man to the reality represented in the idea" (ibid.). This position anticipated Vygotsky's last published work, Thinking and Speech, where he treated the meaning of the word as a unity of thinking and speech, of subject generalization and interpersonal communication. Understanding that it was necessary to move further-toward organic inclusion of human behavior in the psychological analysis, of the real motive power of the speech-thinking process: passions, needs, motivations- Vygotsky turned to the study of the role of these determinants. The last book of The Collected Works of L. S. Vygotsky was published exactly a half century after his life was cut short by tuberculosis. In just a few years, the teacher from a small, provincial, Byelorussian town became an eminent psychologist of the 20th century. The objective logic of the development of science and the social situation in the new Russia was conditioned by the appearance of Vygotsky and the greatest scientific school in Soviet psychology that gathered around his ideas, from which various trends radiated. Affirming the principles of sociodeterminism, historicism, and systemic character, Vygotsky was the first to understand the dependence of the progress of applied, specialized trends in psychology (and in this way, also its potentials to serve people directly) on creating a general psychology as a methodology of the "middle level," which would specify the concrete categories through the prism of which mental reality would become distinguishable as a special scientific subject (distinct from knowledge at the level of "everyday" concepts), accessible to empirical study, operations research, and direct instrumental control. Vygotsky's concept had a permanent influence on the fate of Soviet psychology. As far as its perception in the West is concerned, there is evidence concentrated in the evaluation of 1. Bruner: "Every psychologist who was working for the past quarter century on cognitive processes and their development must recognize the influence of the work of Lev Semenovich Vygotsky" (1. Bruner, Cognitive Psychology, p.9). -M. G. Yaroshevsky

In view here is the scene from Goethe's Faust (Part 1, scene, work room of Faust), where Faust subjects to doubt the Biblical statement, "In the beginning was the word," and replaces it with, "In the beginning was the deed." Lipmann, Hugo (1863-1925), German psychiatrist. His teaching on apraxias was published in Drei Aufsiitze aus dem Apraxiegebiet, Berlin, 1908. He also worked on problems of visual hallucinations and studied aphasia.

The Teaching about Emotions

1. This manuscript was written in approximately 1931-1933. It was given various titles: The Teaching of Descartes and Spinoza on the Passions in Light of Contemporary Psychoneurology; Spinoza,' Psychology Outlines. The Problem of Emotions; Teaching about Emotions: A Historical-Psychological Study. Short excerpts of it were published twice: "Two Trends in Understanding the Nature of Emotions in Psychology Abroad at the Beginning of the 20th Century." Voprosy Psikhologii, 1968, No.2, pp. 149-156; "The Teaching about Emotions in Light of Contemporary Psychoneurology," Voprosy Filosofii, 1970, No.6, pp. 119-130. In the present edition, the manuscript is published in full for the first time according to the text of the only preserved author's version, dated 1933. 2. Lange, C. G. (1834-1900), Danish anatomist. 3. James, William (1842-1910), see Vol. 1, p. 460. C. G. Lange's book, Emotions, was published in Danish in 1885 and later translated into other languages, including Russian (with the title, Dushevnye Dvizheniya [Mental Movements]). Vygotsky cites the Russian edition. The paper by the American psychologist and philosopher, W. James, "What Is Emotion?" (Mind, 1884, Vol. 9), was published in 1884. James later developed the ideas presented in the paper in Principles of Psychology (1890). 4. Dumas, Georges (1866-1946), see Vol. I, p. 470. Vygotsky had in view Dumas' preface to the French translation of K. G. Lange's book, later published in a Russian edition in 1896. 5. In view here is the conception of emotions developed by Charles Darwin (see Vol. 1, p. 462) in Expression of Emotions in Animals and Man (1872). First to apply the evolutionary approach and objective method to the study of emotions in man and animals, he treated expressive movements in emotions as components of adaptive behavior in the struggle for existence. In man, expressive movements in emotions were considered to be rudiments of his animal origin.

270

Notes to the Russian Edition

6. Spencer, Herbert (1820-1903), see Vol. 1, p. 471. In view here is his Principles of Psychology (1855), which influenced Darwin in particular. Spencer considered expressive movements in man in emotions as rudimentary behavior. 7. Malebranche, Nicolas (1638-1715), French philosopher-idealist, principal representative of occasionalism. He tried to combine Cartesianism and Christian theology in its Augustinian variant. 8. Titchener, Edward (1867-1927), see Vol. 1, p. 471. 9. Lotze, Herman (1817-1881), see Vol. 1, p. 471. 10. Maudsley, Henry (1835-1918), English physiologist and psychologist with a natural science orientation. 11. Sergi, Giuseppe (1841-1936), Italian anthropologist with a mathematical orientation, biologist, psychologist, cultural historian. 12. Bard, Philip (1898-?), American physiologist. He worked on the psychology and physiology of emotions. 13. Dunlap, Knute (1875-1949), American psychologist, specialist in general and social psychology and psychology of religion. In view here is a preface he wrote to a collection in which papers of James and Lange were published. Vygotsky cites Dunlap from a paper of W. B. Cannon (1927). The work of Dunlap is mentioned on p. 106 of that paper. In view of the fact that Vygotsky cites many papers on the basis of this work, we shall subsequently indicate this as: cited after W. B. Cannon, 1927, p ..... 14. Perry, Ralph (1876-1957), American philosopher-neorealist, student of James. He had a great influence on Cannon. 15. Cannon, Walter Bradford (1871-1945), American physiologist. The first edition of his book on the analysis of the physiological mechanisms of emotional processes, Bodily Changes in Pain, Hunger, Fear and Rage, was published in 1915. 16. The Russian translation of this book of Cannon, under the title Fiziologiya Emotsii. Telesnyue Izmeneniya pri Boli, Golode, Strakhe i Yarosti [Physiology of Emotions. Bodily Changes in Pain, Hunger, Fear and Rage1 with preface and editing by the Soviet biologist, B. M. Zavadovsky (1895-1951), appeared in Leningrad in 1927. This was a translation of the third English edition of Cannon's book, published in 1923. 17. Zavadovsky, Boris Mikhailovich (1895-1951), see Vol. 2, p. 491. 18. McDougall, William (1871-1938), English and American psychologist and philosopher. He contrasted behaviorism and associationism to so-called goal-directed or hormic psychology. In Introduction to Social Psychology (1908), he explained social phenomena by instincts. This is the work to which Cannon refers (W. B. Cannon, 1927, p. 153). 19. Sherrington, Charles (1857-1952), see Vol. 1, p. 462. 20. Angell, James Roland (1869-1949), American psychologist, representative of the functional trend. Cited after W. B. Cannon, p. 108. 21. Cf. Spinoza, Ethics, Part I, Axiom 6: "A true idea must agree with its object." 22. Holdberg, Ludvig (1684-1754), Danish writer and playwright of comedies. Herman von Bremen - hero of a comedy by the same name. 23. Vagus nerve. In man, the tenth pair of cranial nerves. It is a mixed nerve containing motor, sensory, and autonomic (sympathetic and parasympathetic) fibers. The results of experiments of which Vygotsky is speaking are presented in the well-known course of lectures of C. Sherrington: Interactive Activity of the Nervous System. "Lecture 7. Reflex as a Reaction of Sustained Adaptation." This was first published in London in 1906. In 1935, the lectures (including this lecture) were published in Russian in the book by R. Creed, D. DennyBrown, 1. Eccles, E. Diddell, and C. Sherrington, Reflex Activity of the Spinal

Cord. In the 1930s, in preparing L. S. Vygotsky's manuscript for publication, Z. S. Vygotskaya and A. R. Luria included in the Literature the Russian translation of this work of Sherrington, leaving the text of Vygotsky himself unchanged of course. We considered it opportune also to refer the reader to the Russian translation of Sherrington's work. Subsequently, it will be referred to in abbreviated form: Reflex Activity . .. , p.... 24. Morgan, Conway Lloyd (1852-1936), English biologist, zoopsychologist, philosopher. One of the founders of the theory of emergent evolution (see Note 90). 25. Bekhterev, Vladimir Mikhailovich (1867-1927), Russian physiologist, neuropathologist, psychologist. In the present work, Vygotsky repeatedly cites Bekheterev's investigations of emotional reactions of animals in which the brain cortex, specifically the thalamus opticus, was removed. L. S. Vygotsky dates these investigations at 1887. In 1887, a paper by Bekhterev was published in German: "Die Bedeutung der Sehhiigel auf Grund von experimentellen und pathologischen Daten." Virchows Arch., 1887, pp. 110, 102-154, 322-365. The paper sparked interest in the work of Bekhterev abroad. Thus, W. Cannon, whose data Vygotsky obviously used, cites it (W. B. Cannon, p. 115). In 18821887, Bekhterev published a number of papers in Russia on this subject, including "Induced Movements Developed with Damage to the Brain Cortex," Russkaya Meditsina [Russian Medicine], No.1, pp. 6-8; No.3, pp. 54-55; "Function of the Thalami Optici," Vrach [Doctor], 1883, No.4, pp. 51-52; No.5, pp. 68-70, and others. 26. Pieron, Henri (1881-1927), French psychologist, student of A. Binet and P. Janet. He worked in the areas of experimental psychology, zoopsychology, psychophysiology, pathopsychology, and applied psychology. 27. Maranon, Gregorio (1887-1960), Italian endocrinologist. 28. Lehmann, Alfred (1858-1921), Danish psychologist. 29. Cannon compares the results of the following investigations: C. C. Stewart, "Mammalian Smooth Muscle. The Cat's Bladder," Amer. 1 Physiol., 1900, No. 4, pp. 185-208; E. Sertoli, "Contribution a la physiologie generale des muscles lisses," Arch. Ital. Bioi., 1883, No.4, p. 86; D. N. Langley, "On the Physiology of Salivary Secretion." 1 Physiol., 1889, No. 10, p. 300; I. P. Pavlov and E. O. Schumova-Simanowskaja, "Die Innervation der Magendriisen beim Hunde," Arch. Physiol., 1895, No. 66 (see W. B. Cannon, p. 112). 30. Stewart, Colin Campbell (1873-1), American physiologist. 31. Sertoli, Enrico (1842-1910), Italian physiologist. 32. Langley, John Newport (1852-1925), English physiologist and histologist. 33. Shumova-Simanovskaya, Ekaterina Olimnievna (1852-1905), Russian physiologist, student of I. P. Pavlov. 34. Wells, Frederick Lyman (1884-1), American psychologist, specialist in cognitive processes. 35. Landis, C. (1897-1), American psychologist, specialist on developmental psychology, pathopsychology and psychology of emotions. 36. Dana, Charles (1852-1935), American physician, neurologist, medical historian, and literary critic. 37. Wilson, Samuel Alexander (1878-1937), English neurologist, specialist in aphasia. 38. Konovalov, Nikolai Vasil'evich (1900-1966), Soviet neuropathologist. Together with S. A. Wilson, he described and presented a system of therapy for hepatocerebral dystrophy ("Westphal's, Wilson's, Konovalov's disease"). 39. Kiippers, Egon (1887-1), German psychologist.

40. Bentley, Isaac Madison (1877-1955), American psychologist, specialist in general, child, and social psychology. 41. Vygotsky has in view the symposium on the problem of emotions that took place at Wittenberg College (hence the title, Wittenborgian) in Springfield. Ohio, October 19-23, 1927. The symposium marked the opening of a new psychology laboratory. Leading specialists on this problem participated, including: A. Adler, V. M. Bekhterev, E. Claparede, P. Janet, H. Pieron, W. Stern, K. Buhler, W. McDougall, W. Cannon, E. Titchener, R. Woodworth, E. R. Jaensch, M. Prince, W. Pillsbury, and others. In 1928, the materials of the symposium were published under the title Feelings and Emotions. The Wittenberg Symposium, Worcester, 1928. Vygotsky used these materials widely, particularly the papers of Brett, Claparede, Prince, Bentley, Spearman, and others (see References). Bentley did not write the Introduction to the symposium. His paper was the first published in the symposium (see M. Bentley, 1928). 42. Dal', Vladimir Ivanovich (1801-1872), see Vol. 3, p. 358. 43. Bergson, Henry (1859-1941). see Vol. 1, p. 464. At the beginning of the 1900s, he was the leader of new philosophical trends (intuitivism, "philosophy of life"). 44. Cassirer, Ernst (1874-1945). German philosopher, cultural historian, logician. Head of the neo-Kantian trend in German philosophy. One of the founders of semiotics. 45. Ach, Narciss (1871-1946), see Vol. 2, p. 483. For more details on the attitude of Vygotsky toward the works of Ach, see Thinking and Speech, Vol. 2, pp. 118-184. 46. Wertheimer, Max (1880-1943). see Vol. 1, p. 460. 47. Vygotsky imprecisely cites the words of F. Engels from Dialectics of Nature. The precise text is: "The fact is that to everyone who is involved in theoretical problems, the results of contemporary natural science intrude with the same urgency as natural science investigators-whether they want to or not-are forced to come to general theoretical conclusions." K. Marx and F. Engels, Works, Vol. 20, p. 366. 48. In view here is the following statement of J. W. Goethe: "Nature is unknowable because one person is not in a state to understand it although all of humanity might be able to understand it. But since this dear humanity is never all together, then nature can very successfully play hide and seek with us." Jzbrannye Sochineniya po Estestvoznaniyu [Collected Works on Natural Science]. Moscow, 1957, p. 400. 49. Kretschmer, Ernst (1888-1964), see Vol. 1, p. 464. 50. Kruger, Felix Emil (1874-1948), German psychologist. one of the leaders of the so-called Leipzig school. Works on the study of character, psychology of the personality, etc. 51. Leipzig school-one of the trends in German psychology of the 1920s. Also called "integral" (Ganzheit) psychology in contrast to Gestalt psychology. The principal participators worked at Leipzig University. 52. Muller. Johannes Peter (1801-1858), German physiologist and psychologist, one of the founders of physiology as an independent science. 53. Holmes, Gordon Morgan (1876-1962), English physiologist, neurologist. 54. The plan is not in Vygotsky's manuscript. 55. Woodworth, Robert (1869-1962), American psychologist. 56. Kiril'tsev. Sergei Ivanovich (1858-?), Russian physician and physiologist. 57. Tilney, Frederick (1875-1938), American psychologist, physiologist, neurologist, defectologist.

Lange, Nikolai Nikolaevich (1858-1921), see Vol. 1, p. 460. 60. Vygotsky has in view the following position of Kretschmer: "Hysterical symptoms are forms of reactions of a phylogenetic ally preexisting impulsive mental base" (1924, p. 8). 61. Archer, William (1856-1924), English drama critic. 62. Vygotsky has in view the following point in the work of H. Heine "On the History of Religion and Philosophy in Germany": "Great genius is formed with the help of another genius not so much by assimilation as through friction. Precisely so the philosophy of Descartes did not at all engender the philosophy of Spinoza, but only facilitated its appearance. For this reason, at the beginning, we meet the methods of the teacher in the student" (Works, Moscow, 1958, Vol. 6, p. 64). 63. Fischer, K. (1824-1907), German philosopher, Hegel follower, author of The History of Philosophy in six volumes (1852-1877). 64. "Short treatise on God, Man, and His Happiness," the earliest of the known works of Spinoza (1658-1660). 65. According to Descartes, the six basic forms of passions are astonishment, love, hatred, desire, joy, and grief. 66. Petzoldt, Josef (1862-1929), see Vol. 1, p. 470. 67. Asmus, Valentin Ferdinandovich (1894-1975), Soviet philosopher, logician, cultural historian. 68. Treatise on Passions . .. , or Passions of the Soul, works of Descartes (1646-1649). 69. "Teaching on the Origin and Nature of Affects" is the title of Section III of Ethics, Spinoza's principal work, finished in 1675. 70. "Teaching on the Power of the Mind or Human Freedom" is the title of Section V of Ethics. 71. H0ffding, Harald (1843-1931), Danish philosopher, historian of philosophy, and psychologist. See also Vol. 3, p. 357. 72. Nahlowsky, Josef Wilhelm (1812-1885), German philosopher, specialist in the area of ethics. 73. Herbart, Johann Friedrich (1776-1841), German philosopher, psychologist, one of the founders of scientific pedagogy. See also Vol. 1, p. 466. 74. This expression of Spinoza (Ethics, Theorem 17, Scholia) belongs to the number of favorite comparisons of Vygotsky. He concludes "The Historical Meaning of the Crisis in Psychology" with this specifically (Vol. 1, p. 436). 75. Irons, David (1870-1907), American psychologist and philosopher, specialist in the area of ethics and emotions. 76. Larguier des Bancels, Jean (1876-?), French psychologist, specialist in the area of the study of temperament and emotions. 77. Ribot, Theophil (1839-1916), see Vol. 1, p. 463. 78. Brett, George Sidney (1879-1944), Canadian historian of psychology. 79. Miinsterberg, Hugo (1863-1916), German psychologist. For Vygotsky's detailed evaluation of his methodological views, see "The Historical Meaning of the Crisis in Psychology" (Vol. 1, pp. 291-436). 80. Spearman, Charles Edward (1863-1945), English psychologist, specialist in the psychology of thinking, individual differences, and testology. HI. Vygotsky has in view the following points in the book of K. G. Lange, Mental Movements: about Spinoza: "Spinoza may be closer than anyone, perhaps, to his view of emotions" (p. 89); on Descartes: "Descartes defines joy by consciousness that the subject enjoys its blessing, but we will not find out from

274

Notes to the Russian Edition

him exactly what joy itself is" (p. 82). See Descartes, Passions of the Soul, Chapter 1, §§35-36. The same is true of C. G. Lange's book Mental Movements: grief (pp. 23-28), joy (pp. 28-31), fear (pp. 31-37), anger and rage (pp. 37-44), impatience (p. 44), disillusionment (p. 45). 82. Spranger, Edward (1882-1939), see Vol. 1, p. 465. 83. Dilthey, Wilhelm (1833-1911), German philosopher and cultural historian to whom can be traced the idea of philosophy of life as one of the idealistic trends. He proposed a plan for creating descriptive or cognitive psychology. See also Vol. 1, p. 465. 84. For the relation of explanatory and descriptive psychology from Vygotsky's point of view, see "The Historical Meaning of the Crisis in Psychology" (Vol. 1, pp. 291-431); "History of the Development of Higher Mental Functions" (Vol. 3, pp. 6-41). 85. Elizabeth von der Pfalz (1618-1680), princess, daughter of the Pfalz elector, granddaughter of Maria Stewart. She conducted a lively correspondence with Descartes, which played a role in his developing the Treatise on the Passions . . . . 86. In view here is the following position of I. Kant (Anthropology, Book 3, §71): "To be subject to facts and passions-this is, in any case, a mental illness because both the one mental movement and the other exclude the mind's control. 87. Mantegazza, Paolo (1831-1910), Italian physician, anthropologist, philosopher, psychologist. 88. Piderit, Theodor (1826-1898), German psychologist, physiologist. 89. Prince, Morton (1854-1929), American psychiatrist, specialist in pathopsychology and psychology of individual differences. 90. Emergent ("emergent" -arising unexpectedly) evolution: the conception that considers development as a stepwise process in which the appearance of higher qualities is promoted by the interference of incomprehensible forces. The developed conception of emergent evolution is presented in the works of S. Alexander, Space, Time and Deity (1927), and C. Lloyd-Morgan, Emergent Evolution (1927).

St. Augustine, Aurelius (354-430), Christian theologian who, on the basis of idealistic, free-will philosophy, developed a teaching on mental processes. See also Vol. 2, p. 488. 95. Vygotsky has in view an interpretation of the work of Maxwell, Substance and Movement (§78), translated by H. Hl,'jffding (1904, p. 60). 96. In view here is the following statement of Aristotle: "For now and heretofore, astonishment stimulates people to philosophize" (Metaphysics, Part 1, Chapter 2). Cf. Descartes, R, Passions of the Soul, Part II, §§69-76; Part III, §§211-212). 97. Descartes, R, Passions of the Soul, Part II, §75. 98. In view here is the monologue of Salieri in the first scene of Pushkin's tragedy Mozart and Salieri: "I stifled sounds/And then dissected music like a corpse." 102. St. Teresa (1515-1582), Spanish nun. Janet, Pierre (1859-1947), see Vol. 2, p. 482. 104. Bain, Alexander (1818-1903), see Vol. 1, pp. 465-466.

Notes to the Russian Edition

275

105. Having finished the work Thoughts on the First Philosophy (1636-1640), at the end of 1640 Descartes sent the manuscript to a number of people, including the eminent English philosopher, T. Hobbes (1588-1679), who was then living in Paris. In January-February 1641, Hobbes sent his comments to Descartes. Criticizing Descartes from a materialistic position, Hobbes wrote that the subject of thinking is the body that thinks, and thinking is an activity or property of that body. The spirit is not an independent substance: clear ideas of the spirit rise to clear impressions of the organs of sensory perception. Descartes included Hobbes' critical comments (like those of other opponents) and the response to them in the first edition of Thought (1641). 106. This pertains to Christina Augusta (1626-1689), Queen of Sweden in 16321654, In 1646, a friend of Descartes, Chanut, who lived in Sweden, sent Descartes the queen's questions. In February, 1647, Descartes wrote the response which is the subject here. 107. Stout, George Frederick (1860-1944), English philosopher-idealist, representative of the so-called analytical psychology. 108. See Descartes, R, Metaphysical Thought, Thought 6. 109. In view here is the comedy of A. P. Chekhov, The Cherry Orchard, Act 4. From the last retort of Fiers (going to the door, and pulling the knob): "It's locked. They left ... (he sits down on the sofa). They forgot me .... " The graphic expression "they forgot the man" refers to a favorite expression of Vygotsky. He uses it especially in "History of the Development of Higher Mental Functions," Vol. 3, p. 72. 110. There is no special work of Descartes under this title. In Descartes' On the Formation of the Animal. Description of the Human Body (1648), Section 4 is titled, "On the Formation of the Embryo; on the Parts Formed in the Sperm." 111. Freud, Sigmund (1856-1939), see Vol. 1, p. 462. 112. Scheler, Max Ferdinand (1872-1928), German philosopher-idealist and psychologist. 113. Schopenhauer, Artur (1788-1860), German philosopher-idealist who considered a "peaceful will" as the metaphysical basis for a peaceful existence. 114. This refers to the famous part of Plato's dialogue Phaedo (the time of the action is 399 B.C. and the time of the writing of the dialogue is the second half of the 480s and first half of the 470s B.c.); the Phaedo is the concluding part of a triptych, the first two parts of which are the Apology of Socrates and Crito. The Phaedo (Phaedo of Elida, pupil and friend of Socrates) dialogue recounts the last days of Socrates, his discussion with students, and his death. Socrates argues with Anaxagoras of Clazomenae, ancient Greece (circa 500-428 B.c.), a naturalist-philosopher of the materialistic trend. Socrates says that Anaxagoras ascribes the order of things-completely absurdly-to air, ether, water, and many other things, and he continues (cited according to modern translation): "In my view, it does not matter whether someone would state that Socrates was bound in all his actions to Mind, and then, undertaking to explain the reason for each of the actions separately, would say: 'Socrates is now sitting here because his body consists of bones and sinews and bones are hard and separated from each other by joints, and sinews can bend and relax and surround the bones-together with flesh and skin, which encloses everything. And since the bones move freely in their joints and sinews, relaxing and tensing, they allow Socrates to bend his legs and arms. This is why he is now stooped and sitting here (Editor: in jail).' No, to call such things causes is complete nonsense. If someone were to say that without this-without bones, sinews and everything else that I command, I would not be able to do what I

276

Notes to the Russian Edition

consider necessary, he would be right. But to maintain that they are the cause of everything I do ... is not to distinguish between the true cause and that without which cause could not be cause" (Plato, Works, 3 Vols., Moscow, 1970, Vol. 2, p. 68). 115. Brentano, Franz (1838-1917), see Vol. 1, p. 465. One of the predecessors of the idealistic trend in functional psychology and phenomenology. 116. Phiinder, Alexander (1870-1941), see Vol. 1, p. 471. 117. Geiger, Moritz (1880-?), German philosopher and psychologist who worked in the area of aesthetics. 118. In view here is the following idea of Dilthey: "True, in the study of expressive movements and symbols of conceptions for mental states, new auxiliary means are being discovered; but especially the comparative method, which introduces simpler relations between feelings and motives in animals and primitive peoples, makes it possible to go beyond the limits of the anthropology of the 17th century" (1924, p. 57). 119. In view here is the work of Malebranche, Searching for the Truth (1674). 120. Natorp, Paul Gerhardt (1854-1924), see Vol. 1, p. 471. His basic works were in the area of logic and pedagogy. 121. Bellot, Gustav (1859-1929), French philosopher, specialist in the area of ethics and the psychology of religion.

On the Problem of the Psychology of the Actor's Creative Work 1. 2.

3. 4.

5. 6. 7.

This paper was written in 1932. First published in the book: P. M. Yakobson, Psikhologiya stsenicheskikh chuvstv aktera [Psychology of the Stage Feelings of the Actor], Moscow, 1936, pp. 197-211. Diderot, Denis (1713-1784), French philosopher, educator, author of several comedies. Paradox of the Actor (1770-1773, final edition 1778, was first published in 1830) is a consideration of the nature of the actor's craft, written in the dialogue form, which Diderot favored. This was inspired by an unknown author's brochure on the eminent English actor, Garrick, a brochure which Diderot disputes. Clairon, pseudonym of Claire Hippolyte Leyris de la Tudi (1723-1803), French dramatic actress who appeared in the tragedies of Voltaire and Racine. Diderot believed that Clairon did not identify with the personages she portrayed. Dumenille, pseUdonym, real surname Marchand, Marie Francoise (1711-1803), French dramatic actress who appeared in the tragedies of Racine, Corneille and Voltaire. Diderot compared her to Clairon and believed that Dumenille did identify with her heroines. Three Sisters, a play by A. P. Chekhov, was first presented in the Moscow Arts Theater (produced by K. S. Stanislavsky and V. I. Nemirovich-Danchenko) in 1901. Paulhan, Frederick (1856-1931), see Vol. 2, p. 488. For more details on Vygotsky's attitude toward his work, see Thinking and Speech, Vol. 2, pp. 5-361. Vakhtangov, Evgenii Bagrationovich (1883-1922), Russian Soviet director, pupil of Stanislavsky. In 1913, he founded the Students' Studio of the Moscow Arts Theater, later renamed the Third Studio, and in 1921, a theater (as of 1926, the Evg. Vakhtangov Theater).

Notes to the Russian Edition

277

8. Princess Turandot, a play by Carlo Gozzi (1762), a subject borrowed from Nisami through Lesage, was rehearsed in the Vakhtangov studio from 1920, premiered in 1922, and since that time has been part of the repertoire of the Evg. Vakhtangov Theater as a season opener. 9. Zakhava, B. E. (1896-1976), Soviet actor, director, historian, and theoretician of the theater and pedagogue. He was a pupil of Vakhtangov. 10. The statements of Stanislavsky cited here and below are manuscript materials from his archive. Vygotsky cites from the book of L. Ya. Gurevich (1927). 11. Gurevich, Lyubov' Yakovlevna (1866-1940), Russian Soviet writer, translator, and historian of the theater. 12. The book of L. Ya. Gurevich, Creativity of the Actor, was subtitled, "Resolution of an Age-Old Argument." Gurevich defined the substance of the argument thus: is it or is it not necessary for an actor "to pretend" on the stage (p. 5).

WORKS OF L. S. VYGOTSKY The bibliography of the works of L. S. Vygotsky includes titles of monographs, papers, reviews, forewords, lectures, reports, and speeches as well as the editing of a number of publications. The multifaceted scientific-research and pedagogical activity of Vygotsky was reflected in numerous lectures and reports in academic institutions in Moscow, Leningrad, Kharkov, and Thshkent; he participated in many congresses, conferences, plena, and meetings. Manuscript texts of reports, speeches, and papers of Vygotsky which are not part of the present publication were found in archive sources. It was possible to retrieve a number of papers of literary criticism which Vygotsky wrote when he was 19 to 22 years old. Vygotsky sometimes signed some papers and notes published in the journals Letopis' and Novaja Zhizn' in 1916-1922 as "L. S." or "L. v." Of the works that were found signed with these initials, only those are included in the bibliography whose authorship was established either by comparison with subsequent published work of the scientist or by a mention of these papers and notes found in the personal archive of Vygotsky. The material in the bibliographic index is listed in chronological order according to the years in which the work was written; this will make it easier for the reader to trace the evolution of the scientific views of the scientist. In cases where the date of writing of the manuscript has not been established, the title is included in the bibliographic index of the year of publication. Within each year, the works are listed in [Russian] alphabetical order. If the books or papers were published several times, all the editions are listed under the first publication with an indication of the nature of the change in the text. The bibliography of the works of L. S. Vygotsky published abroad is in the form of a list of works according to the year of publication, and within the year the titles are listed in alphabetical order according to the Latin alphabet. In compiling the bibliography, the following materials were used: 1. 2. 3. 4. S. 6.

The general alphabetical and systematic catalogues, Central Reference Office and the Information-Bibliographic Department of the Lenin State Library of the USSR. Newspapers, journals, and collections beginning with 1916. Soviet bibliographic directories and chronicles. Psychological Abstracts, Lancaster, American Psychological Association. 19311983, N 7S, Vols. 1-70. Index Translationum, Paris, UNESCO Press, Vols. 1-30. Archive materials: (a) A. 1. Herzen Pedagogical Institute, Leningrad. (b) Scientific-Research Institute of Defectology, Academy of Pedagogical Sci283

284

Works of L. S. Vygotsky ences, USSR. (c) Scientific-Research Institute of General and Pedagogical Psychology of the Academy of Pedagogical Sciences, USSR. (d) State Archive of the Moscow Oblast'. (e) Personal archive of L. S. Vygotsky preserved by the family of the scientist. (f) Scientific archive of the Academy of Pedagogical Sciences of the USSR.

The present bibliography is compiled in accordance with the All-Union State Standard in effect for bibliographic description of printed works (GOST [AllUnion Standard] 7.1.76* of 28 December, 1976, No. 2890. In effect as of 1 January, 1978). This list of works, based on the "Bibliography of the Works of L. S. Vygotsky" (see VcJprosy Psikhologii [Problems in Psychology], 1974, No.3), is extended and supplemented by the same author.

WORKS ABOUT L. S. VYGOTSKY This list is the first systematic collection of basic literature devoted to L. S. Vygotsky. It includes monographs, articles, and dissertations published in Russian to 31 December, 1983, and in other languages to 31 December, 1982; only the first editions are listed. The list includes two types of studies. First, works devoted entirely to presentation and analysis of works of Vygotsky as a whole or of any of its aspects; second, works that give a complete (although possibly brief) presentation (analysis, criticism) of the works of Vygotsky (its separate aspects) in one context or another (corresponding pages in the monograph are indicated in parentheses). Not included are works in which Vygotsky's name is only mentioned (containing a reference), but in which there is no integral analysis of his views. This list must not be taken as exhaustive since the criteria of selection of works of the second type were quite arbitrary; also, it is probable that some works did not come to our attention. Some works (a very small number) we deliberately did not consider suitable for inclusion in this list: we are speaking of some critical works of the 1930s that do not meet elementary criteria of scientific [technical] correctness. Works in Original Russian
